Review:
I have over 1000 miles on it. Overall, this is an okay bike. However, be warned: The seller I purchased from included both a 15 mph speed limiter connected, and the tires are pure garbage. I lost traction on wet asphalt and crashed because the tires had so little tread left, less than a few hundred miles in (I read that on average, a tire can last *at least* 1k+ miles of road travel. I replaced one without a problem until the end of last month, due to a puncture. I am now currently in bike shop hell, trying to find a tube and tire combo that won't flatten or puncture within 3 days of use.

I notice an issue with the pedals. Both reflectors just vanished. I'm not sure when or where, but they are no longer there. Additionally: The noise. They sound loose, creaky, and just terrible. They sound as though they could break or fall off at any moment. I've tried taking them apart and cleaning, but obviously to no avail so far.

Another issue is the derailleur. Any heavy force pedaling to scale a hill, or even to get going, the gears can slip, and I can unchain the bike pretty easily just by pedaling backward. Can this be fixed? Sure! however, this doesn't seem like it should be a problem this soon into the bike's life.

Yet another issue is the battery. It is super obvious that the battery charge capacity is much lower than it was when I first embarked. I could go a couple of trips to and from work between charges. Now, I can just make it to work and back on a single charge.

Review:
Reading the 1 star reviews I don't agree with any of them. The battery lasts fairly long and can last longer if you don't always have it on full blast! The bike has fast pick up and it goes around 18 miles an hour, not 11. The folding is very easy and I guess it's heavy but it's also a solid bike with a frame that's literally a handle so I felt moving it around and folding it was very easy. Meaning, this bike was designed with commuting in mind and is very effective. I had a folding bike before and this one is easier. I love the headlight and it's a comfortable ride. The bike is useable without power BUT the small wheels limit the amount of joy in riding it without power. The gears help a little but small wheels are small wheels. No matter how many gears you stick on it, you'll only go so far per rotation so it only goes so fast. I felt the ride on full throttle was comparable to an e-scooter. Oh and it will get you up a very steep hill. You can also use the peddling to compensate if the hill is super steep. I rode it up the side of a mountain road. WHY did I return it and only give it four stars? Brakes squeak like crazy. Support told me ways to adjust but I don't want a new bike that I had to fix. If the next one squeaks I like the bike enough that I'll keep it and work through the squeak but that was a real bummer. Other than that I really like it. Another bonus is that the battery and light are built into the frame so nobody can really steal anything off you. Again, they thought of a lot to make this a great commuter bike.

Update--got a new bike and the brakes do not squeak! So I was sent a bad bike.

Update 2--two years later. Added a bike rack for a saddle bag and also a post for a trailer/and a bike trailer. Both of them work well with the bike. The bike was getting flats more than normal so my bike guy put in that no flat foam (green) and I haven't had flat in over a year of daily use. Brakes will go every five months. This might have to do with the fact that I am always driving this on high speed and the brakes have to slow it down. Other than that I still think it's a great bike. I would like maybe a bike with bigger wheels but then it defeats the purpose of the small size. On that note I did buy a bag to put the bike in when it's folded, which is great for ubers and buses if anyone gives you any crap just say it's an English horn. And finally, I did have a battery issue which was making the bike jolt. Customer service walked me through what might be wrong. I bought a replacement battery and the problem went away. Customer service isn't bad. They guy on the phone just doesn't come off as chatty but he knows what you're talking about and will help you out. If you do need to send it back they will pay for delivery shipping after they fix it (post warranty) but not to send it to them. It's actually cheaper to ship to them then take it to a local place because many places don't have the parts for this bike. That being said, Swagtron does have parts issue if you have an older version of the bike. They released different types of these bikes but they look the same and are called the same model.

Review:
This bike blew me away, for the price it is on the lower end of recumbent bikes but it feels very polished and works great. I was resitant to get this since I made the mistake of buying a cheap upright stationary bicycle (from another manufacturer) a while back and had an awful experience, I was very pleasantly surprised to find that this experience was nothing like that.

THE BIKE:
The bike is quite comfortable, I have a history of back injuries that prevents me from using upright bikes but I found this seat to be quite comfortable, similar to a mesh office chair. I like the mesh better than the faux-leather seat that a lot of commercial recumbent bikes have because it actually lets some air flow around you, instead of just collecting a pool of sweat. It's not hard to clean either since nothing absorbs into the mesh.

THE FEATURES:
The features on the head unit are admittedly not super advanced, it doesn't do fancy graphs and metrics like some of the commercial bikes (which of course run 3 times the price) but it has speed and distance which is really all I care about. It gives enough info to compare ride to ride that I'm perfectly happy with it.

THE PETALS:
The petals were a little weird at first, they're very wide and the straps are a little awkward. They took a little getting used to but they work fine. I don't find myself fully engaging the straps very often so I may just remove them entirely (which would not be difficult).

ASSEMBLY:
Assembly was not the quickest as there are lots of pieces, but the instructions were easy enough to follow that I didn't find myself stuck at any point. One things that was a little weird was that the package of screws had labels on one side, but the screws were accessed from the opposite side of the package, so I found myself flipping it back and forth a bunch trying to send screws flying everywehre just to figure out which size screw I needed for each step. In hindsight I should have just written the label on the back of the packaging. It worked but was a little frustrating.

ADJUSTABILITY:
I'm 6'-0" and I fit just fine into this bike with room to slide the seat in either direction for a shorter/taller person. If I had to estimate I'd say someone up to about 6'-4" could probably fit this.

SUMMARY:
All in all I'm super happy with this and would definitely recommend it. It doesn't have all the bells and whistles that the high end ones do but it works great, sits comfortably, and is a great addition to my home gym.

Review:
[[VIDEOID:f9fa9f49ce345fb4d905d8b780a2e9bc]] I am a huge supporter of balance bikes. We got balance bikes for our children and they all were able to ride bikes without training wheels much earlier than most children - we have 3 kids. Our second and third had balance bikes at 18 months and were riding full bikes without training wheels before 4 years. The posted video is my daughter riding a bike with no training wheels at 3 years 9 months old.

Good quality

Review:
Update #3, 2017-09-23. Adding photos and some more riding and maintenance notes. You can also see some videos I am posting on YouTube for this bike.

I had a spare mechanical disk brake caliper available, with slightly bigger brake pads and disk, so I installed it in place of the original front brake setup. I did it because my original front brake was not operating smoothly (with a pulsing feel, which could lead to an easier wheel lock-up on sand or slippery surfaces). The new brake is smooth. The original rear brake is smooth too. I suspect the original front disk was not of uniform thickness, causing the pulsation (it was true and straight).

I did a couple of range tests and it seems the asvertised range is realistic. Ebikes' range is highly dependent on how one rides them, the terrain, weight of the rider, tire pressure, etc. I did my 25 mile round-trip commute to work almost exclusively in PAS 4 (assist level 4 out of 5), with some lower assist levels here and there when I needed to slow down and almost no level 5 assist (max). Starting at 42V fully charged battery, I came back hone at 36.7V, which is not bad - I could probably pedal at keast another 10 miles the same way, for a total of well over 30 miles. That was done at speeds faster than most regular bikes with recreational riders on them, with less effort, but a little sllower than full on race or light commuter bikes with strong and determined riders on them. The trip has many long not steep hills, some shirt steep ones, and almost no flat areas. Average spees was about 12 miles ler hour. On another trip, I went faster and managed to empty the battery complety in only about 20 miles, using a good mix of PAS 4 and 5. If I stay on PAS 5 all the time, I suspect I won't get more than 15 miles at full power in the typical hilly terrain around here. On flat roads, with 20psi or more in the tires, I think it will do better, but hub motors don't like hills and become inefficient (compared to mid-drives) at slow speeds and steep hills.

Bottom line, if looking for a fun bike that can keep-up (and then some) with regular recreational riders on regular bikes, and you don't have many long steep hills, this is a good option. If you expect it to perform like a more powerful and efficient mid-drive bike, you will be disappointed.

The motor is not loud but is not silent. It is quieter than the tires in most situations, except at full load during acceleration before it reaches cruising speed at whatever PAS level. There is some gear noise that is not terrible but compared to my Bafang BBSHD drive, which is virtually silent, this is a bit noisier under load.

The 5 PAS levels are not spaced very well and I think the power ramps-up a bit too aggressively from stop or low speeds. Say the bike is in PAS 3, which tops out at a very reasonable 15 miles per hour or so. From a stop or slower speeds the bike tries to apply full power, and only gradually reduces power very close to top speed for the PAS level. I would prefer lower power in the first three lower PAS modes but higher speed limits. PAS 4 and 5 can be more powerful, this is OK. But I want a mode that would keep-up 10-15 miles without overzealously trying to accelerate to that speed every time I slow down a bit. Having throttle would be helpful for that, but the controller does not provide connections for one (alternative controllers can be had for $15-$30 that supprt a throttle, and I might upgrade mine down the road).

Update #2, 2017-09-17. I finished adjusting the bike. I further fine-tuned brakes, so now they are firm and strong. I can lock the rear wheel easily and the front is plenty strong too. They are very noisy (screechy), feel rough, and the stopping is not smoothly uniform as the disk turns through each full rotation. Will give them a few more miles and hard stops to see if they settle any better against the disks. Maybe they need further adjustment and cleaning... A few other things are now apparent. First, the deraileur is bent out of shape, and I think whoever assembled the bike did it on purpose - if it is straight, it rubs against the rim in 1st gear and the chain rubs against the tire. The motor vibrates a bit around 20-24kmh under power (PAS 4 and 5). The vibration is felt through the frame and seat and is heard as the rear disk resonates and vibrates and makes a buzzing sound noise. This is only in max power mode, level 5 assist. The bike is smooth and more quiet at lpwer and faster speeds, and no issues under pedal power only at any speed. This only develops under hard acceleration. I'll call the seller and Vilano bikes tomorrow to see if they can help resolve the situation, as this noise is not normal. It seems to imlrove as the bike breaks in (or maybe I am getting used to it).

2017-09-17, day one, morning riding. Surprisingly good power on uphills - I am 200 lb and it can't climb the steepest hills without pedaling help, but makes it very easy, and no issues with medim-steep hills. The spokes are not tensioned enough, especially on the rear wheel - they vibrate audibly at full power, will need to tension them more. This bike stands quite high above the ground: the frame seems like it is made for and can accomodate 24" wheels with up to 2" tires - lots of clearance on the 20" fat tires (except on the side of the rear tire with the chain. The tall tires and large frame bring the pedals quite high above the ground. Good clearance, but at proper saddle height, I can't reach the ground with my foot. That would not be an issue if you like to ride with the seat low, but if you like to ride with the seat up and set for road riding with good leg extension, you may need to be coming off the bike at stops. The battery seems OK so far. I only did about 8 miles this morning at moderate speeds and some hills, and the charge is still well above 36V. So at medium assist levels I expect to be able to do at least 20-30 miles per charge. Range test will need to wait until the wheels are tensioned. One problem is that the display does not show voltage, only a graphic of the battery state of charge. I can see there is sag under full power, but do not know how many volts. I am measuring the battery voltage with multimeter at the bike light wires or the battery terminals when the bike is stopped. The fat tires absorb imperfections in the road very well. The frame is quite sturdy for a folder, though some flex is felt in the steerer. The bike is stable at speed, does not wobble and can ride hands off, but like any small-wheeled bike it is not as stable as a big-wheeled one. More to follow ...

First impressions below (2017-09-16 ):

I just received the Vilano Neutron bike, assembled it, and rode around the block for a few minutes. So this is just an initial impressions light review. Will update as I ride the bike more. I will add video soon.

Super fast shipping from Florida. Came well-packed with no damage at all due to shipping. Good fit and finish, better than I expected from a $550 ebike.

Assembly is easy if you have done basic bike assembly. No tools are included. You will need hex wrenches, philips head screwdriver, and flat and/or socket wrenches for assembly and adjustmet.

Adjustment is not optional! The bike will not work eell or at all without it! Plan to pay for it if you can't do it yourself, but it is basic and standard, so any bike shop could do it. Amazon offers free assembly, take it if you are not sure you can do it (but learn to do it, it is fairly easy, you will need to do it again in the future).

The bike comes with a big, but generic english manual, so many instructions are not relevant to this particular bike. And key instructions are missing. Thre are *no* online instructions on the manufacturer web site for this model either. What is missing is important - how to operate and setup the computer and how to connect the light (the two wires for the front light are unmarked as are the connectors on the back of the light, so unclear which is positive and which is negative - this is a 36V LED light, and unclear if it matters or not if one were to swap the cables).

My bike came almost fully charged, reading 41V (fully chsrged is about 42V, the battery rating is 36V nominal, 8.8Ah. No info on the cell chemistry or individual cell rating is provided. The charger is fanless, totally quiet, rated at 42V and 2A. Mine measures at 41.8V, which is close enouh and came with sturdy USA spec 3-prong plug and cable.

The front wheel hub on mine came way too tight, the axel was turning rough and difficult. I adjusted it before installing the wheel, and was able to make it turn smoothly and without looseness. If I had left it overtightened, the bearings would probably go bad quickly. The rear feels fine as it is.

The front disk brake caliper was mounted way out of alignment, so it needed to be loosened and aligned with the disk. This is expected, no big deal. The rear disk brake was aligned fairly well.

The disk brakes are not great, as most mechanical cheap 160mm disk brakes are. Probably stop better than a cheap V brake, but nowhere near as well as the good disk brakes on my other bike do (but one of these good brakes costs about 1/3 as much as this entire ebike, so this can be excused). Usually as the disks and pads break-in, the stopping power shoild increase.

There are electric power cutoff switches in the brake levers, so the assist will stop if you try to brake. Cable management is decent, with most wires tucked out of the way well.

If your front light is crooked, like mine was, don't try to force it straight, you will break the plastic. Remove it, then use a screwdriver or hex wrench to twist the mounting bracket to point forward and to eliminate any side to side tilt too.

This bike will work well for tqll folks, in my estimate at least up to 6'5" (195cm). The seat post, at it's tallest setting brings the seat higher than on my full size bike, which is a lot, given I have a 37"+ inseam. At 6'4" I have almost an extra half inch of seat height that I will not be using even at full leg extension. The handlebars can can go higher than the seat by about 5". The distance between the handlebars and seat is just a little shorter than on my mountain bike, so the riding position is very upright.

This is a big (tall) bike, it will not be suitabke for very short people or small kids.
- Mine weights 48lb with battery installed (with fairly light aftermarket platform pedals). Has a very convenient carry handle at the baseof theseat post, so it is easy to carry assembled.
- The top of the seat is 44" from the ground at max. extension (to the maximum mark on the seat post).
- The handlebar height is adjustable, and they can't go very low, lowest is about even with the seat at 44", at the max. height mark is 49", or about 5" above the seat.
- At their highest (seat and handle bars up to max) the riding position is almost fully upright.
- From the center of the top of the seat post horizontally measured to the center of the handlebars tube is 25.5".
- Top of seat to top of pedal is 38.5".
- The bottom bracket is 2" forward of the seat post line.
- From center of BB to top of seat is 32"
- Tire diameter is 22" (set that in the bike computer for accurate speed measurement, mine came default at 20")
- The seat is moderately big, feels comfortable.
- Seatpost diameter I measure at 31.7mm, 54-55cm long.

Why 4 stars?
- The battery pack rattles inside the frame a lot. Will need to put some thick foam or other material to secure it better. Noise aside, the battery moving about will likely cause issues with the electrical contacts. And the added vibration might physically damage it internally.
- The front light might brake if the handlebars are allowed to twist backwards (no mechanical stop to prevent this, the light will hit the frame)
- Poor or missing assembly and use instructions
- Terribly noisy tires (4" knobby tires do that, not u expected, but quite annoying)
- The rims are fairly true side to side, but are somewhat uneven around the area where the rim seam is, so not exactly round, and not sure if this can be easily fixed (or at all).
- The rubber cover on the charge port does not fit and does not stay put.
- No fenders.
- Slight wobble in the steering tube assembly due to the folding mechanism not being super tight.
- The chain slightly rubs the rear tire in 1st gear (the tires are too fatfor where the chain is).

Stay tuned for more.

Review:
I waited a bit to provide my review of two Eahora bicycles. I wanted a 100+ miles of use before evaluating my first e-bike. Prior to these bikes both my wife and I road Specialized hardtail mountain bikes for years.
We purchased the EX100 (26 inch tires) and the AM 100 (27.5 tires) In general we could not be more happy regarding the bikes. I am pretty old, so I do not do off road mountain biking any longer. My wife never did. The bikes are capable of trail riding, but I would get the EX 100 should that be your activity choice. The AM 100 while a more sophisticated bike is a bit more difficult to handle on trails due to its size and my age (70+ - I have no business on mountain trails any longer). The EX 100 is far more nimble and responsive, though both are capable bikes.
I can not over praise the 48v, 10.4 amp battery. We have charged them once in our 100+ miles. We use them strictly to assist on hill climbing. They do hold a charge. I can crank it up to over 20+ mph with battery assist on level ground and the 350 watt motor will provide a lot of help on steeper hills (We live in the Ozarks of Northwest Arkansas). In all fairness, the 500 watt motor was not available when we purchased the bikes or I would have gone for it on the AM 100.
The bikes we easy to assemble, but the written instructions were pretty skimpy. I used U-Tube tutorials when necessary. The bikes were well packaged, save the battery, which was floating free in the box on both bikes. One of the battery end caps was cracked. Eahors’s customer service was responsive and persistent, even though I ended up effecting the repair by my choice. I am satisfied with the outcome.
The bottom line is I would purchase a bike from this company again without reservation. They make a quality product at a reasonable price. They have solid customer service. I trust the bikes will last many years.

Expect soon to start pleasure and leisure riding about town to local events and onto park trails.  Though got my bike several months ago my slow start had to do with gathering accessories. Review:
First surprise out of the box was the badging: Kingttu instead of Eurobike. But I noticed it was identical to the Eurobike--including Eurobike badging elsewhere on the frame, etc. Different name, same bike. Second surprise was no quick release on front wheel as mentioned in the manual. But I couldn't see that on photos of the bike itself online. Third surprise was the rims seemed to wobble. I first thought they must be bent or not trued correctly. But long story short it turned out that the tires were molded improperly. Fortunately the manufacturer was very good and responsive about correcting that situation and I was able to get the tires replaced. They were polite, apologetic and cooperative.

Also fortunate that the next surprise was a good one: The bike didn't need much of anything as far as adjustment. Just a little tightening up here and there. First time in decades of biking that I didn't have to adjust anything out of the box so to speak.

And now having ridden it for a bit, I will need to adjust the front derailleur. And not very much. This is perfectly normal. On Any bike the cables stretch, and so one or more of the derailers usually need a bit of adjustment after a few rides. Bikes are not plug and play... Please do not be upset if they need some adjustment right off the bat or shortly thereafter.

Speaking of riding, especially at this price point the bike handles well. As I'm a little older now I'm not exactly a boulder hopping maniac anymore. But I did hit some appreciable speeds on decent trails. No complaints.

Speaking of which... I strongly believe value/performance for the money is the most important measure. This bike is not perfect. But neither is my old Pontiac, which is economical, reliable, practical... But it would be ludicrous to compare it to a Porsche, a BMW... But sadly in all manner of reviews I see such "apples and peaches" comparisons all the time. But again for the money, I'm very happy with the bike. In going to the bike shop (something I try to avoid at nearly all costs because I'm invariably told directly or indirectly how crappy my cheap bike truly is) I told them about a $150 mountain bike that I rode hard for nearly 20 years. And it was still fine. I just needed to upgrade to one with a suspension. (I won't mention the brand as they have gone downhill in quality.) In the meantime I had acquired a few other cheap bikes which at best were not very good. This Kingttu/Eurobike is much better. The only remaining question is how long it will hold up over time. But I don't foresee unreasonable problems.

And again as a matter of proper perspective, most of what are considered to be true trail bikes for the serious enthusiast cost thousands of dollars. (The cheapest bike in the shop where I went was around $1, 300. And that's with no suspension.) But for the average user, for someone like myself looking for easy to moderate trails or for some street riding, this bike fits the bill. But for someone looking to do heavy use on more brutal trails, at this price point you are comparing a Porsche to a Pontiac. My car wouldn't last at high speed or perform anywhere near as well as the Porche, but that doesn't mean it's not a good car (At over 230,000 miles it still gets over 30 miles per gallon and barely uses any oil.) And directly that this isn't a good bike. Suspension bikes ironically are more delicate because they have more moving parts. That older bike I had forever had no suspension. I have little doubt if it did it would not have lasted nearly as long. As they wear out suspension bikes start to handle wonky and can become unsafe without proper maintenance--which can become cost prohibitive on an old bike.

So... Yes, for the money this is a very good bike. Comfortable ride. Handles well. Nice gear range. I like the shifters... No complaints. (But as always as I'm north of 50 I replaced the seat for more comfort. And the pedals as well as that's something I've done on every bike I've ever gotten. I like a little bit more grip so I don't slip off. And they nearly always seem the cheapest or worst part of a bike straight out of the box. I've also tossed in some more bucks for other modifications like bullhorn grips on the handlebars, and a carrier and pack so I can repair the bike if necessary on the trail. But again I would do the same for any bike. It truly isn't fun to break down or get a flat miles from nowhere with no means to repair the bike--and that has happened to me. Lesson learned.

And before I end this review I should talk a little bit about brakes. One of the main reasons I chose this bike is I've noticed that most others in my price range essentially now have side pull brakes. As delicately and politely as I can put it, they are not fun to work on. They are not reliable... Center pull are fine but I was surprised that I didn't see them anywhere. Unlike the side pull, they pull equally on each brake pad and so a whole bucket full of problems are averted.

As for disc brakes, they work great but I would recommend watching a video or two about how to properly break them in and care for them. Mine started to squeal and I learned a few things as I had never had them before. They are now fine. (I just cleaned them up a bit in this case. Didn't need to pull the pads or anything.) But in general they should be properly broken in and maintained. Again bikes are not plug and play...

(As a PostScript since writing this review I've done some research as I couldn't seem to find center pull brakes anywhere. Sadly they've become mostly obsolete... pretty much relegated to "vintage" bikes. (Apparently I'm showing my age.) I read that side pull brakes are "better" as they are easier to adjust. I sincerely hope they have Vastly improved over the years because that was definitively Never my experience with them. Center pull brakes provide more road clearance as well, and the Only time I needed to touch them was when changing out brake pads. That's it. Can't say the same for side pull brakes (I even went to a bike shop or two to have my side pull brakes adjusted and they didn't have any better success than I did. A waste of time and money.) But they are cheaper to manufacture so I think that plays into the equation quite heavily. So personally I'm going to play it safe and stick with disc brakes.)

Review: Generally, I am satisfied with this bike. At 190 pounds it zips me along at 15 mph with no problem. The only thing that I dislike about the bike is a safety consideration. With only 14" wheels there isn't much gyroscopic stability that you have come to expect with bikes with larger diameter wheels and an angled front fork. When I was a kid my bike would almost steer itself. I could take my hands off the handlebars, and the bike would track well. On this little bike I have to keep both hands on the handlebars to feel that I am in control of the steering. I am not even comfortable giving hand signals for a turn. Bumps on the pavement are also more of a problem with the small wheels. My strategy is to go slowly and to wear my helmet.

The disc brakes are fantastic. Much smoother than pads. The gears allow you to go up the steepest Hills sitting down. Smooth shifting gears. Handlebar grips are good and shifting levers on the handlebar are easily accessed. This bike is 7.5 pounds lighter than my previous Schwinn and it is a joy to own and I would buy another. It has a racier feel to it and takes corners at a higher speed better. 4 to 8 weeks following my Vantage F2 purchase I added the following items to my Schwinn Vantage F2 bicycle:
Anodized black number 5 and number 6 mounting screws with washers of varying lengths approximately $55. (Just made a big big difference instead of that bright chrome. Now you cannot even see the screws even up close they are almost invisible)

UDATE JUNE 5, 2020, after two years and eight months I haven’t many problems other than replacing three spokes on the rear wheel. I weigh 245 pounds so some unexpected bumps can be hard on the spokes. I took it to my bike guy thinking I had one spoke broken and he said I had three. I was kind of embarrassed. So I clean the chain with kerosene with a chain brush and use WD-40 bicycle chain lube on the chain and then wipe it dry. In between the kerosene wash and applying the WD-40 I wash down the chain and 91% isopropyl alcohol to get a good drying affect and let everything dry really well, overnight before applying WD 40 bicycle chain lube.
The spoke replacements cost me $30 so that’s my total maintenance cost since the time that I have owned the bike.
OCTOBER 28,2020 UPDATE FAIRNESS: When I received this bicycle in a box I took it to my bike guy and had him assemble it I think it was for 40 or $50 including tuning and whatever else but I live in the Midwest and he does this on the side from his machine shop job. I also live in the country and not in a big city. Well now I do need to have my gears tuned a little bit for the 567 and 8 so that will cost me about $35. But I will give the chain a good wash with kerosene 91% Iopropyl alcohol and WD 40 bicycle chain lube first and see what happens. 99% of the time it fixes most problems. And I have not cleaned my chain for about a couple months.
UPDATE 10/27/2022: My bike man thinks I received a bad set of wheels. He has over 40 years of experience and owns his own machine shop of several tens of thousands of square feet that includes plasma laser cutters advanced CNC machines and he can even build bicycles. Since owning the bicycle we have replaced approximately 15 spokes on the rear wheel alone and approximately two on the front. He said he has a couple customers that way well over 300 pounds on bicycles that have no spark problems. so earlier this year in 2022 I purchased a pair of Bucklos carbon fiber hub straight spoke wheel sets and Continental Gatorskin tires and tubes 700 x 25C. They are essentially bald tires and they have improved the performance of the bike immensely by reducing the rolling resistance by about 25%. Also, no spoke breakage. With straight spokes it is very uncommon to break I spoke unlike J-spokes which are curved at the hub. As my bike man says, and he has no hyperbole in his vocabulary, a good set of wheels is the most beneficial improvement anyone can make to a bicycle.
Therefore I downgraded my rating from a five star to a four star. Mainly because he had never seen anything like this when he had the bike shop open for 20 years.

Review:
I'm not sure if they are calling this bike a "BMX" style because it has pegs, or because it doesn't have a foot brake, but that is about it. Call it what it is. It is a normal 1 speed bike. There is nothing wrong with that, just say what it is. When I was growing up, a BMX freestyle bike not only had foot pegs, but it had the front handlebar mechanism that allowed you to spin the handle bars 360 degrees with out the break wires getting in your way. You cannot do that with this bike because the rear break cable gets in the way.

So knowing this, this is what the bike is about. Its a single gear/speed. Does not have a foot break. It does have front and rear hand breaks. It does NOT come with a kickstand (that is a big con), and it does not have a quick adjust seat. You need an allen wrench to adjust the seat. (another big con). My BMX bike as a kid, we took the kick stand off when we were doing tricks so they don't get in the way, but since this is not a BMX style bike, it should have a kick stand.

Putting it together wasn't too bad. One of the bike pedal threads were screwed up so it took a lot of work to get the pedal on, but I eventually got it. Breaks were a pain to properly adjust.

In my opinion, this is a way over priced "normal" bike with out a kickstand or quick seat adjustment. You can get similar bikes from other places with the same or better quality and features for about half the price.

Give it a kick stand, quick seat adjustment, and reduce the price A LOT, then it becomes a 5 star bike.

Review:
About a year ago I decided I wanted to ride to get back in shape. I live in an area that lends itself to bicycling, with lots of parks and destinations and bike paths, I felt a cruiser type bike was best. My wife purchased me a simple Kent that I really liked. Wanting her to join me, I went on a search for a bike for her to replace her single speed cruiser. I once owned a very nice aluminum Trek mountain bike I loved, and one of the things that attracted me initially to this bike was it also was aluminum. Once her 7 speed EVRYjourney (in blue) arrived, I was so impressed I bought one for myself.

I am 6'1 and my wife is 5'9. We bought a mens grey 7 speed, and a blue woman's 7 speed. I have put nearly 200 miles on mine, and my wife just over 100 so far. I waited to write this review until I was confident about how I felt about these bikes.

I really love this bike. I mean, I really love them both. The color is great, both of them are just stunning. I work from home and I have had two customers comment on the bikes when my garage was open. The laid back seat and frame design is very comfortable for the both of us. The rear rack is awesome, and I purchased the matching bags that sit atop the rack. Those bags are OK - they fit and look good, but would be nice if they were a little stiffer on the sides. I also purchased the handlebar bags, which hold a lock, and whatever else I need. They match well. I also really like the fact it has the screw mounts for a water bottle cage (Though I stuck a JBL flip in those, one matches the woman's blue teal color exactly). I also bought matching bells from sixthreezero, and those are awesome too. Plus a cupholder with a phone holder, because I want to "cruise" sometimes too. Not just work out. This review isn't about the accessories, but if it helps anyone, they match well and I would buy them again. I will say I love the rack and the ability to carry stuff to the park or to a friends house.

The seat, for me, is great. My wife feels hers is as good or better than any other bike she's had - but she is interested in one of the larger seats sixthreezero has that match the brown color she has now. We may try it for her. The grips on the handlebars feel great, and they look cool as well. The tires are a street type tire, and so far neither has gotten a flat. I had a number of flats on mine and my kids bikes in the past, and until I bought decent tires and tubes it seemed like it never stopped. Could just be good luck I guess.

Shifting, pedals, all that... Seems smooth. Im not so hardcore I would really have much opinion beyond the fact that I have had zero issues. I am very mechanically inclined, and I didn't have any trouble assembling these. I took my time and made sure everything was adjusted well, but in reality I didn't do much. The brakes and gearing was fine out of the box, I just fine tuned it.

This bike has brought a lot of joy into my life, and so far has enabled me to lose over 50 pounds. The level of comfort has drawn me to now ride it every day, for at least 6 miles. Now that I am used to riding, this bike makes me enjoy the ride and I forget I am working out. For someone who was sedentary and out of breath riding only a half mile to start, I can go 30 minutes to an hour in comfort. It's hard enough to start exercising, but the style, comfort, and enjoyment I get on this bike keeps me getting on it every day.

If you're worried about assembly, a bike shop doesn't charge much, and I would totally pay it if I wanted this bike and didn't feel comfortable assembling it. I considered an electra as well, but I actually wanted to assemble my own bike, I like saving some money, and every time I see an Electra now I think the logos and stuff are not nearly as nice.

I also have a Schwinn child carrier I pull sometimes, that works great as well.

5/5 would buy again. Both bikes, all accessories, and if I could I would gift this bike to everyone I care about. I attached a photo, if anyone wants to know how awesome my bike really is. :)

Review:
Bike seems very sturdy and well built and whoever designed it put a lot of thought into the display features. It did need a little TLC however out of the box. Firstly, there were so many zip ties used, and some so tightly, I actually ended up scratching the frame removing one. Ya, you could say its my fault, but only partially. On top of that, the chain fell off as soon as I got on and did some peddling. Either it didnt get set on right by the factory, or I actually messed with the gears somehow. Anyway, it took me 2-3 days of research finding out how to remove and reattach using quick links, and the whole nine yards. But hey, if you are going to be a heavy cyclist, you should know these things anyway. Also the chain did scratch the frame during this incident. Ughh.

Finally, the bike did not get me up a very steep hill on pure throttle but I am a fat guy at 200 lbs so who knows, maybe my fault. With PAS 5 though, it was for me easy. I still had to exert so I will switch to PAS 9 next time and see how that goes. Yes, it goes that high.

The frame though, and the features, more than make up. Charging took only 3 hrs first time but maybe there was already a charge. I like the fact you can lock the battery. It appears will be very fun to ride. It is a cadence sensor, meaning the PAS kicks in based on your pedal rotation.

Support after sales is very attentive, and kind, and responsive. They filmed a special video for me to explain something. Overall, I recommend this bike.

Review:
To say I'm pissed off about this bike/purchase, it would be a severe understatement. Thus far, this review has no bearing on the actual quality of the bike, but the condition in which I received it and the decision making (or lack thereof) in regards to stickers. I'm well aware that this isn't $5k bike, but these issues have nothing to do with quality of components. Keep in mind for the things I'm discussing, I purchased two different bikes from DiamondBack, this being one. Problems were nearly identical between the two.

1) The axle lock-nuts were so tight that the wheels would only rotate for a couple revolutions with a decent hand spin. I'm no bike expert, but I knew something was up. When I removed the wheels to inspect the bearings, I could barely spin the axle with my fingers. It felt like the bearings were so compressed that if I attempted to ride it, I would've destroyed them *and* the race (or whatever it's called that they sit in) within minutes. How could a professional bike company set up an axle like this... or 4 of them?

2) Missing a couple hub seals. No big deal, but I can't find replacements online. There doesn't seem to be a standard naming convention and the ones I've found look slightly different.

3) Discs were engaging almost non-stop. As I'm not an highly experienced, this took some time to move the brake calipers around and adjust the backside pad by itself to get it just right. However, the bigger problem was the fact that the rotor itself was warped slightly. 3 out of 4 wheels had significant contact (2 bikes) with the disc pads either non-stop or because they were warped. The boxes were in good condition and the rotors weren't touching anything. How were almost all warped?

4) Only 2 out of the 4 wheels (2 bikes) would be considered true. One on each bike had at least one wheel that was either horizontally or vertically out of alignment (or both). They aren't horrible, just worth noting. Most people I think will just blindly ride the bike out of the box.

5) This is what truly pissed me off about this bike: The fking stickers. Not only are there an no less than ~25 of these fking things on each bike, but the choice of glue is asinine at best. Most of the stickers, while a pain to remove, came off without too much effort and left no glue behind. The same cannot be said about the fking stickers on the wheels. Did you know that this bike has 27.5" rims that are double-walled aluminum? Well, fear not, regardless of the angle you are looking at the bike, you will be reminded by EIGHT fking stickers on the rims telling you this from every single fking angle. Oh, you don't like them? Hell, just remove them like the rest! Nope. The glue is some sort of alien super compound that THREE DIFFERENT BRANDS of "goo off" styled sticker/glue remover would barely touch. I even tried a blow-dryer. Nothing makes this tar-like substance come off the fking rim. I several hours just getting the front rim ALONE done and it's not even done. I tried heat, plastic scraping tools, glue emulsifiers, paper towels, cloth rags, old shirts, pieces of jeans, and finally fking Scotch-Brite pads. It took HOURS to do one wheel poorly. And for the record, I'm 220lbs and leg press 1k. I have a fairly strong grip.

The fking moron at the company who thought it was a good idea to put those stupid fking things and use that fking glue should be immediately fired as this will ABSOLUTELY be the last DB bike I ever buy. Imagine buying a new car and then you find out the dashboard is covered in tar. You'd ask yourself, "why did they put this here? What kind of moron would think this okay?" That's the equivalent.

The rest of the actual bike seems to be good so far, but it's REALLY hard looking past the other things. They matter just as much to me. If they don't to you has a prospective buyer, then great.

As an an ending side note, they don't publish negative reviews on their site.

Review:
My background includes owning a Juiced Bikes CrossCurrent X E-Bike for over two years and I am an avid cyclist who has ridden in numerous long distance charity bike events. I have also taken courses in bicycle mechanics and maintain my own bikes. We bought this bike for my wife, who will ride less and shorter distances than I do. The short answer is that this bike fits her less demanding needs pretty well at a price that is half that of my CrossCurrent X.

First, I will agree with others about the assembly, which is harder than it needs to be but not terribly challenging. I was appalled that the derailleur settings were not even tuned in (considering that the rear wheel came attached) and had to be adjusted to achieve smooth shifting. The seat comes with a clamp you don't need and the bracket that holds it on the bike has to be very strongly tightened to prevent movement. I will say that the components are good for the price you are paying.

Handling is fine and it's nice to be able to raise the handlebars to avoid a hunched-over position. In the highest setting and pedaling at a good clip, my wife is able to hit 16mph, so it's not a fast bike by any means. My CrossCurrent X hits 20 in the lowest setting with about the same effort. A lot of this has to do with the gearing and, of course, you are saving money by having a lower capacity battery.

Over two consecutive weekends, we took trips of 13, 2.5 and 14 miles (totaling 29.5). The bike ran out of juice about 1.5 miles from home on the last trip. My wife pedaled nearly the entire time on these trips. I am not sure what they mean by a 40-45 mile range in auxiliary mode, but this is what we get from the bike. We also live in Southern Delaware where it is extremely flat, so your mileage will vary and will probably be less if there are any hills. Just be ready to charge frequently based on your trip durations.

Review:
I have been riding this bike around for the past couple days and I am ready to write my review:

Disclaimer: This is my first e-bike, and the first one I have ever ridden, so I will tailor this review as such and assume this will be either your first or second e-bike purchase or maybe even use. Suffice to say, it may just be easy to impress me.

This is a very enjoyable bike, both on city streets and on trails. I wanted something to play with, and maybe commute to work on sometimes. At 6" 1' tall, I think this is the first bike I've felt I'm not hunched over holding the bars while still being low enough to comfortably touch the ground. The acceleration of the motor is very nice and helps me crossing streets, and I was able to go 24.5mph in max gear with full throttle and pedaling myself. The hydraulic breaks really are a MUST for a bike with this much power. The battery is more than enough for me, either to make it to the office, or on a trail; I wear out before the battery does. The assembly was a little rough but I managed; do NOT adjust the height of the handlebars with the center nut like the manual suggests, but tightening it did re-compress the bearings. I have a few complaints about the bike so far (see below), but the one that I had to remove a star for is the branding. The company name is on virtually every corner, from the cable organizer to the huge white letters on the side. The most egregious is the headlight and taillight, which both feature HEYBIKE illuminated by 3 bright LEDs each, which CANNOT be turned off. I realize LEDs are relatively low-power, but come on... really? You already put your name very largely on the side of the bike, do you need to waste the battery too? I've paid for your product, I don't want to be responsible for advertising as well... I was able to fix this, but still, annoying. Overall it is very fun bike and I'm having a blast, and very reasonably priced for the performance that I got.

Newbie-tip: The electric motor can put out much more power than you can, so at top speed in top gear your pedaling is largely optional; this makes regenerative breaking a little pointless actually, because the amount of energy recovered would be meaningless.

Suggestions: Even though it is designed as a commuter bike, it could be a more comfortable ride. The included city tires get basically 0 traction, which I especially noticed since my first ride after assembling was after a thunderstorm. I suggest riding it to a local bike shop and picking up some more all-terrain tires, as well as a hydraulic bike seat. These things would be nice to have, but obviously would have increased the price point (since I'm paying for them separately), however they really are worth the extra penny I've found.

A programming menu can be accessed by holding + and - on the input pad, and a manual on the options is available on the Heybike website. My top speed was set at 62 by default which makes me think the speed on this may not be limited by software? I would suggest not messing with other settings such as voltage, current or wheel size; I was NOT able to go any faster testing with these for a brief run.

One complaint I had was the photodiode on the headlight, which makes the lights turn on automatically and cannot be turned off with the switch once enabled. Personally I wish it was just excluded completely, because I would like to make the determination on when it is safe to ride without lights. I was able to fix this problem by opening up the headlamp and carefully removing transistor 960 (I think, next to R54, green in photo).

Lastly, lets get to the branding... While we're in the headlamp, I was able to disable the 3 LEDs for the branding there by cutting the traces to R2 with an exacto-knife (red line in photo), it should be pretty easy to see the logic here. I can't help but remark that without the branding, the headlight assembly could be considerably smaller and lighter. For the taillight, I was able to disable all 3 LEDs by simply removing the left one (I think, apologies as I did not take a picture and it is harder to get at the circuit board for this one; the circuit board is a little messier too and I'm not a hardware guy). Its worth noting after doing this things, the display experienced a glitch and I needed to remove the battery completely to reset it; no problems since. I have yet to fix the non-electrical branding on the side, but I am considering either buying black spray paint and taping the section off, or taking it to a car body shop and asking them to paint it.

The last complaint that I can make about the electronics is the tail-light's automatic activation. Don't get me wrong, I find it incredibly handy that the taillight activates and flashes when I press the brake to warn anyone behind me. However I wish it did not happen when the bike was not moving, as I constantly will use the brakes to hold me in place while waiting for a crosswalk or streetlight. In these moments, I wish the taillight wasn't blinking away... I don't want to just disable the blinking feature, so I have yet to come up with a solution for this...

In conclusion, I am very much enjoying my purchase of this bike. I only note all the issues above to help the company improve. If you think you are competent enough to open the electronics, and make some small modifications to suit your likings, then I think I can safely recommend this bike.

Review: We bought this bike for exercise while on camping trips.  Arrived yesterday (3-19-20).  First of all, it is a thing of beauty...a work of art.  I rode it today, and could hardly stop.  It is easy to set up right out of the box (helps to google if u need help).  The ride was great.  The aluminum body is stiff, so that there is no deflection...meaning, easier effort. I was concerned that the aluminum body would give a stiff ride, but the steel fork absorbs very well, giving a smooth, comfortable ride.  I was amazed as to how well it shifted...It shifts as well and as easily as my high-end all carbon fibre road bike with Dura-Ace components. Downshifting is often hard except with high-end components, but the thumb shifter made it surprisingly easy.  My  road bike is great for long rides, like 30-100 miles...but this bike can serve a pretty long ride very well...surprisingly, given the aluminum frame and the 20" wheels (as a rule, the bigger the wheel, the smoother the ride).  To ride my road bike is a bit of a project...you need good roads, wide shoulders, friends (in case of flats, which occur often, as road bikes have efficient, but thin tires).  With this bike, I feel like I can just hop on it, not have to wear clip in bike shoes and all the other gear, and just ride.  I play adult hockey, and with the virus having cancelled our season, I was concerned about my workout regimen.  I had planned to use this fold-up bike just  for camping, but now I plan to use it daily...Oh, and it folds up very quickly and easily...20 seconds.  The fittings are impressive...all the leads that the components are run through are welded on, and are part of the frame...cheaper bikes usually have clamps for these...which can move.  Even the kick stand bracket is welded into the frame...unheard of.  This bike had good reviews, which is why I bought it...the only knock I read was the seat...but I felt like the seat is fine.  And I've spent a lot of time on a seat (saddle)...My road bike has 25,000 miles on it, plus I had a hybrid and a tandem as well.  For a fold-up bike, it is light at 26 lbs.  By road bike standards, not so...my road bike weighs 16 lbs. .  But that is comparing apples and oranges. This bike is far more than I expected, and with it's ease of use, I plan to ride it daily, even in deference to my road bike.

Review:
Asked for them to trade this out for their Spark model, got this one instead after contacting the seller to ask for either a refund or the other version, which they said was alright. When delivery (and free time) showed up, I discovered it was. in fact, this bike. I have yet to ride the bike as I am beyond dead tired as of writing this review, but the thing came less assembled than you would think, namely the front brakes that came with mine were completely unset, and there was no included hex wrench to allow me to fix this glaring issue. The included, hardwired headlight feels super cheap, attaches nowhere to the included wire basket, has no fixed mount on the front of the bike, and, as mentioned is hardwired. Overall, I feel rather cheated and would recommend going with something else unless you really want to spend almost $500 on something incredibly mediocre.

edit1: After having a days rest, and mounting the headlamp to my cup holder now at the front of the thing, I can say it's a good bike once you get it fully assembled. The spring they used on the front brake to keep it from rubbing on the wheel constantly is a little weak and required a bit of extra tweaking to get right. Gear shifting is a little backwards at first, but is smart for how it is set up. The light is good, but the horn is too quiet to really deal with cars, so an external horn of some type might be needed if traffic is a concern. The weight of the bicycle isn't too bad, and the handlebar is pretty short, which is good for storage. The front mudguard is sketchy, so I removed that before I ended up like the other reviewer who got one that wouldn't stay secure. The color mine arrived is is still an ugly matte black, so if you order, don't expect the exact color you see to arrive. Over all, it's a good bike, but TDL will lie to make that sale. Go with somebody else if you can help it.

Afterthought- The matte black paint looks godawful.

Review: Bike came packaged well, no damage to the box or bike.  Assembly was straight forward, seat, handle bars, peddles and front tire.  However, the instructions were generic.  I just needed to adjust the front disc brake and lube the chain.  After 75 miles biking, I decided to make minor adjustments to the front and rear derailleur. New bikes will get cable stretch and need adjustment after break-in.  The front derailleur was making a ticking  noise which was the chain rubbing.  I looked at a couple of videos on adjusting the front and rear derailleurs. I made adjustment and the bike shifts smoother and quieter.  The disc brakes are good, but my 20 year old Specialized mountain bike with "V" brakes are better.  The tires are 700 X 38c which equate to 28" X 1.5" and are perfect size for a hybrid bike.  The seat was comfortable, I do not understand all the critics about the seat.  My Specialized mountain bike seat is much harder. The GTX seat is not as comfortable as a fat, dual spring cruiser seat, but those seats can cause chaffing.  The bike rides smooth, fast and comfortable with the help of the front suspension forks.  Also, the bike looks good.  The 18" frame is perfect for me at 5' 11" and 210 lbs.  Some have criticized the chain braking and tires pinching.  I keep the chain lubed, shift gears property by avoiding chain stress gear shifting and have no issues.  I keep 65 psi (max 70 psi)  in the tires and have no issues of pinched tires. I find 65 psi a good balance for both speed and comfort. The Schwinn Elite GTX is exactly what I wanted .  A Hybrid bike that is quicker and more comfortable on the road or gravel than my Specialized mountain bike.  i would recommend the Schwinn  Elite GTX hybrid bike.  Pricing will vary depending on supply and demand, but i paid $450 shipped and can't complain.  Most aluminum frame bikes with shimano derailleurs, disc brakes and front suspension cost around $600 plus.  This bike is a good value when supply can fulfill demand at $450.

Review: I bought this bike brand new for my 5 year-old son and he rode it for about 2 years. A bike tech also checked it out prior to my son riding it. For the first few months, the bike rode well, but then several issues started to appear. 1) The front wheel became unaligned after a minor crash (my son was not going fast or doing tricks or jumps).  Minor crashes are common as kids learn how to bike and a good quality bike should be able to withstand them. A nut at the stem had to be tightened to prevent misalignment again.  2) The handle bar fell out of the headtube when I tried to pick up his bike by the handle bar one time. 3) One of the peddles got loose and needed tightening several times. Overall the bike is unreliable and I deem it a safety issue.  I ride a Diamondback as well and it had very little issues so I am disappointed that this bike had a lot of problems.

Review: This is my very first road bike. I've only ever owned mountain bikes so I wan't sure if I'd enjoy road biking but took a chance due to the rave reviews on this product. After taking it for a few rides I'm very happy with my purchase. I love that it included free assembly from Amazon, that may have cost at least another $100 if I had to take it somewhere to be assembled. My favorite things about the bike are its looks and design. It's a very modern and stylishly designed bike and that's what drew my eye to it initially. The great reviews and recommendations though are what made me buy. I bought the white version and it's beautiful to look at. Going for a ride I most enjoy that the shifting is responsive and accurate. Here in very hilly Tennessee there are very few flat areas to ride. Frequent shifting is a must, and so far I'm very satisfied with the way it's handling the hilly terrain.  The bike feels pretty light to me too. But then I've only rode heavy mountain bikes most my life so I may not be the best judge here. Handling and ride feels pretty good. Some people complain that the saddle is not comfortable enough but I didn't find it to be a standout problem. I also bought some shorts with padding built in so that's good enough to make the ride comfortable. The most important factor of all in evaluating this bike is the fun factor. If the bike isn't fun to be on then what's the point? I can most definitely say that this bike is fun to ride. Overall, I feel like it's a great value for the money and a nice start to entry-level road biking. I highly recommend.

Review:
Update: had some issues… note nothing is quick everything takes months and parts ship from Hong Kong

I bought my wife this bike about a month before and have spent the last month trying to keep up with her on my road bike, so I decided to buy one for myself.

Just like the first bike, the assembly was very straightforward and took less than 20 minutes. Again, the battery came charged at about 75% out of the box, so we were off.

The only con to date is that on very steep hills it does bog down and unless you are familiar with electric bikes and all gear functionality, it can throw you off. One note, make sure you know how long you have on the battery with each bar on the indicator. When I was down to one bar the battery was completely drained in about 5 minutes, so I was thankful for no huge hills to peddle on my way home.

So far I have only had contact with customer service once and they have been very responsive.

I spent two hours riding the bike around the national forest yesterday with no issues. l really like this bike and recommend it without hesitation have it to my friends.

Review:
This ebike exceeded my expectations. The battery lasts around two hours depending on how much the throttle is used. The battery lasts longer using the pedal assist only. It was fairly easy to put together. This ebike has changed the way I view going for a bike ride. It is so much easier to justify biking somewhere instead of driving. I'll take it on small trips to the grocery store or on miles long trips to the other side of town.

I get outside more and enjoy the fresh air without overworking myself. Don't get me wrong, you definitely still have to pedal unless you only use the throttle. However, the resistance is significantly less compared to pedaling a non motor bike. Going uphill is when some real resistance can be felt, but the battery takes you right up the hill without you breaking a sweat.

The battery takes a few hours to charge. It has some weight to it and is kind of large. The bike itself weighs roughly 40lbs which makes it difficult to maneuver. You need to store it indoors because the rain is an ebikes enemy. Getting it up the three steps into to the house is a chore. Other than those couple cons, this bike is a real game changer, I have gone on more adventures than I could have imagined in a the couple months of having it. I definitely will be ordering a more comfortable seat and possibly a rack for the back tire.
I am very pleased with how easy this bike is to use. The buttons are easy to read and easy to use quickly. Sometimes you have to switch from the pedal assist to throttle only to no motor in a short amount of time, i.e. going around a sharp turn in town. The buttons on other bikes I have seen have been in odd positioning, but not on this bike. There is also a gauge for the battery life left. This is helpful if the ride is longer, so you know when the halfway point is and turn around.
Great purchase overall.

I'll probably keep it, because I like the size, the disk brakes, and the fact that it's a 7-speed (with only a single front chain ring), but it's disappointing that her foot/pedal hits the tire. It's a bummer.

Review: After researching for months I finally bought the Hotebike MTB 27.5”. I wanted a hidden battery with long range. So far I’ve got around 30 miles per charge. On the comfort side I replaced the seat with a thicker foam seat and I extended the fork headset so I could sit more upright. I’m 6.3’ so my seat is up high. A shorter person would probably be fine with the standard headset. The biggest surprise with this bike was the power. I had not anticipated how much the peddle assist actually assists. I keep it mostly around 2 or 3 and switch to 5 when going uphill. When riding streets I seldom shift the gears down from 21 but on trails I use the full spectrum. Great bike at a very good price. I give highest marks possible to their customer service. They are incredible and I’ve never waited more than a few hours to get an email reply. One of the inner tubes popped when I first filled it. They immediately sent me a new one. I mentioned the battery had some marks on the inside, cosmetic, and they offered to replace it. But when I declined a replacement they credited me $20 instead. Overall I am very satisfied with my purchase.

Review:
I have had this bike for about 2 months now, wanted to use it a bit before reviewing, so far I'd say it met every expectation I had of it. Buying a bike thru the mail is a little different, I have always bought from local stores where i could at least try a bike before buying. I'm 6'0" I bought the large-20 inch frame based on other reviews here and it feels good. this bike is a good for light off road and city driving, as its a little larger and heavier than most mountain bikes its probably not ideal for serious off road mountain biking but that's ok for me, I didn't buy it for that reason.

Today i went on my longest ride to date about 30 miles round trip on an off road trail. bike was fine only comment id make is that as others have noted a softer seat upgrade might be a good idea for anyone who goes on longer rides, really became uncomfortable towards the end of my ride.

Assembly was pretty easy, had the bike together in about 1.5 hrs but the gears would not shift correctly as shipped from factory. I don't know much about setting up gears and didn't want to further complicate the situation so I took it to a local shop to get the gears set up. This is the reason why i gave the bike 4 stars and not 5. other assembly notes, the bike uses Presta air valves on the tires, I did need to buy a pump, every bike I have owned was always had a Schrader, wasn't a big deal to me. The fork does not lock, there has been discussions of the fork on other reviews, not a big deal to me on this either.

Overall, a great bike for the price.

Review:
Ok I have a couple of electric bikes and a few mountain bikes. But I wanted one for my wife to enjoy that didn't look like a scooter or and heavy as one too. She needed something to help her keep up with me and the boys. This e-bike from Schwinn was a big hit.

This bike is a light weight electric bicycle and there is a reason its so light too. Its a mountain bike with a small 250watt motor with a removable internal battery. So this is going to be about the weight of a cheeper mountain bike but heavier than a professional one. It works by giving power to the rear motor to push the bike depending on setting forward with every peddle. This is called PAS peddle assist system. If on the more you peddle the more power you get the faster you go. No peddle no power your bike will not move.

PROS:
The battery hold a very decent charge and recharges a bit over 3 hours. We use very little battery power its rarely under 3 bars out of 4. This is made to do most terrain from street to dirt. The tires are too narrow for sand and don't try to drop off a steep drop with it. You do get front and seat shocks that are for light duty. The shifter is a 7 gear twist shift and very very simple for my wife and kids to use. The front and rear brake if adjusted correctly will stop fast as don't show fast wear down. The controls are simple you have power, the amount of power to assist you, and the remaining power. The motor is responsive and you can feel the power kick it with every increase of level.

CONS:
The electronic gages are simple and this may be a sticking point for some. Schwinn does sell gage/control upgrades. The instructions are NOT user friendly. Ive assembled a few bikes in my time and this would require and I recommend taking it to a bike shop for most new riders. The battery is very sleek and discreet often others don't know its a E-bike. But you must turn on the battery before the electric controller will power on the bike. There is not manual power throttle like on other e-bikes. This is a PAS only you can not ride it like a scooter. The twist shift would need to be removed for a paddle shift for this to happen. Im 5ft 8 and my wife is 5ft 2 she needs to tip toe at stops because of center bar height and I do not. This is not a deal breaker for her just something to consider.

Overall this bike beats expectations and is an a amazing piece of equipment. Schwinn has made great bikes for a long time and looks like they are getting into the e-bike race. We have ridden this on easy trails and a few moderates without issues. It shines when you are tired and going up hill or on the last mile home. You shift down and increase the power up.

Review:
This bike has turned out to be a very good purchase for me.

I live on my boat in Los Angles and am able to attend to 90% of my brick and mortar shopping without using my car since this bike is beefy and has no problem handling a couple of saddle bag type baskets as well as a basket on the handlebars. A 36 pack of canned beer rides securely on the rear rack held in place with bungee cords

I bought the three speed model and am glad I did. Even though the area around us is pretty flat there are a few sloped ramps, on our great local bike paths, that make me appreciate the ability to shift down. Down wind on a level surface, or down hill, the high gear is useful. Some days the breeze off the ocean is strong enough to cause me to shift down when headed up wind. The three speed has no derailleur so the bike has a chain guard.

This bike is very easy to ride and takes a lot of the back and hand stress out of riding my bike. Im 72 and outgrew dropped handlebars about half a century ago.

It took me a few days to get used to coaster brakes again, after decades of cool bikes with hand brakes. But now Im cool with the coaster brake and really like the lack of clutter on the handle bars.

I added a bell, lights and a holder for my smart phone. I also added a really nice rear view mirror which is a great safety feature. I got a nice beefy lock on Amazon and really like the fact that I can drop it in my basket instead of going to the trouble of installing it on the mount on the bike every time I take off.

Since I live in southern California fenders seemed pointless so I skipped that accessory.

The bike is easy to ride with its big tires but is not fast. If you want a fast bike a beach cruiser is probably not what you should buy.

Unless I have some compelling reason to get in my car I do all my local travel on my bike. Its easier, faster many times, and there are no parking hassles or cost. I rode it 25 miles round trip to a doctor appointment a week ago. It was along the beach where there is a nice bike path and lots of scenery. It was not an exhausting experience but then I was not in a big hurry.

As I ride to the gym every morning Im passed by cyclists on high tech bikes who are bent into incredibly uncomfortable looking positions over their dropped handle bars. As they zoom past I reflect on the fact that I definitely bought the right bike for me and also that some people should not be wearing lycra cycling outfits.

The only negative about this purchase was that the packaging on the bike was not adequate to protect it in shipment. The three speed shifter on the rear axel had broken through the cardboard box and got scratched up in shipment. The damage did not look like it was more than cosmetic and I did not want o hassle with repacking the bike to return it so I went ahead and accepted as it was.

Review:
UPDATE: Two years later and I still love it!!

This bike has lots of advantages: It is manageable (I'm 68 and can lift it with some effort), it is a blast to ride on electric, and it's pretty stable and comfortable. I'm definitely glad I bought it, as it gets me outside and gives me exercise I enjoy. But it is not perfect. For one, I had to take it to a bike shop because the brake pads and brake plates didn't line up. No matter how I fiddled with it, the brakes were always applied which made peddling hard. I wish I could say they fixed it, but they were only able to make it better, so peddling is still kind of hard, no matter which gear I am in. The other thing is the peddle-assist. The truth is that no matter how soft or slow you peddle, even on the lowest setting the assist mode will take over and will take you faster than you can peddle. That's fine if you want to go fast, but if you're looking for exercise, you can't use the peddle assist at all. So when I switch it on, I take it off peddle assist mode which allows me to use the grip as a throttle (like on a motorcycle), and I alternate between unassisted peddling, or going without peddling and only using the throttle. I find the latter very useful for taking off from a complete stop, as well as switching into when I am on a rough road or when I start to tire. So I've found a way to make it work for me. But it would have been nice to be able to use the peddle assist mode. The other thing is the adjustables, especially the seat and handlebar height. I find that if I have the adjustment levers loose enough that I can close them, the seat or handlebar just slides down to the lowest level. But if I tighten the lever, I can't close it. So I find I have to stop several times in a trip to readjust the height of either the seat or the handlebar.

I made some adjustments. I found the grip on the peddles too aggressive, so I switched them out for peddles with a less aggressive grip that also fold, which makes it more convenient to store in the car trunk. I cut off the rubber end of one of the handles and inserted a rear view mirror, which, frankly, I don't see how I can live without. I added a softer seat which really helps.

The inconveniences I mentioned are not deal-killers. They are annoyances or disappointments. I still love the bike and would recommend it to anyone who is looking for a fun ride, for exercise, for beating city traffic and avoiding public transportation during Covid. I love that I can peddle as far as I want and still be certain to make it home because I can ride myself back on electric. This freedom is fantastic.

Review:
UPDATE
The reply from Heybike said I received their new version of the bike and they have not yet changed their photographs on the website nor Amazon.
I wouldn’t purchase had I know this.
If you are considering this bike, I’d wait till they change and you can see the differences.
It’s more cheaply made and not as nearly attractive with the dark brown seat and handlebars. Check out my video for all the differences I’ve found so far. Luckily I have an older version close by to compare it to.
I think you should disregard the great reviews since this was described to me as their new version so it truly has not been reviewed. I can tell you yet if their is a difference in ride or feel because I can’t get an answer on how to install the front fender since it is not like the installation video nor written instructions that came with the bike so it seems this is a glitch they didn’t know about. It is just resting on the front wheel at the present time so I could skip to the next step and attach the wheel. Customer service is probably in China since I wait 24 hrs + for a reply.
With all this knowledge, It is my opinion that they are making the bike more cheaply to increase their profits after becoming established as a great value. If you look at their website the price is now $899 and still shows the old cityscape model . They are selling from the photos and reviews of their first version.

Original Review
If you look at the photos in this Amazon listing and also at HeyBike website and their social media, all Cityscape bikes look the same except obviously frame color. We ordered from HeyBike directly because we had a discount coupon greater than Amazon’s normally better price.
The white bike arrived with a different style saddle, grips and the color of these are different as well, dark brown and not tan. It’s ugly muddy brown, not complimentary of the white color. The grips on the handlebars are extremely cheap looking regular plastic/metal grips with a trim strip of faux leather wrapped around them, leaving the end black. Look closely at the first photo. The bike’s light is much less substantial as are the peddles.
The black bike arrived looking exactly like what you see here on Amazon, the HeyBike website and social media.

In addition the front fender will not attach to the white bike —- the is a different shorter screw than the black bike so it cannot be attached to the back as is shown in the assembly video and if you try to attach it to the front of this piece it sticks out substantially behind the wheel towards the rider and rubs the tire on the front. In addition, the piece above the fork that it attaches to is also less substantial on the white bike than the black. So, if it attached to the back, it’s not a solid piece like the black bike.
All in all, I am very disappointed in the white bike and would not purchase it if the photos had been true to the actual product.
I’ve emailed customer support to ask why my bike is different and still waiting on a reply. Please note that you cannot call this company only email and must wait up to 24 hrs for a reply.
My white one could be an older model or but I think it’s a newer version which is more expensive than it was earlier this year and definitely cheaper. It’s a decent e- bike for the price, just not as nice looking. Because if these cheapening component, Personally, I would skip it, if this is their new version and this means the black also because who knows how much stock before the black one ships with these new components.
I’ll update with their reply so you can buy knowing what you will receive.
