Review:
This is my first E-Bike. I researched several brands, and designs of E-bikes and I highly suggest you do your research as well before purchasing. However, hands down, this is about the lowest price you are going to find on an entry level E-Bike for the price. Read on.

I've wanted to commute to work for a while now, but with no showering facilities at work, I wasn't about to ride one of my traditional bikes and sit in my sweat for the rest of the day. This bike has solved that problem. For reference, I am 5 ft 5 inches tall and about 175 lbs. With this E-Bike I can ride to work in full throttle mode, or with minimal pedaling all the way to work (7 miles, with a few fairly steep hills) in about 30 minutes. On the ride home I can pedal like a regular bike, use pedal assist, or full throttle depending on my mood. On my way to work I average about 13-15 mph using just the throttle with occasional pedaling to get up the 2 or 3 hills on my route. I am by no means really putting any effort into the pedaling, but just helping the motor a little as they are fairly steep. I could certainly use just the throttle and make it up the hills on my route and would probably average about 8 mph up the hills. During the rest of my commute (which is on a local bike trail) the terrain is flat which increases my speed to about 16.5 mph, thus the average mph of around 13-15 mph. On my commute home I don't really care about getting sweaty, so I tend to pedal with pedal assist set on the highest level or use full throttle and pedal constantly. With this option I easily average 18.5 mph and occasionally hit up to 20 mph. It's a nice 25-30 minute workout. I will say that even on the highest gear (21 gears included) you can spin out on this bike. I do wish the gear ratio was a little better so that I could really push it above 20 mph.

THE DETAILS

ASSEMBLY... My bike was delivered pretty much damage free. A small puncture in the box that didn't seem to affect anything inside. It took maybe 5-10 mins to remove everything and begin assembling. Tools are included for assembly but I reverted to better ones just because it was easier. There's not too much to assemble, and as long as you have some basic general knowledge about bikes of any kind you shouldn't have any problems doing it yourself. You have to install the pedals, the seat, the handle bars, the front tire, and the headlight. I don't really get all the comments about the front fork being turned around. The headlight bracket should easily tell you which way the fork needs to be turned. Think about it people. Anyway, everything assembled with no problems whatsoever. If you are unsure, search Youtube for assembling this specific bike and watch the videos before you begin.

CHARGING... Although the battery does have some charge out of the box, I would recommend fully charging the battery before first use (follow the instruction). At the very least, plug it in while you're assembling the bike so it can be getting some charge. The charger connects very easily to the battery and has a light indicator when the battery is charging (red light) and when it is fully charged (green light). Don't keep the charger continually connected to the battery after it is charged (read and follow the instructions). Once the battery is charged, place it in the battery holder on the down tube of the bike and snap it in place. Mine needs a little pressure to get it to click fully into place. It should not be removable once clicked in, and will require the key that came with it (set of 2) to release the battery.

THE FIRST RIDE... Once the battery is charged and locked in the battery holder, you'll press the red rocker switch at the base of the battery to turn it on. On the handle bars, you'll press the ON button for a second or two and red lights will illuminate to let you know it's on. By default the E-Bike will be in the lowest level of pedal assist mode. In this mode you will get some assistance from the battery after a few pedal revolutions on the bike. You'll feel it kick in. The throttle does nothing in this mode. If you click to the middle level then you'll get more assistance from the battery, and it is noticeable. On high you'll get the most assistance out of the battery. It usually takes 3 or 4 pedal rotations for it to kick in, but it does an ample job of assisting you while you're pedaling, especially on hills. Keep in mind that you must pedal for the pedal assist modes to work. Once you stop pedaling then the battery disengages and you coast on your bike as you normally would. If you resume pedaling, the battery assist will kick in again after a few pedal revolutions. If you click all the way back to low and then one more click, you will be in throttle mode. In this mode you'll use the half throttle grip on the right handlebar to power the bike. No pedaling is needed in this mode, however, you still can if you want. If I'm at a complete stop I tend to pedal a couple of revolutions before turning the throttle, again, just to assist the motor from having to propel me from a dead stop. If you keep the throttle engaged fully on flat terrain you should easily see 15 to 18 mph depending on your weight, etc. NOTE: From the factory the bike comes with one of the wires connected in the wire box to limit your mph to under 15, but you can disconnect that wire to achieve the 18-20 mph speeds. Go to Youtube and watch the video on how to do this. Easy, peasy. While most of my commutes are short (7 miles each way), I was able to take this out one weekend and rode about 48 miles on it non-stop with full throttle and pedaling constantly. The battery finally drained at about mile 49-50. From a time perspective it took me about 2 hours and 40 minutes with a short break to ride the 50 miles, so the battery lasted a pretty decent amount of time with constant use.

1 Month In... I've now been riding this bike to work every day that the weather is cooperative and really love it. I don't sit in traffic during morning or afternoon commutes. I get a little bit of a built-in workout in the afternoon, and have had no issues with the bike at all. Unlike other reviews, the horn and head light have worked without any problems as well. I will say that I agree totally with everyone's assessment of the seat that comes with the bike. IT IS BAD! Uncomfortable is an understatement. However, it's a pretty easy fix. I replaced it with one of my other saddles and I'm riding perfectly comfortable now.

A FEW CONS... During my maiden voyage (first ride) one of the wires inside the wiring box became disconnected after I went over a fairly large bump. I immediately lost all power to the bike and had to finish the ride the old fashioned way. After arriving at my destination I contacted Ancheer and worked through the issue. They were very responsive to my emails, and after removing the box where all the wiring terminates I found the disconnected wire, reconnected it, and have had no additional issues whatsoever. It was at that time that I also disconnected the wire the limited the bike's mph to under 15 mph. While the disconnected wire wasn't a huge issue, the bike is VERY heavy and even with lower gears it can be tough to propel this bike on hills. As a comparison, my main road bike is about 18 lbs. and this bike clocks in at 50+ lbs. IT IS HEAVY no matter how you slice it. You get use to that pedal assist pretty quickly. Many reviewers have complained about the squeaky disc brakes. Here is where I believe you need to be a little more educated on bikes in general. Disc brakes require a little finesse and adjustment to eliminate the squeak. There's this really great thing called the internet. Google how to remove disc brake squeak, or get on Youtube. There's at least a dozen good videos on how. I watched a couple, made a few adjustments on the front wheel of this bike and viola!, no more squeaky disc brakes. Bike's are mechanical and need tune ups and attention. Just like a car, if you neglect it, it's going to let you down. Not handy with tools? Find a good local bike shop and service your bike yearly, every 6 months if you ride heavily. They can also assist with derailers that aren't completely in tune. The ones on this bike weren't when it arrived but I wasn't expecting it to be perfectly tuned for this price point.

CONCLUSION... As I said at the beginning of this review, after researching E-Bikes for about a month before purchasing, this is the lowest price point that you're going to find for a complete E-Bike. I've been very happy with it. I am genuinely disappointed when the weather is bad and I have to drive me car to work. I would recommend an E-Bike for anyone who wants to take up biking but may have some mobility or stamina problems with riding a regular bike, and would specifically recommend this Ancheer E-Bike simply for the price point. Is it the best built, best quality bike...no, but it is a very good entry level E-Bike.

Review:
My kid loves this bike. It is cheaper than one of those other training bikes but it works. I like the look of this one better too. The wheels are a hard rubber which was great for us. It has been used a lot this summer and it still works perfectly. Our kid went from being scared to even sit on a bike to being a daredevil. He learned at his own pace and he'll be ready for a bike with pedals by next summer.

The only sad thing is that I don't get to have that moment where my kid thinks I am holding onto the bike, but really I let go a long time ago. Then they realize I am not holding onto the bike anymore and they freak out and fall. The we get to have the special talk about how they can do more than they think they can, they just have to trust in themselves.

Review:
I should have bought this assembled at a bike shop. This is a very nice bicycle, better than the stuff at walmart/etc. Very sturdy. My complaints are with the shipping process and assembly.

1) Front axle poked through the box, damaging the axle nut so bad it couldn't be threaded back onto the axle. I'm trying to get one from the manufacturer. Someone either at Amazon or UPS noticed this, as the large hole this created was covered by many strips of clear packing tape.
2) I was sent two left pedals and no right pedal, so we cannot use the bicycle. I'm trying to get one from the manufacturer.
3) Despite it being pretty well padded inside the box, there were deep scratches on the frame and handlebars.

Now, if the bike had arrived in decent shape with all of the correct parts, I still would have preferred a shop assemble it. As mentioned in other reviews, everything is overtightened - front/rear wheel bearings, headset and bottom bracket were all too tight. This required disassembling the bike further and putting it back together. I imagine it would be very hard to for a child to pedal the bike in the state in shipped in.

Unless you have the right spanner wrench for the bottom bracket and a headset wrench (cone wrench for wheel bearings is included) I would let a dealer assemble it. Plus there you can inspect it for any blemishes.

It even says on the box that the bicycle is only to be sold in an assembled state.

I tried to exchange the bike, because I do like it - but its currently out of stock. This is a 2012 model bike and it seems the new 2013 models are becoming available, so I may never be able to exchange it.

Review:
I didn't buy my bike from Amazon. I've had mine for quite some time. I love this bike and I've had several people compliment it and ask about it. I'm not being paid or compensated in any way for this honest review.

The Company, Sixthreezero, has outstanding customer service. The bicycle is not Walmart type quality; this bike is more of what you'd buy from a bicycle shop, even though it is made in China.

The bike is extremely lightweight since it's made of aluminum and not steel. The saddle is wide and comfortable. It's easier to mount if you have a physical problem. The pedal/seat design allows shorter as well as taller people to fit.

The working components are name brand components.

When I got my bike, it took a couple of hours to assemble, but it wasn't difficult. I put a small scratch on the fender. I emailed the company with the intention of buying some touchup paint.

They offered to send me some, so I gave them my address. I got the paint about a week later...along with a new fenders, at no charge.

Don't be surprised if the bike fender and/or wheels, need adjustment. When you buy a bike at a shop, this is done for you; that's why they cost so much more in shops.

The spokes will most likely need adjustment. This is easily done at home with a special inexpensive wrench, which you can find here on Amazon. You can find YouTube videos to help you with this. If you've never done it, like me, it'll take a while to do it properly, but it's not difficult at all.

The bike is a quality bike, well thought out.

Review: Bought for 3 year old grandson. Bike was packaged safely with cardboard around every painted surface. Only issue is something I saw in some other reviews.  The bolts on the rear wheel were so tight from the factory that it was very difficult to pedal.  The front wheel (which I installed) would spin multiple times when pushed. The rear wheel would spin maybe 2 times before the friction stopped it. We had to loosen the nut holding on that rear wheel a bit just to let the darn wheel spin.  They took some effort as it was over tightened. My grandson could not pedal it at first but we were able to make it work. Since this same issue has shown up several times, seems like a quality control issue.  They must have the Incredible Hulk tightening the nuts in the factory as the torque is far above what is necessary.  Fixed now. Rest of the bike was fine. Good size for a 3 year old.

Review:
I am a disabled average-height woman and I was able to go on errands with significantly less pain by riding this bike. I had a lot of difficulty setting up this bike because of pain and because the instructions don't include details that I required since I have little technical ability and had never ridden an electric bike or motorcycle. It is important to extend the bike first then raise the seat. I raised the seat first and the bike got jammed when I tried to extend it, so I had to take it to a bike shop to set it up correctly.

The box arrives with tools, so it's not necessary to own tools to do the setup. Yet, I was unable to setup the front wheel correctly. It was not attached to the handlebars. The repairman had to fix that problem also.

I'm glad I got the bike, but I want to warn buyers that the setup procedure is not simple. The bike has a device that has a white light and horn, but I was unable to set it up by myself so I connected it with a spare wire from the packaging. It is not secure and moves forward. Also, when I rode the bike to the supermarket two miles away I realized that I need mirrors, so if you buy this bike you must purchase at least 2 mirrors for safety.

I watched a lot of videos to learn how to setup and ride this bike. To go forward you have to rotate the right handle backwards. To stop or slow the bike you have to rotate the handle forwards. Maybe this is the standard procedure for every scooter, but I had to carefully watch a video several times to figure that out because I have never ridden a motorized vehicle before except the supermarket scooters which have different handlebars.

I strongly recommend practicing on a large flat area such as an empty parking lot to learn how to ride this bike if you are inexperienced. There will be trouble if you are riding on a slanted sidewalk or road, encounter potholes, or there are rocks and other objects on the road. You must be on the alert every second looking at the road surface and watching for cars, people, and animals who might suddenly appear. Also, the ad said this bike goes 15 miles-per-hour but the instruction book said the maximum speed is 13 miles-per-hour.

Review: I got this bike awhile ago and I can’t believe I forgot to review it as much as I ride it.  I do a lot of trail running, and I wanted a bike to use on days where I gave my feet the day off.  This bike has by far exceeded my expectations.  I ride it on a lot of trails in and around the Phoenix metro area and it is more than capable.  The terrain varies from flat sand and dirt to rocky hills and washes.  The bike is well built, can easily handle fast rides through this, and I feel comfortable riding it in just about any of these places.  I have tried it for some minor downhill riding but I would recommend a brake upgrade if you want to get serious about this.  There’s nothing wrong with these disc brakes, but you know what I mean if you are a serious downhill rider.  The 29in wheels are excellent for trail riding as well.  I am 5’9”, but if I was any shorter I would recommend the next size smaller.  Anyway I highly recommend this bike if you don’t want to spend thousands on a Trek that you don’t need for trail riding.  I also purchased the more premium Salvo model that has SRAM parts and a rear air shock, and it is even better!

Review:
This review is in reference to the Pluto 2.0 version of this bike in 16 inch. The bike comes in a box partially assembled, and with some very basic knowledge it is possible to assemble it within 30 minutes - but I do not recommend you do this.

Even though this and other kids bikes from CHY-NAH are quite expensive, it has more to do with higher shipping costs than it does with quality. In fact, I'd say half of this bike's price is shipping. My point is that corners were cut, and thankfully, most of them were on assembly and not the bike's components themselves.

The frame is solid and looks good, decent tires, and the wheels are fine (not true, but who cares it's a kids' bike). Where the main problems lie, and this is likely the cause for many of the negative reviews, is that the bearings for both the crank and headset are poorly lubed and not properly adjust.

Headset
---------------------
This bike has a threaded fork (the part that holds the front wheel) with two bearings - one up top and one at the bottom of the steerer tube. On my bike, they were both poorly lubed and over-tightened. To fix this, you carefully remove the painted hex nut at the top of the steerer tube, and then under that is the actual headset which you should be able to unscrew with your hands. Once this is out you can remove the entire fork along with the bearings. I cleaned bearings and bearing cup surfaces with mineral spirits, then slathered on my favorite grease - lucas oil green (which seems to work great on everything even though it's marketed as being for heavy machinery). I then reassembled the front end.

When you tighten the fork nut (the nut that holds the fork; not the hex lock nut), it should tight enough so that there is no play or wobble in the fork, but no more. If if is over-tightened the bearings will bind and it will make a grinding sound. On this bike you can just hand-tight until it free of play, then back off slightly. When you put the lock nut back on (the hex one) you can tighten it with a wrench and this will cause that bit of slack to be taken up. Remember it should turn freely with little or no resistance. If you feel resistance, it's too tight. If it wobbles then it is too loose.

Crank
---------------------
Before removing the crank you will want to loosen the rear wheel, which means taking off one set of outer bolts and loosening the inner bolts plus the coaster-brake's anti-rotation screw on the left side. Do this and slide the wheel forward to slack the chain. You will also need to remove the plastic chain guard. I completely removed it because it is not necessary...and will only serve to frustrate future maintenance.

To remove the crank on this bike, you would remove the hex lock nut on the left side (opposite the side where the sprocket is), then, by using a flat head screwdriver and a hammer you tap to loosen and remove the crank bolt that holds the whole thing together. The crank bolt has two notches and you stick the screwdriver into one of the notches on an angle and tap the top of the screwdriver gently with a hammer to loosen this. It should not be tight. Once it's out, you can pull the entire crankset out through the right side.

Here, again, I cleaned the bearings and bearing surfaces with mineral spirits, lubed everything with my grease, and reassembled. Much as with the headset, when you go to tighten the crank bolt that holds it together it must no bind. You have to get it just right. Remember, if it spins freely without wobbling it's good. If it grinds or sticks when you fling it, you over-tightened. If it wobbles, it needs to be tightened more. This is really done by feel. Once the tension is good, tighten the hex locknut with a wrench to keep it in place.

Again, you may find that once the locknut is tightened your "just right" bearing tension is now binding. To fix this, loosen the locknut, back off slightly on the crankset bolt (there may be the slightest wobble) then re-tighten the locknut.

Front Brakes
---------------------------
The front brake pads were out of alignment with the rim, and they were maladjusted. The position of the brake handle was a bit far-off for tiny kid hands. I adjust the brake pads by slightly loosening the nut that holds them to the brake caliper then squeezing the caliper with my hands against the rim. Perfect alignment both times; the top of the brake pad should be as close to the top of the rim as it can be without rubbing on the tire. Do that and tighten.

Next, you will see a phillips screw sticking out side-ways on the brake handle. This lets you adjust the distance of the handle from the handle bar. By turning it clockwise it will lessen the gap, and that's probably what you want. I turned mine all the way in and it reduced the gap but in my view it's still a bit large for kids.

Lastly you can loosen the cable nut that holds the brake cable to the caliper, then squeeze the calipers together but not all the way, leaving about 1/2 inch gap between the rim and the pads. While holding this position, use your other hand to tug the brake cable and pull it tight, then tighten the bolt back up. Doing this will drastically reduce the free-play and the distance that the brake handle needs to be pulled to actually brake.

Chain Tension
---------------------
If you did all of the above, you will have loosened the chain. The proper tension for the chain is to have a bit of slack, which will prevent binding and wear on the sprockets as well as reduce the chance that it pops off. There's no rule for how this is done but slide the rear wheel back until the chain is taught, then nudge the wheel forward slightly until there is the slightest sag in the chain. The sag should barely be noticeable.

Everything Else
---------------------
The rest of the bike setup is your call. The handlebars can be angled and height-adjusted to fit the rider and the seat has a quick-adjust clamp. The height of the seat top should be even with the hip of the rider standing next to the bike, and you can make small adjustments from there.

Conclusion
---------------------
My overall opinion on this bike as far as children's bikes go is that it's very good for starting out, but most people are not bike builders like myself and thus are not going to do this extra work. Unfortunately for these kinds, this bike will probably not last much more than a few months if it is ridden often. The simple fact is that they will experience premature wear from under-lubed and over-tightened bearings in the crank and handlebars.

Review:
I wanted to get back into mountain biking so I figured I start out with an entry level to intermediate bike. I saw that the Diamondback Overdrive had some good reviews so I bought online. I'm six feet tall so I ordered the large.

The bike arrived in 2 days! Couldn't believe how fast it shipped. Everything was perfect.

When I pulled the bike out of the box my initial reaction is this bike is much more substantial, and looks much better, than the photos depict. The frame is quite large and chunky, and the tires are substantial. From first glance I knew this bike would be well worth the money.

Setting it up wasn't very difficult. I affixed the handlebars, attached the front wheel, put the seat post in and attached the petals. I worked pretty slowly, being thorough. I think the whole thing including unboxing and cleaning up was less than an hour at a very leisurely pace. I assembled the bike by myself just using the assembly instructions that came with the bike.

I took the bike for a quick spin around the back yard. Everything is dialed in. The shifting is perfect, the brakes well adjusted. I even guess the correct height of the seat post! My initial riding impressions is that I wouldn't want a smaller bike (a normal mountain bike) or a bigger one (a 29'r). The 27.5 feels just right. No wonder it's a popular size.

The shifting works fine. The brakes are more powerful than the old center pull variety yet not strong enough to send you over the handlebars. They work fine. The seat is very hard, however I plan to ride with padded cycling shorts. This bike is not your Sunday ride with the kids bike, but purpose built to cut it up on the trail. The shock was dialed with a lot of preload, probably set up for street riding. I backed off the preload half a turn. I don't think you'll reasonably get 4 inches of travel out of the shock, more like 2 ish under normal riding conditions which is probably enough.

Now for a detailed critique of the bike and components. Keep in mind, you get what you are paying for, this is not a $2,000 mountain bike but rather a very substantial bike at a great price. The frame is large and substantial. The red paint looks good. The frame geometry is set up for someone who wants to ride aggressively (but perhaps not super aggressive). The tires are no-name, and surprisingly soft, not like the hard carcass tires I had on my mountain bike from days gone by. Perhaps the tires are softer for better grip, I don't know. The handlebar, I think is some kind of alloy, and is textured black. The grips are fine, the shifters shows the gear your in which is a nice touch for sure. The breaks are on the smaller side and manual (not hydraulic) so they are medium powered. The seat is hard with a sticky surface, designed to be used with bike shorts for sure.

I will provide an update when I do a little more riding. I want to see how the gear ratios work out for the type of riding I plan to do (mostly flats with a little technical riding thrown in, no drops or deep descents).

All in all, a great value. Should provide hours of enjoyment and even a little aggressive riding on the flats and trails. Great value for the money.

Review:
The first thing you should know about this bike is that it does not have a throttle. You have to be pedaling to get any assistance from the electric motor. I am still a fan of this bike, even though it is hard to beat an e-bike with a throttle. Just note that this isn't that kind of bicycle. Skip to the bottom for the TLDR version of this review.

Also of note is that we have another e-bike for comparison, so that is the lens I'm looking through. That other one is an Ancheer bike that does have a throttle along with the pedal assist, but it was half the price of this one with similar quality in my humble opinion. It is not a knock against this bike, as this is a Schwinn. But both get similar range and have 10.4 AH 36V batteries. The Ancheer even has more gears, but this review is all about the Schwinn.

The battery has to be switched on for you to be able to power the bike on. I'm not a big fan of that since the battery (and battery switch) is on the underside of the bike, but that is the design they chose. It gets quite dirty if you go through mud as well, but it is nice that the removable battery is integrated into the frame. The battery can still be charged without removing the battery. It is clear that it wasn't just a bike on which an electric motor was added, but it was designed from the beginning with the intent to make an electric bicycle. You can get up to whatever speed your legs will push you on this thing. I've hit 25 Mph on a flat straight away with some effort on max assist, but I could have gone even faster if I had the room.

I would say that the pedal assist is not the most aggressive on this e-bike. On the most aggressive setting, I'd say they got it just right. For me and my rider geometry, gear 5 of 7 is the sweet spot that lets me hit the fastest speed with the lowest effort. I've crashed once before on a different e-bike because the pedal assist was very aggressive and I needed to slow down, so I appreciate the tuning here. Stepping down on the pedal on this bike won't send you careening out of control regardless of gear. And speaking of gears, you only have 7 sprockets on the back. Our other e-bike has 3 front sprockets and 6 rear for better granularity when choosing gears and riding over different terrains and grades. I would have liked to see more than 7 gears, but I mostly use one to three so it isn't a real complaint for me who hasn't taken this mountain biking. The other thing is that they may have chosen to do that way to keep people from "crossing the chain" which when you set the most extreme gears on front and rear which puts lateral force on the chain and almost always makes it pop off. The other plus is that you only have to worry about gear shifting with your right hand.

When it comes to design cues, I didn't think I would appreciate the swept handlebars as much as I do. It really felt a little sketchy to assemble that part out of the box, but I think I got it right. I still feel some type of way at higher speeds, but I think it is all in my head. The swept bars probably keep the width down a bit, but doesn't feel like it sacrifices control. I think the layout is probably more ergonomic than a straight bar that I'm used to. I still don't like how the battery is on the bottom, along with the pedal assist sensor. You can see from the picture how muddy both get, but I didn't seem to have any trouble getting the assist to kick in when everything was all muddy.

The bike feels well balanced and the weight is not too much. It handles my weight well, although there is only a non-adjustable front fork and seat post suspension to dampen your ride. Both do their job to dampen the ride a bit, as do the thick mountain bike tires. I don't find myself bottoming out the suspension easily like I do on the Ancheer I sometimes ride. I've had to replace an inner tube already, but I may have aired up too much when riding over gravel so that is ok. I can't really be sure. The Shimano shifter is a twist to shift style. I don't have a preference here, but only 7 gears to choose from still seems like a con to me. The mechanical disk brakes are good and don't give any cause for concern. Overall, I like the bike and the quality is good. It also happens to be a good looking bicycle. The controls to turn the bike on and off are very low end, but they get the job done. I use my phone with a GPS app to check my speed, but you could install one of those light/horn/alarm/speedometer combo devices that are here on Amazon for around $25-30. Only one type gives accurate speed in mph, so do your research. Since this bike was around $1400, I can't give it 5 stars. They have some serious competition from Ancheer on Ebikes.

Here is the TLDR (too long, didn't read) version...

Pros:
-Well balanced on weight distribution and rider geometry for a person 5'3 to about 6'2
-Well tuned pedal assist (3 levels).
-Removable rechargeable battery integrated into the frame
-Nice aesthetics (subjective, so see pictures)
-Non-adjustable front suspension and seatpost suspension

Cons:
-Battery needs to be switched on before you can turn the bike on (needless extra step?!?)
-Battery and pedal sensor gets muddy, since it is on the underside. (not a usually a problem for the sensor unless you get in a big mess, but you remove the battery to charge it and you'll have a muddy battery somewhere charging)
-Low end control interface/buttons (but they did include a walk mode for moving the bike that makes it go 4 kmh so you can walk it around more easily if you hold down the button. Also there is no throttle, and maybe should be added at this price point???)

4 stars

Review:
I wanted a folder that would fit in my dinghy but one that was easier to ride than a 20inch. I tried a cheaper folding mountain bike and was disappointed. I also thought about building one, but once I added disc brakes it came out to about the same price and didn't have the 3 spoke wheels. I think these wheels will be more durable and rust resistant than wire spoke wheels.

I added a headset extension to bring me to a more upright riding position as well as a seatpost rack, wider saddle, larger grips, and folding pedals.

The suspension is a little soft for my 200lbs in front and back, but not uncontrollable. I was able to adjust the rear suspension but the front doesn't appear to be adjustable.

My only complaint is that the bolts are starting to rust so I will replace them with stainless or titanium bolts over the next few days few weeks.

I highly recommend this bike for it's value and features.

Update: I have now put several hundred miles on this bike. I have had to replace the suntour freewheel with a Shimano freewheel. This could have been because it was shipped loose, or because of something I did. Either way it was an improvement and it shifts quicker and quieter now. I also had to adjust the cones on the front wheel. I am still very happy with this bike.

Review:
I bought this bike about a year ago after my old bike got totaled by a car. I’ve put over 2,000 km on the bike so far and it still works very well. I wish I could get it to display imperial units (miles) but I have yet to figure that out.

It can hit 30k/h pretty easily with a bit of pedaling at full throttle. Downhill it can hit 40. It’s a fairly comfortable ride, and I managed to assemble it alone while having a sprained wrist, so not too hard to do.

Review:
:) Got this bike very quickly and well-packaged (note: it's in the unfolded confirmation with an obvious bike box, so apartment-dwellers with small or nonsecure package areas should beware). Company was great about responding quickly and effectively to my special shipping request to hold at closest FedEx facility.
:) I'm not at all experienced at assembling things and didn't have any tools on hand, but even I managed to do the basic assembly in about half an hour, and only because I was so awkward about twisting hex nuts to the right tightness with the provided hex wrench. Without that complication, I'd estimate the average user would take no more than 15 minutes at most -- it takes a bit of time to unpack everything, since it's well-wrapped.
:) I could ride the bike right away! Took it outside for a test-ride that went pretty well.
:) It's aesthetically fantastic, with a gorgeous solid body and comfortable saddle.
:) It's nicely put-together in factory, with appropriate greasing and fixing of joints.
:) Everything you really need, including wrench and extra parts, is available in the box.

:| The wheels are wider than city bike wheels but narrower than typical mountain bikes. I managed sidewalks, curbs, and myriad bumps just fine, but for some people it's a matter of strong preference.
:| There were slight scratches on the frame from the box which I'll cover up before long to avoid rust, but I think it's a shipping issue. It's otherwise well-protected.
:| Without default out-of-box settings, there's some optimization to be done on shifting and brakes. The speed was rather better than that for a cruiser, but not quite up to mountain bikes' usual speeds. Given the good condition of the gears and shimano shifters and everything, I am pretty confident that it could be improved, but just something to note.
:| If you like more upright bikes, just note that the handlebars are a bit low (I'm 5'3" and still a bit bent forward on the tallest setting) -- that's conducive to stability and greater speeds so no complaints. Handles somewhat wide, but easy to get used to or adjust as desired.

These didn't seem worth docking a star for because they're matters of personal preference and idiosyncrasy. Very worthwhile purchase overall.

Haven't tried folding it yet but shouldn't be too bad. I don't think, from the diagrams, that one needs to disassemble as much as instructed. Not very light for bikes in general, but is as good as or better than equivalent-sized folders.

Review: I needed a new bike to get back and forth between my house and my university (a 5 mile distance), and I didn't want to spend a small fortune on the whole kit and caboodle. The men's bike had better reviews than the women's one, plus it looked nicer, so I decided to order the smallest size and hope for the best. While I was worried that the men's small would still be far too big, it fit me perfectly at 5'3". The only color available was gold, which looked slightly tacky in person with all of the stickers. I ended up peeling everything off, adding some reflector tape to the front and back for additional safety, and changing over the bike grips for comfort (I also chose bright red to make the bike look less attractive to thieves). The bike is perfect for commuting - I have to carry it up and down a small flight of stairs, and while I struggled with my old steel bike, it has been a breeze navigating the stairwell with the Denali. Shifting gears is also very smooth, and I've found myself looking forward to my morning commute. If you're looking for a basic, no frills commuter bike delivered to your door, this is a great option, especially if you're a student on a budget and there are not very many used bike stores around.

Review:
READ IF YOU FIND THE BRAKE CABLE TOO SHORT
Like others, I experienced challenges assembling the bike, specifically attaching the brake line to the handle bar brake. For me, the cable that attaches to the handle bar appeared way too short, and boy was it frustrating. I struggled enough to where I decided to call the manufacturer for a new part. To their credit the manufacturer was very helpful and shipped me a new set of the top part of the gyro brake - free of charge.
Fast forward - the cable isn’t the problem, it’s the way it was pre-assembled. The trick is to disassemble the top of the gyro (green arrow) thread the cable (per instruction manual) into the handle bar brake, then re-connect the to the top part of the gyro. You may need to adjust the lower part of the gyro as well (yellow).
I hope this helps others that find themselves in a similar situation. I consider myself fairly handy, but my lack of experience with the gyro brake assembly was the real problem. Lesson learned indeed

Review:
I have owned Ebikes since 2011. This is the first time ordering this particular brand. What caught my eye is that the foot pegs are placed where pedal usually go, rather than located on the front wheel spokes, (where you have to reach your legs forward), so someone short like me can reach them; and it can fold to fit in a small trunk.

I was unable to find the app in google play and the manual didn't mention anything about it. Apart from that, this bike is heavier than advertised by about 8 pounds, but it's solid, and well built with a closed motor case to allow you to ride in inclement weather. Because these batteries cannot be purchased or swapped out, I do want to provide some helpful tips to prolong the life of your battery. Do not store the bike in your car or outside when temps are very cold/hot. Doing so will guarantee a dead battery within a year.

Do not ever charge the battery without allowing bike to cool down first; meaning, never charge immediately after riding, let the bike sit for at least 30 minutes. Except for the very first charge, never allow the battery to charge to 100% unless you will use within 15-30 minutes. Only charge to 100% on rare occasions if absolutely necessary. Never, store the bike in a hot or freezing garage. Never, ever, ever store the bike at a 100% charge. Always store between 25-30% to 70% max. I promise you, this will greatly extend the life of your battery. When storing (preferably indoors) you only need to check the battery once every two months, and then top off the charge if needed. I repeat, never charge to 100% on the top off.

Unless battery is very low, check the bike every 15 minutes when charging. When charging/unplugging; plug the cord into bike before the wall, and unplug from the wall, before the bike after charging. Please note, except for brakes and tires etc, this bike cannot be repaired. Bike shops cannot get parts. Ebike shops will not work on them because they are meant to be disposable. Please allow the battery to drop to 20% before charging and charge only to 80%. This will provide you about 1000 charges on the same battery before the battery is no longer able to keep a charge.

You might think I am silly, but I have extended the life of all my bikes years beyond the manufactures warnings. This bike is fantastic on flat road and slight hills, but does not have the torque for big hills. This bike far exceeds the Swagtron in every way; including tire size. In closing, the first thing I did was swap the seat. No matter how tiny you are, the seat is uncomfortable even on the shortest ride. The seat I got looks like two butt cheeks, and is very comfy. I don't have phone or would post a photo of seat. Just type in big butt bike seats and there are a ton of great choices all reasonably priced. Happy riding! You will not be disappointed in this bike. Best purchased out the 4 I have tried in this size range.

Review:
Worth every penny.
I bought this bike at a local sporting goods store at the same price, so I did not have issues with Amazon not being an authorized seller as some other customers apparently did. With that being said, this is the best bike I've owned. I have put a little over 100 miles on it in all four seasons and have had no problems at all. It offers a smooth, quick ride and also the ability to climb hills with ease thanks to Shimano gears. Every bike I have had has had Shimano so I expected the excellent quality. But this was the first Diamondback bike I've bought and I am impressed with the quality. Also, a big plus is that it can handle all four seasons including being left outside in a Utah winter and ready to go for spring with a tuneup. Bottom line is that it's a great buy especially if you get it with a warranty as I did through the store I bought it at. I recommend this for anyone looking for a cheap ride around town.

Review:
This is still an awesome bike for this price range. This is the second such bike I have bought, the first being a orange one for my son ([[ASIN:B003YT1B8E Bikestar 16 inch (40.6cm) Kids Children Boys Bike Bicycle Classic - Orange]]. All the things I have had to say about it still apply here as well, but this one has some upgrades! While the orange one used a one piece crankset, this one uses a 3 piece setup, and it looks like it is a sealed cartridge bottom bracket as well.

It has metal fenders, a rack (with spring clamp), and a nice seat, kickstand (high quality as well), street tires, and front brake (not a cheap side pull one). The step through frame allows my (just turned 4yr old) daughter to mount the bike easily. I received it mostly put together and all the parts where in it box. All I had to do was put the handle bar stem into the fork, drop the seat post in, and bolt the rack and fender screws in. There is instructions, but you really don't need them. They didn't include any grease for the seat post and handle bar stem so you should get some. I used [[ASIN:B002MQYQ2S Park Tool PolyLube 1000 Grease - PPL-1]] for mine.

The rack is pretty good. It has a good surface area to use along with a spring clamp that is no joke. It probably won't hold as much weight as adult racks, but it is solid. We use it to strap a gallon of milk to or just about anything else that will fit. The reflector is nice and big but, like all reflectors they are worthless, I put a [[ASIN:B0030BOUWU Portland Design Works The Red Planet 5 LED Tail Light]] on it. The spacing on the rack is the 50mm horizontal mount popular in Europe so I used a [[ASIN:B003Z7WIQY Portland Design Works Rack Bracket]]. By using velcro ties it is able to carry a [[ASIN:B005YPK99I Kryptonite Evolution Series 4 Standard Bicycle U-Lock with Transit FlexFrame Bracket (4-Inch x 9-Inch)]] on the rack perfectly.

Last time I had a hard time putting the handle bars on because of the brake cable lenght, this time I just took the cable out of the lever (easy enough to do, since they are designed that way). That allowed me to manipulate the handlbars easily. Then I just put the cable back in the lever.

The chain guard could use some work as well. It is made of plastic. While the "outside" of the guard is a full guard, the "Inside" only covers the front chain ring. This leaves a good portion of the chain open to the elements. The "outside" is split into 2 pieces using self taping screws, while they work fine, I can see the holes wearing out after a while. The back portion of the guard is attached to the frame using the back wheel axle nuts. This can making it tricky getting the chain back on the sprocket.

Still a little heavy for a kids bike, but it is no heavier than the 12in bike it replaced.

The tires on it are 16x2.1 street tires. I wish they wasn't as wide, but it does have somewhat wide rims on it. On the other bike I was able to put 26x1.75 on it. Over all though they work really well, but the white walls have already started to turn yellow. I don't have any good solutions yet to keep them bright white.

We used this bike as a everyday commputer bike to daycare for about 3 months. Not once has she wanted to go back to her old bike. She has also done 20 mile rides with with no issues. I don't expect any issues with this at the moment, so I hope to beat the 1200 miles that the other bike has on it.

I figured that I would get some issues with chain slack like I had with the other bike. So I started this one right with [[ASIN:B001GPR0IS Origin8 Chain Tension Adjuster, Silver]]. Not once has the chain came off on me.

The paint on this bike really stands out. The pink is darker than I expected but it is still very nice. I did find a spot or two where it was chipped, but given that it is a kids bike (and she has crashed it a few times now), it is really minor. The bell is ok, it sounds nice and is screw one, but once crash and it falls apart. Not a biggy, I replaced it with a better one [[ASIN:B001C3EJJM Avenir Dual Action Bell (Black)]]

I would advise doing a little research on assembling bikes or take it to a local shop for a once over. Like all new bikes, out of the box it needed some adjustments. The grease in the rear hub might be a little heavy as well and it seemed stiff at first, but after a few miles it really loosened up.

Review:
This bike is BEAUTIFUL!!! Just got it today and took it for a quick spin around the neighborhood with my wife, this was the birthday gift I got her. I'll jump right in and give you my first impressions.

PROS:
- Its extremely sturdy and well built. I'm actually shocked at the quality of the frame and the components. Thick down tubes and good welds everywhere, I think I'm going to get years of use from this bad boy.
- It comes nearly fully assembled. Start to finish, it took me less than an hour to put it together. They've done most of the tricky stuff and you really just have to situate the front tire, the seats and the handle bars.
- Super wide tires. I plan on keeping this at our shore house because it doesn't have gears and flat ground is where this thing needs to be. The wide, white wall tires are gonna be perfect for the occasional patch of sand and I shouldn't get bogged down, that's just a bad accident waiting to happen with two people on a bike when you lose control of the front tire. These fatties should cruise right over.
- The paint is perfect. No low spots or missed coverage. In fact, even the handle bars, seat posts and all other metal pieces I can see have been coated, there's no bare metal anywhere. This should keep visible rust at bay and keep the entire bike looking sharp for a long time.
- I'm a big guy, 6'3, 200 pounds, and I'm very comfortable on this bike. I was able to raise the seat pretty high and the handle bars come back to you pretty far so you'll be seated completely upright and look like royalty as you glide down the road.
- The kickstand. Trust me, it's perfect.

CONS:
- No tools included. You don't need a lot, but if you don't have Allen wrenches in your aresenal, you're going to need to get your hands on some (add them to your order, they don't take up a lot of space in a junk drawer and you'll be surprised how much you use them for other things). You're also going to need a few, different sized wrenches. I think the manufacturer should have included these specific items in the set, but I'm guessing their exclusion lends to the lower price of the bike.
- The pedals. They're great looking out of the box, but aside from the post being metal, the pedal itself is plactic. I forsee this plastic getting brittle over time and snapping off. I'll eventually upgrade mine anyways. If you swap yours out, be mindful that the width of the screw is SMALLER on the rear crankset. If you put this bike together, you'll know what I mean.

That's it, the pros far outweigh the cons.
For the money, you'll be hard pressed to find something of this quality without spending hundreds more.

Review:
I’m 5’10, 175lbs.
The bike melts hills but when they get steep, the bike still needs your help with the climb (although not very much) so don’t expect to just use the throttle up the hills. The 300W probably goes up hills with pure throttle no problem.

I got the bike for exercise so this is fine for me.

The front tire was slightly bent on arrival, which I had to true using a rim truing tool I bought.

I have replaced the seat and pedals, as it comes with cheap versions of both. (they had to cut costs to offer this at $650-800) I also added a saddle storage pouch and a phone mount to keep stuff from falling out of my pockets while I ride.

The shock absorbers are decent, and a shock absorbing seat is a great add on for around $30. Woodsy trails are doable, but don’t expect to go rock climbing with it.

The brakes are good, not great, when trying to do a hard stop from 25-30mph. Good luck to you if you accidentally bump the brake rotor while using the bike, it’s white-hot after a few stops. Leaves a cool scar but I don’t recommend it. Maybe a rotor guard would’ve been nice. I wouldn’t have had to reach down to adjust my wheel if it came from the factory straight.

The brakes are still squeaky two months in.

The throttle is limited to about 21mph, but if you pedal hard it is very easy to hit 30mph on this bike. 30mph on the bike feels controlled, but not utterly safe. This isn’t a Tour de France bike, so expect some shimmy at speeds that high. I’ve hit 36mph downhill so far and didn’t really want to go faster on something so light with B- brakes. Having straight wheels out of the box would’ve been awesome, and it was packaged very cleverly, so I’m not sure how the wheel would’ve been bent in transit. Do not yank the bike out of the box, as a previous reviewer showed himself doing just that, then complained about a bent wheel.

Tires are par for the course, better than what comes on most bikes out of the box, and they look more expensive than they are.

Battery lasts forever on a three hour charge, I have yet to kill the battery on a normal 20-30 mile ride. This differs based on how much pedal assist you use. For the best battery life, keep it between 20% and 90%, as this is where batteries like to live for the longest lifespan.

Level one gives you a nice 5mph boost to get rolling.
Level five gets you to 21 mph as fast as the bike can handle.

The motor after two months has started making some noise, but still works. I wear headphones 90% of the time so it doesn’t bother me. The bike is garage kept and not ridden in the rain - I’m the guy that keeps the chain oiled and checks the tire pressure before riding - so why the motor is making noise is beyond me. I’ll take it apart after the warranty and update the review if anything is broken.

Assembly was decent, took about a half hour. Another hour or two tweaking gears, adjustments, and brakes, but I’m picky.

The screen it came with is a different version than shown, but with the same functions.

The gear shifting isn’t clunky, but I wouldn’t call it smooth either. Like any bike, don’t shift it under load. I’ve found it easiest to just leave it at the highest torque setting with just a little pedal assist to get me rolling.

Definitely a value for the price, but makes me wish I got a better model for an extra $1000 somewhere else, or at the very least went with the 300W model for a couple hundred extra.

I got tons of crap from my kid and my significant other for buying this, two months in they both want one as their Walmart bikes they insisted on buying at the same time are falling apart and I can go for much longer rides. (Told ya!)

TL;DR
If you want an eBike under $1000 that will do the job around the trails and suburbs, with maybe a few small modifications to make it more comfy, this is it. If you’re looking to do hardcore mountain biking up expert level slopes on an eBike with pure throttle, save another 1-2k and get a better one or just get an electric dirt bike for a similar price. B+

Review:
To preface this review, I should note that the company offers people who write a review on Amazon a free bell, which probably accounts for the large numbers of reviews this bike has. But the content of this review will not be influenced by the bell.

My history: I hadn't ridden a bike in about 15 years, but decided to buy one after renting bicycles for several months at a local park. I wanted a bicycle with good reviews that wasn't too expensive, and came upon this bike after weeks of research. I purchased it from the company's website when I saw they were having a sale for Memorial Day.

Assembly: maybe it's just because I'm so new to bikes, but assembly wasn't as easy as some of the reviews would lead you to believe. I ended up taking it to my local bike shop to have them assemble it, as I could not get the brake system to attach properly to the wheel. But I'm also not much of a mechanic, so maybe it's just me.

Appearance. It's beautiful! The paint scratches relatively easily (my cheap box-store watch scratched it when it accidentally bumped into it). It's reasonably heavy in a good way and feels sturdy. I think I'd have a hard time lifting it to put on a roof rack so if you're kinda weak like me, take that into consideration. It fits just fine in my trunk rack.

Function: it rides very well on paved surfaces. It almost feels like gliding on water - very smooth. I'm not a fan of the gear changing mechanism, which is a bit hard to change, especially when you're on the go. I mainly keep it in second gear as a result and rarely change it. I've only ridden it on paved roads around town so far and have yet to take it to a less polished trail. I've also ridden it in grass, which is a real pain, though I won't count that against the bike, as most bikes I've tried are bad in the grass.

I'll update this review when I've had more time with the bike, as I've only been riding less than a month. But so far I enjoy it! I'm interested to see if it holds up over the years and is worth the price tag. In my honest opinion, I wouldn't pay more than $399 for the bike. Keep an eye on their website for discount codes. I bought mine for $350 and feel that price is just right so far.

Review: Decided to buy this as the base to build a 3000w AWD monster. The frame has held up great. The original 36v battery now runs my auxillary power and I've stuffed two massive 48v 20ah batteries in the ammo cans welded to the cargo rack. Speaking of the cargo rack the top underseat holes aren't threaded through the frame instead they're a threaded insert so if you want to throw 80-100lbs on the rear you will likely need to drill straight through and bolt it on that way. Aside from that the original Shimano 21 speed is on it and still working great. I upgraded the rear brakes to an Avid BB7. The front would have been the same if it'd have fit but I couldn't find anything yet aside from the crappy calipers that came stock on the front that fit. You should probably upgrade the brakes calipers and pads right away if you expect to put more than 200lbs on the bike. All in all it worked well as 350w and works much better after a little modification and nearly 10x the power.

Review: Yes, you could spend thousands of dollars on an eBike and get something much higher quality. However, if you're looking for a basic model to try out eBiking this is a great choice. The assembly is easy as far as bikes go (took about an hour for me to do, I'm not an expert). All tools needed are included. I added the plastic mud guards you see and a more comfortable seat. The other reviews are correct that you're probably going to want a better seat (Giddy Up! Bike Seat is what I got - super comfortable). The battery easily handles a 2-4 hour ride if you add some of your own pedal power. I haven't even gotten it up to top speed yet - it is very fast. Components are not top of the line, but decent for the price and work just fine. It works great on dirt roads and singletrack and pavement although too heavy to do tricks or any serious technical mountain biking. I'm looking forward to using it a lot over the summer. Tons of fun!

Review:
I biked about 2k miles last year with my Walmart $100 MTB, mostly on a paved trail. I bought this road bike about 2 weeks ago and had about 120 miles on it. So far I am neither happy or unhappy with it. Here are my initial thoughts on this bike:
Pros:
1. It is a lighter bike than my MTB, though it is still about 30lbs. Tires are thinner and can be inflated up to 85psi. All these result roughly 10% gain on speed with less effort to pedal compared to my MTB.
2. Dropped handle bar gives you more options to position yourself against the strong wind.
3. Easy to assemble. I just need to true the wheels and adjust the brakes and shifters.
Cons:
1. Seat is not comfortable especially during a long bumpy ride.
2. No bottle holder or threaded holes for one. You need to buy the holder separately along with a tap bit.
3. The derailleur shifters are mounted on the stem. It is not that practical if you need to shift constantly.
4. The front derailleur shifter does not have “catching mechanism”. It is like a linear pull where you have to estimate the catching positions of each of those three front gear rings. Luckily I don’t need to shift it that often.
5. Tech support wasn’t available at the time I called two weeks ago. I left my phone number and have not got a call-back.

Bottom line: It is a great cheap bike for commuting on a flat road. If you bike to exercise, you might need to ride a little bit longer to burn the same amount of calories than on a heavier bike.

Review:
I'm 73 years old and haven't ridden a bicycle in many years so I decided to get an e-bike. I was hesitant to buy online, but the price and the reviews were favorable. So far I'm delighted with the Ancheer.
The instructions are adequate; the video on the ad is better than the printed instructions, but it's fairly easy to assemble. I comes with a pouch of all the tools you need; I keep them in the battery bag in case of need.
The Bike is foldable but the instructions and video say nothing about folding it. I did a search and found a video that explains how.
It has disc brakes, front and rear shocks, Shimano shifters and headlight. The shifting is so smooth I sometimes wonder if it actually shifted. I usually use it in pedal assist mode, that's best for me. In direct e-mode, it has trouble with steep hills but the assist does well.
I haven't had it long enough to really judge longevity or battery life.
Overall, I'm delighted with it! It's great on the new walk-bike trail they built on an old railroad line near my home.
Happy Biking!

Review:
Ok, I wanted to put a few hundred miles on this bike before I wrote a review....so here goes.

I love it. It's huge and fast as hell, and I love every second of it. That being said...

You're gonna have to get a new seat. The seat that comes with the bike is a torture device. Don't even try. Just buy a new seat along with the bike.

You're going to want to set it up for your body type too. I got the XL, and I feel like it's a good size for me. I'm 6'2", 200lbs, and once you adjust the seat, I was good to go.

Now, this isn't a top end, blasting down the Himalayas kinda kit. I try and ride six days a week...maybe an hour or so at a time...so I'm a mid-level type. I'm also 40 and don't wanna die. So take that into account.

If this bike has been in your cart for awhile, pull the trigger, I promise you won't be disappointed.

EDIT: Okay, I'm closing in on a thousand miles on this bike, and thought I did get a tune up at a local bike shop, I have noticed a few issues with a bit of bottom bracket wobbles. I think the frame and tires are fantastic, but you may want to upgrade the drive train after a bit.

Review:
We got two of those bikes (red and silver) after reading all the positive reviews. Buying experience from 2KSIVER was good though there was several days delay - looked like they were out of stock (though the web site listed them in stock with 5 left). But they answered all emails promptly and did their best to get bikes delivered in time for our vacation.
The bikes came securely packed and almost fully assembled, the red one had a small frame damage but that was not critical. No tools needed - unfold the frame and pedals, turn the seat, install the handlebar, inflate tires and you are ready to go. They do the trick - both easily fit in the back of our Subaru Outback with rear seatbacks down and that was what we needed the folding bikes for. First impression was good, bikes seem well build and ride is relatively comfortable thanks to double suspension. Few negative comments after the first ride:
- Rear derailer in one bike needed adjustment, had to un-tight the cable screw and readjust cable length otherwise the gear number on the hand bar did not correspond with the number of the sprocket.
- In both bikes real derailer guards were bent and prevented switching to gears 5 and 6. Had to manually straighten them. That and the previous problem with derailer would be impossible task for a lot of people with no mechanical skills - means visit to a repair shop.
- Front derailer does not have gear number indication.
- Despite of the alloy frame and rims they are relatively heavy - 34 lb.
- They do not support riders heavier than 200 lb - come on, who they are for, just teens and girls?? should be good for 250 lb at least.
- Both I and my wife found the narrow seats very uncomfortable. Ended up with spending $60 more for two wide soft seats (and Columba does make the wider seats part # 20A for the same price as the original part # 26A ones!). Besides, being 6'2" I had to order a longer 12" seat post separately. It should be possible to select seat post length and seat style while ordering the bikes!
Other than that - nice bikes for the money, overall quality and durability seem to be good.

Review:
This review is for the Huffy Hardtail Mountain Trail Bike - 26 inch wheels/20 inch frame, Gloss Nickel.

I was not much of a biker and have not rode a bike in ten years. When looking for a "bike", was not sure what to get or what to look for when purchasing online (i.e. can't try it out before purchasing). I picked this model mainly because of the low cost ($275.00) and good reviews. Next, I was not even sure how to pick the frame size or wheel size. After some internet reading, the 20-inch frame size was the best option for my 5'10 height and chose the 26-inch wheel size (seemed proportional to the 20-inch frame). My main objective was "joy" riding and not really doing any biking on mountains or off-road biking.

The bike came in new - very little assembly. Only had to install the front tire and the handle bar assembly. The packaging include a multi-purpose tool (wrench) and hex wrenches to assembly the bike.

Once the bike was assembled, I had two issues:
1. The rubber brake pads were slightly rubbing against the rim. I had to play around with it a bit to adjust the brakes. Was able to adjust them where they were perfect.

2. Now, the hard part. The front derailleur was not working correctly. I could not shift into some of the gears and could also hear slight clicking/tapping when peddling. Very long story short, I had to watch many videos on YouTube on how to adjust the derailleur (i.e. low/high limits, cable tension..etc). Also, without a bike stand to hold up the bike, this becomes a tedious effort. For those of you who have had to do this, you know the pain. So, after many many hours of adjusting the limits and cable tension, got it to where it is 99% better when using the gear shifters (from and back).

The bike size fits me perfect. No issues with comfort in riding. I can easily adjust the seat height and handle bars for a better fit.

Overall, this is a decent mountain bike for what I need it for. Not looking for anything special. I do enjoy riding it and happy with the purchase. If you are looking for a bike for cruising or joy riding, then a mountain bike is not for you. (mainly because of the treads on the wheels).

Couple items to know about:

1. Change out the bike seat ASAP. Too hard and causes pain/rash within 15min of riding.

2. If you remove the front derailleur tension cable, make sure you put it back on the correct way or else the gear lever will not work. It will feel tight and you will not be able to shift any gear. I learned this the hard way after I spent about three hours trying to figure out why I could not shift the gear lever. Finally, I realized my very simple mistake. When you wrap the tension cable around the locking nut, you need to loop it in a clockwise motion, not counter clockwise. - lesson learned. (see attached picture).

Review:
Update four months in. I love it, and have upgraded a lot of parts, but as is, the build is still good enough. The only critique is the toe overlap for me (size 11 shoe). I’ve gone for 165mm cranks and barely get any overlap with road tires, but a 32c cyclocross tire hits my shoe decently well...maybe if you do tracklocross, you time your pedal stroke better than I do.

I do mostly road riding with it, but use it fixed as well, and I have a 4 tooth freewheel-cog difference, with no brake pad rub issue.

Original Review:
More of a spec review than tested review, as the bike isn’t together yet. Here are the details that are hard to find.
It’s the 2018 model.
Wheelset. No name hubs/rims. Rims are 20+mm internal width, hubs are loose bearing, needed more grease. Solid M9/10 axels. Probably 2,200g. Front was just over 1kg, didn’t take the included 18t no name freewheel off to weigh the rear. Includes 17t track cog, too. Tires are Kenda Kadence Sport 28mm, wire bead. Looks more like a 25mm.
Stem. Cinelli. 100mm 135g for Large; simple and clean.
Seatpost. Cinelli. Single bolt, not light.
Saddle. Cinelli branded San Marco. Mid level padding, medium width. (142ish, 280ish grams).
Handlebar. Cinelli. OS 680mm-ish, small rise. Tektro MTB levers and vbrakes.
Headset. Cage bearings. Needs more grease.
Chain. KMC singlespeed.
Crankset. No name. 170mm, 130bcd Road Double, with generic 46t chainring, 1/8th. Looks fine. FSA RPM square taper bottom bracket.
Frameset itself is beautiful.

General thoughts: Great bike with the main weak points being the headset, seatpost and the lack of a good seal on the hub bearings. Nice they included decent tires, wide rims and a freewheel. For the price point, the wheel weight is fine. I wish they’d used bar top road levers and short reach vbrakes instead of MTB spec, as I prefer drop bars and road levers when ss or cx. Also, opt for the free pro build up, even if you wrench a lot. Cinelli warranty requires proof of it.

Don’t forget to prep the frame inside before building it up.

Will update after a few hundred miles on it.

Review:
[[VIDEOID:f92bc77255010bd68588c0deb590d7b8]] So I loved this bike, I rode the hell out of it for.. 2 days

Then it almost sent me under a bus when the pedal broke off in traffic. I got to clock less than 15 miles on this before it happened, and rode it on mostly level ground.

The pedal and pedal arms are both aluminium and extremely prone to wear by friction after which the pedals will.. fall off because there is no thread left. Unluckily this happened to me while I was riding next to a truck and I almost ended up underneath it.

Schwinn has cut corners on this, by making a manufacturing change that couldn't cost more than $15. Casting or pressing the parts from steel, OR even reverse threading on both sides to prevent the pedals coming loose. This change would have meant that they couldn't reuse the casting mold for the pedals and would need one for each pedal and for this reason the decision was made to drastically reduce the life of the bike.

This bike suffers from greed for revenue at the expense of the end-user. This bike could have been amazing and would have been a 5-star review.

Greed for profit margin has ruined this bike and I cannot in good faith recommend this even to the worst of enemies.

Review:
I'm a casual bicyclist, and it suits my purposes perfectly. I was going to pay a local bicycle shop to put it together, but they said that it would take a month for them to get to it, so I put it together myself. Turns out assembly was a breeze. The handle bar came pre-assembled with shifters and breaks already attached, I just had to bolt it down. I just needed to add the tires which are quick release and the seat, and it was ready to go! (A mountain bike that I used to own was a nightmare, the gears chattered, the brakes were difficult to adjust and the handle bar was almost impossible to tighten completely, it took forever to assemble, which is why I was willing to pay for the assembly of this one.)
The bike seat wasn’t comfortable at first because I haven’t ridden in years. I just needed to get used to it. It’s fine now.
The shifting is excellent, no chatter. The click shifters are so easy that I don't even think about changing gears when the peddling gets easier or harder. I never had click shifters before, and I don't want to go without them ever again on a bicycle. The grip shifters on my old mountain bike would cause me to accidentally shift gears when I was going over bumps from time to time.
The mountain bikes that I used to own, always felt small like I was trying to ride a child size bike. This hybrid feels like an adult bike to me, and really is a complete joy to ride. I quit riding my last bike when I hit a rock and was thrown over the handle bars and landed on my face. This bike feels like that is less likely to happen. The disc brakes are great, they work when the tires get wet, no slipping!
Even though the hybrid bike is taller than my last mountain bike, it feels lighter. The peddle crank also feels like it's longer than the old mountain bike, giving me a more comfortable stroke, which I like.
For its price, $350, I don't know if I could have gotten a better bike.

Review:
The color is coral with other parts blacked out, 21 speed. The assembly only took about 30 minutes only because my husband bought the men's model in matte black and assembled his first.
His came with a "Made in China" label on it which accounts for the poor quality control.

His came with a misaligned fork due to a welding mistake, it made assembling the front wheel impossible. It was misaligned by the diameter of the axle plus a little more. When sixthreezero was contacted, they stated they do not honor warranty of "used" bikes. His bike came as packed at the factory and obviously had never been assembled. It was labeled as "used" because of the holes in the shipping box which caused no damaged whatsoever. It was suggested to him to buy the forks and fix the lack of quality control issue at his own expense. It was also suggested that I repack the bike and return it to amazon.

Expecting a $400 bike to be perfect out of the box, we discarded the box and packing materials as it was unboxed due to the small space we live in. Returning the bike was not an option.

I like my bike very much. As for my husband's bike, he offered it to someone for $50 and explained the bad welding. He couldn't sell it. For now it seems like it will wind up in the dumpster, as a useless paperweight or as a rusting piece of junk in the yard.

He regrets his decision to buy sixthreezero; regrets suggesting it to me and regrets recommending it to the neighbors who commented on it during assembly.
He wishes he bought the Schwinn Huron, Sanctuary 7 or one of the Huffy models. Maybe in about six months he can afford another. You can read his honest review titled "Terrible Quality Control" at the sixthreezero men's Around The Block bike.

Review:
Bought this bike for ease of transportation to and from my job which was a town over, a few months ago after having finally received my tax return as I can't afford to pay for an actual car. I apologize in advance for the lengthy review but I wish to warn potential buyers in detail about what they're getting.

It arrived quickly and intact, wasn't all too hard to put together despite some slightly confusing things which many other reviews have gone into detail about already...

At first I thought I had a gem of a bike after getting everything set up and figuring out all the controls, et cetera. To it's credit the battery has a good bit of capacity to it and the motor is capable of kicking out around like 20 mp/h or more depending on what you're on and if you're pedaling or not.

But moving on to the HUGE problems I've had with this bike, everything worked fine for the first week before I learned that this bike's got some really cheap, poorly manufactured parts.

Within the first week and a half or so, the locking mechanism for the bike battery somehow inexplicably completely broke, the key lock mechanism quite literally fell out of the slot entirely, leaving only the locking metal pin to keep the battery in place.

Additionally within a week after that, the seat that came with this bike actually completely broke off of the post upon hitting a very small pothole on the road in the middle of the night on a freeway, leaving me to have to ride ~6 more miles back home standing up... Not cool. (I'm 5'10 and 210 lbs for the record... I'm a tad on the heavy side but that's still ridiculous for how easily it broke!)

I had thought that this could have been a simple fluke in manufacturing with these parts and contacted support for this company to order replacements (which by the way is operated by Chinese people using a translator so there's a slight language barrier at play)

Replacements for the battery locking mechanism and a new seat arrived about a week and a half after several days' worth of back and forth with support to try and explain my problem exactly so I can get replacements.

Alas, I kid you not, within three whole days of very light, bump free riding, the new battery lock broke in the EXACT SAME WAY. The seat seems to be holding up but that's a moot point considering major problem #2 which I'm now dealing with.

After a combined total of like two and a half months of riding this bike with a conscious effort to avoid potholes across perhaps ~200-300 miles for a grand total I've ridden, several spokes on the back wheel suddenly break all within the same day, counting at least five spokes broken and at least 3 more damaged.

I contacted support to ask for a replacement wheel since I lack the tools/cash, physical ability or knowledge to repair a wheel with broken spokes but they were extremely ADAMANT on only sending me replacement spokes which I can't use. I had to get a hold of a local professional to attempt to get help in fixing this, and even with his tools, it was beyond his ability to fix because the cheapo tires they use bent pretty easily...

I'm now looking at having to spend around 150-200 dollars out of pocket simply to buy a new wheel and get the motor hub transferred on to a new rim with new spokes because of shoddy manufacturing, and I can barely afford my rent as it is... Pretty awesome.

I'd suggest steering FAR clear of this bike if you don't know much about bike repair because you may end up in my situation as well.

It also had a large gash in the rear tube, so the tire wouldn't hold air (not a deal breaker since I still had a good tube on Bike #1). Diamondback then sent me a front wheel packaged better and by itself which arrived in good shape.

Hopefully the front wheel holds up while mounted on the bike. If not, I'm confident Diamondback will take care of me. If you are considering this bike, read the recent reviews and see if they have fixed the shipping problem that is cracking those front hubs. I bet they will. Review: This is a great e-bike as long as you understand that the reasonable price means it will not be as zippy as the more expensive e-bikes.  We have this e-bike and a Pedego Latch e-bike, both purchased at the same time.  The Ancheer e-bike is great for hubby as he likes to pedal hard for a great exercise workout.  Since I work out at home a lot, I use my Pedego Latch more on throttle than peddling to go farther and see the scenery on the bike path while also getting a mild workout.  The Ancheer takeoff speed on throttle is about half of what you get on the Pedego Latch.  The Ancheer bike using the throttle less went down from 5 bars to 3.5 bars fairly quickly while the Pedego Latch using the throttle more went down from 5 bars to 4.5 bars.  Once up and running, the Pedego Latch can breeze by the Ancheer easily on a straight stretch.  The Ancheer is a handsome, sturdy ebike.  Hubby is very happy with this purchase, with the exception of the battery which is not encased in a hard shell and hangs off the front of the bike in a material case.  He wishes that Ancheer offered a better battery and battery placement/cover; would have been willing to pay more for that option.  The Pedego Latch has a much heftier battery that's encased and then sits in a hard case on the back of the Latch, giving you a longer running time. Knowing the limitations, he would not reverse his decision to purchase.  The bike arrived early in perfection condition and the Amazon assembly went smoothly.
