Review:
I purchased two of these bikes. One came with the display broken/cracked, the other had a warped brake disc. One had a chip in the paint and the bell was broken. One came with the chain guard bent. They obviously have a problem with quality control. I contacted the supplier, and they sent a new display and brake disc the next day.
The first time my husband rode his bike, his shoe caught in the chain guard and it bent into the chain. He had to remove it. I didn't bother to put my chain guard on, since it was warped already. They ride fine without it, you just have to make sure your pants don't get caught in the chain. But really, my previous bikes never had one, so I don't miss it.
The horn on my bike is pretty funny; the speaker faces me (it's behind the light), so I'm the only one who hears it. I guess the people behind me can hear it. Maybe it will warn them to not run me over when they get to me?
I am 5'7" and purchased the step-over. I wish I had purchased the step-through.
Aside from the quality problems mentioned above, overall I like the bikes. I love how wide the tires are. The ride is smooth, and seems sturdy. They aren't too heavy. I rode up/down mountain roads yesterday, and the battery was still over half full after 23 miles. I would recommend this bike.

Review:
We bought two of these bikes--one for my 2.5 year old and one for my 1.5 year old. Here's what we've found:

-The younger one is short so we sawed off a bit of the seat post in order to get the seat lower and it allllmost fits her.

-it's hard for the kids to figure out which way the handle bars go. We attached a basket to the front to help a bit and that works well. Still, the handle bars turn all the way around and look almost the same whether they're facing the correct way or wrong way.

-the tires ripped open on one of them. I called the manufacturer and they shipped two new tires without asking any questions. No hassle. It was great. I don't know why the tires ripped open because I'd filled them properly and it doesn't get very cold or hot where we live.

-having air in the tires makes for a great and smooth ride for the kids. It's also quiet.

-we love having the adjustable seat. The handlebars don't adjust, but we haven't needed that. By the time we would need that, the kids will be on peddle bikes.

-there is a foot platform that my younger daughter can rest her feet on while we push her. Since she can't reach the ground while sitting on the seat, the platform is nice to help her understand the concept of riding a bike.

-the foot platform gets very scratched every time the bike falls. We are mountain bikers so we don't mind scratched bikes.

All in all, we are very happy with this bike!

Review:
This bike is really good for the money. With all the mixed reviews, I didn't know what to expect, and when I saw the box was a bit beat up I was a bit concerned. But once i opened it up, inspected all the parts before putting it together, I can say that the bike looks great and no parts were damaged in transit.

I haven't had this bike long enough to comment on the longevity or long term durability, but after a few rides I'm very satisfied with this purchase. The only reason this bike loses a star, is due to it lacking a mount point for a water bottle and because I had to tweak the front brake calipers a bit to prevent them from touching the wheel when not being used. The handle bars are also a bit low, which means I'm hunched over when riding (but it does provide me with a good arm workout).

One thing to note too, and I saw a lot of questions about this on the product Q&A page: this is a larger bike, even though it says it's a medium size. I'm 6'1" and this bike is a perfect size for me, however I'd say it might be difficult to ride for those under 5'9"-10", so that's something to keep in mind before ordering.

Overall though, solid starter bike to get me into cycling. 4/5!

Review:
Great bike.... Love it... as you can see i customized it. Added the bike seat, the rain guards, put a few reflector stickers in red on it and some bike lights on the tires that i usually put red when i ride at night.

Aside from that, set up is pretty easy for someone who doesn't know too much about bikes lol. For the most part instructions are easy to follow. I can say make sure you know which is the left and right pedal when installing. I had on the wrong sides which cost me half an hr. Also there are two blots with a long screw you dont need. I miss read the instructions and thought i had to put it in the front tire. (You Dont lol) It Does max speed of 35mph as stated. Im 225 lbs and can go a easy 33mph. Down hill or down the Williamsburg bridge i hit 45mph.

Also to use throttle mode set assistant level indicator to 0 on the lcd display by pressing down. Pedal assist works great.

Few down sides.
#1 On the description of the battery it says throttle mode gives you about 22 miles as i posted the picture above. Thats not true. I ride from Rikers Island to lower Manhattan which is give or take 10 miles on bike depending on your route. When im riding on a full battery just on the throttle, I feel like after 6 or 8 miles (20 mins or throttle riding) the battery is already down two 2 bars out of 5 bars. I then have to switch to pedal assist. (Maybe my battery is defective which is why it dies so fast on throttle mode)

#2 It would make things easier if you didnt have to switch from throttle to pedal assist. If you were able to have both on at the same time it would be cool. Throttle to start and then just start pedaling with the assist. Instead you are force to use the lcd display to switch from one to the other each time.

#3 Lcd display hard to see at night. It would of have been better with the back light on the lcd display.

Thats about it. OVERALL GREAT BIKE. LOVE IT.

Review:
My 26-inch 3-speed Urban Lady ("Vanilla" color, which is actually more of a creamy-bright yellow, like eggnog!) is a rock solid bike, lovely to look at, and a joy to ride. From a childhood spent on "sting ray" style bikes (relatively low & upright seating position) with "banana" seats, as an adult I've never been able to feel comfortable or confident on bikes that require that you sit high and lean forward. So recently I figured, heck, I'm in my mid-fifties... why shouldn't I be able to ENJOY riding a bike, who cares what "everybody else" is riding?? I get lots of smiles & compliments every time I take my Urban Lady for a spin-- we're an unusual sight in my urban environment!
Note: the bike is quite heavy, and its kickstand is a bit anemic; I replaced the kickstand with a double-legged one that swings handily out of the way, to the side just like the stock kickstand. Also note, the color of the fenders is NOT a particularly good match to the color on the bikes; they don't "clash," but you can easily tell they come from different dye lots. Finally, while the seat may be comfortable to some, I did not find it so; I replaced it with a cushier one, and then also added a gel bike seat pad on top of that... heaven! :-)

Now for the unpleasant parts of my buying experience:
I am completely bewildered by the glowing customer service reviews /comments that BeachBikes has for the various bikes they sell in Amazon. I got a better price by buying directly from the BeachBikes website, so I dealt directly with them. Here's my story:
I initially purchased a 26" bike, then thought I might be more comfortable on a smaller one, so ordered a 24" to try out (they have a good return policy). In the end I decided to keep the larger bike.
1) the BB website did not have 26" fenders visible for order with my first bike, so I had to call to order it. Never got email confirmation/tracking # of the fender order, so several days later I had to email them and ask for it. It took a couple of days for them to get back to me.
2) The link to a video with assembly instructions was sent with their delayed reply; the model did not exactly match the bike I had purchased, and some of the videography and instructions were too vague-- one of the results of this was that if it were not for my husband noticing, I would have been trying to ride around with my front wheel fork facing in the wrong direction.
3) They claim that every customer gets assigned their own "personal mechanic" to field questions during assembly; my mechanic must have been fired or was out on vacation, because I never got any help. Halfway through assembly i ran into a problem so I called during their business hours. I was sent to their voicemail, and NEVER received a return call in spite of having left a detailed message. Thank goodness I have a few basic mechanical skills, so I figured out what I needed to do. I know they're a brick&mortar with live customers to serve, but calling customers back, at least within 48 hours, should not be too much to ask. I've called them three additional times during their hours, at different times of day, and have NEVER had a human answer the phone. Email replies from them are also inappropriately delayed-- several days, and sometimes they need an extra email to remind them that you emailed them the first time!
4) Once I had tried out both the 26" and 24" bikes, I made my decision about which one to keep. I DID get good packing instructions from customer service, and (with no small effort) was able to get the bike back into its box, and the fenders back into theirs. Shipped it all FedEx Ground using labels BeachBikes provided, with notes inside the box stating my name & order #. I have FedEx confirmation that BeachBikes received & signed for my packages two weeks ago... but they have still NOT contacted me confirming my return, NOR have they initiated a credit to my credit card.

Based on the reviews on Amazon mentioning BeachBikes' customer service, it may well be that they are choosing to focus their service efforts on Amazon customers; my experience says that if you deal with them directly, you are definitely NOT a priority or a valued customer,
The bike itself deserves 4 stars (one star off for the weak kickstand and for one particularly nasty quirk in its assembly requirements); since this is a "product" review and not a "seller" review, I don't want to penalize the bike for BeachBikes' behavior, so I have resisted the impulse to rate it 2 or 3 stars because of them.

Review:
I purchased this bike because it doesn't look like a typical e-bike with that monstrosity of a battery along the frame where my water bottle should be. The battery on this bike sits under the rear bike rack where nobody will notice.

The bike looks like a regular bike and rides like one too. No need to turn the battery on if you would like to get some exercise and ride around the town. The 6 speeds are ample enough to ride comfortably and maneuver the hills. The seat is comfortable and I am glad I don't have to purchase a more comfy seat. The handlebars are upright and I don't have to strain my back. The bike is a little heavy to put in your car or carry up the stairs though. It is a larger bike; I am a light, F at 5.5' but it is not much of an issue when riding the bike at all because I got the seat low enough. The step through frame makes it easy to get on and off the bike.

The bike took about two hours for me to assemble, but I took my time. The hardest thing was to align the front wheel to make sure the brakes don't rub. If they do, take it to your local bike shop to tune it up and make sure all is well with assembly. I found that I could not lower the seat all the way because the battery was in the way. I changed the notches along the rack mount so it would allow seat to be lowered another inch perhaps. The front wheel doesn't have that quick release lever, so just make sure you tighten the bolts in. Maybe you can replace bolts with quick release.

I took it for a ride in my neighborhood and was able to figure out the control pad in just a few seconds. I found that the throttle was a blast right away. The assist modes were startling as it is delayed and accelerates to a max speed. Best to engage the assist modes when already at a faster pace. The throttle is great because it has wider range of sensitivity than the assist mode and is instantaneous. I took the bike on a packed sand trail to test it out and I found the bike was a little hard to control, the front wheel felt light and wobbly. Bike is meant for the street or packed dirt path and rides fine in these conditions.

The battery is still at about 3 out of 5 bars after almost 20 miles of cycling so far so that is good news. The throttle is my favorite e-bike feature, and it can give you that little boost when you need and can keep you out of trouble as most people can't run faster than 20 MPH :).

Pros:
Looks like a regular bike and rides like one without E-bike functions - no resistance.
Looks cool, well made and a comfortable ride with good seat.
Battery is decent
Throttle is great, good range of sensitivity.
Has front and rear lights and a horn.
Has a USB port for charging devices on the battery.

Cons:
Bike is heavy and on the larger side.
Assist modes are delayed and abrupt, be careful of the acceleration and what's in front of you like people or traffic . Works fine if already at fast pace.
Bike has motor in rear, so heavier and balance is off. Lighter in front and can pop wheelies if too fast or out of control. Lean forward.
Brakes are not great, use both left and right.
Handlebars/front wheel are wobbly, normal due to heavier rump.

Review:
The manual isn't very complete - you probably need some experience with bicycles. I bought two for the grandkids, and they are successfully using them daily for their 4-mile commute to high school. The tires are a bit flimsy, and they came barely inflated. If you simply add air, you might blow the bead off the rim if you aren't careful. You need to massage them until the bead seats evenly. The grandkids had three flats in the first month from tiny seed pods, so I installed liners and Slime tubes. The fenders are flimsy, perhaps a hazard because the rear fenders have come loose.
Even without power, I like the feel of the bicycles. The disc brakes work great. (I hope pads will be available). The shifting is quite nice. The front suspension also feels nice, but might be too soft for aggressive riders, and the seals weep a little. Power feels fairly good, speed isn't, but local laws require 20mph cut-out in order to be categorized for unlicensed riders under 16 years old.
One of the batteries had a minor defect. The receptacle wasn't tightened down, and the dust plug fell off it's mount the first day that bicycle was used. I removed the lower cover from the battery pack, melted the adhesive with a soldering iron, loosened the nut far enough to reinstall the dust plug, tightened the nut, and reflowed the thermal adhesive. It's been fine so far.
The kickstand design is very nice, the toe-to-front-wheel clearance is good. These bicycles look sharp. I would buy them again, and might even buy a third one. The price is very appealing.

Review:
This bike is gorgeous. Make no mistake, this was one of the primary reasons I selected it. The retro cruiser frame with its smooth lines and curves in a shiny black finish definitely draws attention, especially with the bright chrome accents and the white-wall tires. Everywhere I go people stop to comment on this bike. I wanted a bike that functions well but also had character and elegance, and that's what I got with this bike. It really is a beauty.

Note: to really spruce up the bike, as well as to help protect against splashes, I invested in a pair of chrome fenders. I was not disappointed. They completed the bike's look, and help minimize mess during/after rains. The set I purchased: [[ASIN:B002KVOV2A B002KVOV2A]]

The other reason I purchased the bike, besides its cool look, was for riding. Shocking, I know. I had previously used a similar bike, an old '67 Huffy Corvair. I was reluctant to replace the old bike because of its style, its smooth ride, and its reliability. I bike a couple times a week in an urban setting, (Chicago) and while liked how balloon-tire cruisers handle potholes and irregularities in streets, the fixed gear and back-pedal breaks of the old bike weren't very good for fast starts/stops in a city in traffic, and was tiring to keep up with traffic with for long street rides. I wanted a bike with similar comfort, but more breaking control and faster speeds with less pedaling for extended rides. It was difficult to find a cruiser with shifters and hand breaks, but this bike was the perfect match, particularly for comfort.
The balloon tires are even fatter than those of my old bike, lending even better cushion against the ever-present potholes in Chicago's streets. The speeds adjust to match the preference of the rider, so I do not have to pedal non-stop as I did on the fixed gear cruiser. The size is a nice step up for me as well...at 6'1" my old bike, even at highest seat position, did not allow for my legs to extend properly during pedaling. Not so with this bike. Make no mistake, this bike is BIG. This is a good thing, particularly for riders of the long-limbed variety. This is a great bike for smooth, comfortable riding and control.

The downsides:
The biggest downside, which was easily corrected, was that the ring washer for tightening the pedal shaft was not properly tightened. This meant that ball-bearings that kept the pedals smoothly, but tightly rotating were slowly falling out if the bike was jostled. I caught it in time, tightening the ring with a screwdriver, and though with a few bearings missing the pedal housing is a little loose, it's certainly still fully functional.

The bike is of the heavier variety, so if you have to carry it you'll have a workout. I added a rack, fenders, and baskets so I am aware mine is heavier for that, but just bear in mind this will be something to think of if you need to lift the bike often. I haven't weighed mine, but if you are looking for a light bike for fast rides, thus might not be the bike for you. It never is too much of an issue keeping a good speed, but this is more geared toward comfortable rides than fast ones.

The chain guard brackets are not very rigid or strong, so if it is bumped the guard can rub on the chain during riding. so be careful about the chain guard, and be prepared to carefully bend it back into proper position should it be bumped at all.

The bike seat is very comfortable, but oil the springs on the mount, as the seat was creaking as much as my old '67 within weeks of my purchase.

The shifters work decently, but do not always 'catch' as you change speeds, so it requires a quick switch/switch back for the gear shift to 'take' at times. It doesn't affect performance, just a minor nuisance.

Though minor, the sticker accents are very obviously just that. Stickers on an otherwise VERY classy bike.

All in all, I have been very pleased with this bike. It is extremely comfortable and capable, and works well for riding about town in an urban setting. (And looks darned good while doing so.) I affixed a basket rack and three baskets for grocery runs, and fenders for other weather situations, and have been quite happy with the bike. Getting it where I wanted it took some tuning here and there, but now that it's where I want it to be, the bike is a joy to ride. The price point for such a bike is also very affordable. I got more than my money's worth on this purchase. Will this be the last bike I purchase? No. I can tell the quality, though good, won't be able to stand up to decades of wear like my previous cruiser, but for this price and for the added comfort and convenience, this is well worth the money spent.

Edit July 18th, 2015: Two years of use, and the bike itself held up far better than I expected. I was contemplating adjusting my review to five stars, when the bike seat broke as I was riding it. The seat was easily replaced, but for simple street biking at my weight (175 lbs) I would not have expected the seat to be the first part to go.

Overall I think this is a really great bike for the money!
I gave it 4 stars because I think there are a couple little things that could be better.
#1 Low ground clearence. I was able to solve this on my bike and after talking with customer service I think they are working on it and would be willing to send you the rear suspention that will lift the bike.

https://www.amazon.com/dp/B08TSZ5FDQ?ref=ppx_yo2ov_dt_b_product_details&th=1

Review: I bought this L100 last fall/winter with a "used, like new condition" and couldn't be happier with it. I ride it to and from work every single day. I have put it through some rough riding, jumping everything i can everywhere I go. This is my first all chromally steel bike, and I can't imagine ever getting anything but that now. I can forsee it lasting a very long time with proper maintenance and care. Just wish it had a front brake and a slightly smaller back tire, as these tires do make it harder to accelerate do to the amount of grip and friction they have. 2.4" wide tires are much much different than anything else I've ever had. I think 2.3" would be perfect on the back. Have some good lithium grease ready for the bearings on the front fork when you install. This bike has been my best friend for the past 8 months or so, and besides just regular maintenance and tightening the crank and chain once a month, and a couple flat tires repaired, it had been very reliable and indestructible as far as what I personally put it through. If you've never had an all chromally (frame, forks, handlebars, crank arms) you are in for a real treat. I would hands down buy this bike again. Either that or piece together and build my own.

Review:
For the price there is a lot to like about this bike:
1) Super smooth 24 gear trigger shifting - no more half way shifts from twist shifters
2) Full suspension - off road and boardwalks no longer shake out my dental work
3) Disc brakes - Smooth confident stopping
4) Super rugged build - Frame and wheels look and feel like they can take a beating
5) Quick release front and back wheels
6) Kickstand included
7) Large frame and wheels - Ideal for taller (over 6ft) and heavier riders

But for the price their had to be compromises... here's where I think they cut costs
1) The bike is heavy... I mean ruggedness and large size contribute but this thing is close to 40lbs, expensive exotic materials could've helped.
2) It's mass produced and each part does not seem custom calibrated, so getting it checked by a bike pro is a good idea.
3) The directions and overly wordy and contain instructions for other bikes and equipment making assembly seem more complicated than it is.

For me... in Florida I am not pushing this thing up a lot hills and am using it mostly for local trails and roads so the the weight isn't much of an issue. I found the full suspension a joy, at first it feels like your tires are just a little low on air, but once you go on rough terrain you can definitely feel a difference compared to road or even front suspension bikes. I assembled the bike myself so that I would be more familiar with the bike, but then brought it into my local bike shop for a tune up, this saved my half the cost of having them assembling it out of the box and I feel I got both the bike knowledge and the confidence that it was put together properly. I thought my assembly of it was pretty easy and good enough, but between when I dropped it off and when I got it back they routed brake lines better, calibrated the brakes and rotated the handle bars to their ideal location. So having a pro finish it is highly recommended.

Like every bike I've owned I think the stock seat could be improved and I'm going to need to add a water bottle holder, but overall I'm very happy with this heavy duty, smooth riding beast of a bike.

Review:
I was looking for a bike to ride with my 4 yr old son for the summer.
In the past, I've had to take my cheap bike rack and strap it on my itty bitty toyota.
This takes much time, and is a bit cumbersome, especially when trying to install a child seat, etc.
So I thought, hmmm.... folding bike? That sure would be more convenient.
Throw them both in the trunk, and I'm ready to go.

So off I went researching potential products. Here's what I found out:

Now, I've been researching bikes for three weeks in order to purchase something.
My budget isn't very extensive really - about 400 max.

I wanted something that was durable and simple . . . and most importantly - safe.

Here's what I discovered . . . from many of the reviews I read - some things to note-
- There are some folding bike reviews that describe stories of hinges that break or loosen where the bike folds
- Folding pedals might be a plus for storage, but on some models these pedals seem to fail routinely if anchored into a cheap crankset
- According to reviews, most entry level folding bikes don't seem to really be intended for the day to day abuse of commuting
- Most reviews proclaimed that light recreational use on most entry level models was great, but once bikes were used more regularly for
traveling or commutes, parts began to fail - so good components really matter.
- All of the above comments seemed to appear more than once for various models - so read the reviews - or the good ones anyway.

I wanted a bike that could be easily serviced over its lifespan:
I looked heavily into some Dahon Models, such as the Vybe C7A, Speed D7 and even (especially) Speed Uno.
All are really good bikes, but . . .
According to many reviews, Dahon doesn't seem to offer good customer service in the US, unless you live near a dealer, which I do not.
So if a component on the bike breaks, where and how do I get a new one? I'm sure this is possible, but not easy.
The Speed Uno is nice looking - but coaster breaks are not efficient at stopping the bike. The pedals on this model seemed to have issues too.
I looked heavily into Retrospec. Very reasonably priced and good looking stuff. But good luck on trying to get anyone to email you back.
Again, what do I do if something arrives broken or breaks on the bike? Sure, it's possible, but not easy.

But you know what's easy? You can look up Allen Sports, and they've got a phone number - not just a twitter account - but like a REAL phone number! In fact there's even THREE on their website AND and email address. You can call and talk to a REAL PERSON and get help - and parts!!
Wow man! Sign me up!

I don't need a bike small enough to fit into a suitcase - and this guy is a little on the big side when folded - so what.
You can fold the thing up - the wheels lock together with a METAL COMPONENT that is WELDED to the FRAME! So Cool!
That's really good folks - and not common apparently. After that, the bike and be strolled around like a funny looking
little grocery cart, so it's still quite portable when you're on your feet in spite of its weight.

Finally, the folding mechanism on this thing is apparently pretty solid - and the frame is too - just read the "foldingbike20" website reviews.
I highly recommend this site by the way. The frame and that folding mechanism really matter oodles - esp. if you plan to use the bike more regularly. Other models didn't seem to measure up to this test with regular use. Stuff seems to break down. Pins fall out, stuff comes unscrewed - things get loose - right at the heart of the bike. This one apparently though is pretty stout.

So why am I only giving this thing only four stars? Because I will receive it tomorrow finally! Woohoo!
So I will confirm my theories shortly with more info. to follow.
All of you out there who are about to purchase a folding bike - you cannot go wrong with a price point on this thing for 300+ bucks.
With what I've found out so far, I feel confident I've made the right choice.

Review:
- - - FINAL UPDATE - - - -

Eventually the motor in the rear hub failed, no clue what it was exactly, but that's the business end of the entire bike so it couldn't be fixed. It last maybe 2 years. I do live in a very wet and humid climate, and I did ride it in the rain on more than one occasion (inevitable here). Given the money I spent on it, 2 years is a pretty short time. Kind of bummed out, changing rating. You may have better luck in a different climate, don't even know if they sell these anymore.

- - -

******** Note, this review is really for the [[ASIN:B007422TRE XB-310Li Folding Electric Bicycle]], which has nearly identical components except for the folding frame and the battery form factor.

UPDATE: A few more things to think about:

1) The brakes on this bike are of a somewhat baffling make, which means if you ever need to replace the pads you may well run into some problems. Even the manufacturer cannot supply the pads alone - you have to get them together with the calipers for $36 a pop.

2) There is a better version of this same exact bike for the same exact price: EW-475 by Electric Wheels, that has a number of key upgrades, including a 36 volt battery, which is absolutely crucial (it will give you more range, and a lot more power/speed, making the experience a much more confident one). Another good thing about it is the sturdier battery mount, which another reviewer reported problems with (mine broke also, and I had to order a replacement from the manufacturer). A final plus about the upgraded bike are the folding pedals and possibly better seat, a bell, fenders, etc (though you will need to add your own rack for storage - worth it overall, IMHO).

So if you're interested in the XB-310li, I would seriously consider the EW-475 first.

- - - [on to the review] - - -

*** You can get this bike online for about $999, so don't pay too much.)

*** WARNING. If you get this bike, don't use the throttle and pedal assist at the same time! You will probably burn out the controller, like I did once. And keep in mind that the pedal assist is almost always on, unless you turn it off with the key.

I've owned this bike for about a year and a half. I am quite happy with it. For the most part, it's got quality components where it really counts (battery, which is now LiFePO4 chemistry and should last for years, gear shift, motor, etc). The speed and range are both quite suitable, especially given that the battery recharges in about 3 hours or so (6 if you deplete it fully, which isn't recommended by the manufacturer). I have no problem riding it in either pedal assist or throttle mode to work or to a friends house, recharging it there, and riding home. If I forget the charger or something, it will get me there and back without a problem. (Max range is probably about 20 miles, maybe even a bit less if you use the throttle and have a lot of gear).

That's another good thing about it. Gear. Cargo. The rack is quite nice, and I've added two quality paniers (search for Arkel Utility Baskets online), as well as a small tool bag, and a handlebar bag. I mean, I load this thing up! And for the most part it handles it very well. Acceleration can be a little slow, especially uphill, but I usually feel I have enough power.

Note that the bike itself is heavy, which is both good and bad. It's good because it will protect you somewhat in case you get hit by a car, as I did not long ago. I got clipped by a car going something like 30 mph while I was going 18, and the only thing that happened was that I tipped over somewhat suddenly to one side. The weight of the bike absorbed a lot of the energy - otherwise I could've gone flying into the air.

Oh yeah, I might as well reiterate that it's slick! It doesn't even look like an electric bike unless you scrutinize it, which I sort of appreciate. I like to fit in, especially if I have to use sidewalks. Not only will you fit in as a bicyclist and not some strange-contraption-rider, you will probably get complemented.

The downsides:

- You will need to replace the uncomfortable seat and the cheap headlamp. Not a big deal. (Oh yeah, the tools they supply are cheap and sort of useless too.) Best seat I've found is a Sunlite Cloud-9, can't remember what kind exactly. You'll probably want to head to the bike shop so you can actually test it out, otherwise you might end up buying and returning a bunch of saddles online.

- The handlebars are kind of low and far away and not adjustable. I think this is typical for a mountain bike - gives you better control or something on slopes - but it's annoying for a commuter and can cause back and neck strain. I've been meaning to get different handlebars/special grips which can fix this.

*** UPDATE: [[ASIN:B004P8BBUQ Ergon GC3]] grips with "bar ends" solved the whole handlebar business! I highly recommend getting them in a "Rohloff/Nexus" configuration, where the left grip is shorter than the right, so that it fits perfectly with the throttle. (I can't confirm that Amazon actually provides the proper configuration, since I bought them from a different site. According to one review the set on Amazon are both the same length...you might want to get them elsewhere...but get em!)

- This thing is heavy (relatively - its actually light for an electric bike which tend to be even heavier monsters). Good and bad, like I said. Bad because you will not enjoy yourself if the battery fails or runs down and you have to pedal it under your own power. Pretty much impossible, especially with cargo. Also, you will not want to mess with hoisting it up onto a bus rack. Or into a car trunk.

- Not truly waterproof, though I've been lucky. I haven't had any problems personally (survived many severe downpours), but I've read on forums about the motors and/or controllers shorting out because of rain. Having tinkered around with the controller case a bit, I'm not surprised. There are no rubber gaskets or seals around the controller - just loosely fitting plastic.

- - - [final notes] - - -

I have a few complaints, but I'm quite happy given the price and its performance over the past year and a half.

If you get this bike, I very highly recommend you got to BMSBattery (dot) (com) and search for 36V10a "little frog" (that's the type of battery case you have). It will set you back another $285, but you will have a spare interchangeable battery that will have 50% more speed, torque, and range than the stock battery. Its really a must have upgrade.

- - -

Oh yeah, and fill the tires to 65 PSI like it says on the tires, not whatever low ball number it says in the manual (checked this with the manufacturer). Get armadillo puncture proof tires, with "thorn resistant" threads, and a bottle of [[ASIN:B000ENOPPO Slime]] and you should never need to worry about getting flats.

Review: Been on the search for the right bike for a while. Finally found this one: sixthreezero 24"/3-speed. Looked through the reviews, decided to go with this size since I'm almost 5ft and did not want a bike that's too high/tall. It's got a nice Teal-Blue color, comfortable-looking-seat, and lots of reviews at more than 4.5 stars. Review:
We bought two of these bikes for my husband and I to ride around the neighborhood or take while we're camping. The shipment was excellent as they arrived earlier than expected (shipment was from NJ or somewhere in the NE) and the boxes were intact with no damage. Husband assembled both bikes and said, for the most part, the instructions were pretty clear and he was able to figure out the confusing parts.

The bike is sturdy and heavy, around 60 lbs. It's foldable and folds at the frame, but the handle and pedals don't fold and the bike is still bulky and heavy when trying to insert the folded bike into the cargo area of a hatchback car. We ended up having a hitch installed on one of our cars and purchasing a sturdy bike rack (from Amazon) that would carry at least 120 lbs. in order to transport these bikes whenever we wanted to take them to the greenways in our city.

The battery is large and heavy, but there is a bag that carries it at the handlebar so it's easily removable to bring the battery into the house to charge it. There are 2 cords leading out of the battery, one to charge it and another to connect it to the bike. You have to thread the connection cord through the small hole at the bottom of the bag in order to connect it to the bike wiring, and that is a bit of a headache to work it through the hole.

The controller to activate the pedal assist works pretty well while cycling. It activates within 2 seconds of holding down the on/off switch and you can feel it kick in to propel you forward. I usually turn it off when I am going through tight spaces like a small, narrow bridge or lane to stay in control of the bike, but definitely use the pedal assist feature on incline and hills. We have hilly terrain in our subdivision and some streets require me to use the medium setting to get to the top of the hill, and it does it quite easily. The low setting is usually enough for me to get around. I haven't needed to use the high setting because so far the med setting gets me up the steeper streets. There is a throttle on the right side of the handlebar that allows you to use the pedal assist feature continuously without pedaling but I haven't needed to use it often. I definitely love the pedal assist feature to get up those hilly streets!

We had to switch the seats to wider seats because the one that comes with the bike is very narrow and uncomfortable. We also got sturdier and louder bells as the one that came with the bike is lousy and has a puny sound. We added bottle holders easily to the bike frame since there were 2 screws for that purpose already on the frame. I do wish they would have included mirrors to help when we're maneuvering and turning on the street. That's something else we'll have to buy. Also, there is no speedometer but I found an app for my phone to help monitor my speed and distance.

The fenders are kind of flimsy but they work. At my height of 5'3", the height of the bike is okay and I can easily stand on the balls of my feet when the bike is stopped, but I end up hitting and kicking the back fender regularly when getting on the bike, and I have to straighten it up.

We're really happy with these bikes and so far haven't had any major issues (knock on wood.) We especially love the lower cost for this electric bike and that it looks good, works very well, and has a year warranty.

Review:
Can't add ratings as I haven't yet completed assembly and test ridden the bike, and there's a couple reasons why. First of all whoever put the front fork of the bike together on the frame put it on backwards. And the other problem is that the fork itself was welded crooked. The fork dropouts and the brake caliper are angled incorrectly and isn't parallel with the frame.
UPDATE: 2 year, 10 month update!
This bike's frame is pretty much indestructible. Schwinn designed a very durable frame, but is outdated in terms of geometry and it's derailleur hanger; it's not removable/replaceable. The head tube angle could be much slacker, somewhere around 67 degrees, and internal cable routing would be nice, too. Other than those "issues", I have grown to love this bike. I take it every where: commuting, agressive leisurly city riding, and to a few local trails. IF upgraded with the right parts, this bike will be very comfortable, very forgivable, fast even, and easy to learn mountain biking on. Mine has been upgraded with the following parts:
WTB ST-i25 wheelset w/Continental Race King 2.2 tires
Shimano HG-400 9 speed cassette
Shimano Alivio 9 speed rear derailleur
KMC 9 speed SL gold chain
Shimano tourney 3x crankset with Shimano bottom bracket
DNM AOY-36RC rear shock (165 x35mm)
SR SunTour XCR32 120 MM front suspension
Shimano BR-M375 front and rear resin pad brake calipers
9 speed Shimano Altus rear shifter, 3 speed front shifter

Review:
I have been using this ebike to commute 13 miles to work. It works out great! Got many compliments from other coworker. Since I have to put it in the office, I need a foldable ebike which is small enough but powerful enough to get through the distance. The gear ratio to the pedal assist setting in my opinion is balance. The wheel size with the max speed 20 miles per hour works pretty good - not shaky and easy to handle.

Delivery: Ok but not great. I wish I could get a notification ahead of time. Since the package showed what is inside the box. It could be a problem of being stolen. I live in an area where lot of people pass by daily so I was little worry. I instructed to deliver to my backyard so nobody would spot it and steal it. Of course when you use UPS, which I hate the most, the delivery guy just put it right in front of my door. Definitely not following the instruction. Also it was delivered little delayed.

Battery: Pretty good. I wish qualisport could increase the battery size or an option to upgrade the size. I usually able to get through 13 miles without problems. Keep in mind, when is cold weather/ winter/ windy condition, your battery drain much faster. So far luckily I only had couple times that the battery drained out before I got to the destination.

Brake: That is bad. It works but it makes squeaky sound from the beginning. Probably just need some adjustment. But when you spent that much money for a new bike, you expect that shouldn't be a problem.

Folding part: I wish there is some kind of mechanism to hold the bike together when you fold and push it. It always separate. Kind of annoying. So I have to tie it up. However, I love you could sit on the seat even when folding.

Frame/ Chain: The chain keeps hitting the frame on the back wheel. They should put some frame protector like soft cushion on it. Definitely I would suggest you to buy some protector to put on the back frame to avoid the chips. It could get badly.

Motor: Quiet responsive and has enough power to go uphill with pedal assist. I am talking about 60 degrees uphill that I had to do daily.

Tire size: I think they use a good width to add stability for this size of the bike.

Customer service: There isn't any instruction on how to install the bike rack and fenders. I contacted qualisport. Responded fast and very professional. Great people to deal with. Then I had a problem with the battery making those sharp crazy frequency noise that drive people crazy at work. I emailed them and they charged me $15 shipping fee to send me a new one. It seems those batteries are hit and miss. I had a electric scooter. The charger has the same problem but that company just sent me one without any charge since it is still under warranty.

Overall: I am pretty satisfied with the purchase. Hope the bike would lasts long.

Will provide updates when I hit 1k miles mark.

Review:
The Sola bike came well-packaged and was fairly easy to put together. Essentially, you will install the handlebars, front wheel, pedals, and the seat. It took about 45 minutes. There is a link to a YouTube video that shows how to install/assemble each part.

The bike feels sturdy and well-built. Most importantly, the bike rides very smooth. The torque electric system provides pedal assist based on how hard you pedal and it feels very natural and seamless. There are 7 gears and 5 levels of assist, so you can meticulously calibrate the amount of pedal assist you want. This is an awesome feature because you can get a good workout by easily lowering the assist or increasing it when you've run out of steam and need to climb a hill!

Of course, the bike also comes with a throttle mode, which doesn't require any pedaling at all. The battery is easy to remove and charge. You can also charge the battery while it's on the bike. The range for a full charge is around 40 miles, but I've never pushed it to the limit.

I also want to comment on the customer service I have experienced with the Hey Bike Team. It's outstanding. My customer support rep has quickly and thoroughly answered any and every question I had about the bike. Also, my bike didn't come with a quick release rod for the front wheel, and they quickly gave me credit for that part so that I could purchase it on Amazon. They even gave me the part number and sent a link to make it super-easy. Hey Bike supports their customers.

Review:
I bought this bike in both black and white (my boyfriend and I wanted matching bikes) to ride around town. I live in Idaho where there are several inclines so i wanted something with multiple speeds. I'll admit i was wary about ordering an item that had less than 4 stars. (When looking for items on Amazon, I generally go to filters and hit both prime eligible and 4 stars+ to weed out any iffy items.) I

am so glad that I purchased this item! I haven't found any problems with either bike so far. When we received the bikes, they were in slightly varied states of assembly. After a few minutes of putting the bikes together, we realized that, although we knew what we were doing, the bikes would need to be tuned up by someone who REALLY knew what they were doing. BRING YOUR BIKE TO A PROFESSIONAL. They found all sorts of little things that we would not have noticed. Since these bikes are cheaper, they obviously aren't guaranteed great assembly in the warehouse. The only one truly negative thing the bike shop had to say was that the gear shifter was cheaply made so shifting could be a problem but that it wasn't a safely hazard.

Since we have been riding, my boyfriend and I haven't had a single problem! We ride a minimum of a mile each time we ride and both get to our destination safely and in a great mood! People in the previous reviews have complained that the brakes are poor, that they don't coast for very long, etc., but we haven't had a single problem and I'm crediting that to the fact that we had ours professionally worked on. We were a little concerned how hard it might be to ride a bike with such small wheels (smaller wheel base=a lot more peddling) but we wanted the bikes for more exercise and although we have to pedal more than a bike with 26" wheels, we don't find that we are much more tired on these bikes than normal! We love them!

This bike has been a hit to everyone who has never seen a folding bike before. I love folding it up for those who are curious and it is SO SIMPLE to do! it takes me a little longer than a minute but that's because I'm weak handed and very cautious. haha Otherwise neither my boyfriend or I have trouble folding or unfolding the bikes. I did have a problem with my seat not wanting to stay up but with a little tweaking, I no longer have that problem! (I'm 5'6" and weigh 170 lb). When folded up, both our bikes fit in the back seat of his standard sized car no problem!

The bike weighs around 30 to 35 pounds which to some may seem like a lot but both my boyfriend and I carry our bikes up the flight of stairs to our apartment relatively easily. Once again, it's a good workout!

As for the bike seat, yes, your butt will hurt at first. Mine did the first few rides! But right about the time I decided I was going to buy a different seat, i found that my butt didn't hurt nearly as much and now it's actually relatively comfortable. Give it a chance; break in the seat! (Ice your butt if you have to the first few times. haha)

My only complaint are 1: the gear shifter - I am a bit weary about changing gears much because it feels a little sensitive. Mind you, it hasn't given me any problems. 2: The smaller wheel base takes some getting used to, but I expected that. Since the tires are much smaller than traditional adult bikes, the turn radius is much smaller so it's easy to over-turn and throw yourself out of balance. Make sure to practice a bit before going out there and racing someone around the neighborhood.

Do not adjust your screen; this review has been posted twice; one for the white bike and one for the black.

Review:
I have ridden bikes for years, but this bike (Tommaso Monza) is my first road bike. Before settling on this bike, I tried to buy a Schwinn bike, believing that’s a more established brand. After Schwinn seller failed to deliver the product twice, I started looking for alternatives. I was attracted by the good customer rating and high road bike sales rank of Tommaso bikes on Amazon. So I made the purchase. One month later, here are my initial impressions of this bike. 1) The bike represents good value for entry level road bike. I am looking for a lighter bike with good gears, so I can reach better speed than using my current mountain bike or other leisure bike. With a weight of little over 20 pounds this bike is much lighter than my current bikes, the Shimano gear also works well. Other brands (like Schwinn) selling at higher prices with lower quality gears. I immediately got a 15% boost in speed from my first serious ride on this new bike. I think more improvements will come. 2) The manufacturer delivered product on time and in good condition. With all the supply chain issues caused by the pandemic, this is not a given. 3) I was planning to let a bike shop put bike together for me, but after watching an instruction video on the company’s web site I feel it’s easy enough to assemble the bike myself. The bike came in ready for assembling, just need to put the handle bar and front wheel in and tighten the screws with Allen keys (not included with the bike). I did need to watch more YouTube videos to learn how to adjust the brakes, but I feel that’s good learning experience for me to have a little bit more knowledge about bikes in general. 4) One reason I chose this bike is it’s size. At 5’7” I normally fall between size Small and Medium. That leaves the question of which size I should choose open. With Tommaso I happen to solidly fit into size Small according to the company’s sizing chart. I verified the detailed geometric information (like the length of the top tube, seat tube, stand-over height etc.) and compared them with other brands. I felt confidently enough that the bike size will fit me well to make the online purchase without actually trying the bike. So far I feel the new bike size fits me well. Whenever you change the bike you need to adjust your posture a little bit, that’s normal, you will get used to it, and that doesn’t necessarily mean the bike is not the right size for you. I will use this bike’s size as benchmark for other future road bikes. 5)
The bike comes with an owners manual which in my opinion is useless because it’s designed for multiple different types of bikes. There are some miner parts (mostly light reflectors) in the package, that the company did not provide any instructions on how to put them on. I think the company can do a better job in assembling, maintenance, usage instructions to make people’s first encounter with a road bike more smooth experience. For me I don’t even know how to change gears with a road bike —- I have to look on line to find out. But overall I am happy with the purchase. I will find out more about the bike as I put more miles on it.

Review:
The chart is a bit optimistic about height, make sure you look up what size bike will fit your kids instead of relying on the chart here. I got the 18" version without training wheels for my 8 year old daughter because she seemed to fit within the size chart (around 4'5"), but it seems that you need to put the seat all the way up for that size to work. The handlebars don't raise, only the seat, so even though it technically did fit, she's already at the top end which should only be used to buy time until the next bike. Thankfully my 5 year old son actually fits into it on the lowest setting, now I just wish I had gotten a model with training wheels as he is still learning. So I'd say if you're going by the chart, err on a size up. As far as the bike quality goes, it's actually quite good! My kids were able to zip around the block and it encourages more outdoor play and exercise. The kick stand is the sort that is mounted on the wheel instead of the center of the bike, which I personally find odd and not as sturdy as the middle, but I see this is the style on a lot of new bikes so I can't fault this particular one.
Honestly aside from the sizing chart being a little iffy I think this is an excellent bike. It was very easy to assemble as well- just attach the handle bars and screw in the peddles, it comes mostly assembled already!

Review:
Bought the bike last week. I would recommend buying this bike based on its value for money and my limited experience so far.

PROs:
1. Shimano tiagra 2x10 shifters are smooth
2. Aluminum frame with carbon fork is a plus
3. Love the blue color of the bike, not too light, not too dark, not gaudy but just the perfect shine to receive good compliments from fellow bikers
4. Easy to assemble, took me 40 mins from unpacking to assembling the bike.
5. Ready to ride option requires only fitting saddle, handlebar, front wheel and pedals. It's easy to follow the instructions online and assemble the bike in less than 45 mins. Note, I didn't have a bike stand. With bike stand, it shouldn't take more than 25-30 mins.

CONs:
1. The front derailleur and rear derailleur were not properly adjusted, tension in the strings were not correct, so I was not able to shift to big chain ring from the small one. I was also not able to shift the back gear to the largest gear for the same reason. I had to watch "park tool" YouTube video and tried to fix it. But this being my first bike and no prior experience with geared bikes, I wasn't able to do it. Got it done today at a bike shop for $40.

One feedback for the seller is to have the adjustments done properly before shipping. This will give a 5 star experience for users.
This was the reason I gave one less star on my review.

Overall: good buy and if you don't mind spending $100 on professional assembly.

Review:
After several months with this bike, I could scarcely be happier. Sure, I'd love it to be a bit lighter, but for the price, I am THRILLED.

I use it mostly for that last mile commute in NYC- getting the mile from the subway to home or destination. And it's perfect- i dont even need to fold it for the subway, I can carry it through a turnstyle. It's actually easier to carry up or down stairs as a full bike then folded, with the full 35 lbs all in one hand.

I can't speak to mileage, since ive never used it for a long commute, or tried to go till the battery was empty. But I've taken 5 trips of about 1.5 miles at full throttle (Im about 210lbs, plus clothes, backpack etc), hills and all, and it does great and the lowest it's ever gotten to was 2 out of 5 bars.

The pedal assist is intuitive and great. I will say, trying to pedal this without any power at all is not fun- the wheels are small and there's no gears, so it's rough going. Do not plan to get stuck without power. And it takes 3-4 hrs to refill fully from our home outlet, and thats not from empty- usually about 3-4 bars. I dont know how long it would take from empty.

It folds up compactly so if space is an issue, this wont take up much. In a NYC apt, that means alot.

My biggest complaint was the seat- it's narrow, not well padded and without any gap in the middle as most modern seats have to take pressure off your perineal nerve. It started to get pretty painful- so I bought a new seat on Amazon for $20! That's been great. So, if they would just include a good seat, this bike would be perfect.

Ive gotten tons of comments and questions about while riding, it's popular round these parts. I recommend it.

Review: I really wanted to like this bike... when the box first showed up i was a bit concerned with the 30lb shipping weight, this bike is built for 30-55lb kids. giving it the benefit of the doubt i opened it up and found a broken front brake lever (not the end of the world, kids don't need a front brake) the frame was well built and the paint was nice. as i continued to assemble the bike i noticed the neck stem was torqued way too tight from the factory and the bearings weren't smooth. this is a big problem, notchey steering is a safety concern and makes the bike hard to ride/steer. being a fix it type of guy i continued the build with plans to repair the neck later (why should i have to repair a brand new $100 bike?) overlooking the couple factory flaws i continued assembling until i struck my last nerve. I spun the front wheel and those bearings were over tightened, notchy and the wheel was untrue. I tried the rear wheel and the coaster brake clicked like tourettes and the spokes and hoop where also untrue. feeling defeated i cut my losses and sent it back, I spent $80 more on a Haro Shredder... I should have just started with a known quality brand. I don't claim to be a bicycle mechanic but i do work on motorcycles and I would say this bike is more in the "good enough" $50 range, It just didn't make the cut with me.

Review:
I have owned this bike now for over two months and feel I can leave an honest review. I looked at several different brands and models before narrowing down to this particular bike.

I did have to make a few adjustments to make this to my liking. I immediately changed the seat, added a tail light, cup holder, side mirror, and a cell phone holder.

My husband has a 750 watt ebike and it is harder to keep up with him while we are out for rides. The bike does have power but compared to a 750 watt motor it’s night and day. Now, don’t get me wrong this bike still performs well. I live in a mostly flat area (Phoenix) and there are not hills near me.

The mileage isn’t too bad. I always pedal and mainly ride in level 3. I do wish they would add a screen in the future. I think I might be doing that next so I don’t have to use an app to see my speed. The fastest I got the bike to is 18 mph and that’s in level 3 pedaling fast to try and beat my husband. Let me say his bike goes up to 23mph so there’s no beating his bike.

Overall, this is a good bike at a great price. It has held up and I do appreciate the suspension it has. The bumps don’t feel bad. I also think the gears can be confusing for beginners. It did take some time for me to get use to them.

I will also update over time if the quality of the bike has any issues or my biggest concern, the battery. I’m optimistic how long the battery will last and how many charges it lasts for.

I did add a photo of the box it came in, how it looked when I took it out of the box, and how it looks stock when assembled.

Review:
At the outset, let me say I am by no means an expert bicyclist. I can ride a bike just fine, but I don't consider myself to be very knowledgeable about the quality of parts and what is considered to be an entry-level part, and what is considered to be an upgrade. I did not want to spend a huge amount of money since I was just starting bicycling, and after doing a lot of searching and reading reviews, I bought this bike for $345. Although it's a little more than I wanted to spend, compared to what it would cost at your local bike shop, I think this is a very good bargain. I have been riding this bike for about two weeks now, and decided it was time to write a review so that others could use it as a reference to make their decision. Here's a "Plain English" review of this bicycle based on my experience that I hope will be useful to someone else.

I am 5'10" and the 22 inch frame fits me just fine. Shipping from Amazon was great. The bike arrived on the promised date. It looks pretty cool, almost looks like one of the $700 and upwards bike. I am not very knowledgeable about how to use tools, but despite that it was fairly easy to put the bike together. I did take it to my local bike shop to get it tuned up, cost me 65 bucks, but I think it was worth spending that money. Once tuned up, it is a pretty fun bike to ride. It is also very lightweight, and it is extremely easy for me to lift it and take it down the two flights of stairs from my apartment. Once on the road, it seems pretty sturdy, the gears shift pretty well and the bike handles well on the road. In other words, even when cars have whizzed past me at 40 miles an hour, I haven't felt the bicycle wobble. The 16 available gears are plenty in my opinion. After all, I'm not racing 20 other experienced people on the road. I still do not fully understand why some people have said the shifters are cheap, but thus far they have been extremely serviceable to me and have been quite easy to use and shift.

The manual is not very helpful in terms of written explanation on how to assemble things and could be a lot better. I especially struggled with making sure the front brakes were set up properly, they kept touching the rim of the front tire, and I could not get the right adjustment going for quite a while. The cables that shift the gears were not set up correctly, so shifting gears was not initially smooth. The front tire was a little wobbly. My local bike shop got it all straightened out during the tune-up process. (This is why I said earlier that I was happy to spend the $65 to get it tuned up). The tires themselves are pretty flimsy, and once I have gotten used to my bike a little more, I fully intend to get a better set of tires. The current ones are a little too slim for comfort. Having said that, I haven't hit any potholes thus far, so I don't know how they will hold up should something like that happen. A few people in other reviews have complained about the brakes, and I fully concur with them. The brakes are not the best ones out there. If you are one of those who live on top of a hill and have to go downhill at 20-25 miles an hour, I strongly suggest getting the brakes replaced as soon as you get the bike. I mainly ride on flat roads except for going downhill from my apartment complex, and for my routes, the brakes work just fine. The seat is a little hard, but I got used to it pretty quickly. I rode for quite a while without bicycling shorts or padding, and the hard seat did not really bother me much. However, I got my bicycling shorts yesterday and rode my bike wearing them for the first time, and it did make the ride more comfortable. So if you are one of those who goes on long bicycle rides, you may consider getting a better seat.

All in all, my personal opinion is the bike is well worth the money spent including the money spent for tuning it up, and is quite a fun bike to ride at a low cost. I would definitely recommend it for anyone who doesn't want to spend a couple grand on a fancy bike, and wants to just have fun riding it and getting healthier.

Review:
After reading through other reviews about this bike, I decided to take the plunge and get this as my first e-bike. Here are some thoughts I had as I put it all together:

If you can assemble Ikea furniture, you can put this bike together in a couple of hours. Save yourself the assembly money. Just don't miss the separate insert explaining that before you do anything, use the wrenches to rotate the gooseneck 180 degrees. I overlooked this step, and my handlebars looked weird.

The tires, seat, and pedals feel very cheap, but usable for now. These will be the first things I upgrade. Others have commented on the low quality of the shocks, but as an avid cyclist friend of mine said, you won't get quality shocks unless you spend a couple thousand bucks on a bike. These seem to be pretty ok for my needs, though, in my opinion.

I had assumed (mistakenly) that the battery would be charged via USB like everything else nowdays, but sadly, the charger is a huge brick and not very compact.

Headlight doesn't work, but it's very cheap and something similar probably costs about $4 in the store. I'll get something of better quality.

A little bell was included. Really? My kids laughed at me when they saw that.

Everything seems to have fit together very well and easily. I just took it for a quick spin around the block, and other than the cheap parts I mentioned earlier, everything seems pretty solid. I'll update this review after I get some miles in.

Update, one year after purchase: I started to have some issues with the chain slipping, so I took it to my local bike repair shop. He found the issue and tweaked it, but said he notice that the gear cassette appears to be integrated into the motor, so if the cassette needs to be swapped out, the entire motor will need to be replaced as well. It isn't like most bikes where the cassette can be changed out on its own. He said most of the problems he encounters with e-bikes is where the motor puts out more torque than the gears or chain can handle and wears them out faster.

The bike did come very very quick and I was actually surprised of how quick it actually came. It came in about three days so the shipping is something that is definitely a positive. One of the main reasons I ordered this bike to begin with.
Now the assembly. I have never in my life had such difficulty assembling a product let alone a bike. At first I thought maybe I was the problem and I asked 6 of my friends to help me out. Putting the wheel and pedals weren't that difficult. However, the seat and bike handle were NOT EASY nor was it fun to assemble. I followed the instruction manual however, I felt as if that was useless and I had to go on youtube to watch an assembly tutorial.
With the handlebar one thing you had to do was rotate one of the screws and it simply wasn't turning. Not even Luke Cage himself would've been able to rotate this screw. All 6 of us grown men couldn't rotate a screw. I had tp go to a tire shop just for the guy to use a specific tool to rotate it.
I received the bike about a month ago and the seat is still not assembled. At this point I need to bring it to a tire shop because it is impossible to assemble it. The instruction manual just isn't explaining it. The seat is on the bike however it isn't stable and not properly assembled.
I'm a 6 foot 2 180 pound man and this bike is surprisingly difficult to ride. It takes every ounce of strength to ride uphill in New york city and most of the times I need to go uphill I simply get off and walk. On a flat surface it is also surprisingly difficult. It isn't impossible however, it isn't easy to simply ride on a flat surface as it requires a lot more force than usual. This bike is very difficult to ride and that is coming from a basketball and track athlete.
I tried using the bike to deliver food and when I tell you it is super difficult to ride the bike i genuinely mean it.
The bike is not lightweight contrary to what it says in the title. The bike shows a man holding like it's nothing and don't get me wrong its not super heavy either but it is not lightweight either so that's false advertising in their part.
The bike isn't horrible. It is a very beautiful bike and I got many compliments from others. The aesthetics of the bike are beautiful. If you are looking for a strictly beautiful bike than this is for you.
However, if you don't care about aesthetics and care about functionality portability, and convenience than this bike isn't for you.
Admittedly I am disappointed with this purchase

Review:
I bought this tricycle for my wife, a senior citizen, for Christmas. The bike arrived promptly. Unfortunately, one fender was broken. The seller replaced the one broken fender immediately with not one but three replacement fenders. Assembly is no problem for those mechanically inclined and the video on the Amazon website is helpful. For those less mechanically inclined, however, professional assembly is strongly recommended. It takes a bit of time and patience to put the tricycle together, but the effort is well worth it.

One issue is the saddle. The saddle tilts either forward or backward under the weight of a rider (my wife is only 110 pounds). Tightening the nuts further strips them. The ridges on the mating parts under the saddle as well as the bolt that secures the saddle to the stem are made from a very soft steel. The seller, though, once again, responded admirably and within a few days a replacement saddle, this time one with a backrest, which normally is an extra expense, arrived. Unfortunately, this saddle has the same issues. Rather than risking stripping the nuts again, after adjusting the saddle to the right angle, I spot-welded the mating parts, which resolved the issue. It prevents further angle adjustments to the saddle but front back and height adjustment still work. For those without access to a welder, a high-quality adhesive would likely do the trick as well, as would a stronger stainless-steel replacement nut and bolt from Home Depot, Lowes, etc. As stated, not a problem for someone mechanically inclined or even good problem-solving skills.

Overall, a very good bike for the price and a very responsive seller. It is recommended for everybody who wants a solid bike and does not mind one or the other initial set-up problem. Without these start-up issues the tricycle would deserve a 5* rating

Review:
First off, the company provided 1 tracking number, but sent 2 packages. The package with the tracking # was the lock. I panicked, thinking I had been scammed. I messaged them and that evening I got a 2nd tracking number for the bike.
Second, the bike came mostly assembled, which was nice. The instructions are unclear in some areas, but I've put many bikes together, so it wasn't a problem. The biggest problem is that several of the bolts were way too tight. The one that holds the front fender required my husband to get his tools out and loosen it. It took him quite a bit of effort to do it without stripping it. The other ones I couldn't get off were the ones that hold the bracket for the bottom of the basket. I still haven't gotten those off, I just figured out another way to attach it.
If you have never built a bike before, or worked on a bike you will most likely need help. I'm sure there are a ton of tutorials online.
For the actual functioning of the bike, it rides nicely. It's much smoother and more powerful Than lime bikes. The seat is a bit hard, but a gel cushion fixed that.
It is quite powerful. I'm 220 lb and I was able to take hills fairly easily in the lower gears.
My first ride out, the lens for the headlight fell off. The light functions well without it, so I left it off.
For the price, I think the bike is a fair value. I would have given 4 stars if it were easier to assemble.

Review:
I have just gotten into mountain biking and biking around town. So far, I am loving this bike. For the price, it has everything someone would need for getting started in the sport. Here are a few items of note from my experience so far:

1) The main reason I got this model is that the frame was similar to higher end models. That way, if I continue to keep interest in mountain biking I can just upgrade components over time while keeping the core frame.

2) An annoyance I have had so far is that the little clips that hold the brake cables to the frame don't seem to be very sturdy. Lost 3 on one side while driving with the bike on a rack. I have since replaced the clips with small zip ties.

3) I did have my back tire go flat from just jumping up on a curb at a park. I thought that was weird considering I had used it on rougher trails with no issues. I chalk this up to a fluke, but just making note. Of course, the tires are one of those items that later on I may look to upgrade.

4) The shifting controls are nice, but the gears themselves sometimes feel a little clunky. Again, at this price, I don't expect it to be amazing and it is something I may upgrade later on down the line.

5) I did replace the handle grips with new, more ergonomic ones. Some people have mentioned the ones that come with it are not that great. I would have to agree.

But overall, I love this bike. Love the still and the feel of riding it.

Review:
I recently purchased this bike thru Amazon as I was unable to find this model locally. I have had Diamondbacks in the past and have been quite satisfied with them. This order arrived quite quickly, even with the free shipping option, and though some assembly was required upon arrival, not surprisingly, I was able to put most of it together without a problem, taking it to a local bike shop for final checkout. I have been riding it for a couple of months now and have found it an very satisfying purchase. In comparing it to a Specialized Vienna, which I had also recently acquired and have found to be a very good bike, I find myself using the Diamondback most of the time, commuting to work everyday, and occasional longer rides out of town. I have the largest size and, with a handlebar post extension, at 6'6" find it a very comfortable fit, for a change. The gears shift quite smoothly for the most part, and the brakes have a decently tight feel to them. It is nicely light to carry when necessary but still has a well-enough constructed feel to it while riding. Overall, I am quite satisfied with this bike and can comfortably recommend it to anyone looking for a low-to-medium priced hybrid bicycle.

May 27, 2010
As a follow-up to my initial review, though overall I am still quite satisfied with the bike, I have found that I need to replace the plastic/composite pedals already after just 6 months or so. I don't feel like I've been overly hard on them but I've already had one partly break off on me. I have replaced them with metal pedals so hopefully I won't have this problem again for some time. I would still recommend the bike though and will probably look for something similar in the future, when the day comes.

Review:
PROS (in my subjective opinion)
• Easy to assemble
• Arrived in excellent condition
• Packaged well.
• Quality parts
• Classic/vintage/throwback-to-the-1980's design
• Smooth ride
• Fast
• Comfortable
• Fun
• Very sturdy
• Stylish and minimalistic look that stands out from other 26" big BMX bikes
• Painted-on graphics, logos, and script instead or decals/stickers
• Nice welds/welding work
• Tektro brakes (they work well)
• Quick release for seat post
• 3-piece Flight Pedals
• Bear claw pedals (stylish & durable)
• Seat/saddle is solid/decent and is much better (style & comfort-wise) than the seat/saddle that comes with the Redline PL-26, but I still put a gel cover on it anyway (pictured)
• I like the "things" that hold the break cable & cable housing in place beneath the top tube. It looks good and keeps the cable out the way.
• "V"-shaped handle bars are a nice touch although I am contemplating changing them to put my own touch on the bike.

CONS (in my subjective opinion)
• Grips aren't the most comfortable; however, they are attractive (not pictured, but I swapped mine out.).
• Chain tensioners work well but they aren't most attractive or best on the market (I swapped mine out)
• Bear claw pedals (potentially can scrape your shins and calves)
• Would have been a nice touch if Redline Bicycles included a padset and custom schrader valve stem caps.

VERDICT (in my subjective view)
• An excellent bike and definitely worth taking a look at (with the intention to purchase) if you're in the market for a 26" BMX bike. I am having fun cruising and "BMXing" with it.

Review:
Disappointment during assembly continued through riding. A tri-key for a hex socket comes with the bike but it doesn’t fit everything. Still need specialized keys for a couple bolts. Then the discs on the rims aren’t aligned with the brake pads. Even the rear tire that was factory installed wasn’t aligned right and needed adjusting.
Adjusting this review because I’ve used it a little more and just getting more and more disappointed. The bike likes to shift on its own. Riding on flat ground, I get about 10-15 cycles on the pedals and then it shifts. Usually it’s just one gear up or down, sometimes it jumps 3. Similarly, if I put any additional amount of force on the pedals (for a very small hill), then the bike shifts also. This type of shift is extremely frustrating and dangerous. It always drops to the lowest gear it can. So putting some additional force on the pedals, expecting that resistance from the chain, then the bike does that drastic jump in gears. Luckily I’ve caught myself each time before having some major road rash or an accident. 100% would not recommend this bike.

Review:
It was time to ditch the beloved 3 in one push handle tricycle & upgrade my girl to a big girl bike for her 4th birthday:

I went to Walmart & purchased a bike. And within a week we were replacing tire tubes, chains, poor quality training wheels, and crying lots of mom guilt tears before I ran to my Amazon app begging for forgiveness for choosing Walmart .

I scrolled through hundreds of bikes, thousands of reviews, & researched enough to feel confident that royal baby was going to be great.

It arrived two days later in a well packaged box & I was so pleased that it was already 95% assembled. I’m a single mom who was raised in a home where my dad did all assembly and I never learned. So its just me and YouTube doing the job & Y’all I’m not even gonna lie I can’t assemble shit without loosing hours of my life, kids loosing patience and me loosing all of my sanity. By the time we’re done it’s just not even fun anymore. Royal baby’s bike gave me a frustration free experience & within ten minutes I had the bike together, all of my sanity & a positive mood to watch my baby ride!

I had zero complaints about the bicycle. The quality was visible, the bike is solid and it’s heavy weight didn’t restrict her being able to peddle like other reviews stated.

6 months later the bike experienced a minor issue with the handle on the seat & I emailed Royal baby requesting the replacement parts. In under thirty minutes and one single email my requests were more then resolved. They responded so apologetically, without hassle or questioning and zero hesitation they offered to replace the bike with a new bike of the same model or to choose a different model of my choice from their website free of charge. It arrived two days later and my girl happily is riding every day.

I am so pleased with this whole experience that I tell the moms in Walmart bike isle to just go to Royal baby and save the hassle. If you’re on the fence just give it a chance you’ll be so pleased!

My only complaint about this bike is the kick-stand. It is mounted at the rear tire and is a bit tricky to operate properly. If not done correctly, it doesn't come fully forward, and the bike then has a tendency to tip. Review:
First of all, I am not a pro biker and I am 54 years old. I bought a 26" bike 3 years ago for myself and 2 x 20" bikes for my kids. During the corona pandemic, I decided we should cycle more in the neighborhood. There are lots of hills in our neighborhood and I sweat a lot during climbing. I got e scooters for my kids.
I bought the entry model, 350 Watt motor, 36 V 7.6 A battery, 26" Ancheer and I love it. I can cycle for 2 hours without sweat. It has 5 support levels, I usually use level 2 or 3. It has disc brakes, 3 x 7 Shimano gears, not a fancy headlight, water bottle like battery and enough power for fun.
Bike comes 80% assembled, I was able to finish it in an hour. Rear shift protector came bended, I had to correct it before adjusting the gear. Front fork was reverse, I had to switch it ( brake disc should be same side as rear).
I believe this is a great beginner level e-bike at a good price.
