Review:
I'm an inexperienced rider; 45 years old, 5'8", about 160-165 pounds. I say "inexperienced" although I used to ride all the time when I was a kid - rode proper road bikes in my teens and early 20s and all but gave it up since. I wanted to get back into riding after a doctor suggested regular exercise; I never liked running and my tolerance for treadmills, etc. is limited, but I remembered that I enjoyed riding when I was younger so I thought I'd get back into it. I'd had a Schwinn Caliente (24") and then I rode my brother's Schwinn LeTour II (or III, not sure) for a few years.
I tried a couple of Atlanta-area bike stores and was horrified to learn that the best I could do for a new bike was about $[...] for a last-year's model. Stores like Sports Authority had no road bikes at all. I was willing to pay in the $[...] range, but there simply wasn't anything.

I was worried about what I would get for the price, but I told myself that really, all I was looking for was a more or less direct replacement for the LeTour; I'm not trying to be Lance.

By way of clarification, this appears to be a Chinese-made Kent bicycle, re-badged as a "GMC Denali." I don't think that this branding is counterfeit; I think this bicycle was produced as part of a GM SUV promotion (i.e., buy an SUV; get a bike free) and they had a LOT of bikes left over. As some have noted, it is a weird agglomeration of parts with its twist shifters and mountain-bike gearing. It's attractive; the photo does not do the electric deep blue pearl finish justice. In sunlight, it's amazing.

So far I've ridden it up to thirteen miles in traffic on moderately hilly roads and up to sixteen miles on a flat coastal road with a proper bike lane. I'm not very in-shape so sometimes I have to drop it into 1/1 and creep to go uphill.

Shifters. Once the limits were set, the chain hasn't been dropping off the edges. To take the rear gear reliably from, say, 3 to 4, you might have to twist the thing 3-5-4 to really get it set properly. I have not had any tooth jumping. On flat road, I'm finding myself going between 3/6 and 3/7; I don't know if this means I need a taller sprocket somewhere or not.

Several people talked about punctured tubes almost as soon as they started riding the bike. The first time that happened to me, I think I pinched it coming into the driveway with the pressure at about 90PSI (it needs to be 100 the max at my weight and when I set off I take it to 105, assuming that it will be down to 100 at the end of my ride). However, I lost a tube again for no good reason and I investigated. It turns out that there was little or no cleanup after the spoke nut holes were drilled out and several of the holes had sharp crescent-shaped metal shards attached to them. I went around both wheels with a rattail file and got rid of all that and I replaced the thin rubber liners with heavier-duty ones. The wheels on my bike were reasonably straight and true.

I started wondering about what I perceived to be excessive rolling drag so I pulled off the wheels to turn the shafts by hand and they felt pretty crunchy and stiff on both ends; I stayed off the bike subsequently and had them rebuilt at a bike shop but I'm not sure how much better they are - the drag seems better at least. I would suggest having these bearings checked and repacked not long after you get the bike. They are of an old-school sort and I learned that not all bike shops can even really work on them.
The tires have held up pretty well for having been pinched and having been removed and put on a few times; I have a pair of Michelin replacements but I expect I'll keep the OEMs on until they pop or wear out. They are 700x28c and I have yet to see tires that size in a bike shop or anywhere else; you'd better mail-order one or two and keep them as a spare because you may not be able to find one local to you.

I replaced the seat with a Bontrager that has the center groove and opening.

The brakes could be a lot better; the caliper arms bend appreciably during use. When I had the wheel bearings overhauled, I tried to have the shop replace the calipers outright but they did not have any that would fit. I'm finding the angle of the brake levers awkward. This is one area where you might consider making some changes as soon as you get the bike. You might consider taping up the rubber brake lever housing boots as soon as you get the bike because I've already torn up one and there's no point in fixing them. You don't strictly need them as they're really only there for a little padding; I wear padded Bell gloves anyway, so I'm fine in that regard.

It made me nervous going really fast downhill initially; it's harder than I remember it being back in the day to hold the bike in line going around a turn at speed; I feel like it's a geometry and weight-distribution problem and not necessarily a flaw of the bicycle, but it will be interesting to see if it's any different with new tires. With the tires pumped up to 100PSI going really fast can make your eyeballs vibrate too much to see clearly if the road is rough (raising butt up off the seat even just a little sorts that right out).

So to sum up, if you buy this bike and really want to ride it 1) replace the seat, although the original isn't horrible 2) make sure everything's adjusted 3) put some time, money, and attention into the brakes 4) clean up the wheel machining and upgrade the liners *immediately* 5) have at least one spare tube and tire on hand.

Review: I bought this bike based on its description and it's reviews. In general I feel it was a good value for the money. The finish on the bike looks nice. It is thick and glossy and not overly prone to chipping.  On the road the bike is reasonably quiet and stable. It does not rattle or squeak. The suspension was very firm at first but now has smooth out nicely.  Only the rear suspension is adjustable. The gears are shifting smoothly so far. The brakes feel soft and compared to other bikes I have owned.  However they seem to stop the biket in a reasonable distance.  This bike is targeted for shorter users, I would say not more than about 5' 10".  I am 5' 7" and still had to replace the seat post to get a little more height.  Also the original seat post was slightly too small and was very hard to tighten. I ended up wrapping foil tape around the seat post to eliminate this problem prior to replacing the seat post entirely.  The replacement seat post also had an offset which gave me a little more rearward adjustment for the seat.  I changed out the tires to my favorite brand which is continental town and country.  The tires provided were not bad, but I was not comfortable with the way they rode and they had a bad smell. I also replaced the so-called "quick release" handlebar bolt with a standard one.  I had numerous problems with the quick release handle mechanism provided, it kept coming loose and each time I tightened it it seemed to work and weaken the bolt.  I had the bike shop tweak the derailers and dial out a slight warp in the front and rear wheels.  If you are not a bike tech plan on spending $20-$50  at the bike shop to get this bike right.  With that said and done this bike still  represents a value in the current folding bike market.  It is more and more becoming my go to bike because of the ease of folding and it's good road manners, and in my opinion the bike looks pretty cool.  I would recommend this bike for shorter riders looking for a decent folding bike at a good value.

Review: I feel the sixthreezero Around The Block Men's 7-Speed meets all my expectations. After being at home working for over a year, not wanting to return too quickly to the gym, and instead choosing to walk the neighborhood for exercise, I was done! My wife suggested I purchase a bike. I have had all sorts of bikes over the years - racers, mountain bikes, low cost/low quality bikes from box-stores, and expensive high maintenance bikes from specialty bike stores. I just needed a good quality reliable bike where I wouldn't be hunched over the handle bars, that would be easy to ride, had enough gears to provide a reasonable workout, and could be versatile. The sixthreezero Around The Block Men's 7-Speed has actually made me feel liberated. I'm riding it regularly around the neighborhood, and on weekends along local bikeways. I even took it for a spin on the beach on the hard sand. 4 stars for easy to assemble - the guide is for a bike that doesn't come semi-preassembled, which mine did. I started with the guide to be "safe", but quickly resorted to common sense assembly. Three stars for comfort - the seat was not easy on my derriere, until a placed a removable cushioned seat cover over it - a cheap and easy fix. 5 stars for packaging. Amazon needs to take a page out of sixthreezero's packaging handbook.

Review:
This is the bike, I know I will have forever! It checked every box for me: a hybrid, excellent quality, stylish and comfortable (the seat is so amazing). I wanted a bike that I could add a basket to for running errands, one I could take to the beach on a weekend away but also hit trails with long bumpy roads and hills for a good workout. It rides so smooth. Maybe I was roughing it before because I forgot how smooth a hilly ride could be with the right bike! (21-speed adjustment and a shock absorbing seat, a luxury I didn't know I needed).

Here's a tip though, you'll need to get the tires pumped and if you've never put a bike together be prepared to possibly get the brakes and tuning done professionally. During assembly I couldn't figure out how to finish the brakes and tune the speeding. The assembly booklet was super helpful. It's in color with detailed pictures and descriptions for assembly from start to finish and it was my very first time ever putting a bike together so I'm so happy the assembly book was such great quality but I just didn't want to screw any of the important stuff up. I got all the way to the end of assembling the bike and then called around for a good quote from a local bike shop to finish up the tuning and brakes and add air to the tires, (it was only $35). I would definitely recommend doing the same unless you've done this before. Overall, I love my bike!!

Review:
Love riding the bike. The amount of effort needed for the final result, however, was full of pain and emotional anguish. I broke the chain on my first ride because there is a plastic rim placed in between the wheel and the derail-er gear changing mechanism. I have no idea why there is such a plastic piece, there are no warnings anywhere about said plastic piece, and cutting it off was ridiculously difficult. I had to order another Hiland bike, and with the knowledge and experience I had from assembling the first bike, still took me many grueling hours of adjusting the brake pads, handlebars, and seat to work acceptably. The seat is also very small and will most likely make your butt sore for a few days after if you plan on sitting on it during your ride. Now I still have to deal with returning the first one that got completely destroyed during my first ride.

All that aside, it is very fun to ride and is one of the few things that allows me to enjoy this sub - par excuse of a life I live.

If you are not a complete loner like I am, and can actually get someone to help you, assembling the bike can have the potential of being a fun and fulfilling experience; and riding it has boosted my confidence greatly with the feeling of freedom I have achieve through all different types of environments.

As a single mother with a very limited budget, this was a big splurge for me so that I could ride bikes with my son. Review:
Purchased this directly from Mongoose fall 2015, thus not an Amazon Verified purchase (at the time, Mongoose was running 50% off on the entire Tyax line).

Background:

This Tyax Sport is a decent quality bike, but far inferior to the identical model sold in Europe. I'm a pretty avid biker, re-started into the hobby a few years ago (was a big BMX'er back in the day) and started with cheap bikes. I also have a home in Ukraine, and while there I ordered two Mongoose Tyax Sport bikes (2014 model year) from a seller in Ukraine. These bikes are the Russian version (for Russian market).

These Tyax Sports were FANTASTIC! Got 2, and both my wife and I love the way they ride. All Shimano geartrain, great hydraulic brakes, great grips, great seat, absolutely loved them.

Then, last year, came back to US and wanted to have the same thing here. Ordered the Tyax Sport (2016 US model) from Mongoose. It arrived, and I was GREATLY disappointed. Bike did not ride at all like my European versions. Has very cheap-feeling mechanical disc brakes, drivetrain feels a little sloppy, not tight, just in general it feels like a MUCH cheaper and worse quality bike - amazing, as it's supposed to be the same model.

Fast forward to now. Just came back from Ukraine again, there I purchased a new Tyax Pro (not sold here currently, top of the line for Tyax) there and had it shipped back to US. Was going to get a Tyax Expert for my wife, but came across a deal for a 2016 Tyax Comp, for a great price, on Amazon. Compared specs with the same model in Europe, and amazingly almost all the components (drivetrain, brakes, fork, crank, etc.) are different - inferior - and CHEAPER - on the US version. Still, at the price, thought I would give it a shot, and maybe just have to change the brakes with some Shimano hydraulic units.

Got the new Tyax Comp here and it's OK. Really, just OK. Drivetrain feels quite good, but still not as good as our (lower model) EU versions.

So, back to the review for this particular bike. For $200 it's OK - just OK - but shame on Mongoose for selling such a vastly inferior product for the US market compared to its identical model European counterpart. The Tyax Sport bikes we got in Ukraine are FANTASTIC, absolutely great, this one is kind of crappy. Maybe better than a $99 Huffy at wal-mart, but that's about it. My Russian Tyax Pro arrives next week, and I can't WAIT to try it out!!!

PS - don't take my word for it, for example you can compare specs for the Tyax Comp where we purchased it in Europe. Compare all components with the US Tyax Comp and the differences will become immediately obvious.

Review: I would think twice before buying this bike again.  I was attracted to the bike from the description because I am an older rider and like the idea of the ease of getting on and off the bike. When I called to inquire if the bike was fully assembled, I was told it was 85% assembled and that the few remaining pieces would be easily put on.  The manual was a generic manual and really gave no helpful information specific for this bike.  Also, you need to have a variety of metric tools including a 14 mm deep socket, 10 mm short socket, 6 mm Allen wrench, 5 mm Allen wrench, crescent wrench, 5/32 Allen wrench and 8, 10, and 15 mm open/box wrench (only the 5 and 6 mm Allen wrenches came with the bike).  With great difficulty my husband and I put the bike together over a 3-4 hour period. Once finished I went for a ride only to find that the chain and gear shift mechanism weren't assembled properly at the factory, so I couldn't even ride it.  $80 later, after taking the bike to our local bike store for a complete overhaul, I was able to go for my first ride.  The seat that comes with this bike is not for those of us with larger girths.  I felt like I was going through childbirth again, so I ordered another seat more befitting my derriere. The base price of the bike is good, however, after adding an additional $110 for the seat and overhaul, I no longer think this bike is such a good deal. I wish I had bought a bike from my local bike store where I could have gotten more personalized service and a guarantee on the bike parts.

Review:
This bike does what it says on the description, with a few plus and a few cons. First it was easy to assemble; I have extensive bike knowledge, so from the box to fully assembly took me 20 mins. I removed the battery for inspection and charged it according to the instructions. The battery charged fully in 3.5 hours vs. the stated 4.5 hours (I don't know if it was partially charged at the factory). I inflated the tires to 65psi as recommended. I rode the bike without the battery to have a feel of what kind of speed and resistance I'd experience with the single gear; the bike will get you to where you need to be without a battery at a decent 10-11mph on a flat, paved terrain. Depending on an uphill grade, 8-10 mph if you are a decent rider. Be mindful that this bike has 14" wheels and weights over 40lbs...it is not going to be fast unless you are going downhill.
First ride with battery: Sturdy, responsive, but bumpy feel. 3/4 of a mile into my test ride I hit the first hill and turned on the module. Pedal assist kicked in immediately and I could feed the bike picking up speed, probably 12-13mph (I climb this area at 10-12mph on my 700c Gary Fisher Commuter). In seconds I caught up with a rodie commuter and had to hit the breaks because I couldn't pass safely. When I was ready to pass I hit the throttle and passed him effortlessly. He complained that a tiny-wheel bike must have an electric motor to pass a road bike on a hill...I gave him the thumbs up and he yelled "That's Rad, bro!"
At work I tested the flat parking lot with and without the electric assist. With assist/throttle: 14-14.5mph. Normal bike: 12-14mph before it starts spinning out. I weight 175lbs at 5'6'...I like to have my correct full extension of the knee at the bottom of the pedal stroke, and this was accomplished at the maximum safe mark on the sit tube. I would estimate that this bike will fit a person (close to properly) no taller than 5'10". I would say this bike will be ok to commute for up to 6 miles one-way; after that it will be uncomfortable.
Improvements:
1. the single speed is a setback; it needs 5-7 gears to complete the package...this will force the manufacturer to increase wheel size (16-20")
2. suspension on the fork and/or the seat tube for regular street commuting.
3. at least 16" wheels if you are not going to add gears.

Review:
I did not have any experience assembling bikes, but putting this together took me about 30 mins. The brakes need to be adjusted and there was a little wobble on the front tire. I just looked up on youtube on how to fix those issues.

First time riding it, I thought the handlebar/stem part was wobbly. It turns out i needed to tighten the bolts even more. The frame seem to be very sturdy. The only issue I have is with the derraileur: it is off by +1 gear (ie. 1 is 2, 2 is 3, etc). It is no big deal to me because it still gives me access to the highest gear which is what i need (gear 7, but my shifter will show I'm on gear 6). The only drawback is i won't have access to gear 1, and I won't be using this on hills so I don't need that.

This is ideal for short commutes (~7 miles). I changed my handlebar to bullhorns so i can have different hand positions and still be able to fold the bike.

Overall, this is a good quality bike for the price.

Review:
I have owned the bike for 6 months now and have been commuting daily with couple of miles ride each way and take it on the train. When train is full I fold it down and creates enough space for me and my folded bike. I do have to cross a bridge over highway, and can do so at speed 4 with some effort. Love the bell, the stand, and the folding pedals. With just a few additions to it - [[ASIN:B000RYAKHC Planet Bike Blinky "3" 3-Led Rear Bicycle Light]], seat cushion, and [[ASIN:B0009R96YK Mirrycle MTB Bar End Mountain Bicycle Mirror]], it is a complete bike. Also bought [[ASIN:B0017RAMCQ Schwinn Intercept Adult Micro Bicycle Helmet (Blue,Adult)]] - don't ride without it!

The bike had arrived mostly assembled, the front tire required to be installed, which took me about 1 hr as I am new to bikes and installed it incorrectly. The 20'' tires does mean more effort than 26'' bike and the max seat height is not for anyone taller than 5'7''. Review: For the money, this is a decent bike:  Steel frame fixed gear with 700C wheels.  I was not expecting much, but it seemed to hit what I wanted in a fixed gear urban commuter.  It has the attachment points for carriers and fenders.  The wheels and tires are 700x25 with presta valves and rated for 90-110 psi. The wheels are aero-profile needing longer stems (60mm).  I was looking for cro-moly steel (4130) frame, but this is 1020 steel frame and less money. The weight is light enough. The rim brakes are decent.  The brake pads are grippy but could be better for adjustment. I did need to adjust the pads to grip the rim in the right place. Tools and reflectors are included. The instruction book is universal, and not specific to this bike. The only thing that needs to be assembled here is the front wheel, handlebar, seat post, and pedals.  Check the fit and tighten other bolts. The pedals are good enough and clearly labeled for L or R. Put some grease on the pedal threads when you put them on.  I added a bell and a water bottle cage.  The attachment points and hardware are good.  Seat is comfy.  The 47 cm seems to be a good fit for me as I like the smaller frame for stand over height and casual riding. I am 5' 9".  I like it.

My wife bought this as an alternative to a pedal bike. I’ve ridden it more than she has! Im 5’8” and 73 years old. Its fun to putt around our community. I prefer riding on the sidewalks as riding the roads is a little uncomfortable with the “old” drivers we have.

In motor only mode it moves along quite well-I’ve only taken it up to 14mph as I feel uncomfortable with it above that speed.

My only complaint is I find the seat becomes quite uncomfortable for me after about four miles of riding.

Review:
This is a sharp looking bike however the weight is radically off. It weighs at least 30-35 pounds and is listed at 25. Since this was one of my reasons for buying this bike I am very disappointed. I owned a transit searcher previously and it was listed at 25 pounds - and this was accurate. However this is what I found on Schwinn's website:

16: Sorry, but our product development department doesn't publish an official list of bike weights. Other companies often release misleadingly low weights, which would make our true weights look falsely heavy by comparison. We encourage you, though, to have your dealer weigh any of our bikes on-site. If, on the other hand, you already have a bike and would like to know how much it weighs, you can easily get that information by weighing yourself holding the bike on a bathroom scale and subtracting your weight without the bike. .(BACK TO TOP)

What a crock! So, it's not Amazon's fault...Seems like the policy by Schwinn is really to keep everyone guessing and to hope that their weights are posted inaccurately. This really is nonsense as you can go to most shops and find other bikes and pick them up. (like Trek) But it is relatively hard to find the model of Schwinn that you want and like in stores. So, they push buying in stores even though the products are pretty limited. Why not just be honest and list the true weights??? I may end up selling this bike. Carrying 35 pounds up my condo stairs on a regular basis after a long bike ride does not sound appealing. Will definitely have to change the tires out for hybrids now if I do keep it.
Bummed in Texas

Review:
I’ve had this bike for about 6 months now. For my needs it’s perfect and has held up well. I’ve been around town with it, on longer trips on the road, in the mountains on pretty rugged trails, and for a 15 mile beach ride (on the wet sand). It has held up well and the electric power, with 3 settings, has enabled me to go places I normally wouldn’t go with a regular mountain bike. The electric range is excellent and I really like the fact that you can recharge the battery a bit going downhill or pedaling faster than 14 mph in low or no power mode. This recharge system is unique and you can learn how to take advantage of the downhills on your trip to extend the power even longer. Although it’s on the heavy side (50 lbs.) you can still conserve your battery and ride it with no power on a flat road or put it in the first power level and get a small extra boost that makes it ride like a lighter bike. At the highest power setting I can easily get it up to 24 mph without pedaling very hard. It also has a throttle for no pedaling power so if you’re tired you can cruise without pedaling and enjoy the scenery.

For the price you get a lot including hydraulic disc brakes, shimano gears, tubeless tires, 3 power modes . Don’t expect it to be a high end mountain bike (which could easily cost 2 to 3 times as much). The components are descent (the quality of the hydraulic brakes is just ok, they work fine but mine make a bit of a rubbing sound). If you don’t want to spend a lot, aren’t looking for a high end electric mountain bike, and want versatility with a long power range than I would recommend this bike.

Review: I just received my Giordano Trieste today and assembled it but haven't ridden it yet.  Before buying I compared this to other steel gravel bikes with Shimano Claris components.  I had seen a Mercier Kilo on Bikes Direct website for $650 and many others at REI for $800+.  Would like more gears, but at least this Claris group has the brake cables routed under the bar tape rather than the old sticking out style.  Crankset looks decent, has a hollow spindle.  Wheels are quando, heavyish but again, the price was right!  Wheel spacing is 135 rear, 100 front, both QR, so there are many options out there if you want to shed some spin weight on a future upgrade.  My medium (listed as 54cm) weighs about 29 lbs... sure you can get in the low 20's or high teens on a good steel bike but you would need to add a couple grand to the price tag.  Disc brakes are cheaper Chinese ones, but you can always buy better pads when these wear out.  Add your preferred pedals, maybe a better seat and you will have plenty of enjoyable miles!  I anticipate the steel frame and fork to provide a pretty sturdy, plush ride compared to aluminum/carbon.  Only things I would mention is the dropouts feel cheaper (stamped whereas most bikes have machined parts).  Tires are more geared to road than gravel, but those can be changed and the frame/fork are wide enough to accommodate most 700c tires, possibly even some narrow 29ers. All in all I believe (and I have been watching every major online bike retailer for months) this is the BEST DEAL out there in terms of a sport/entry level steel gravel bike.  If you can handle a bit of heft in exchange for a screaming deal BUY THIS BIKE!

Review:
I'm going to try and keep this simple? I'm 6'6" (198cm) and just bought this bike to replace and older bike I had to put out to pasture. I love the 29" tire size and I wasn't sure but this bike fits me just fine. I'm only a casual rider, I might do 15 to 25 miles a week but this bike is great for where and how I ride.

I did add a couple of things, WTB Slick Comp 29" City Tire because I don't really ride trails. I also added a GIYO Mini Bike Pump and replaced the stock reflectors with BV LED Bicycle Light Set. I tried out the pump in the location (pictured) with some zip ties first but it worked so I ended up mounting it there. The one thing I am missing are some fenders (mud guards) and you will need those for these large wheels, especially if you ride trails.

The bike came virtually assembled. All I had to do was spin the goose neck around, mount the handlebars install the front wheel and give everything a once over. Please check all of the nuts, bolts, screws... Oh I also got a locking skewer set. I don't really appreciate the quick release skewers but as I said I'm just a casual rider. My concern is more how fast a thief could get my wheels off not how fast I can get them off.

Love the bike for what it is. Don't expect to enter any competitions riding this bike. Don't expect to set land speed records. It isn't the lightest bike I've had but for the price I feel like it is a good value.

Review:
I bought the 18" version for my 7 year old son who is around 46" tall. It fits him well with what looks like plenty of room to grow. Like other reviews have said, this is a high quality bike for the money. It looks great, and I think the weight is very reasonable. I picked this model (with the aluminum frame) to save a couple of pounds. The water bottle, cage, and bell are nice extras and the front caliper brake is a nice introduction to hand brakes for him. It was easy enough to put together. And my son loves it, so overall it's a win.

However, just like other reviewers have noted, there are some problems to be aware of:

1. I needed my own wrenches to really crank down on the nuts that hold the pedals onto the crank arms. Couldn't get enough leverage with the supplied tool to keep the right pedal from falling off.

2. The front brake was not adjusted correctly from the factory. It was rubbing on the rim and making the bike harder to ride. I needed to adjust it to keep it from constantly rubbing on the rim. It's now easy for him to reach and modulate.

3. The front wheel is not true. But this isn't a really big deal (I think).

4. The chain was way too tight from the factory. This was making the bike much harder to ride than it should it have been. After adjusting it, the bike was much easier to ride (and quieter). The chain will probably last longer now also.

5. When brand new, the crank is tight. Once again, making it harder to ride. After about 10 miles or so, it loosened up and became easier pedal.

6. The pedals don't really spin as easily as they should. This probably makes the bike a little harder to ride than it should be. I don't think I can do anything about this however.

After making the adjustments above, the bike became a lot easier for my son to ride. Before I adjusted the chain and the front brake, he was tired after 3-4 miles of relatively easy biking - not much fun for him. Now he has no trouble with 10 or more miles.

Review:
I have been a life long bicycle rider - with a brief hiatus for about 10 years. I'm 47 now. My wife and I had nice road bikes that we stopped using when we moved to a place with lots of hills.
We moved again... and they got stoled out of or yard. Boooo!

I've been on the prowl for a replacement bike for a while when I was turned onto the One-Wheel. It looks nice but with a price tag of $2,000+ - no way. I was intrigued by it's electric power when I had the bright idea of getting an electric bike. I had seen a couple go by in my neighborhood and they looked fun, and fast.

Now with gas prices going up as much as they are my big V8 truck was costing me a lot of money to just run errands around town. My hardware store is only 2 miles. Honestly, I can get to Safeway and back faster than in my truck because I don't have to park and go in (I pull my bike inside) and as soon as I leave the door, my fancy electric bike will whisk me away, no walking back to my parked vehicle, no waiting for traffic.

Shipping: It shipped very fast and I had my bike in only a few day.

Assembly: I know bicycles, so assembly was very quick as nearly everything is preassembled - it's just taken apart for shipping. Handlebars on, front wheel on, attach pedals, connect connections, tighten all allen heads for safety and your ready to go - as soon as you charge the battery. Very simple.
ALL the tools you will need for assembly are included. As well as some you won't need unless you are servicing the bike and need metric tools.

Instructions: Poorly worded instructions translated into English. Pics are so-so. But if you know anything about bicycles, it's a breeze.

Size: The bike is a real nice size. I am 5' 11" and it fits me well. My wife is shorter, 5' 6", and she can stand over the top bar no problem.
I like how the handlebars are adjustable up for someone who wants to sit taller.
I also like how the brakes have pre-threaded holes for a mirror or accessory that can thread into the brakes.
I like how the brakes are - Left hand, front. Right hand, back. I have another bike that is reversed and i do not like it at all.

Overall impression: I love it. Cruise around pedaling with 15% of the effort of cycling. Or use the throttle and don't pedal at all, just coasting along quietly.

My very first ride was from my town to the next town - about 7 miles one way. I had charged the bike the night before and was ready to go the next morning. I had not cycled ANY in over 10 years and I was wondering how I would do on this long ride. I did consider cancelling, but I was optomistic and went for it. Not sure of the battery life (although it's specified in the description) I pedaled there on medium. It was an easier ride than I've every had. I did my business in town, stopped to say "Hi!" to my Mom and show off my new toy, and was back on the road for the ride back. My battery still showed full - so I decided to return home with the bike on High and see what it could do. - IT. WAS. AWESOME. Never before had I made that ride so quickly and I was not more than slightly winded after pedaling the entire way back. Ok, not the entire way, when I got tired I twisted the throttle to keep my speed, and rested my legs. It was quick and easy.

I highly recommend this bike. U wanna get somewhere without using your vehicle? This is it. Get some sunshine! Get outside! Feeling tired... no problem! This bike has your back and will take you where you want to go with just a twist of the throttle or a spin of the pedals.

Review:
[[VIDEOID:ecff7742d5e47c5cbe12958aba3fb345]] Update 2: After going back and forth with the seller and replacing some bad parts bike has been running great. So far I put in well over 100 miles since I replacing parts and so far no issues. Although there was a delay with communication since they are in China they have hung in there and did everything they could to get my bike running and happy with it now. If you do decide on getting one you might want to get comfortable working on your bike in case you have to replace motor or other things. I had no shops anywhere near me so I had to do the work myself, learned a lot.

Update 1: Seller contacted me and after very slow communications (once a day because they are over seas) they offered to send another motor and a refund of $70. Motor is going to take over 20 days to arrive from what they said. At least they responded and tried to assist, waiting till the next day for a response was painful though.

Take aways
1) Decent bike when it works.
2) Get better tires and use tube liner to help resist flats.
3) If you get this dont expect quick responses or fast turnaround for replacement parts.
4) Unless you are mechanically inclined and capable of working on the bike yourself you should pass.

I got the bike yesterday, tried assembling it today. The left pedal's threading was stripped, and I discovered while reading the online manual the the Manstra is supposed to have both a front and back brake. I only had the front one.
The assembly was fairly easy for everything else. Tools are provided, and they aren't those cheap plastic tools that break on you either.
The color didn't look exactly like what I ordered (grey), however, it was still a pretty color. The bike is true to its size though. I was scared of getting a smaller bike, but I got just what I ordered.
Everything else is great, really. Just need to purchase new pedals (I was going to do that anyway, even if the left pedal worked- the pedals look a bit clunky) and pump my tires.

UPDATE: I haven't had any problems with the bike, and it hasn't fallen apart yet HAHA I actually wanted to update this review and rate it higher because it's such a comfortable ride.

Review:
First, I just gotta say... I freakin love this bike! I have now had two Insights because my first one was stolen and I needed another bike ASAP because I use this to commute. Well, as soon as my first one was stolen I didn't even need to think about what bike I would get to replace it. I have tried other bikes that are comparable, but none out there can beat the price for this quality. No big box store is gonna give you a bike with quality components like the Insight. Wallymart or wherever may be a little cheaper, but the extra $100 spent on a quality bike like the insight will ensure thousands of miles with no repairs probably, except flat tires. Also, the Treks, Specialized, and Giant bikes I have tried, which cost hundreds more than the Insight are not any better in my honest opinion.

I am a college student who commutes between 3 campuses and work so I ride this bike probably 20-30 miles a day on average as a and I also take it on 2-3 hour rides once a week or so. I have racked up almost a thousand miles on it I'm guessing and it's still rolling strong. The Derailers work great, all the components are quality, I've never had to replace anything(except tubes obviously) and it's an all around wonderful bike. It is soooooo light too! I love that I can pick it up and swing it around easily so I can put it on bike racks with ease.

Bad Qualities - The bike does have brakes that could be a little better. Also, the stock tires could be upgraded in my opinion, but these are not deal breakers. I have ridden this bike without upgrading anything besides my seat and the tubes and I've been more than happy. The other great thing about this bike I think is that is has components that are high quality and will last... so I don't need to replace anything, but when the time eventually does come where I want to replace something in a year or 2 I'll just swap it out and this great bike will be even better!

All in all this bike is a steal and I highly recommend it. If you need the best of the best than spend hundreds more, but my advice is to buy this and save yourself money and a headache. This bike will satisfy you, no doubt!

Review:
I’ve ridden this thing 2.5 times. I’ll explain the half time there.

I got it out the door the first warm day since I assembled it. There are trails here to walk or ride so I started on the sidewalk that lead to them. I got about 10 feet. The seat slammed down and broke the plastic reflector. The handle bars twisted but the wheel didn’t turn with them. I carried it home.

Ride #1. After fixing the seat and handlebars I went out another day. I got to the trail, went maybe 100 yards, then the seat turned 90 degrees while I was standing to pedal up hill and I almost crashed when I sat down because I didn’t know the seat had shifted.

Ride #2 today. It held up better. I got about 10 minutes of riding time. Suddenly the handlebars came loose again and the wheel in front spun around freely, nearly causing me to crash again.

No amount of tightening will keep any of the bolts in this thing in place. They all slip almost immediately. The fenders rattle like crazy (just remove them), handlebars come loose with zero notice, the seat comes loose where it connects to the frame AND where the tube connects to the seat itself.

If you want a death trap, by all means buy this thing

Review:
Updated after 9 months of ownership.

This is my first bike in a decade, since middle school probably. I am using it to occasionally commute about 2 miles to work, and for cardio on weekends on paved bikeways. This bike required a fair bit of tuning to basically everything upon receipt which is typical for a new bike. That said, I think the biggest negative about this bike quality wise are the wheels, as they require more truing than I would like. Spokes randomly loosen and the wheel just doesn't seem to naturally be true. I can get them almost perfectly true, but the wobble incessantly returns. I had to learn a lot quick in order to get this bike into functional and quiet riding condition and keep it there. Overall though the bike works pretty well. All of the minor annoyances that I had with it could be tuned in order to fix except the front derailleur. I really don't like the triple chainring front crankset, as it seems like no matter where you adjust the derailleur to, there will be some chain rubbing. I am considering a double crankset setup because of this. All in all, only the wheels and the front crankset are the "you get what you pay for" negs that I can give this entry level road bike.

The matte black is sexy as hell and gets compliments.

FIT:
I'm 5' 10.75" with a 33.5" inseam. My inseam is long for my height, meaning that I have long legs and arms for my height. I got a size large, and I'm not sure how I feel about it. It definitely works for me, but something tells me that a size medium might've been better. The large comes with a 120mm handlebar stem, so I replaced that with a 95mm stem to save me 2.5cm. I also have a 6' 5" wingspan so that helps as well. I'm honestly not sure if a medium would've been better for me. The problem is that my triceps seem to get more tired than I'd like, but I'm not sure if this is an encurance/fitness thing. I've heard that it's better to size down than size up when it comes to bicycles. Overall, I would just suggest that if you're at the top of a sizing range, DO NOT size up unless you have long arms (a wingspan that is 3"+ longer than your height), have a long inseam for your height, and plan on riding aggressively bent over. Otherwise, stick with the sizing chart and do not size up if you're at the top of a sizing range.

Review:
I like to give reviews early to give my first impressions of a product. First of all, this is packed very well, so chances of damage in shipping seem minimal. (Mine had no damage.) I had a little trouble getting it out of the box, as everything is hooked together with plastic zip ties. The black color that I got is gorgeous and very classy looking. I haven't gotten it assembled yet, as there are a number of things to attach (front fender, front wheel, pedals, seat and front tire). Assembly is a little challenging and I had problems even though I am usually good at assembling things. One thing that stopped me from putting it together is that the manual recommends you use a lithium based grease on moving parts when you assemble. I had no grease at all, so picked up some from a bike shop. I learned something important from the bike shop. DO NOT use lithium based grease recommended in the manual if you live in a very hot dry climate. It will dry too much and lock up the wheels of the bike. Other places where it is colder or with more humidity, it works fine. The grease that I got is called Polylube 1000 Polyurea Grease. The manual also confused me about the brakes as it describes brake cables, but I discovered that this model uses coaster brakes, so there are no cables. (Apparently they wrote one manual for several different models of bikes.) I am glad for the coaster brakes, as I learned to ride a bike with coaster brakes. I never liked hand cable brakes, as it always seemed at some point the cable would break or brakes always needing adjustment. Note that I got the single speed model and it should be perfect for me. Seems ideal for casual flatland riding, which is what I plan to use it for. Now I just need to get a helmet and bicycle pump. The tires do come inflated, but not sufficiently. Update: I got the bike together, sort of, but the front fender was not positioned correctly. Also had a big problem with the seat post. Be very careful to make sure the seat post is attached to the seat before you insert the post into the frame. I dropped the post into the frame and it dropped in so far that the seat would not attach to it and I couldn't get it out again. BE VERY CAREFUL about this.
I ended up taking this to a bike shop and they got it out and attached and adjusted the seat; adjusted the handlebars and inflated the tires to the correct pressure. I also had trouble mounting the front fender, which I had mounted wrong; they fixed that too. They also adjusted the chain, which was a little too tight out of the box. They tried to adjust the back fender, but the screws holding it were cranked in so tight that they would not move. A word about the grease. Grease is only inside sealed bearings and you can't get to them without special tools. The bike has proper lubrication out of the box, so only after some use and some time would you need to lubricate it. The one place I did use grease is on the threads on the pedals when attaching them and the bike shop confirmed that was the right thing to do. Unless you are really familiar with bike mechanics, best to get some professional help on this to make sure everything is good. I should knock off a star or two from my rating for the problems I had and the confusing manual, but it is such a nice bike that I am staying with the 5-star rating.

Review:
I gotta say I'm pretty impressed with this bike out of the box -- shifting and brakes were adjusted perfectly and wheels were fairly true but the hubs were a little tight, but easily adjustable and now very smooth. Wheels and hubs seem like they are built to last and are not very heavy at all, even with the tires.

The Shimano shifting and Tektro brakes are great -- better than what came on a $2k 'boutique' bike I bought a few years ago. The rest of the parts are almost as good -- hub engagement is not bad at all for a budget hub, also much better than my previous more expensive bike. Gearing is totally adequate for me at least with even steps between gears which is nice, plenty of low for climbing, but could run out of gears on really fast downhills.

Frame seems great -- Lightweight, seems well engineered, everything bolts on smoothly and straight, and the welds are all good and visible which I like better than the toothpaste looking welds on some bikes. Can take an internal dropper too. Fits me perfectly based on the website suggestion, but I still need more rides to tell about the stem for sure.
The bike rolls smooth and fast! Can really cover ground with the 29er. This bike climbs great and is really comfortable. I climbed a big hill in my neighborhood just because and I never do that! I LOVE the orange color!

The seat and grips are much better than expected, and the pedals are ok too -- very light and spin smooth and should get better with use. I thought I'd swap all those out immediately but have decided to try them all longer. The tires are also pretty good, not too heavy, roll ok on pavement/hardpack, and pretty good in the dirt so far. Another pleasant surprise.

The only real gripe I have with this bike is the fork -- it is really heavy and just so-so in performance. I did ride with it for a day but then I swapped it out for a lighter air spring fork. Not a terribly expensive upgrade and the performance increase is incredible -- wheelie game is much improved haha! And yes, this bike wheelies great!

My first real BMX bike in the 70s was a Mongoose Motomag -- it's GOOD to be back on a Mongoose!

**Upgrading this to 5 stars from 4 because I realized the hardware to upgrade to 12x148 rear wheel is built into the frame! No additional dropout hardware needed! Upgrade at your own pace if desired. Love this bike!**

Review:
The concept of a collapsible, dual suspension electric bike with disc brakes sounds amazing. The reality is far less impressive. I spent years as a bicycle cop, riding Treks that were capable and durable. This bike does not offer that confidence. It has the fit and finish of Chinese metal. You will always wish you had something just a little better.

When I first put it together I charged up the bike and took it out with naive excitement to have the wind whip through what remains of my flowing locks. The bike would not run on any level above 1 without automatically shutting off. The seller was responsive and sent me a second battery. Unfortunately, I continued to have the same issue.

I researched these Chinese bikes a little more in attempting to fix the problem. It appears these battery packs often have recycled or reused battery cells that may have faults. In order to get them to work they need to be charged well beyond just getting it to full charge the first time. That fixed one battery, but not the original that came with the bike.

I did eventually get the bike to work even though the battery won't lock into the cradle that holds it. It just rests there. Now that it is working, it is exhilarating to ride. It has nice power and pull to it. However, I'm 5'9" and the frame is just a hair too small for me. It has since become my daughter's bike and she loves it. It now makes it very difficult to keep up with a 10 year old on my old 21 speed. But she sure is happy, and far less tired than the old man.

I highly suggest getting an electric bike. It brings the joy back to riding. This is likely not the one you want. If yours does not work, it is quite expensive to send it back. I would look for one with more and better reviews.

Review:
We started off with the training wheels attached, and that definitely helped him get used to the bike. The training wheels don't have a lot of adjustment, so they kind of just go on at the height designed, which means these training wheels are only a couple of inches off the ground. That's OK if your kid really doesn't have good balance, but you'll quickly realize that these are a hindrance in their ability to progress (the training wheels are touching constantly).

Fast forward a couple of months, and it was time to take the training wheels off. The bike is a 300% better product now, and rides great. My son zooms around on this bad boy all afternoon with his buddies. Without those training wheels it rides so much better - they were creating so much drag.

The bike doesn't come with a kickstand, which I think is a huge miss. Once the training wheels come off, it just gets laid down on the ground with not in use. I searched online for the replacement part from Mongoose, no such thing. Mongoose doesn't even sell the kickstand.

When searching for kickstands, there are lots of newer age versions that attach to the rear frame, but I wanted a classic center mount (which this bike is setup for). It was hard to find one, but I finally did. I shouldn't have had to spend another $20 for the kickstand given the price of this bike.

Review:
After purchasing this bike I can confidently say this is the best value, 16 inch, premium bicycle available on the market. I researched bicycles for my four and a half year old extensively and I try to submit a review for any product that I believe is truly a quality product. That said, when I stumble across an excellent product, I try to communicate such so as to ensure that the value is recognized and so that more expansive products will hopefully follow suit and come to market from that company as a result of the recognition. To this point, after extensive research, this bike came into play because of having hand brakes (no coaster brake), light weight, belt drive, high quality components, great geometry and overall value considering the aforementioned.

Before getting into details about the bike, let me me first say that I'm submitting this review about six hours after receiving the bicycle earlier today. Not only is the bike solidly built and phenomenal looking in person but my son who had been riding a balance bicycle rode this bicycle without my help on the first day. I received this bicycle from Amazon two days earlier than initially expected on April 14, 2020 at about 10:00AM Eastern time and my son was riding on his own just after 12:00 in the afternoon. To say that it blew me away is an understatement!!! Needless to say, he doesn't want to stop riding and I attribute that a great deal to the excellent geometry, solid components, lightweight frame (and killer looks) of this bicycle.

First, this bike has adjustable, hand brakes as opposed to coaster brakes. Many of the less expensive department store bicycles have a coaster and in fact coaster brakes are what many of us learned to ride with. However, with product research and product evolutions, have come better ways to do things. One example of this is the advent of balance bikes (as an alternative to training wheels) as a precursor to riding a bicycle. As mentioned, seeing was believing today because my son got on this bicycle and rode within fifteen to twenty minutes of pushing himself along (as he did with his balance bike). I am planning to post a video. As such, hand brakes for children, as opposed to coaster brakes, have become a most desirable way of braking due to their strong braking power on both front and rear wheel whereas coaster brakes only brake on the rear wheel. Also, the absence of the coaster brake on the crankset, results in children not stopping suddenly if they should pedal backward accidentally (which children often tend to do when learning to ride a bicycle without training wheels – their reflexes cause them to pedal backward when they are trying to ride and balance the bike and this causes them to stop and fall off the bike). Lastly, with coaster brakes, you cannot freely move the pedals backward so it’s harder for a child to get the pedals in the 2 o’clock and 8 o’clock position when they are trying to start off riding and want one of the pedals in the up position. In short, hand brakes make it easier for them to rotate the pedals and step and go. As mentioned initially, it’s important to note that some hand brakes are not adjustable but adjustable hand brakes are all the more imperative for a child’s hand because it allows you to dial in the right reach to ensure that your child’s hand can grasp the brake lever according to their hand size – super important. The Belsize has adjustable hand brakes which is a premium feature and absolutely necessary.

Secondly, this bike is about 13 pounds. Weight is extremely important for younger riders particularly when training wheels come off. Most department store bikes are in the 21-25 pound range which essentially means that if you have a little one who is say, 40 pounds, for example, they would be trying to hold up half their weight, or more, when trying to ride a typical 16 inch department store bicycle of 21 plus pounds. To put it differently, for adults that ride bikes, it’s like a 180 pound adult holding up a 90 pound bike but in fact, even worse for a child because a young child’s strength isn’t proportional to their weight at such a young age. Therefore, 20 plus pounds is heavy particularly when they’re trying to learn something like getting on and off a bicycle. A heavy bike simply results in the experience being less fun and like anything else, they won’t use the bicycle as often due to getting tired and frustrated. That said, the Belsize 16 is lighter than most of the premium name bicycles that are out there and in fact, I only found ONE other comparable, premium bicycle out there that was as light or lighter and that well known kid’s bike manufacturer (that begins with a “W”) weighs in at just under 12 pounds and retails for just under $400 but DOES NOT have a belt drive. (As an aside, I like the geometry on the Belsize bicycle much better than the previously mentioned bike.) That alone makes the Belsize 16 a superior value.

Thirdly, as mentioned above, this bicycle has a belt drive which is a more costly and longer lasting drive system than the conventional chain drives that bikes have been made with for years. Belt drives are a new innovation and they’ve gained in popularity in kids bikes and adult bikes, alike. The belt drive is known for it’s ease of maintenance and clean attributes because it doesn’t need to be oiled, cleaned or adjusted like a bicycle chain. Also, unlike chains that needs maintenance, a rubber belt is ready to ride and lasts many times longer than a bicycle chain. Not only is it maintenance free and lasts longer but it is arguably a smoother ride because a belt doesn’t have the tendency to skip or come off the cranks like a chain will. No dirty greasy chain and longer lasting means a superior value and a safer drive system that your child is less likely to get hurt with. I should also note that the photo of the bicycle in this ad does not show this but this bicycle DOES come with a belt guard (aka. chain guard) that can go over the belt drive to protect a little one from getting a shoe lace caught, etc... I didn't install mine but the fact that this comes with one is an outstanding, extra safety feature and affirms that this bicycle is really phenomenal for a first time rider.

Finally, the overall components on this bicycle ranging from the Tektro adjustable hand brakes to the Kenda tires are premium components. The bike’s light, aluminum frame, very smooth and quiet belt drive and aforementioned adjustable hand brakes are attributes associated with premium bikes that sell for $300-$400 and that makes this Belsize a tremendous value. In my view, this is clearly the best value kids bicycle available, bar none. I should also note that the bicycle comes with a manual for putting it together and it is quite easy to assemble as it really just requires putting the handlebar, front wheel, pedals and seat on. Not difficult at all. I should note that before purchasing, I had questions about the bicycle and A11N Sports Company who manufactures/sells the bicycle was extremely receptive and prompt in answering my questions. It gave me even further confidence in the quality of the product because the company selling the bike followed up, answered my questions and demonstrated excellent customer service. I can't say enough about this bicycle as my four year old is already up and riding on the same day it's received and he LOVES the bike!!

Review:
I've had the bike now for 2 months and am very happy with it. Compared to others on the market, having 500W power and 48V battery storage is a big plus. Bike rides very smooth, averages 15-17 mph. The battery lasts about 24-26 miles without pedaling; that's with both uphill/downhill roads. I had to change out to a wider seat because the original was too uncomfortable. The brakes screech a lot because of weight ratio, so I will have to get that adjusted or replaced. The 20 inch tires are a little small and makes the bike unsteady sometimes, but of course smaller tires gives you better mileage and maneuverability. If you're using it for offroading, I would get the big wheels. I love that the bike folds in half, has a basket holder in the back, and has a built-in front light connected to the main battery. The charging is fast, 6-8 hours. Assembly is very easy, I had one issue with a broken tail light, but it was eventually rectified by the seller.

Recommended: Only use a "U" lock for the frame, and always lock up the front tire. Change to a wide seat, and get a rear-view left mirror.

Review:
The Royce Union bike is a good one. Like many mountain bikes today, it only has front suspension. This isn't a big deal, and I really prefer it. I've ridden a full suspension bike before, but I don't sit down much and prefer the stiffness. I guess its just the way that I ride.

Putting it together was easy, especially if you've ever put a bike together before. But i think it would be easy for anyone. Just put on the wheels and pedals and tighten the handlebars in place. And you can add a shifter protector.

The tires came inflated, at least partially, which was a surprise. The bad news was that one of the tires arrived flat, and ruined. I picked up a new tube for $5, I suppose I could have repaired the old one but I didn't try. I did remove a star for this, but the bike itself is very good so I didn't want to ding it to severely. I also didn't contact the manufacturer as it was so cheap to fix that problem, but the downside is that I don't know how they would have fixed the problem. Sorry about that :/

By the way, I've done a lot of biking, and this bike is pretty heavy and doesn't have great gear, but, its got good features within its price point. you would want to compare it to a Diamondback or anything from wally world, but nothing you'd find in a specialty bike store. that just wouldn't be fair. The shifters work well, its easy to adjust, the seat is kinda hard.

Overall its a great bike in its price range.

Review:
This is a gorgeous, and affordable bike. I love the drop bars, but they do seem shorter in length than others I’ve had.

The Volare is super lightweight, and gear shifting has been smooth. I’ve only had the bike a couple days but had not noticed any issues with the tires as other reviewers mention.

Some parts of the body are slightly marred by obvious welding points, but it is barely noticeable.

I don’t personally care for pedal straps so I removed those right off. However, these are made well with plastic and webbing. Very easy to put on, or take off by hand. No tools needed.

I plan on replacing the seat with something more comfortable but stock seats are not usually the best. This one is no worse than others.

This bike feels tall. Very tall. I bought the medium as that was the one in store. At 5’7 I can just barely stand upright over the bar.

The ride with the Volare is smooth, and it picks up speed well.

Brakes are in perfect working order, responsive, and easy to squeeze.

There is no body rattle.

I’ve had comparable bikes from Novara at REI at more than twice the price here.

I’m withholding a fifth star because I haven’t had the bike long enough to put it to the test, but overall this is a great bike to start out with, I would recommend it to friends.

Review:
First, a bit about me. This is my 3rd road bike and first Tommaso. I have not owned more expensive road bikes. All of mine have been under $1000.00. I am 6'1 and 250 lbs (more muscle than round but that's another story for another time).

I shopped around and looked at several bikes including Trek. The Tommaso Imola and the Forcella came up in several Google searches for great bikes. I read many great reviews and decided to pull the trigger on the Forcella and purchased it on the Tommaso website.

It arrived in less than a week. I was waiting for it like a kid in a candy store. It arrived in a huge box, albeit light. Once I cracked it opened I smiled. There was a handwritten message thanking me for my purchase on the inside cardboard insert. Once I removed the insert, I was blown away by how impeccably packaged everything was. When they say you can be up and running in about 10 minutes, they weren't kidding. I only needed to put on the front wheel and the handlebars. They supplied the Allen wrenches and grease for the seat post. The pedals were already installed. I changed the pedals to my own so I could clip in and added a Cateye computer. The next morning I was ready to roll.

I have had to the pleasure of taking this well-made machine on three bike trips. One 22 mile ride and two 10 mile quickies. The bike shifts nice and smooth and boy is it fast. I'm 250 lbs and had it 27-29 MPH on the flat pavement areas around my neighborhood as often as my legs would allow me to. I'm sure I can get it over 30 mph with no problem. I've also taken it to the park and tackled many inclines and declines. It performed like a champ. I don't recall ever having this much fun riding a bike and I'm many decades-old:) The brakes are also phenomenal. I know disc brakes are the rage but the stopping power on this beast is incredible.

There are tons of great reviews for Tommaso bikes. I am now a true fan. Thank you for making biking so much fun with your well-built machine.

Review:
Shipping was very fast & FREE.
( In the spring Hey Bikes were free S &H )
( But as of 7/1/22 the FREE Shipping has vanished, as I tried to order another last night, they wanted $70 )

Anyways, the bike exceeds any & all expectations.
Light enough to bring in & out of our home when we ride.
The engineers were genius by placing the battery under the seat for a central point of gravity & not making the crossbar to huge.

Also right at the lowest point of the step-through is a handle, perfectly located to grip as you lift your bike on or into your transportation, or in my case perfect for traversing 8 steps.

I have not worked on or tinkered with any bicycles since my teenage years, now at 61 I knew it was some assembly required, but how much.

Truth be told, not very much, all tools and parts were included.
Bike comes wrapped in all kinds of protective papers and wraps.
Rear tire, chain & frame are completely built, you only need to install the pedals, front fender & tire, handlebars & headlamp

Less than 45 minutes invested, plus the tires were sealed and inflated per specs.

I posted several pictures to show it with its features, one last pic shows how I zip tied the key to the frame as to never ever lose it.

An E-Bike that will do 21 mph, range of at least 40 miles, for under $900

Sign me up, ordering a Hey Bike Ranger 7/2/22

Review:
I rated it at 3 stars, redline has not aged well. Redline bikes were 100% made in the USA back when I use to ride in the 90's. They use to be very obviously proud of their brand so to see them now manufactured and lower quality is really sad to me. Their logos and decals were more detailed, the dropouts were 3D, they had better welding and just overall they use to be a quality brand you can trust. With that said I don't entirely suggest anyone buy this bike, I can list off all the things wrong with it but to make a long story short you get what you pay for, a lower quality but kind of decent bike. The front end is very heavy due to the super thick hi-ten forks, the cranks aren't the best with rubber end caps. The graphics are painted and clear coated so you can never remove them and they are rather minimalistic.

If you are serious about racing don't buy this bike, for the price it's also not a good bike for kids, it's meant to be an entry level racing bike. Don't buy a bike like this just to ride on the streets. It's not comfortable at all for long rides, the geometry is strictly for racing.

The good aspect about it is that it's a rather nice frame shape with a euro bottom bracket and an integrated head tube. It has modern racing geometry. The frame mainly earned the 3 stars.

Please don't buy this for your kid, they need a smaller BMX bike like a mini size or an expert NOT A PRO size. This is an adult size BMX bike fit for riders in the height range of 5'5"-5'7".

Review:
I will start by saying that I love my Eahora XC100. I love the way its style and design are far more attractive than many of the boxy, more expensive models available. I get many compliments on it. Since I only want to use it for pedal assist on roads or paved trails I find its power and performance are plenty for me. HOWEVER, if you buy this bike know the risk you are taking by buying directly from a foreign manufacturer. While I believe Eahora customer service has a positive attitude and desire to please the customer, if your bike has a problem it will be costlier in terms of both time and money to have it fixed, even if it is under warranty.

Riding my bike in May 2021 it stopped working and flashed an error message on the screen. I am not very handy, so I called around to find a bike shop to diagnose the problem. Out of five or so shops I called, only one said they would take a look at it. The other shops only serviced e-bike brands they sell or bikes that use the same powertrain as those they sell. It took 4 months of back and forth emails among me, my bike shop and Eahora to finally diagnose the problem - a faulty controller. I am sure the delays can be attributed both to Eahora as well as my bike shop as they have been slammed with repair requests over the summer. Once Eahora finally agreed that the controller needed to be replaced, I still had to pay to ship the part from China (over $60) even though the bike was under warranty when it broke. On top of that I had to pay the bike shop over $320 for the diagnosis and repairs since the warranty only covers parts.

I am happy to report that the repairs were successful and the bike seems to be performing better than ever, leading me to suspect the controller was faulty all along before it finally died. As long as the bike continues to perform well I will still feel I have gotten a good value, despite the extra cost and loss of 4 months of use. However, I personally will not take the same risk if I buy another e-bike. I would only purchase a bike from a dealer or manufacturer who can provide local warranty repair that covers both parts and labor. If Eahora were able to make arrangements for this kind of service in my local area, I would not hesitate to buy Eahora again.

Review: I live on a dirt road with fast drivers so I don’t bike a lot. I don’t get much chance to get to the one trail near us. I read the reviews and thought, maybe this inexpensive starter bike would allow me to test the waters. I also don’t (yet) know a lot about bikes. I was able to put it together, only to find out the right brake was not attached at all. I contacted Schwinn, who sent me some videos but it took a good deal of research to figure out how to get it attached and taut. Sure, I should know this but a bike also should arrive with the brake actually attached. In Schwinn’s defense, at this point they offered to help pay to get it fixed at the local bike store but we got it working. Next, I thought I was ready to go so I attached a water bottle holder. Except the top screw was cheap and something is wrong with the threads so it just stripped. My husband had to use a vice wrench to remove the screw and we were able to half get another one in. Fine. I can live with that. Only it turns out the chain is completely detached from the bike. Great. So my husband reattaches it. Finally it’s ready to ride after 4 hours of assembly, learning, and fixing. Get on the bike and the 6th gear just keeps popping out. I give up. It’s going back. It also arrived almost completely assembled which would be a bonus if they hadn’t put the fork on backward. I imagine the technicians at Schwinn snickering as they assembled this one. “Let’s see what else we can make this poor naive person who doesn’t know anything about bikes figure out.”

Nice frame. 16" version weighs about 18 lbs total.

brakes are strong, very firm. good stopping. Our child took to them within 1 day.
brake levers are good; albeit 100% plastic.
gear spocket guard works good
16" wheels are perfect; less than 1mm of deflection in any direction (checked with a dial gauge)
Pedals are "OK" at best, I don't think they have bearings. Doubt there would be much gain upgrading them.
Handle bar is OK; it's a cafe racer style which looks cool. But for some kids a bar with a 3" rise might be more comfortable. Just be aware you'll need to redo the brake cables with longer ones.

The installation instructions were seriously poor. Lots of stuff missing; like setting brake pads correctly and checking chain deflection. Also difficult to read the printout; very low quality.

The rest of the adjustments are pretty normal for any new bike...

We cut 1" off of the seat post to get it a bit lower for my 5 year old
We adjusted the brake levers in (closer to the bar) so they are easier for a grab for someone with small hands (simple screw stop adjustment on the brake lever)
All brake pads had to be squared/aligned to the wheel rim (this is an important step for best braking performance)
Adjusted the brake cables

Bike has gone 30+ miles already and has been very good on the trails. For the money this is a great deal. No complaints.

Review:
My husband and I can't be happier with this balance bike. My son (5-year old) was able to ride a regular bike independently just a few days after using this balance bike. It's like magic, but I think the theory behind it is that kids need to learn how to balance themselves before being able to ride a bike alone. Let me briefly share our story:

My son had been reluctant to let us take down the training wheels from his regular bike. We tried very hard to help him learn how to ride a bike, usually by holding the handle and/or seat while he paddled, but he totally relied on us to keep the bike balanced and we got so exhausted. He didn't enjoy the practice either and just wanted the training wheels back. It seemed that the regular bike, a gift from our friends, was just too large for him and he had no idea how to control it. We eventually decided to get a balance bike, as many sources advised us to. It was one of our best investments. Our son felt very relaxed playing with the balance bike because it was so much shorter and lighter for him, and he could make a stop easily as he wished. He liked to show us how long he could stay on the bike without touching the ground (and enjoyed breaking his records). I could see that he was quickly learning how to balance himself in order to stay longer. He played with the balance bike for just a few days (each time about 15 minutes), sometimes followed by some practice with the regular bike. The difference was obvious; he was able to control the balance better. We gradually loosened our hold of the seat and eventually let go of the bike (while running closely aside)---that's such an unforgettable moment, our son was riding a bike alone, finally! We were all so excited about the accomplishment.

I definitely would recommend getting a balance bike for small children. Now we totally believe that it's a justifiable investment. Your kid may feel bored with it initially (my son tried at 3 and didn't show any interest, that's why we didn't get one), but the key is to have them get comfortable with handling a bike alone. Training wheels really can't help them learn balancing. Many of us probably never used a balance bike in our childhood, but we probably also rode a bike independently much later, not like 4 or 5 these days. And balance bikes, especially with renown brand names like Schwinn, have great resale values, so we can always get a bit back when the transition is over.

Anyway, we are very happy with our purchase and would recommend this balance bike.

Review:
This was my first road bike. I was looking for a bike to commute to and from work, and pretty much nothing more. I don't know much about bikes or cycling, but I had my brother, who is a great triathlete and into cycling, look at this bike. He said the the bike looked really good for the price and that the components were from a very good company. He said the things I would most likely have to replace would be the shifters and breaks because they go the fastest. And really the shifters are the only issue I have with this bike. I really do not like the shifters. I don't know if I got a bad batch or what, but the gears stick often, and the chain rattles a lot as if I'm always in between gears. There's really only two gears I can be in when it doesn't sound like the chain is rattling. Other then that I am happy with the bike, and it makes a great bike to commute to work.

The bike comes in a box and is very easy to put together. I'm sure you can take it to a bike shop and they will put it together for you for a fee. I put mine together myself, and then took it to a bike shop for a tune up to make sure everything was put together correctly. I would recommend this bike to someone starting out in cycling or someone looking just for a commuter bike. If you are looking to get more into cycling and like this bike, I would say upgrade the components, cause I would hate to have these shifters in a race.

Review:
Excellent bike. Rode it from Baton Rouge La to Wichita Falls Tx. Assembled and tuned bike myself, after watching hours of Park Tool videos on derailleur and some other videos on brakes,washing and lubricating. I like this bike better than my Trek. Although I think they're getting lazy. The only reason I gave it 4 stars is because I lost a rear derailleur hanger bracket bolt and called Vilano Bikes to buy a replacement. But they acted like they didn't know anything about the Bike(the one they claim to manufacture), even after talking to the techs there. Had to go to Capitol Cyclery in Baton Rouge, and without the bike I showed the tech the same pictures I sent Vilano Bikes and in half a second they handed me two set of fasteners for a bike they don't sell. Vilano Bikes in fact didn't know about the spokes as well and same results with Capitol Cyclery, two seconds later they told me what I needed to know and sold me some spare spokes. My theory is they do not build the bikes, but buy the parts and assemble them and slap a sticker on it and call it "hand made". Please don't let my rant about the company's ethics detract from the product itself. The bike is awesome and tried to buy their Women's Classic Urban Commuter Single Speed Bike, but China hasn't sent them the frames yet so I went with Diamondback Serene Classic here on Amazon for FAR less than they wanted at the Local Bike Shop. Plus I won't get charged 150 Dollars for assembly and tuning, you know, putting on the front wheel, handlebars and the pedals. Then tune the already adjusted drive train and brakes. Don't get me wrong, they know their stuff. They are just pirates. I think the bike is great because it was most likely built in China, and who knows bikes better the several hundred million bike riders.

Update 10/18/2018: I have ridden this bike from Baton Rouge to Reno Nv. My route took me from Baton Rouge, Reno, San Fransisco, Santa Barbara, Palmdale, Bishop, Mammoth and Finally Reno again. With typical maintenance, I hate to say it but I love this bike. Going to get a trailer and ride to the east coast during the winter.

Review:
In essence, this is a very pretty bike that holds up to rather rough terrain and is just as comfortable to ride as my old bike. And while I am enjoying the bike, I would not buy it again.

First, I bought the 17.5” frame if I recall correctly, which was $490 when I bought it - it’s now listed at ~$630. This bike was rarely worth $490, and that jump is just straight up greed. At that price go to your local bike store, cause you can honestly find something more built for mountain biking at the same price point (~$600), maybe for even cheaper.

But the price isn’t the real issue here. What the issue is, is the lack of care even put into building this bike. It works, but the tape that’s supposed to go over the spoke holes isn’t lined up in the some spots so the holes are exposed to the tube. And the tape also likes to come up with my tube whenever I need to change the tires.

The tires. They’re trash. They’re not built for mountain biking. They’re thin altogether, and incredibly thin on the sides. I had to replace the tubes with thorn resistance ones, and literally it’s been just about a week and the front tube popped again, first time a thorn resistant tube popped on me, and the first time a tube popped that quickly. -EDIT: The tires are on the cheaper side, but they aren't as bad as I initially believed.

I’ve put in probably 100+ miles into the bike since I’ve had it, and again the overall build isn’t bad, but if I knew what I knew now, I would just go to the bike store. I bought on here thinking I’d have to spend way more for a bike, but that’s not the case. And the $ I’ve had to put into it despite only having the bike for about a month is pathetic.
