Review:
I knew this was a red bike from the product description, however, I was shocked at the ultra "Cool Factor" of the paint job on this bike. It is not a gloss type pearlescent but rivals that quality, with an iridescent matte finish that is absolutely outstanding in appearance. Hard to describe but definitely the best looking, rich red, that I have ever seen on a product. Everyone who has seen it so far have all had the same reaction..."Wow. THAT is cool." And I am sure the other colors in this line with this finish will be equally impressive.

When putting it together, however, I noted that the finish on the red can be pretty easily marred or scuffed, if not being careful. Particularly, where I was using a box wrench to crank down the bolts on the front fender attachment, my wrench grazed the fender finish near the bolt head and left a scuff. It did not go through the paint but you could definitely see where it happened, although it was a very small nick and in a spot that most would not see. So, at least, take care in the use and handling if you want to keep the paint job pristine.

It does take some time to assemble due to the awkwardness of the parts which swivel and move around, getting away from you as you are trying to assemble. My advice, which is not suggested in the assembly instructions is to first mount the handlebars (as suggested in step one), and then proceed to mounting the seat next, so that you can flip the bike upside down to deal with the front fender and the front wheel. Upside down the bike is resting on three points which make it stable and static for mounting the front wheel and fender. Instead they instruct you to do the seat after the wheel mount, which is totally not the best sequence.

The bike comes (thankfully) with all cables attached, in place and functioning (i.e. sprocket gear change cable, and brake cables front and back). BUT, you can't mount the front wheel without partially disconnecting the front brake cable, because the wheel will not clear the pads since they are already set in place with a gap that is in extreme proximity to the wheel rim. Absolutely no way you can get the wheel onto the forks without releasing the brake cable where it connects to the two pads. Also take a picture of how the cable assembly is routed within the connecting hardware that activates the pads. I did not think to do that and the instructions don't clearly show that, so I had to refer to the rear brake cable assembly routing to see how it is supposed to go. Both are identical in how they work, so take note.

AND, a big mistake I made initially was not realizing that the forks only go one correct direction. There is a label on the forks that says "Rear", so be sure to orient the forks with that label to the rear of the front, when mounting the handle bars. I didn't see that label, nor realize that they are not "unisex" directional, meaning some forks are straight up and down with no subtle curves or bends to them, but this definitely has a "forward facing" angle and a "rear facing" angle. I did it wrong the first time, which made the chrome brace which connects the back of the fender to the front axles, too short to reach the axle. So I had contacted the manufacturer to troubleshoot the brace prongs being too short to reach the axle mounting point. After sending customer service photos of the problem, they asked me to check the orientation of the forks to see if the "Rear" label was facing forward. Yep. So they are very responsive and helpful if you have a problem. They also advised me that there are is good video support, for assembly, at a website, parktool.com, if you run into any type of confusion while assembling. I did not go there, however, since I already had most of it figured out.

Also note that the front fender mounting bracket bolts to the backside of the forks, not the front.

I am 6'-2" and weigh 190 pounds so I was curious to see how comfortable this bike is to ride. And I am very pleased by the comfort and ergonomics of the bike. There is a wide range of verticality of the handlebars, which allows you to rotate them up or down to the perfect angle that suits you for comfort, based on your size and arm length. The wide seat is comfortable, with just the right amount of cushioning and I found the gear changes to be smooth and effortless. I like the way that is done with the controls on the right handlebar. The front of the grip adjacent to the gear indicator, has a ribbed rotational ring, with which you can select, by turning, to the numbered gear that you want to be in. The shifter is responsive and smooth.

After the initial trials of assembly, I find the bike a real pleasure to ride. And you will turn heads with the awesome red paint job.

Review:
Ok. Here’s the deal. I bought this bike as a gift for my nephew’s 3rd birthday.
We have been taking him to the local BMX Ramp Park with his Strider bike for a few months, and it seems that he’s ready for a ‘pedal bike’ - so, here we are.

I wanted to get him a bike that looked similar to the BMX stuff that the bigger kids at the Ramp Park ride, but didn’t think it was a smart idea to drop $300(+) on a ‘real’ (Fit Bike Co, Kink BMX, etc) that he is going to grow out of in like a year.
All of the inexpensive 12” bikes tend to look pretty ‘childish’ with all the stickers & crazy stuff...so I decided to simply modify one of them to fit what I wanted for him.

Anyway. I grabbed this bike, and upon unboxing, I just eliminated all the nonsense I didn’t like. I took off the chain guard & used a grind to remove their mounting tabs. I cut-off the silly hand grips and replaced them with inexpensive ‘true’ BMX style units. I pulled-off all the ‘silly’ stickers and swapped them for ‘real’ BMX brand logos. Finally, I took off the dumb reflectors from the wheels.

Now he’s got a bike that ‘looks’ like a true BMX Park bike - and it ended-up costing me about $10 more than the price of the bike (and about $200 LESS than a Fit Bike Co).

Anyway. Build quality seems really good for the money. Assembly was super-easy & tools required to do so are all included with the bike.

All-in-all. It’s seeming like this was a good purchase.

* note: Attached pictures do not reflect the ‘out of the box’ product. As stated above, this bike was modified to suit my wants/needs.

Review:
Hover-1 has several electric transportation options. They do e-scooters, hoverboards, and e-bikes. I’ve known them for the hoverboards that my kids loved. Hover-1 is owned by the DGL group, an electronics company, founded in 2001, with a focus on Bluetooth products and electric toys. So they’ve been around awhile and have a reputation for quality in the realm of e-vehicles. So as far an e-bikes go, they are certainly a manufacturer to consider.

As far as e-bikes themselves go, I had many questions: why an e-bike? What’s the advantage over a regular bike? Do I need to pedal? If so, what’s the exercise benefit? Is it like a mini-motor cycle? I just didn’t see the point. To me, you pedal a bike. Does the battery powered motor really make that much of a difference? Even though the weather out my at this time isn’t perfect for biking, I was able to give the Hover-1 e-bike a spin to see how it all worked and answer my questions.

First off is the packaging and instructions for assembly. I dreaded this at first because I have a so-so history of calibrating bike brakes and putting on chains. Fortunately, my fears were alleviate when all of that was taken care of by Hover-1. The bike essentially comes with six separate pieces: The frame of the bike with back wheel (the battery, brakes and chain are IN PLACE), the seat, the front wheel, the front wheel lock, the pedals, and the handlebars. The biggest challenge I had was getting the front wheel to fit in place. It took he a good ten minutes to fit it in place. At first it seemed like the frame was too narrow — too tight a fit, like I’d have to remove the nuts on the sides of the wheel. And the instructions were of any help on this, just technical drawings. However, I was able to finally get it in place and lock it with the wheel lock. As mentioned, the instructions were made up of technical drawings. In some cases, you really had to use a magnifying glass to see where everything fit. As far as tools go, you’ll get a small wrench and two hex wrenches. That’s pretty much all you’ll need to get this together. Eventually, you’ll may want to calibrate the brakes better, but to my novice eye, front and back brakes looked and operated well.

First usage: It’s a slick looking bike. I like the thicker three-spoked wheel along with Kenda tires. Kenda has been around for over 60 years. Great tires! As another reviewer has said, when you apply the throttle to engage the motor, it does so gradually, building up speed rather than jerking you forward. Peddling is necessary on setting 2 – the motor provides you with extra oomph as you peddle (answers my third) question) – the higher the throttle, the more assist you get. Setting 3 will propel the back wheel with no peddling; however, it will drain your battery faster. I got up to the 15 mph as advertised on a level road. The advantage in this case you can go longer distances without taxing yourself as you would with a regular, manual bike (answers my second question). There is still an exercise component – you’re still peddling. And you can ride it like a regular bike if you don’t turn on the battery and use the throttle (answers my forth question). And to answer my fifth question, no, it’s not a mini motorcycle. First off, it’s not mini – it’s a good sized bike, and second, it requires you to peddle rather than just get on and ride like a motorcycle.

The battery takes about 40-45 minutes to fully charge - I forgot to add that yes, it cones with keys to lock it in place and a charging plug. And it’s fairly heavy.

And to answer my first question, why an e-bike? I can now see several benefits. The first is that it provides a means to travel farther with less effort. There’s an extra boost with the throttle to assist with the pedaling allowing you to ride longer, faster, and go farther. This would explain the popularity of these bikes in the Metroparks. The pedal assist gives riders a boost, allowing those getting back into exercise or senior-aged riders to enjoy themselves more by reducing stress on the joints. It allows people who may not otherwise ride a traditional bike (due to aches and pains) to join in on the fun of riding outside again without as much physical exertion. This is also a great option for commuting if you live a few miles away from work. Even though this has a range of 40 miles (on the lowest throttle setting), this allows a more efficient means to ride your bike to work without looking like you just completed the Tour de France. There’s also e-bike insurance you can by through certain insurance companies to cover damage caused by theft, damage, collision, fire, etc. So there are indeed benefits to getting an e-bike. This is one from Hover-1 is definitely one to add to your list.

Review:
For a bike that cost $330 I'm pretty pleased. I'm a 50 year old 6'2" overweight man so I think I'm right in the market demographic for this bike. It arrived quickly. The packaging was slightly damaged. Looks like there was some stress where the axles of the bicycle met the cardboard inside. Fortunately Sixthreezero overpacks everything, so the bike was well protected from damage. Before getting into assembly, note that you will need to pump up the tires before you can ride. It's also helpful to watch some YT videos about assembling these bikes. Also note that the serial number is on a yellow sticker on the outside of the box, so don't throw the box away yet. Note too that the Sixthreezero Forever Warranty is available on their site for possibly less or more money than elsewhere.

Assembly was not extremely difficult. I'm fairly handy, but I don't think the average person needs to pay for assembly. You don't really need extra tools because the bike comes with phillips screwdriver tipped allen wrenches and a multi wrench that fits all the bolts. That said, it might make things easier if you use your own open-ended wrenches and a pair of pliers. The multi wrench hurts your hand if you're really torquing. If you're an older guy there are more things you can do to make assembly easier. First, if you can find a way to elevate the working surface it would make a huge difference. All the crouching while working was difficult. Also you'll want glasses. The text on the instructions is very small. Scissors will help with cutting loose all the packaging, which includes some zip ties. Finally, work somewhere with plenty of light if your bike is black, because it's hard to see the low-contrast parts sometimes. If you're not a fat geezer like me though, no worries! On the technical side I will say there are a few difficult points to the assembly. Make sure to get the front fork the right way around before putting on the handlebars. The brakes go towards the front. Also the handlebars themselves should be positioned with the mounting bar angled forwards and cabling towards the front. The hardest part was attaching the rear fender and rack. The fender attaches in two places on both sides (four total) and it shares the forward mounting points with the rack. I had a devil of a time putting the bolt through the rack and fender mounts and attaching to the right side of the frame. The shift cabling is right there and gets in the way, plus the fender mount seemed a little bent and didn't want to let the bolt go through all the way. Here I used the pliers to bend it a little and give me a little more bolt to work with. One bummer is the nut to attach the front fender was messed up and wouldn't let the bolt go through all the way. I couldn't tighten down the front fender and had to leave it off until I can get another nut. Note that the chain is not on the sprocket and you will have to guide that on while turning the crank. I was wearing gloves and got my finger caught multiple times, so be careful. I also had to adjust the front brakes, which had a bit of play. I did not have to adjust the derailleur like some others had to, but there are instructions for this included.

Once the bike was assembled and the tires pumped up I took a spin. It's a nice ride and feels solid. The shifting worked great. I'm not sure if I maybe need to adjust something, but it does feel like I'm sitting too close to the handlebars. This is odd because the deal with this bike is it's supposed to have a swept-back frame for comfort riding. Another thing is no matter how much I tighten the seat it seems to move while I'm riding. I will admit, I'm pretty heavy, so perhaps that has something to do with it. I'm a little bummed because there are some ugly stickers on the bike that do not come off easily. The pedals and sprocket are marked with L and R so you get the pedals on correctly, but these stickers just shred into tiny bits when you try to get them off. I'll have to take some compound to try and rub them off, and hopefully not mar the finish. There's also a big white "Warning" sticker and "Made in China" right on front of the downtube at the bottom. The kickstand seems a bit small and inadequate. Also it's very tight and squeaks when you kick it down or up. I gave it a shot of WD40, which helped a bit.

Overall I like the bike. It's not a cheapo budget bike, but doesn't cost much more than they do. It's pretty good value for a bike that doesn't cost upwards of $600. Being a large heavy rider further limits the choices, and this bike is pretty good so far. Time will tell.

UPDATE 4/20/2020: I'm still happy with the bike, but I'm finding it needs more adjustment than I originally thought. This is also turning out to be a bit of a nuisance. I am not a bicycle expert by any means, but YouTube videos explain everything one would want to know. I've been having real trouble adjusting the back brakes. They started rubbing and making noise, causing friction, etc. Adjustment should be fairly straightforward according to what I've learned, but no matter what I try it doesn't seem to help. I found out the barrel near the brake lever on the handlebars is bent. I'm not sure now if it was this way when I got the bike, or if something happened. I've been taking great care of the bike and I haven't dropped it on the ground or anything. Given that there was a problem with a different screw right out of the box, I think it was probably just bent to start with. The bottom line is inspect every part of the bike when you get it.

UPDATE July 2000: SixThreeZero customer service has been great. Through no fault of their own, they were forced to be less-than-responsive when I had troubles during the COVID-19 crisis. Since then they have reached out to follow up, and when they found out about my problems they reimbursed me for all I had spent to fix up my bike. I would recommend people inspect their bike when they get it to evaluate whether there are any damaged parts, but then contact 630 customer service as I'm sure they will make it right.

The bike is cheaply made; here's what I mean: 1) tires seem dried out and won't last long, 2) the adjustment screws for v-brakes are made of softest aluminum and strip easily, 3) the seat is hard and not comfortable, 4) it took quite a bit of adjustments to get the chain stop jumping in the rear, 5) the rear wheel is out of balance - it's not annoying to ride but you can feel it when cruising down hill. Review:
I did not buy my Jetson from Amazon, but directly from Jetson. I received the bike right on the day they said it would come. I had ordered a bike cover with it, and the storage compartment that goes over the second seat. I never received my bike cover, and the storage compartment was pretty cheaply made. I e-mailed Jetson the day I received my bike and let them know about the missing cover.

I got the bike ready, and charged up the battery. This took well over 6 hours not the stated 4. I rode the bike around for the weekend and it rides really nice. My eyesight is not the best, but the display is large enough for me to read, which is great. I think I got about 25 miles and the bike top speed was dropping from 20 mph, down to about 15. So I figured the battery must be needing a charge. I was not worried about getting 40 miles to the charge. I am quite a bit over 200 lbs and knew I would not get that range. I took the battery out and plugged it in to charge for the second time. The light on the charger went from green (stays this way until you plug it into the battery) to red (charging) and then after just a few seconds the light went out completely. I was worried that something went wrong and after an hour or so I checked the charger and the light was not on, and the charger was cool to the touch. It warmed up pretty good when it was charging the first time. After about 4 hours I wanted to see if it was charging at all and put the battery back in the bike. No charge at all. I called Jetson the next day (Monday) and let them know something was wrong. I did not hear back from them so I called them on Tuesday as well. Again on Wednesday, then a few days later and then again a few days after that. I have had the bike for over 2 months now and they have never returned my calls, or the e-mail I sent on the day I received it. I can never get in touch with a real person, just an answering machine.

I got so frustrated that I went on the web and found the charger (from one source in China) and ordered it. It was 53 dollars and I had to wait about 6 weeks for it to show up. I am riding the bike again. I like it, but I am pretty angry at the company. I will keep trying them to see if they will make things right. If they do, I will come and update my review.

I am updating my review on 11/16/13.

I was contacted by Jetson after this review, and they have made the situation right. After this review my bike had a catastrophic failure. (I think it was the motor, but am not sure) I let their rep know what happened via email and Jetson sent me another bike. They also reimbursed me for the charger I bought on my own, and for the bike cover that I never received from my original order. I am very happy that they did finally contact me and make my situation right, but am frustrated still that the whole thing took so long to get right.

Review:
There's so many good things about this bike but let me give you a major reminder that will address many of the negative reviews about this bike:

You need to have some mechanical knowledge of bikes in order to have ANY bike work well. If you don't know how to adjust a rear derailleur or rim brakes, then just be prepared to be frustrated with any bike that has gears or rim brakes...especially at this price.

Ok, with that out of the way, here's the review:

MECHANICAL:
Once you get the rear mech (derailleur) set correctly (meaning the chain is properly in line with the gears) it shifts great and stays in line well. 

The components are great, having a brand like Shimano is always a plus and great to see at this price

The twist shifter is a little firm, my 4 1/2 year old struggled at first to shift, but got the hang of it pretty quick and now has no problem shifting up and down.

The chain stays on well

SIZE:
The size is great, I think it does run a little small for a 20", but in our case that was a good thing because we wanted a bike for him to grow into.

ASSEMBLY:
Assembly was super easy. Ideally I wanted disc brakes to avoid the annoyance of having to regularly adjust the rim brakes, but it is what it is. Can't fault it too much considering the price.

UNIQUE FEATURE:
One of the BEST things about the bike, and honestly it has more to do with me being a mountain biker than my kid actually caring, is the cockpit setup. It has modern style handlebar and stem components just like what you would find on a modern mountain bike. This actually plays a big part in the control that the rider has. Classic/old bikes have the "Y" style handlebars that put the kids hands way above the frame of the bike. All this means is less control, but it's cheaper because they don't have to separately spec out a handlebar/stem that will fit a modern head tube. It might be a small feature, but it sets this bike apart.

OVERALL:
A solid, well performing bike that looks great: all at a great price.

MOST IMPORTANT:
My son loves it, and that's the main thing, right?

Review:
This bike is heavy as heck.

If you are just going to use it with training wheels, it's probably fine IF your kid can get it moving.

But for the beginner without training wheels it's just about the worst choice you can make for your kids.

Because it's so heavy (as heavy as an adult bike!) it's hard for the little ones to get it moving; learning is so much harder than it should be.

The handbreaks are horrible for kids. Absolutely unusable no matter how you adjust them.

Tip: Take off the pedals and let your kid get used to pushing this monstrosity around a bit before trying to learn to pedal.

BTW I thought schwinn was a good name but after a little googling I found out that they sold out to a canandian company that owns several Asian factories. If you think this is any better than a walmart $40 bike, then you thought wrong.

Review:
This was a gift to get someone I know into mountain biking.

As a seasoned rider and someone who is familiar with the bike industry, I can say that this bike (comp version) is a good value for the msrp. It shines where other recreational hardtails fall flat. While the top versions of similar bikes like the Giant Talon and Trek Marlin have air suspension forks, the Tyax comp makes up for with tubeless compatible wheels, which are costly to do after market. Pacific Cycles did cheap out on the non-tubeless wire-bead kenda tires, though.

The 4 stars comes down to quality control and incorrect info on Mongoose website for the technical specs. The bike I received has a mixture of Shimano Deore and SLX 11 speed drivetrain components where the website lists ALL slx componentry. In the industry, manufacturers reserve the right to spec bikes with similar OR BETTER componentry due to the possibility of supply issues. The spec of my bike a DOWNGRADE. The manufacturer product photos are outdated too.

As far as quality control, this comes to boxing up bikes and a problem that Mongoose factories have issues with. There is no hard protection over the rear derailleur when the bike is in the box, so my bike came with a frayed shifter cable and the paint rubbed off on the rear derailleur. I’ve bought other Mongoose bikes and have seen bent derailleur hangers, too.

Review:
While I have a mountain bike and a road bike, I had been considering getting something like this - basically a cyclo-cross bike.

It's intended to ride longer distances primarily on pavement, but allows you to go on dirt roads or trails. In this case, the 28" wheels give you a definite advantage over ruts and bumps, though the lack of a front shock will be a definite limit if you try to go on more than fast single-track.

The components are the 7-speed Shimano Tourney with 2 chainrings and 7 cogs for 14 speeds - range isn't bad, though the steps are a bit wide. Tourney is the absolute bottom level mountain bike component group in the Shimano hierarchy, but I was pleasantly surprised that it functioned smoothly. The issue with the lower component groups is durability, adjustability and repairability. As long as you aren't too rough on your bike, this is more than enough for the average recreational rider who might be doing a 10 mile ride a couple of times a month, or who is commuting 2-3 miles each way. While I ride a lot more, I have a more serious mountain bike exclusively for trails and a road bike for long distances, so this is more for riding around the neighborhood where there are several grass, dirt, and gravel paths to cut through.

The disc brakes are mechanical meaning that there is a cable going to the disc calipers instead of hydraulic tubes. While this is a less expensive version, I actually don't mind too much because while it doesn't work as well as the hydraulic systems, you can't blow a seal and lose all brake function either.

One thing I would have preferred is a proper seatpost allowing more tilt adjust - this would allow you to rotate the seatpost and move the seat farther forward . The standard seating position is fairly far back from the pedals which is a more relaxing riding position when riding on the tops of the handlebars, but I prefer to be more forward for less strain on my back when on the drops (the lower side of the handlebars) then

The front rack is very solid, and lugs are provided for a rear rack. The stow sack under the top tube is a bit gimmicky, but at least it doesn't
One of the trickier parts of assembling a bicycle is setting the brakes - with disc brakes however, that is a complete non-issue. Being pretty handy with bicycles it took me less than 20 minutes to set up, if you're not familiar, there are plenty of how to setup new bike videos on YouTube.

Review:
My previous bike was a mongoose ledge...around $200, but rugged and heavy. Since i primarily ride on the street, i wanted something a bit lighter/faster. This totally fits the bill.
The bike arrived nearly fully assembled. Just had to turn the front fork around, install the front wheel (quick release, so no biggie), handlebar and seat, then pumped up the tires.
Once assembled, i took it out for a spin. A few adjustments to the shifter/brake placement on the handlebars, and it was good to go. Gears shifted flawlessly, brakes were adjusted beautifully. This thing is a real pleasure to ride!
There are a few nice extra little touches...the bike has mounts for two water bottles, and rear cargo rack...pre-drilled, with the hex nuts. It also came with a small tube of black touch-up paint, and the invoice had a hand-written/signed note from the person who did the assembly/tuning (thanks, Mike!).
There is no kickstand, but i installed a Greenfield kickstand (285mm, black), and it fits perfectly...no hacksawing involved.
Overall, i'm thrilled so far. I haven't had a bike this nice before, and i now know what i've been missing. I'm gonna be putting a LOT of miles on this badboy.

Review:
I'm happy with this purchase. I bought it for my 8-year-old who wanted a bike with gears and handlebar brakes and has outgrown his single gear bike.

Overall, the assembly was easy. I didn't use the tool included with the bike and instead used regular tools. I find that makes things easier than trying to manage with a thin piece of metal that has unlabeled cutouts in place of wrenches. The hardware is metric, if that helps.

I appreciated that the brake lines were already attached and run; the brakes just had to be latched and adjusted. The gear line (it has one shifter) was also already attached and run, making assembly easier.

The difficult part was making adjustments, or rather, getting the adjustments right. I saw in other reviews people complaining about the brake levers being too far from the handle bar for a kid to reach the brakes. Those can be adjusted to be nearer the handlebar. Doing so is easy; however, you then have to adjust the associated brake itself so the tension isn't too tight or loose. That part is difficult because you have to loosen a nut, pull the brake line, and then tighten the nut without releasing the tension you just put in. It takes some trial and error, and I found using pliers to pull the brake line makes things a little easier.

Another note about the brake levers: they feel very flimsy when adjusted to be closer to the handlebar. I'm not sure what the lever is made out of (I think plastic), but that's not the issue: when adjusted close to the handlebar, the lever moves up and down at the joint and feels like it could snap off. I haven't had issues with it yet, but time will tell.

Review: I like to ride and explore my quiet little hamlet. After turning 62 this year and wanting to stay active, I decided to hang up my single gear cruiser and get this bike. After watching lots of YouTube reviews I chose this model the city cruiser. Delivery was very fast and it arrived in good condition. I did a thorough inspection and found no problems with the bike whatsoever all the welds were good and solid and the nuts and bolts were tightened well. I did notice the front shock didn’t have a preload adjustment which was some thing I saw when watching a YouTube video but I know manufactures change designs and specs all the time otherwise it was just as I expected. I took several test rides and each ride I discovered some things that needed to be tuned which is very normal for a bicycle. I tuned the derailer and also trued both the disc brake calipers. These two adjustments increased the efficiency of the bike dramatically. I am easily able to cruise 25 to 30 miles and still have a half my battery life left. This is using some peddle effort, but overall it’s a very nice well put together bike. I increased the number of modes for electronic drive from 3 to 5 which is easily done using the programming features of the electronic controller. I Tuned each mode to match the gear ratios I prefer when pulling through my neighborhoods, this will also greatly increase your battery efficiency. Honestly in the fastest mode I can’t keep up with the thing peddling like mad even in seventh gear. Top assist mode, that’s really for sprinting this thing really moves. GPS verified the 20 miles an hour without much pedal assistance. Thank you heybike for making a good product.

Review: I can't really comment on the bike in terms of what it would be like riding on rough trails.  I bought as a street bike for the fat tires as I figured they would be more stable on the road.  I like the bike as it is very attractive.  I had a bit of a problem adjusting the brakes and one problem putting it together.  As some folks noted in their review, the handlebars appear to be on backwards where the gear numbers are facing away from the rider.  However, they just need to be rotated so the numbers face the rider (see page 45 of owner's manual.)  They are probably put that way to protect the bike during shipment.  In order to rotate them, if you squat down, you will see the hex nut located in the middle of the handlebars when you are facing the bike.  The nut needs to be loosened with a 6mm hex tool.  The factory tightened it so much I couldn't get it loosened in order to make the adjustment.  The hex nut is round so you can't grab it with pliers, and I didn't want to strip the nut.  I had the idea to cover my brake/gear wires with a towel to protect them...then blast the nut with my blow drier about an inch away on the hottest temp for about 20 seconds or so.  It came right out so if you have the same problem, give this a try!

Review: Was looking for a complete race ready bike with quality components for $400-$600. After some shopping around and reading reviews this one seemed to be the best deal. It arrived quickly with no damage or missing parts. It’s mostly assembled and the rest of the assembly is rather easy with some basic tools most everyone has at home. After looking over the quality of the parts and taking It out on a few rides I’d say this is an excellent bike that’s basically ready to race right out of the box. I gave it a 4 but it’s probably more like a 4.5 but that’s not an option. The deduction is for 5 issues I had 4 of which are mostly minor complaints. 1st issue I had was the brake handle. It’s really long like you’d see on a motorcycle. Besides being a little unnecessary added weight it’s also something that could get bent or broken in a crash or perhaps caught on another rides pants or shirt in a close racing situation. It works fine but I will be replacing it. 2nd the seat pole is too short for casual street riding. It’s padded for street riding but way too short and at a funny angle. The should’ve either made it slightly longer or just saved putting padding on It that’s just more added weight that’s unnecessary. 3rd issue is the chain seems pretty flimsy and cheap. It works fine again and may be no issue for most people but it too will be replaced. 4th issue is being an aluminum frame they should’ve included chain tensioners. Any ruder that rides hard is going to have issues with the chain creeping up which could lead to a popped chain plus It will shred the hell out of the aluminum when It does. This was noted by many others and I resolved it by ordering the tensioners at the same time I ordered the bike. The 5th and last issue was the only one that I’d consider a major issue. The pedals that come with the bike are complete junk. They’re plastic and don’t spin freely at all. I was never going to use the stock pedal anyway as I have clipless pedals but nobody should use those pieces of junk. Just expect to buy pedals for this bike if you don’t already have an extra pair. They really should use a better set of metal platform pedals on a race bike designed for a larger rider. One other thing for taller riders the top tube on the Pro XL is only 21.25” instead of the usual 21.5” so if you’re over 5’11” you’ll probably want to go with the Pro XXL. Overall this is a great bike for this price definitely an incredible value and I would recommend It to anyone looking for a ready to race bike that won’t break the bank. Can buy It on the 6 month EZ pay plan for only around $90 a month which is what I opted to do. Hope the review wasn’t too long and was helpful.

Review:
The L500 is a really nice and solidly built BMX at a great price. It looks very sharp with the chrome finish, green accents and minimal branding (which makes it look a lot more like a high end botique BMX compared to the L100 and lower models).

The main upgrade over the L100 model is a freecoaster hub which allows you to ride backwards/fakie without backpedaling, so that is really only applicable to advanced riders who are capable of bunny hopping into a 180 and riding backwards. The other upgrade over L100 is a half-link chain which allows more precise adjustment of the rear wheel position in the dropouts (but many riders prefer half-link chain because of how it looks more than caring about the adjustability factor)

So while the L500 model is great and I would recommend it to advanced riders who prefer a freecoaster hub.. the L100 model is definitely the best value and probably a better choice for most people.

Out of the box, it is mostly pre-assembled and really easy to put together even if you don't know much about working on bicycles, all you will need is a 5 mm and 6 mm hex wrench. That said, with this and any other new bike that's been shipped there are always going to be issues with the setup out of the box that will require more knowledge to fix, such as adjusting the brakes and truing the rims.. so if you don't know what you're doinig its best to take it to a local shop and pay them $50 or so to assemble and set it up.

My L500 weighs 25.2 lbs fully assembled (I replaced the included nylon pedals with metal pedals, so stock weight would have been slightly lower).

Review: Imagine this… you just ordered this bike for your daughter, it arrives days later, as you sit around the dinner table with your family. But what’s this? You have to assemble it. Typically not a hard accomplishment. Not this time! Not a single bolt is tightened or piece is assembled. You have to get up from dinner, collect 100 pieces of the bike, leave your home, and work several years through get your engineering degree. After graduating, you have to continue your education specifically for bike assembly. The bike parts have been gathering dust for several years and your family is all but a memory. Finally the day comes, you’ve received a PhD in Bike assembly. You once more collect the pieces of the disassembled bike and finally part-by-part it comes together. A fully functioning bike fit for your child! You go to reconnect with your since removed family, roll the bike to the front door, knock, and your daughter answers. Just the person you needed to deliver this bike. Problem is she’s 20 years old now, with an iPhone and a boyfriend covered in biker tattoos. She doesn’t want a bike meant for a kid. You’ve taken years of your life and lost your family all for this bike. Moral of the story, it’s worth taking the 20 minutes to drive to Walmart to buy an already assembled bike - trust me. However much you don’t want to drive to the store now, it’ll be worth not going through the frustration when it arrives. But now that I got it put together, it seems like an okay bike.

Review:
For folks familiar with how to put bikes together, assembly is pretty easy... not much to do. I did end up having to tune up the brakes and shifter just a bit to get them to work smoothly.

My daughter learned to bike early, riding a kids 16" BMX-style bike for her first few years... it was okay, but very heavy and no gears of course. We couldn't go far before she was tired. We then graduated to more of a beach-cruiser bike, which was 20" and slightly smoother and easier to pedal, but still heavy. When she started riding this 24" bike, the difference was remarkable. It's so light, and the geometry is more like a fitness or road bike which allows her to really go fast and to have a lot better endurance.

She even likes to take it on smooth dirt trails and gravel roads, and the medium-sized tires are an excellent compromise of efficiency for long road rides and cushion for rough roads. Really an excellent, excellent kids bike... the best I have ever seen, honestly. And I've raised 3 kids on bikes.

Review: [[VIDEOID:266582a634b6daccdaf286adfce76aa7]] I love this bike so much and highly recommend making this purchase. As a 5'3” woman, it still fits me fine with the seat all the way down. Anyone taller would definitely just fit better. Totally a one-size-fits-all with the adjustable seat! I was looking for something that had the option to be an all-electric bike, all manual with gears, or pedal assist. This bike has it ALL. It's exactly what I wanted. On the pedal-assist option, there are 5 speeds … watch out, the fastest one FLIES! On the all-electric option, you can control how much “help” you want with the throttle or just hold down the throttle for an effortless ride. On the manual bike option, you can shut off all the electronic controls and shift between 7 gears. Other features I love are the loud car-like horn, the insanely bright lights for night riding, the INCREDIBLY LONG battery life, and the comfy, cushy seat. I tested this bike out on a 10-mile mountain trail for one of my first rides. I'll be honest; it's probably more of a road bike. But through rocks, leaves, mud, dirt, sand, water, huge roots, and very steep slopes, this bike held up beautifully. (The few slips I had were user errors!) It doesn't take too long to charge and I'd guess it would probably take a day of riding to completely drain, although I haven't drained it to zero battery yet. The bike is so beautiful… the brown leather handlebars are very comfortable to grip, and the rack on the back is a great extra touch too. In summary, I can't think of enough good things to say about my bike ! For half the price of some of its counterparts, it's just as good, and a really amazing, high-quality e-bike! Lastly, it shipped quickly and was easy to assemble. Must BUY THIS BIKE

Review:
I don't write reviews often. However, I had to here. I took a chance on this product and so far, so good! I'll try to write my review and answer some questions along the way.
I love this bike! It looks good, easy to assemble and rides very smooth. I thought it might be too tall since its a 29er. But it fits me perfect ( I'm 5'10" - 5'11" on a good day). Assembly was a breeze, however I did take it to a local bike shop for them to go through it, make some final adjustments (brakes mostly) and true the wheels, which already looked pretty good. This cost about $100. But it was well worth it to get the bike to 100% before riding. I do not baby mountain bikes so it needed to be 100%!! I went right to some local trails on its inaugural run. About a 7 on a 10 scale, and the bike performed flawlessly! Now it should be mentioned, I did make a few aftermarket upgrades. I bought a Daway gel seat (with rear safety lights), Mzyrh. Mountain Bike pedals, Fifty-Fifty grips and a water bottle holder (screw and mounts already in frame). Since I was doing all this, I also got a new Base Camp helmet with removable sun visor. All these upgrades including helmet were right around $150. So that's bike $300, upgrades $150 (all bought through Amazon) and full nut & bolt tune-up $100, to give a total custom bike cost of $550 out of pocket. I'm sorry, but you're just not going to beat that!! I can't articulate properly the performace of this bike. The gears shift and respond excellently, the front shocks travel well and one other thing, the bike is pretty damn light too!! It weighs in right at 35 lbs. Not too bad!!
Overall, this is an awesome bike! I hope it holds up. If so, Vilano will definitely make a name for themselves! Good luck and I highly recommend this purchase!

Review:
[[VIDEOID:ea36d6a4ce03ea47ff51f6c9f82c7f25]] Overall, the bike looks gorgeous. The sharp looks are honestly a large part of why I picked this, and I was not disappointed to see it in person. The paint has a sparkly pearl white finish underneath a very uniform clear coat, with no runs or imperfections. I expected the accent trim to be simple black decals, but even this is embedded into the paint job under the clear coat. Everything seemed to be assembled very well, with no problems out of the box. The LED headlights and taillights are very bright, and make for a great night riding experience, helping you to be seen on the road. The horn, while a bit ridiculous sounding, is usually good enough to warn pedestrians on walking trails.

I found the battery life to be excellent, provided I was not traveling up steep hills. With the stock gear ratio, pedaling alone gets you to around 7-8mph, which is a bit too slow for my liking. It’s enough to help the motor up hills and when accelerating, but it’s difficult to use primarily as a bicycle, as it feels like a mountain bike stuck in first gear. The motor generally gets me up to around 13-14mph. This is a bit shy of the Amazon listing’s claim of 18mph, but a quick check with the wheel off the ground confirms that the speed is limited by the computer to 25kph, or around 15mph on my unit. The speedometer has no unit conversion, so you’re stuck with a readout in kilometers. The bike also claims to have three distinct modes, but I’ll be darned if I could tell any difference between them. All three modes provided the exact same amount of pedal assistance and allowed me to use throttle in addition.

I’m fairly tall at around 6ft, and found the seat height to be far too short. I wound up ordering a taller seat post, which made the bike feel much better to ride. I also modified my bike with an aftermarket crankset, swapping the 44t front sprocket out with a 56t one, allowing me to pedal up to around 13-14mph, more closely matching the motor’s ability and making the bike feel much more balanced. To hack the speedometer to show MPH, I found it’s possible to open the programming mode by holding the mode button while turning on the bike. While there’s no unit conversion option, you can change the wheel size it uses to calculate speed. The KPH to MPH conversion factor is .621, so 14” wheel *.621 is around an 8.69” wheel. I changed P2 (wheel size) to 8 and P3 (offset) to 1.08, which gets pretty close to the correct reading in MPH.

Ultimately, if you’re looking for a really good seated electric scooter, I would highly recommend this, as it’s wonderful for that purpose. As a bike, the limited gear ratio makes it a bit limited while pedaling unless you modify the bike with a larger chainring sprocket. I think the pedals were meant to help get it started, and then used as footpegs the rest of the ride. The inclusion of functional pedals means this may be legally classified as a bicycle, making it road legal in many states and therefore it could be ridden in areas where scooters may be banned or frowned upon. I hope Sailnovo can make an improved version, with more user configurable options in software, a higher gear ratio, and an included taller seatpost in the box.

Review:
Buyer Beware before purchasing . Battery is "Wartime New Energy Co. Ltd" with a model number of "WT0230" Search for replacement battery before purchasing 48v-10A model T-276 27&1/2 inch TotGuard ebike. ( I am still Looking for a replacement Battery . ( I wonder if battery style is even available on the market and if seller knows it is not ? ). Battery Information should of been posted upfront on sellers page ( -1 star for lack of important information on "Advertisement Page"). The Computer for selecting power assist reads in highest power assist at 39.8mph while the same speed traveling in mid power assist selection reads it at 22mph. Big discrepancy in the reading of traveling speed. ( No star deduction for this as seller does provide a PDF file that attempts to explain how to set the computer control of the bike. Thank You Seller for this important information. ) . Front Forks are leaking. Like a motor cycle or any other motorized Bike, this posses a serious health risk to the rider. ( I contacted the seller and awaiting a response ). If the battery does not remove easily , make sure you turn the key counter clockwise to disengage the lock. You may have to step on pedal and use both hands to lift and pull the battery upwards towards the front of the bike. This is due to the mounting plate not being properly set in the plastic holder by the manufacturer before shipping to be sold. the mounting plate can be adjusted up or down by loosening the three Philips screws ( first check for marks on the mounting plate where the locking pin is binding. I have not adjusted mine yet, but I see marks where it looks like this plate was used or screwed on wrongly and then screwed on properly afterwards ) . I am wondering if the Brake switches are even wired in as when I engage the brakes , the motor continues to push me forward for 2 seconds before disengaging ( Yes I stop pedaling when I engage the brakes ) . I am looking over the PDF files to see if there is adjustments in the computer for that, just in case the wires are actually connected and it be the computer not set properly . I will update my review about Seller's response on bad front forks and if and when I can find a replacement battery. as for the PDF instructions I need to read more concerning the computer settings. over all though, it does not seem like a bad bike.

Update :
Seller Responded quickly within 24 hours. Leaky Forks is just excess grease packed around the Sweeps of the forks. I would suggest to anyone that has never rebuilt the forks on their bike to watch some videos on it. At some point the seals are going to need replacing along with replacing of the oil inside . Although I am still surprised that the sweeps allow grease to slip by. If this was a motor cycle I would be very concerned. The PDF the Seller provides on the lower part of the page was handy for setting up the computer .
I find the front drive train to be a little to small when in power assist . I prefer to pedal a little slower with applying a little more leg power at a steady pace. So I will be looking to change out the front drive train with larger. I Noticed 2 days ago that the TotGuard Ebike official site was completely gone ( that is a bit concerning, So no help support can be relied upon by TotGuard.)

Update #2:
Ok just gave up looking for information about the parts used on this bike. It is suppose to have a Shimano drive train , but the only things marked shimano are the derailleurs and shifting levers. No Shimano markings what so ever on the the front Chain rings that I can find or back chain ring cassette. Brakes are another thing which there is no markings on what the brakes are in order to determine which brake pads fit it . Battery is still a mystery . Review:
I haven’t been on a bike in over 20 years. I’m 38 now and trying to get in more exercise. I searched all over for a 10 speed style bike and they were pushing $350.00 just for something decent- and that’s just for a manual pedal style bike! I happened to stumble upon this and I was a little skeptical at first about having a motor/electric..batteries etc. but after watching a handful of YouTube reviews on this bike, I was sold. I couldn’t pass it up especially for the price!

The bike came packaged extremely well and it was overall easy to assemble. Weighs in about 40ish-lbs. It sounds a little much, but when you pick it up- it’s not bad at all. Only took about 45 mins to secure everything in it’s place. My packaging included a manual but if yours doesn’t, there are YouTube videos out there of people putting this bike together. I found those extremely helpful! I would recommend someone to assist you if possible. It’s a little awkward trying to assemble by yourself. The bike does come 90% assembled and they provide some tools to help assemble it. Just need to attach the handlebars, front tire, headlamp, secure the battery and pedals-add some air to the tires then off you go! The frame is incredibly well made and really solid! I made a few tweaks to the brakes so they don’t grab too quick and tight. You don’t want to be thrown over the handlebars! Also, take your time aligning the front tire. Make sure it’ spins freely without the caliper rubbing against the brakes. That took the most time for me, but yet again I’m a newbie and there was probably an adjustment I could’ve made to make it easier.

I’m pretty tall, 6’3 so I bought a $15.00 gooseneck extension here off Amazon to help raise up the handlebars so I’m not hunched over when riding. If you’re over 6ft tall, I highly recommend it! Also, the seat on the bike is well made but not very wide, or much padding for the butt. I would definitely recommend upgrading to a wider gel padded seat for a more comfortable ride.
The front suspension shocks can be locked/unlocked and has a sensitivity adjustment! I keep it unlocked so it absorbs the impact of bumps.
Handle grips are comfortable and have a nice contour to them!
All terrain, well made tires!
I read some reviews that the tires are not quality. I disagree! It states on the instructions that the inner tubes hold up to 60psi of pressure. BAD BAD IDEA! DO NOT DO THAT! You will blow the inner tubes if you go that high. When you pump up the tires just go slow and squeeze into them until they are firm. Sit on the seat and test it with your full body weight until they are not flattening to the ground. That’s it. No need to over think it.
The gears, and crank system on the bike are name brand Shimano. Might not be top of their line, but it’s a name brand quality piece! The gears function overall really smoothly. It might need a slight adjustment? But having owned this for three months I haven’t had any issues at all with the gear shifting. I only use a few gear settings and that’s it. If you’re ever unsure, don’t risk your safety. Take it to your local bike shop just to make sure everything is functioning properly!

Performance wise, the bike is amazing! I’ve put 47 total miles on it! The indicator screen shows that the battery is still fully charged! Granted, I only mainly keep the pedal assist on one(when it’s needed) I will turn the pedal assist off when it’s not needed just to conserve the battery life. I haven’t hit a bunch of big hills on the bike- just around town/small inclines and declines. On level three, this baby packs a punch up to 21mph. For a bicycle, that’s pretty fast!! Don’t underestimate it, this thing takes off quick!

There are two ways to use the motor/assist. The throttle on the handlebars and the pedal assist. On level one- start pedaling and you’ll feel the motor kick in and get you up to about 6mph. Once you hit the brakes it kills the motor assist which is nice. I keep the assist on level one(the lowest level) for working up small inclines. Still gives your legs the ability to pedal without burning yourself out. If you want to speed up the hill without pedaling, just click up to the second level notch. Use the throttle on the handle bars and you’ll zip up that hill with no problem at all. Also, it’s great if you just want to cruise the streets for a joyride without any peddling. Note: when you’re on level two & three just plan on not pedaling if you’re on flat pavement. You might be able to pedal if you’re going up a steep hill? But it’s best to just use the throttle on the handle bars when riding on flat terrain.

The screen is super easy to use and easy to read. All the controls are laid out well and everything is easily accessible within a thumbs reach. Display screen lights up at night, the battery life, mph, trip distance, total distance, and a few others that I haven’t yet explored.
I’m surprised how loud the horn beeps, and how bright the headlight is! I primarily ride at night and it provides plenty of light to see!

Lastly, the charging dock is well made. The battery came practically fully charged. I did give it an hour of charge after unboxing it just to make sure all works properly. Nice battery life indicator light on the battery that shows green, yellow & red. Not sure how long it takes to charge a dead battery, but I think I remember reading it takes around 6 hours?

So in closing, I guess the only dislike I have about the bike is the name, Tot Gaurd. Kinda tacky…doesn’t make sense. So I found a couple stickers with a Mountain on them and covered up the name. I also added a few decals to the battery just to give it a little personalization. Perfect-o!!
The only real concern I have is riding this bike in the rain, or on really wet roads. I’d be afraid of the water affecting the display screen, battery and motor. As you know, electronics and water don’t mix so to be on the safe side, I wouldn’t ride it on rainy days. Second, I’m not too sure in regards to customer support and warranty? My best guess would be to go through Amazon as a chain of communication with the manufacturer. Aside of the gooseneck extension and bike seat being a little on the small side-it’s a short coming that every other bike has out of the box even at the 1k price points.

For the money, this bike is worth every penny.
5 stars!
Buy it!
You’ll love it!

Be free & ride on!

Review:
I purchased this bike a year ago.

My advice is have a bike shop tune the bike up immediately when you get it. I got a $35 Groupon bike shop tuneup special, and the bike has not required any further adjustment -- shifts are positive and sure.

Once the bike did try to fold up on me when I was riding it -- I think the fold lock mechanism could be designed better; it has a plastic part that is supposed to lock the mechanism in place. I was not hurt and the bike was not damaged -- I apparently did not quite completely lock it -- but it was a weird experience, like, what is happening here! If you take care to completely lock it, you shouldn't have a problem. For positive locking, I also put a velcro strap around the lock mechanism to hold the lock arm in place, just to be sure.

I kept this bike in my office -- it will fit underneath a table -- and rode it for exercise at lunchtime on occasion. The Schwalbe "Big Apple" tires quite often need more air, so get a hand air pump. The tires are great for urban riding situations (potholes, uneven pavement, etc.).

I had the optional Dahon fenders, front and rear, installed professionally (I probably could have installed them, but just don't have the time). They work well. Nice to know that fenders from Dahon are available should you later decide you need them. Another rear fender option are very cheap rear fenders that clamp to the post. On a folding bike the finders get hit and pushed a lot, so keep that in mind.

I commute by commute train (BART), and this bike can be taken aboard BART trains anytime, even during commute hours.

I have absolutely no regrets with this purchase.

Review:
The bike:
This appears to be a great little bike. Solid construction, the 7 speed…

7? Yeah, the listing says 6, and I expected twist shifts, but this is a 7 speed index (“trigger”) shift bike.

Where was I? The Shimano 7 speed indexed shifter on this bike is SO superior to the twist shifts available on lower end bikes. My little guy is thrilled at the upgrade. The brake levers are clearly lower end, but they get the job done.

Packaging:
Double packed in two separate cardboard boxes, the bolts on the sides of the wheel STILL managed to poke through the packaging. No apparent damage, but what is going on on the way that they can’t yet manage to avoid destroying bike boxes?

Instructions:
In 2018 I attended a full week of resident training on bicycle maintenance. Worth every minute. I have the tools, I have the training. For me, assembly was trivial - not thanks to the manual, but despite it.

Without my training, I’m afraid that the manual provided - which is intended to cover the assembly of ANY Diamondback bike, mountain, hybrid, kid, adult, etc. - would be about worthless. I’m picturing a single mom trying to give her kid a great bike and finding herself completely out of her depths. All the info is there, but there’s so much extra info in it that it makes it hard to determine exactly what’s relevant unless you already know what you’re doing. And if you already know what you’re doing you don’t need the manual.

Conclusion:
I’d recommend this bike to a friend for their kid. Then I’d assemble it for him/her or recommend they take it to a bike shop to put it together.

Review:
After four month of ownership and 500 miles, the bike has proven to be reliable. I am using the bike throughout the week as commuter bike for the 9 miles (one way) back and forth to the office, and on weekends for fun, or "biking" smaller errands. The bike came damaged (back reflector was pushed into the fender, see pic), but that was rather caused by the delivery company and not TOTEM USA. The customer service was professional and helped fast. They even send me some smaller LED lights for free, with the free spare part delivery. The fenders are a gift on rainy days. I was able to achieve the 75 miles range with one charge, by using the pedal assistant on the road and in the park to up to level 3 pedal assistance. Range depends also on weight, wind, uphill......
So, why just 4 stars? I have a hard time getting a cargo back rack, and TOTEM does not offer one at this moment of time. Commuter bikes needs a cargo rack, and I ended up buying a rack from Walmart. It will be replaced when Totem will offer the long promised rack. The assembly manual is rather challenging. The kickstand was almost lost because the screws where not torqued. Check all screws!
Overall I would buy the product again.

Review:
Overall, very good bike for the price. A couple things worth mentioning that are consistent with other reviews:
-The shipping packaging was almost non-existent; it is just a bike and a box, so depending on the carrier, some parts may be bent.
-I was missing the quick release bolt for the front wheel, but after calling Mongoose, they mailed me a new one no questions asked.
-If the wheel turns ~30 degrees, your toes will probably hit the tire while peddling unless you have the ball of your foot exactly on the center of the pedal. This seems common with 29" tires, but it seems like they could make the frame an inch or two longer so it isn't a problem... After some getting used to, it isn't much of a problem.
-Even though it is aluminum, it isn't a particularly light bike
-Even though it comes partly 'assembled', it is 0% adjusted, and you will need to fully adjust calipers/brakes/lines front and back, as well as all gearing. The manual is generic to all Mongoose bikes, so you need to find the section specific to yours. Once adjusted, everything works properly, but plan on taking a few hours to reach that point.

Review: I bought two of these: this one, and another one (purple). It seems unnecessary to review both of them but I might because I hate this bike so much. It was easy to assemble, which was great, and then within a week of light use it started falling apart. My kids are new to bike riding so they aren't doing any daredevil jumps or anything, just up and down and around the driveway. Week after week, I have had to put the chain back on and rebuild the pedal assembly. The problem is that the pedal assembly is extremely poorly made, doesn't snugly fit in the housing, and so it wobbles and the whole thing falls apart. My kids both have gotten hurt over and over as well. At this point, after re-building many times, the nuts are starting to strip. The company has sent me a replacement interior collar, as one of the ones in my daughter's bike was missing a ball bearing. But it doesn't matter, the replacements break as well. Compounding the problem, many of the parts are riveted rather than screwed on, so you can't really even take it apart easily. It's a cheap piece of junk and not worth the money or time invested in rebuilding it over and over again. And meanwhile my kids are constantly coming in upset that their bikes are broken again.

Review:
I was new to biking, but I tend to "go hard" on most things and this bike was a perfect starting point! It's much more than a beginner bike and I'd say it covers beginner to intermediate and can even keep up with my friends $2,000 bike (we've done 50+ mile rides). I put over 1,000 miles on it and counting. I do recommend taking it in to a LBS to have them check it out even though it pretty much came put together and only required putting on the seat, pedals, handlebar, and adjusting the brakes a bit. Was a first time working with bikes for me and it took me 30 min..

Upon taking it in to a LBS for a safety checkup they noticed the headset was too tight, the loosened it, tightened it, and just couldn't get it to where it had no play. They advised me what to say to the Tommaso warranty people and Tommaso sent me new headset bearings no charge at all.

Overall great value, worth the money, worth getting this over over bikes priced $0 - $1,000. Actually has the same components as other bigger brand bikes in the $1,000-$1,200 range.

That said, the quickest upgrades to make or prioritize: new seat, new wheels & tires, and of course clips if you want to start pushing into the intermediate space.

Review:
We received both of our bikes yesterday after about a week of shipping time, which is reasonable considering it does contain batteries. Both were very well packed and protected and in VERY large boxes!

The unpacking was very easy, although the assembly was a bit more challenging. There were a few times where the instructions weren't terrible specific and we had to puzzle things together, but in the end it all made sense. It took about an hour to put together the first back and no more than a half hour for the second one.

I would definitely recommend keeping some pictures of the finished product on-hand so you can refer to it just to make sure everything is facing in the right direction.

Once they were assembled and inflated, they worked like a charm! The electric motor has a decent kick to it without being overpowered, the pedal action and gear shifting is nice and smooth, and the control module was very easy to manage without being confusing at all.

Folding it was extremely easy as well. Just lift up a lever, pull out, and it folds in half easily. You can carry an allen wrench with you to rotate the handlebars as well if you need additional room, but we were able to fold and store both bikes under our staircase with little to no trouble at all, taking up far less space than the two fully assembled bikes would.

There is a decent amount of weight to the bikes, probably about 45-50 pounds without the batteries. We're having a hitch installed on our car so that we can carry them around due to the weight, but they certainly aren't unmanageable by any means (and I'm hardly in great shape - hence getting the bikes in the first place!).

If you're looking to experience a Class 2 electric bike I would definitely recommend this one. The price was right, the weight limit is very welcoming to those of us who are stockier/heavier, and the riding experience is an absolute joy. Assembly was a bit confusing the first time around but once it's together it's very easy to figure out and use, and can be a great gateway bike to serious bicycling!

As a side note: Look into whether your area has any Rides-To-Trails. I highly recommend them!

Review:
This is a so-so bike and will be more than adequate if you just want to ride around a little bit on very level roads/paths, etc. The steel frame is very heavy but does definitely does make for a much smoother ride than a lighter weight aluminum or dare I say it, carbon bike. If you want a good liking retro bike, worthy of riding around Nantucket, with a cute basket affixed to the front or on top of the pre-installed rear rack, and you're ONLY riding around for a few miles on flat roads, then this is the bike for you. HOWEVER, and this is a huge however, you don't even want to be in this bike's zip code if there are any moderate hills. Not to brag here but I'm relatively fit, I bike 50 to 60 miles daily on the weekends - not on this bike, bike 12 miles round trip to my Power yoga or gym class daily on the weekdays (this is to say I LOVE working out) but this bike and hills brings a sigh and groan to me every time. Notice the HUGE chain ring on the front - yep, that's what's going to get you (or in this case not get you) up those hills.

PROs:
-best booking retro bike on the street and color is spot on like in the pictures
-nice that they already had the rear rack and most of the bike assembled
-nice upright seat position for leisurely rides
-perfectly fine - not great - for what you're paying. I wanted to try a steel bike and I thought what do I have to lose for $200?

CONs
-#1 - see above about the hills. This 40 lb behemoth is really, really heavy. So the single chain ring, 7 speed bike coupled with 40 lbs makes for an unpleasant ride on anything but the straightest of paths. You'll kill your knees with this one going up hills.
-A lot of the components sound ok on the website but in person they seem very "cheap." My chain already makes this rhythmic weird noise every time I spin through one cycle, the brakes squeal ALOT when descending and the shifters are very, very tight. It gets easier to shift with time but it's still takes more wrist strength then it should. And don't even get me started on the bell - what a waste of metal.
-Kickstand is a joke - I'm always stopping my bike from toppling over.

NOTES
-I assembled the bike on my own and took it into the shop for an issue w/the steering column and the a brake check. Expect to pay $50 or more for his kind of check and even more if you're not going to assemble on your own. I'm glad I took this in for a check as he noticed one of the brake cables was unattached and brakes need to be adjusted - mine were crooked.
-Seat is just OK -there are much more comfortable seats but the tan color looks great with the bike and I love the fact that it's not leather

In summary, for $219 and with Free S&H, this bike seems okay. It's a great starter bike if you're just trying to get your toes wet with biking or you want to bike around for just a little bit around your neighborhood with the flat streets and sidewalks. And you do get the included rear rack which you can probably put on your next bike. If I were to do it again though, I would pay a more and buy a bike in person with the potential for a return or exchange. I would return this bike - although I'm past the 30 days - it took me forever to unpackage and assemble - but I don't think the return shipping would be worth it.

Review:
While Firmstong's web site says this bike will accept training wheels (20 inch), that's not correct out of the box. The rear axle is too short to be able to add them. Firmstrong was little help in trying to find a solution to this problem, so I came up with my own.

I also purchased the Wald training wheel set at the same time I purchased this bike. The training wheel supports are 3/8 inch thick and the support bracket is about 1/8 inch thick. I took the rear axle nut to the hardware store and was able to determine that the size and thread count for the rear axle is 3/8 inch 24 TPI (fine threads.) Armed with this information I bought a 1 inch coupling nut of the same size and thread count (its one inch long and open at both ends) along with a 3/4 inch hex head bolt of the same size and thread count, plus two lock washers. To install the training wheels I removed the original nut and washer from the axle and put a lock washer, the coupling nut, the training wheel bracket, a locking washer and the hex head bolt. Because of the length of the coupling nut, I wasn't able to install the training wheel horizontal supports, but the whole assembly is tight enough that it shouldn't be an issue. It should be sufficient for my granddaughter to learn to ride safely. As an alternative, the 3/8 inch axle extensions sold on Amazon should work if they have the same 24 TPI thread count.

I can fit both in the back of my car (pic posted) and I read somewhere that the bikes were not geared and they would not go very fast, but they were not really built for that.  In response I've also posted a video below (NOTE:  the speedometer is probably not calibrated quite correct, my GPS watch showed the speed 3-5 miles/hour slower.  Most of the speed was picked up downhill but it was moving 10-20 mph before starting down hill.  Follow all safety guidelines in the manual and wear protective gear).  Overall excellent bike for the price, I would recommend both, if you have $100 extra get the VIA.

Review:
I bought this Bike about a month ago and have ridden it numerous times. Generally speaking I think its a good bike. But I am 5'8" tall and I have a hard time swinging my leg over the seat and cross bar to get on it.
When coming to a stop I have fallen twice. I have lowered the seat about as far as it will go and that has rectified the problem but the seat is so low that when I pedal my leg does not fully extend as it should which makes the ride a bit uncomfortable. The seat on this bike is small and narrow and very uncomfortable. I had to replace the seat. The handle bar grips on the handlebar are hard rubber with a very rough surface that hurts your hands after a few minutes. I have had to replace the grips. The bike comes with no fenders and back rack. For the price of the bike (over $1000), you would think they'd add fenders and a rack. I've had to add a rear carriage rack. There are no rear view mirrors. I have had to add them. There is no explanation ib the manual that comes with the bike that explains the two gear gauges that are on the handle bar. The bike has a rough ride, not smooth at all. Front shock absorbers don't do much to lessen the bump you feel going over small objects . But the worst ting bout this bike is customer service. I have written an email to the company and left to phone messages stating that I had questions and they have not responded back to me.
If you want to return this bike after you have assembled it, you are in trouble. Their return policy basically says that the bike has to be returned in the same box wrapped pretty much the same way they shipped it which is really impossible to do. The return policy states several ways they will deduct from your refund if the return does not meet all their requirements. If I had it to do over, I would not buy this bike. I would get a step through bike that had all the items on it that I mentioned do not come with this bike. One that had a thorough operating manual and a more reasonable return policy.

Review:
I bought this bike as my first road bike, and I mainly chose it because of the price. The bike looks great. It takes a fair amount of set-up for brakes and gears adjustment, unless you're very used to working on bikes, but I just set aside a couple hours and didn't have much trouble with it. Compared to the mountain bike I had before, it's very light (though surely on the heavier side for road bikes)and rides fast. After a few days of riding, the gears settled and the gears started jumping quite a bit around the middle gears, especially when climbing. After an adjustment, this was fixed but is starting to happen again after about a month of riding, along with the front brake becoming a bit maladjusted. Seems like this bike will obviously need a more constant rate of upkeep than a more expensive bike to keep it running smooth, especially on the gears. But for the price? If you're looking for your first road bike and this is around the budget you have, get this bike. Great value for the price.

p.s. I'm 6'1" and the medium bike (gold and black) is the perfect size.

Review: After more than two months of waiting, we finally received this bicycle in a package. Assembling is pretty straight forward, the rear wheel,  3 x 7 speed change mechanism, front and rear brakes have already been pre-assembled. All I need to do was to assembly the handle bar, front wheel, paddles, rear rack and seat to the main frame. Only challenge is the handle bar adjustment; For shipping the handle bar was folded to central column to save space, so it needs to be restored to riding position. I need to loosen the hex socket bolt at T joint, rotate the handle bar and re-tighten the bolt. However, this bolt is REALLY tight, I have to use a cheater bar to loosen it. Other than that, The rest of assembly is rather painless. As usual, I adjusted front wheel bearing gap, brake pad position and brake handle travels, applied WD-40 to all the cables and joints, which did make brake and speed shifting lighter / easier. Speed shift mechanism works fine, however, you should not expect the same fluid ride, brake and speed shift quality as more expensive bicycles. It does provide decent and comfortable ride. Our teenager daughter really loves it and enjoys riding it with her friends.

After having the bike for a few months, I think it is far to review now. When I first bought the overdrive, it was pretty easy to assemble overall. However, I did take it to a bike shop to ensure I did everything correctly. Better safe than sorry. I took it out to a few local trails and parks with no issue. And after taking it to a more advance trail, I had a medium sized fall and broke some components. If you look at my photos you can see the day I got it versus a more recent photo.

Overall, as long as you stick to more basic trails and stay away from more technical areas, this bike is great! Good frame, great value, and aesthetically pleasing. Once you hit more advance single track trails, jumps, etc., you may need to upgrade a few parts. A great set of new tires would be first and foremost (stock tires are not very grippy)

Diamondback created a bike that give you the ability to grow. So if you want to dip your feet in the shallow end first, this maybe a great option for you.
