Review:
Review for HiBoy P7:
I am very happy with this eBike overall and I would highly recommend it. It came well packaged in a very strong box so there were no issues with damage in shipment. Assembly was easy and the only adjustment I had to make other than seat height was on the front brake because of some rubbing. The bike is well made with nice welds and a high quality finish. The battery is integral to the frame which gives the bike a very nice look like eBikes that are much more expensive.
My first test ride was about 15 miles on level and hilly paved roads. What I found was that I could not go faster than 18.1 MPH in gear 9 on level roads in P5, even if I stood up and pedaled as fast as I could. It was the weekend, so I waited until Monday to contact HiBoy support via phone. After calling and getting a recording, I got a call back as promised in the recording and the person nicely told me that he couldn’t help me directly with technical issues with the bike- his only function was to direct me to contact support via email. That was not very efficient, but I have to say, once I contacted HiBoy support via email, they were very responsive.
I was contacted back via email overnight by Kaya from the support team. Since the emails are coming from China, I typically received them between 9pm and 5am EST. Kaya said that the controller was limiting the speed to 18 MPH, (which is 28 Km/ hr), and he would send me a new one to remove the speed limit. He did not explain why the first controller was programmed to limit the speed to 18 MPH when the Amazon listing stated in multiple places that the top speed would be 28 MPH. Maybe there was confusion over kilometers vs miles per hour?
I received the new controller in about 10 days. Kaya had kindly made and sent an instructional video so it was easy to install the new controller in about 15 minutes.
I took the same test ride route and the performance was dramatically different. Before, it felt like I was riding an Exercycle when I reached 18 MPH, now I was able to reach 21.5 MPH in P5 when pedaling normally on level road and 23.5 MPH when pedaling hard. The battery level also remained higher than with the previous controller. This is OK for my purposes, but I contacted Kaya because the Amazon listing stated I should be able to reach 28 MPH.
As always, Kaya replied promptly overnight and instructed me to perform a test which involved operating the bike in throttle only mode and then moving the pedals with the rear wheel in the air in P5. I reported back that I was able to reach 23.4 MPH with throttle only and 29 to 30 MPH while moving the pedals. Although Kaya replied back that the engineers said that was within specifications, I believe their description on Amazon should be changed to reflect “real world” speed on level road for person of average weight, (165 lbs)- that is, 23 MPH. The only way to reach 28 MPH would be on a downhill stretch, so I think it is a little disingenuous to state otherwise and it only serves to give a customer unreasonable expectations. That being said, I am perfectly happy with the performance of this bike now that I know what I should expect and I am very happy I bought it. At $999. current price, it is an outstanding value compared to any other eBikes with batteries integral to the frame that I saw listed on Amazon.
The improvements I would like to see HiBoy make are:
1) Change the website description and specifications to more accurately reflect the top speed of 23 MPH that a user could reasonably expect. The engineers know full well that if the top speed is 29 or 30 MPH when testing with no load, that when adding 225 lbs, (the weight of bike and average rider combined), it would be virtually impossible to reach a speed of 28 MPH without the help of gravity, as in, downhill. I was only able to reach 28 MPH when pedaling downhill, (incidentally, if coasting downhill, the speed will be limited and gradually reduce to 23 MPH depending on the grade). The top speed should be listed as 23 MPH, not 28 MPH.
2) Increase front sprocket size slightly for slower pedaling at top speed.
3) Provide ergonomic hand grips that are more comfortable on wrists. It is difficult to find aftermarket replacement grips because the right side grip is a half twist throttle grip.
4) Add rear LED taillight for safety with switch, same as included headlight which is excellent. Headlight and taillight could be operated by same switch since it is powered by bike’s own battery.
5) Provide larger mode/ speed display with the ability to make some adjustments to the controller with settings via the mode/ speed display. Display is OK to read but power and setting buttons are small and close together, so it is hard to change settings while riding, particularly when wearing gloves.

All in all, I would not hesitate to recommend this eBike, even without the above improvements. If the website description was changed to reflect a more accurate real world top speed, I would rate it 5 stars, but as it is, my rating is 4 stars. If the description and specs are changed on Amazon and on the HiBoy website, I will happily change my rating to 5 stars.

Review:
My fair review after 6 months of riding this bike.
Total: 3+/5

Battery: unbranded, ugly looking blue box inside the bag. Obviously it's not a good idea for the manufacturer to put it in the bag in compare with bikes that have battery attached to the frame. Fully charges in 5 hrs approximately.

Battery indicator: shows 5 bars when it's fully charged. 2 bars meaning 1 in real life. 1 bar meaning almost 0 in real life. Get worry if you have 2 bars left, they will be gone quickly. So, handlebar battery indicator doesn't show exactly the power level left in battery.

Handlebar: not adjustable, which is terrible. I got my back hurts almost everytime on this bike. I'm 6'' height, obviously this handlebar is not for me and people same height. It can't be lifted up.

Suspension: good, no complaints.

Tires: you should get away original ones and purchase real mountain tires. Original ones more likely should be used for streets. I got original tires flat in my first week. I recommend to use tubes from GoodYear brand, 26'' tubes you can find in Walmart.

Seat: regular mtb seat, strong enough, no complaints.

Light: no complaints. Battery powered medium power light.

Throttle: goes well, smooth, no complaints.

Brakes: disc brakes, easy adjustable. They creak time by time but no serious complaints. Use both brakes at the same time like on motorcycle.

Motor: goes well, not so powerful but enough for the streets no complaints. 250W.

Gears: Shimano 3x7. Very good brand. I like Shimano gears. No complaints. Go perfect.

Chain: no complaints. Don't forget to clean it time by time. Google it!

Frame: folding is a plus. Sturdy alluminium alloy frame. No complaints.

Wheels: good, no complaints, sturdy and strong aluminum alloy.

Assist mode: max assist mode with pedaling gives you 17 mph.

Pure electric mode: 15 mph.

Review:
I bought this bike as a travel bike. It surpasses that in every way and is a regular bike I ride, not just when traveling. My main bike is a Surly Disk Trucker. I have built it for long distance riding/touring. It is a great bike, also, however I can't take it with me on a lot of my trips. That is where the Dahon Mariner D8 comes in. With the gear range (30"-90"), fenders, and the rear rack standard on the bike it is an amazingly rideable bike as a stock bike. I was impressed with it from the first time I rode it.

The only "change" from the stock bike/components I have made thus far is I swapped the pedals for combo platform/SPD clipless. Due to some knee problems in past years from riding all my mileage riding is with SPD pedals/shoes. However, the ability to switch to platforms helps me when I need to make a quick jaunt in regular shoes or if I am high on miles and I need to give my calf muscles a break by shifting my foot positions (that I can't do while clipped in).

The gear range on the bike (30"-90") has been OK for most riding I have done. However, I do wish it had more low-end gearing for climbing hills. On the top end I find the gearing adequate as I won't be going faster than I could pedal in top gear on this bike anyway.

I did rate this 4 stars for "sturdiness". For what the bike is, it is a very stable bike and I have no problem riding it. I ride it regularly. However, one must understand from the get-go that the smaller wheel bikes are "twitchy" compared to regular wheel size bikes (26", 700c, etc). That isn't a bad thing, just expect it and if you do I'm sure you will also find the bike very ridable as I do.

For comfort I rated the bike as 4 stars. The reason for this is I don't find the straight bars "comfortable". That is just me. I get nerve and circulation problems in my hands so I constantly move my hand positions to circumvent that (on every bike). My hand positions are quite limited on the straight bar. That is one of my future upgrades - bars that give me more positions (probably drops, but not sure just yet). I will say, though, for me the stock saddle is very comfortable. Depending on the length of my ride I may not even put on bike shorts. If I'm riding all day the combination of the saddle and bike shorts is great.

As for set up out-of-the-box - if you are a decent bike mechanic you can set it up. The wheels need aligned, brakes need adjusted, magnet clip (folded bike has the frame halves that lock together with a magnet clip) needs aligned, the rear derailleur needs adjusted, and the chain guard on the down tube needs adjusted. One note on the chain guard - adjust this so it is close to the top of the chain and it isn't rubbing the side of the chain. I have had the chain bounce up and through the gap in the guard on a ride where it was a challenge getting the chain down on the ring. Once adjusted it shouldn't give you any trouble. Fold latches should be "tight", but you shouldn't have to muscle them closed. There are adjustment nuts to tune the pressure. You want the latches under good pressure, not loose, and not super tight. I have never had any sense of the joints being "loose" or feeling like the frame was going to open or bars fold while I've been on it - very sturdy design. The rear derailleur may take a few tweaks to operate without a hitch. I didn't get everything dialed in on my bike until about 50 miles. Its all smooth now.

Don't throw anything away from the box/packaging until you have the documents and parts. There is a small bottle of paint they give you also. It should be in the small parts box with the pedals etc.

For my phone holder on the bike I use a RAM QuickGrip on the lower steer tube with an X-Grip mount. I use a piece of string or a zip tie to lock the phone in place so it doesn't bounce out. There are other phone mounts RAM makes, I just haven't tried them. The combination I use works and I have another X-Grip I use with a 7" tablet as well. I use the tablet for larger scale mapping/navigating/trip recording on some long rides. My main bike computer is a Garmin Edge 1000, however (in the pics of the bike set up that is what goes on the bar accessory mount, then my phone is just used as a phone and to compare routing to Google Maps when needed).

If you are looking for a do-everything compact bike to take with you, or you deal with small spaces for storage on a regular basis (between home and commuting to work, for example) - this is a fantastic bike so long as you expect the "twitchyness" from the small wheels and don't expect to be climbing many hills.

Two upgrades on the horizon for my uses of the Mariner D8 are:
- Handlebars - more ergonomic/more hand positions
- 2 chain rings and front derailleur - I may bump the large 53t ring there now up a hair just to have an extra higher gear combo and add a smaller one around 40-47t so I can drop down low in the hills

Last note on panniers on the stock rack -
I use the "front" panniers of my Ortleib Packer Plus series, the Sport Packer Plus's, as rear panniers at times. I have the clip mounts to where the bags are as far back on the rack as I can get them. I have no problems with heel strike at all. A lot of people and bike shops probably will advise against loading a folding bike down due to clearance issues, but I have not found that to be the case at all. I wear size 10 shoes and use SPD clips. The combination with the bike and panniers is a winner for me. If you tried to get larger rear panniers on the bike you would be in for some issues on two fronts - drag on the ground when leaning in turns in addition to heel strike - unless you could come up with a way to extend the bags to the rear further. This, however, may change the handling of the bike with the weight distribution.

Would I buy the bike again? Absolutely!
Would I recommend the bike to others? Absolutely! And I let them go for a test ride on it too if we get the chance.

Review: This bike was not easy to put together (and I am quite handy). The shifter levers and brake levers had to be loosened from their shipped positions on the handlebar and re-tightened in their new positions. The front brake came assembled on the front fork, but had to be loosened to make room between the brake pads to push the fat tire between the pads. In that process the brake cable came off its anchor and had to be crimped and put back into the brake anchor, which means we had to re-tune the front brake. We put everything together, tested it - it rides well - and plan to now redo everything with the addition of lithium grease. Overall I would suggest a 13" frame for girls of the height targeted by this bike, instead of the 15". Girls have shorter torsos and ours appeared to need to stretch to reach the handlebars and brake levers.

Review:
This review will focus on assembly and overall impressions only. This is a Christmas gift and it is only late September so I won't know about the bike's durability and functionality for another three months or more. What can I say, my wife likes to get her shopping done early! Also, if you are looking for a bike to pass on to future generations of young riders, you might want to look at something a little more expensive than this one. But at this price point there is nothing to lead me to reject this out of hand as a trainer for one or more siblings.

Tools are included for assembly, but they are not really the best. But I used the included whale shaped multi tool to tighten all nuts as I wanted to ensure that someone receiving this would be able to complete the task. Unfortunately, the included hex head wrench proved to be useless as it did not fit any of the handlebar assembly bolts. For those with access to proper tools I recommend you have a 14mm open end wrench and a 6mm hex head hex. In addition, the reflectors need a #2 Philips screw driver which was not included.

Instructions for assembly are in a little booklet and are rudimentary, but if you've seen a bicycle before you should be able to follow them without much trouble, even if you are mechanically challenged.

Assembly consists of mounting the front wheel, the training wheels, pedals to crank, seat, and handlebars. Reflectors are optional but recommended so make sure you slip them on to the seat post and handle bar stem prior to mounting those items.

The training wheels just require you to remove the rear axle nuts and round washers, slip one on each side and replace the round washer and nut on each side and tighten. It is nice that these are included, but I hope my grandson learns to ride without them in about a month because I can't believe the inexpensive plastic outrigger wheels will last much longer than that.

Front wheel is similar in nature, remove the two nuts and locking "clips", drop the axle into the fork slots replace the clip washers and nuts and retighten. Make sure the locking clips engage in the fork holes to ensure the axle will not work out of the front fork slots.

The handle bar requires you to loosen the tightening mechanism slightly so that you can slide it into the steering head tube. Remember to slide the clear front reflector onto the shaft first and slightly tighten. Align the bar with the front wheel and using the 6mm hex head, tighten it securely.

The seat simply requires you to slip on the rear red reflector, slide the down tube into the frame and then rotate the locking lever until snug and tighten the assembly by closing the "clasp"

The pedals come with a clearly marked R and L demarcation. As I remember the L screws in counter clockwise. Securely tighten nuts with either the whale wrench or your own 14mm open wrench.

Adjust seat height and training wheel height to accommodate rider's needs.

Review:
I haven't had a bike in 15 years, but I wanted to feel the wind in my hai...helmet. I live in a really hilly place (Seattle area) and didn't think there was anywhere I could ride that wouldn't half kill me. The bike has been really fun. I'm a heavy woman in my late 40's and I've had no issues riding it around and up some pretty steep hills. The brakes feel a little weak, maybe they need to be adjusted. I slow way down to go downhill and I use the assist to go uphill. On the steepest hills, I can't just use the throttle. There's not enough oomph to get my large derriere up a steep grade.
My husband is handy and had no trouble assembling it. I need to have him adjust the handlebars, so hopefully that is easy.
It is very nice looking. I like the blue color and fat tires. It's a bit bulky and heavy, but not unexpected for an e-bike in this price point. I did change out the seat to a wider one with more cushion. I only ride for a few miles at a time and I've had no issues with getting several rides on one charge.
Overall it's a good value and it's helping me to get out of the house for some exercise.

Review:
I have been using the bike for over a month. I find it very reliable. I use it for road biking. I used to have a 24 speed 29” bike that gave me more speed and was more challenging. When I ordered this one, I was aware of the diminishing. However, this bike is challenging enough for me.

When it arrived, it required some assembling but nothing major. From the beginning and over three weeks the disk rotors (front and back) were rubbing some against the break pad. I assumed that of let alone the accumulation of rubbing would fix the problem itself. This is important because the rubbing creates friction and it holds you back when going up hill. Easy fix. Read the instructions on how to adjust disk brakes and do it. You will figure out easily.

I recommend this bike, especially to the bike enthusiast. Enjoy it.

Review:
(update October 31)

I absolutely love riding this bike - because of it's height (I got the 21 inch), the light weight, and the disc brakes. (incredible stopping power; most sets of disc brakes cost over $100). This is a great bike for the price. In fact after riding it a month I came back and bought a second one - as a spare for me, and one for my daughter(s) to ride when they visit.

(end update)

I am very happy with this bicycle - it is lightweight and tall (21.5 inch version), and fits me perfectly. It comes with front and rear cable-controlled disk breaks.

A couple of things to know about assembly.

- The handlebar is reversed for shipping - which means the brakes and shift levels are opposite where they should be. To fix this loosen the two bolts which secure the handlebar (w/a 5mm allen wrench), and flip it around, so the handlebar dip is in towards you when you are on the seat, which the point facing towards the front tire. (I learned this the hard way - after first moving the brakes and shift levers around without moving the handlebar.)

- attach the front disk brake on the outside of the spacer.

- remember that the left pedal has a reverse thread - you tighten it to the left instead of the right.

Once you understand those things, assembly is pretty easy. The back wheel comes inplace on the bicycle, with the disk brake already installed.

P.S. After I rode the bicycle for a while I noticed the disc brakes were squeaking, and the discs were hot from friction. When I turned the bicycle over and spinned the wheels I could see they were not spinning freely.

This was easily fixed by loosening the two two bolts that hold the caliper in place (with a metric allen wrench), and shift it slightly while you spin the wheel. When the wheel spins freely, tighten the bolts, and check again. Do this for both wheels.

Review:
These are GREAT bikes, hands down one of my best Amazon buys. That being said, there's assembly issues you need to know.

On the positive side (which imo far outweighs the negative) these are just well made bikes. We bought one each for our 5yo twins. The bikes were on the brink of being too big, but I figured worse case scenario, we'd garage them for a few months. No need. Having only trike experience prior, both my kids got on and went. They're super sturdy, with minimal wobbles on a good surface and definitely inspire confidence. They look sharp, and really are just perfect for the beginner rider. My kids also think the water bottle is the coolest thing since toast, so I'd recommend leaving that at home unless you're in for a "water break" every 2-3 minutes.

The downer, which I read about in previous reviews as well, is that things that need to be tight, need to be TIGHT on assembly, and then some. When we put our two together (easy assembly, took 5 min tops), my husband did one and I did the other. While I'm generally pretty handy, we found that the one I put together kept losing a peddle. Like it would spin off mid ride. Husband hulk-style tightened it, issue resolved. The handlebars did the same, and still need some adjusting occasionally. The worst is that the training wheels WILL loosen up. It's just going to happen. We had one kid blow out down the bike path at 100 ft/hr (he was really ripping for a Pre-K kid), only to turn it into a full out bronc ride when left his training wheel in the dust, got the speed wobbles, then finally ate dirt when his bike dropped it's shoulder and ducked right. I was a proud cowboy mom, he was a sad, bloodied biker kid. Had to carry him home. So my word of advice for those of you who either don't like to see their kids upset or just don't like to carry around dead crying weight, is to invest in a spool of thread seal tape, wrap up your peddles and training wheel threads, and tighten them until you or it almost breaks. Happy riding.

Review: There was photographic evidence suggesting that the box had been opened and bike unwrapped:  staples on box had been popped and bent under and box sloppily resealed with Scotch tape. there were black and white zip ties holding various parts to the frame; certain lose parts were sloppily rearranged; cardboard wrappings on frame and seat were disheveled and the tape partly unstuck; the front wheel was attached to the frame so that the axle bulged out so far that it likely would have penetrated the box had someone not padded the bike with four pieces of two by two feet cardboard ripped from another bike box labeled "Greenline Bicycles," which  Beachbikes doesn't sell. I unwrapped just enough to test-drive the bike. It skipped gears. The manual, which was for a different bike, was no help. I contacted seller. He denied that the box had been opened and refused my request for a full refund and return postage. I was up the proverbial creek. Like the guy who takes his car in for a lube and gets it back with a dent in the fender.  Fortunately, I figured out how to adjust Shimano shifter and when I removed the remainder of the wrappings, there were no scratches.  All's well that ends well, as they say. Yet there was a moral: buy from a reputable local bike dealer.  So you pay $500 instead of $229, but you get more, such as a carrier, chrome rims, fenders, hand-locking seat and front wheel nuts, front caliper brakes, and a better quality saddle.  Check it out. More importantly, you get to inspect and test drive the assembled bike before you pay.  And should something malfuntion or break, you know you've got a bike the dealer can fix.  I consider myself fortunate.  Suppose the bake came with a bent frame.  I would have had to pay $40 freight to return the bike plus a restocking fee, for how could I prove to the seller's satisfaction that the bike arrived in that condition? I my view, mail-order bike sellers should have a very generous return policy.

Review:
I do not live n California; $569 for the EveryJourney new is a lot of dough for a bicycle and at $569 there is serious competition. My daughter's Electra Townie was stolen at college and she likes flat foot bikes. I simply couldn't find another 26 inch townie for my 6 footer. Outdoor equipment is hard to find.

So... the Townie is $629. Make no mistake about it, this bike is a 100% knock off of an Electra Townie. However, six three zero is making some cash because every, and I mean every part of the bike that moves from the axles to the bolts used to put it together were manufactured with one thing in mind: to make the manufacture of the bike as cheap as possible while still reasonably knocking off the townie. The goods about this bike are even mediocre: lets cover them:
1. the finish is a fun color and it comes with fenders and a cargo rack. The finish had particles underneath it and the fenders weren't straight and the cargo rack was welded in a way to make sure you knew it was done the cheapest way: Note to six three zero: dont stamp your brand on something that has a garbage weld all around it. Your brand name is stamped right in the middle of the back of the cargo rack and it was welded by someone that couldnt pass a middle school welding test.
Verdict: not too awful, but the electra blows it away.
2. Clearly shipped from China to the US in a box and dropped at your door to put together. My $629 bike is assembled and tuned. This bike is waaaaay too expensive for what it is. The parts I assembled though were... OK... mediocre too. The handlebars were good, the cables were all connected. The wheel assemblies are dirt cheap and the tires are garbage. only made to hold 40 pounds according to the tire, not the 40-65 other good companies use. Direct knockoff of the townie tires... without the quality. the quick detach wheel gidgie is junk. the rear wheel doesnt have a quick detach... really? makes it hard to attach my daughter's pup cart... its 2021 for heavens sake.

But assembly was easy! after i straightened the fenders and replaced all assembly bolts with something not made out of cheap chinese pot metal and tsk tsked the cheap wheels and tires and derailieur it went right together. no way the seat stays up on a 6 footer with the garbage seat bracket included but the post and handlebars were shiny and decent looking.

Also, the instructions for assembly and tuning were good. It will need a lot of tightening and tuning so Im glad. Im going to replace the seat. noone could be comfortable on it.

Once sorted out the bike rides OK but the pedals are stiff one one side and made from pot metal... once again, pure junk but shiny and cute.

For this kind of money just be patient and find a townie. This bike is mediocre and there is zero chance the components are going to hold up to a year of campus beatings. The bike is not awful but for the price point I was expecting a much higher quality set of components, smooth shiny paint and a seat not designed by the Marquis De Sade. If this bike was $300 and available at the local big box store fully assembled I would give it 5 stars, really. But Ive got 600 bucks tied up in the bike and components I just simply couldnt bear to look at already :-) Nope, nope and nope

Sad update: The bike as purchased was unrideable :-). Another $125 dollars at the local bike shop to have it gone over and repaired. Wheels weren't straight from the package, brakes were unmanageable (poor components but the guys fixed them and one of the pedals wouldnt rotate (yes they were installed properly, it was just a non functioning pedal... So now I have over $700 invested in this bike and its just not $700 worth of bike. Yuck

Review:
I was shopping online for a mountain bike with full suspension and disc brakes after being disappointed with what I found In local stores. I came upon this bike, a remarkable bargain! Anything comparable in the stores is $500 and up while online you might find something similar for about $350. Looks great, a nice sporty silver bike, full suspension, disc brakes, designer style saddle, AND 29" wheels for $240!
I almost passed this deal up because of the 1-2 star ratings I read but I think those people should be banned from reviewing anymore products! For the price you are getting a fantastic bike to enjoy. IN FACT, the same day I got mine, my wife went for a spin and she loved it so much that I ordered a second!
To be fair and keep things in perspective, I'm 5'6 150lbs, and I'm not looking to do any crazy extreme off-roading, jumping stunts with it, just wanted to go on the street, bike trails etc and for that this bike is very capable.
IMPORTANT- my wife is only 5'3 and after lowering the seat all the way down (you have to remove the reflector) she is able to ride this bike with confidence and would never go back to 26" wheels so don't despair but I would't get it if any shorter!
Bike came very well packaged, very fast even with super saver shipping and only needed some minor adjustment of brakes and rear derailleur to be up to my standards. ENJOY.

Review:
I decided to start my daughter off correctly (for the real world) and get her a mountain bike style bike. (It having hand brakes and 6 speed shifter) It is her first bike. I figure if she gets good at this, it won't be difficult to train her AWAY from the styles that peddle backwards for brakes. Well, it worked fantastic! She never fell once and only hit the wrong brake ONCE. I simply detuned the front brake 30-40% less effective and that bike still stops on a dime!

It is a very well made bike, very sturdy, and was a snap to put together. I'm also very happy about the derailed guard. It has some significant wear in 3 months, but derailed looks like new.

I would urge any mom/dad to buy this bike. I would, however, suggest they get some flashing LED lights and put them on. The kid will love them and you get some peace of mind your are making poopkins way easier to see!

Review:
Not a bad mountain bike, especially considering the price. Comes almost completely assembled, and almost anyone can finish putting it together. Just basic things like the seat and pedals. The frame is a nice light blue and even though it's a hardtail, the front suspension works pretty well for light off road and street use.

21 speed Shimano gears shift fairly smoothly and didn't need any adjustments, and the brakes work well. Cable activated disc brakes front and rear work perfectly fine for everyday use, no issues at all with those.

Obviously at this price some of the components are not the best quality and I was expecting that. But overall I'm very happy with how it rides and the feel of the seat and handlebars. Frame is aluminum so it's somewhat lightweight but it's not going to be the strongest thing and I wouldn't recommend it if you want to do any serious downhill riding. For just cruising around or going on dirt paths, it works well and I would not have guessed it was under 400 dollars just from looking at it. I'm not a bike expert but I've owned a few in my day and I'm really happy with this one. I don't expect it to last forever, but so far it's been a very solid mountain bike. Parts can be upgraded if you wanted to do that, it seems to use standard sizes for everything. I'm just at the height limit for this bike and I'm pretty comfortable riding it. It is a full sized bike so it may be a bit big for someone under 5 foot 2 or so.

Review: I got this Royalbaby Moon 5 Kids Bike (16 Inch Wheel Size) with Training Wheels and a Kickstand for my 4.5-year-old daughter (45" tall). It wasn't too hard to put together, but the instructions were a bit confusing to follow. I wish the instruction manual had much bigger pictures so I could accurately see where to put each piece. I had issues with getting the plastic cap off of the handlebar insert (it would not budge). I ended up trying using a knife, scissors, and finally a wrench to get it off. I also had issues with one of the pedals screwing on correctly (make sure to follow the direction of the little sticker on the pedal parts). I love that there are two handlebar brakes and they were decently easy to teach my daughter how to use. I also had an issue trying to get the brake lines to align correctly on the front with how it was described in the instructions. The bike wheels also don't have spokes, which is something I've never seen before. Other than the mild confusion I had, this bike is super high quality. It is extremely sturdy with the training wheels (no tipping!). The wheels definitely need to be inflated, so that's next on my to-do list.

Review:
This Mongoose is pretty not bad right out of the box. I have ridden a few Mongoose bikes prior, and have always like their ergonomics. This one is no different. The paddle shifters are short throw and precise out of the box. The derailer on the main rubs a little when in second, but its a quarter twist of a screw later and its perfect.

Pros:
Out of the box, bike is very well setup- needing only a few minor adjustments for your preference, and size.
Came double boxed from Amazon, both boxes arrived very well even with overnight shipping.
Stock seat is almost usable.
Nice fat tires, with nice irregularly spaced and designed tread.
Zoom front susp. is very responsive. Action is very quick and no clunks from the front!
Rear swing susp. is responsive, quiet, and solid.
WONDERFUL brakes.
15 minutes from arrival to riding.

Cons:
Reflectors on rims look out of place (terrible color, design)
Stock seat is almost usable.
Pedals have plastic hubs.

All and all, Im happy. Ill update this review after I get some miles on her this weekend.

08/18/2012 UPDATE:

I went on a good ride today, putting about 12 miles on the bike from black top to sand. Had some very steep climbs, as well as some switch backs and high speed runs.

PROS:
Great brakes
Great suspension
Great balance, even over mixed rock.

Cons:
Front brake squeeks- but it is a new bike

09/02/2012 UPDATE:

So in the past few weeks I would say I have put just under 100 miles on the bike. Everything works wonderfully, tires dont go flat, shifting is super smooth, and NO BRAKE SQUEAKS!!!
Top speed; for me, seem a pretty solid 35mph- that being on flat paved surface, 3&7 respectively. My dad owns a XR-350 ant it too, works very well- @2500mi on the clock on that guy.

Im happy- she is a keeper!

05/13/2014 UPDATE:

Although this bike is no longer available on Amazon, I feel I should complete my review. I have taken this bike on many "advanced" mountain bike trails with great success. With only minor derailleur adjustments this bike has been rock solid. Never have had a flat, suspension works well as do the brakes. Having warn though my first set of tires, and hundreds of miles, I can say with confidence this bike will last a few more new tires before it gets sent to pasture.

Excellent buy! Good job Mongoose!

Lower the seat down so your kid can sit in the seat, and then they will whine all day because they can't get their baby in the seat. (Bonus: The baby seat is made of flimsy plastic attached by metal screws, so it should be broken off after just a couple uses (bonus bonus: probably leaving sharp pieces of plastic behind!)!)
2. There's a great part on the instructions where it says, "If you bought the other components, such as the mudguards, basket, kick stand, please read the corresponding instructions to install them." I was disappointed because I thought for sure they were going to leave these things off the instructions. Luckily though, after searching through every paragraph I realized that they wouldn't disappoint...the instructions they told you to check don't exist! So now I got to spend 20 minutes trying to figure out how to attach the basket (there's secret zip tie type things that are the same color as the basket zip tied to the bottom of the basket, hiding in plain sight!). To install the reflector I got to open up Amazon so I could look at the photos (which helped me remember to leave this review.) I saw that to install the reflector the handlebars had to be raised up some (can't be at the lowest setting otherwise my tiny kid might have too easy of a time with it!) So now that I was done assembling the bike, I got to disassemble it and raise the handlebars and re-straighten everything just to fit the reflector on.
3. The "bell". Those quotation marks are there on purpose. Look for any metal object near you, and strike it was another metal object. That's a "bell", as far as the bike manufacturer here is concerned. That's all it is - it's not a real "bwing bwing" classic bike bell...it's a bell-looking piece of metal with another piece of metal next to it, which you pull down on some rubber attached to a spring to hopefully have it clack against the "bell" and make a tiny "dink". Amazing. No more worrying about your bicycle bells annoying (or warning) passers-by with this one. Luckily for me the bell broke off when I tried to assemble it and now can never be installed. If you want the bell tight enough so it doesn't spin when you push it, know that you'll likely rip through the plastic connecting pieces with minimal torque. Have fun.

They sell bikes for half the price at Walmart...they'll probably be terrible bikes, but hey, this is a terrible bike too, so you win either way. Save your money, go to Walmart. While you're there at least you can pick up a real bell.

Review:
My 2.5 yr old has so much fun with the balance bike, that he inherited from his 4 yr old brother, they ride bikes together almost daily. We had to remove the cap/ plug on the bottom of the bike to allow the pipe for the seat to go lower than designed - it wasn't difficult to do, it allowed my just 24 month old son to start riding. It took a few weeks to get used to sitting rather than walking with the bike. Now he is 2.5 and he has done a couple miles bike tours with the family. My older son went from the balance bike to a 12" kid's bike within 10 min of teaching himself, he was 3.5 yrs old, he never had training wheels. He transitioned to 14" a few weeks later. The little guy will be ready for a real bike by 3 yrs age the latest, probably earlier. I wish the video posting worked on Amazon but it doesn't, it is incredible how much speed the balance bike allows, and how small of a curve the kids can ride with it. We take this balance bike everywhere, it's light, rides well on all kind of surfaces, and beats the slow walk of a toddler any day. Downside, toddlers don't have the coordination to hand break and balance the bike at the same time, and toddler hands are not strong enough to pull the hand break, therefore shoes take a beating. 3 yr olds can pull the break though. Other downside, it's not for 4 yr or older kids, the highest seat setting will not allow the legs to stretch out enough, we tried it with a friend's kid. Well, the idea is that the kid can ride a real bike by age 4.
Compared to other balance bikes: way better than wooden bikes, way more durable than many other metal bikes (ours went through 2 yrs of all weather conditions, it gets mostly left outside - summer, winter...), best of all are the rubber air tires, and it has hand breaks.

Reason for 4 stars, I read that the bike is no longer made with air inflatable rubber tires, one of it's best features. I hope they go back to that.

Review:
I don't know who to blame for these issues, was it manufacturing? was it shipping? was it distribution? I don't know. What I know is the state of what I got, and here it what I got:

The front tire was irreparably warped. Minor wobbles can be fixed simply by tightening or loosening the spokes. Which was required for both front and back wheels upon arrival, but this is expected. What was not expected is that the front wheel was warped so extremely that, although you can still ride the bike, you can see it wobble as you are riding it.

All of the stickers were starting to peel or was damaged; probably due to poor handeling and parts hitting it as it was being shipped, for all the parts (wheel, handlebar, main body/rearwheel, pedals where not neatly stowed away in some snuggly fitting foam, or cardboard, but it looked like they just tossed it all into the box and said "it's no longer our problem what happens to it." Some parts of the paint were chipped. The pedal was broken, which is amazing because it looks like a metal alloy, aluminum perhaps, and it is snapped in half.

Also the front fork was about 1 inch narrower than the wheel so it was impossible to get the wheel in. I had to get the crank from my car that is used to lift the car when you change your tire to open it up a bit so that the frame will deform a bit. This step was by far the most stressful (among truing the wheel, adjusting the break pads, adjusting the break wires).

So far there doesn't seem to be any issue with the chain, crank, cogs, breaks etc. rides fine; it's just a crappy quality.

I took off all of the stickers and the neon green handlebar grips so that the body is completely black and the handlebar is completely grey. It is a pretty good looking bike, I must say. Also a part of the handlebar was missing paint. Not that it was chipped off, but that it simply lacked spraypaint.

also, in case this is your first purchase of a BMX bike, you should know one thing: you can't really do any tricks with this. The bike weighs about 3 times as much as my road bike, that is, this BMX bike weighs like 35 pound. Try spinning this thing around. Good luck. The ones where you can actually do tricks are the ones that cost like +$300. Also the fact that this has 24 inch wheels means it's practically as big as a small mountain bike so don't plan on being a bad ass with this. It is a cruiser substitute. It moves, and it breaks.

I would not buy this as a gift to anyone for it just LOOK crappy (irrespective of the quality).

I don't care about the looks and I plan on tossing this away after a year or so., leaving it out in the sun and rain until than, only to be used to travel about 1 mile/day to go to my coffee shop. If you want a bike to take care of and grow old with, get something that is worth protecting.

Review:
I started biking after a 10 year hiatus over the summer. I was using a big heavy cruiser from a University bike-share program. I would go on bike trips with some of my friends who had hybrids and road bikes and quickly realized I needed a road bike that would allow me to keep up, but but didn't break the bank.

I narrowed my road bike search to the GMC denali, Schwinn Prelude, and Vilano Tuono. I ended up with the GMC Denali because it had excellent reviews. I ordered the GMC Denali, two spare tubes, a lock, helmet, bike computer, air pump and bike tool. All in, the total cost was 228 dollars ( I had substantial gift cards).

Assembly:

With my Topeak bike tool, assembly was no sweat. It took me 30 minutes to assemble the entire thing. The bike weighs exactly 29 pounds.

First Ride:

My first ride was a 10 mile jaunt around my town in New Jersey. Dawning my helmet and a tote bag with a pump, lock and tube, I was on my way. The ride was moderately smooth, I realized the front brake approached the point of uselessness. It took me 5 minutes to adjust this.

Fit:

I am 6 feet and 220 pounds. After some research, I decided the 57cm variant was the best for me. The bike feels good and it is very responsive. Too make long rides a little more pleasant, I will shell out for a new seat.

Tires and wheels:

The stock wheels are good. I don't see myself replacing them. The tires are good, but will be replaces with Cheng Shin tires as soon as I get the chance. I pump these up to 100 PSI with no problem. I am a fairly large person, so I will look into buying tubes that allow me to pump up till 125 PSI.

Handlebars:

These style handlebars are definitely hard to get used to. The tape seems solid and will definitely take normal wear and tear.

Shifters and Deraillers:

Out of the box, these work well. I have had only one loose chain. I will use Youtube to help me tweak these.

Overall, this is an excellent beginners bike. I recommend buying this.

Review:
This is a fantastic folding bike for the price. Though the assembly wasn't quite as easy as the 'lovable doofus' on YouTube made it seem, it wasn't that bad, either. Once I got the gist of it, the bike went together easy enough. The problem was the brakes—they needed to be adjusted so that they were aligned correctly and weren't rubbing against the rim the whole time.

That took some searching and tweaking, but there are tons of YouTube videos on that topic.

The real headache for me was the fact that it came with the front brake cable twisted around the fork of the bike, making it impossible to line everything up properly. The noodle was positioned on the opposite side from where it needed to be. I had to disassemble the front brake to move the cable, which turned out to be a helpful anyway, as I adjusted the brakes.

I've been riding this for a week now, mostly sidewalks and streets, with just a little off road. It holds up well, though its tires are a lot narrower than my regular off road bike. I probably won't take this on too many rough trails. But for getting around after hopping out of an Uber or off of a bus, it's been fantastic. And it fits perfectly in one of the smaller storage bins on my RV.

Review:
I wanted to put a few thousand miles on this bike before reviewing and feel pretty confident I can give an accurate account of the pros and cons. I bought it as a commuter to work but my commutes involve some distance and climbing so I wanted something sturdy but fairly inexpensive.

Pros:

Cool look
Sturdy frame
Comfortable
Tiagra is good now? I've had a good experience with the groupset
Good commuter if commute is flat

Cons:

Terrible brakes
Incredibly heavy
Value versus price
Paint job is trash and chips if you look at it wrong
Not suitable for serious riding
Performance Bicycles partnership is a joke

It's a bike. And it looks good. Unfortunately it's not a very good bike. Not for $1000. You can get more value at your local bike store in a Giant, Trek, Specialized etc. The brakes are awful and it's very heavy so if you intend on riding off road or doing any climbing you'll expect to make some expensive upgrades to the brakes and wheel set. This won't change the fact that this is one of the heaviest aluminum frames I've ever ridden. Aluminum has come a long way over the years but Tommaso hasn't gotten the memo. I use it as a commuter but tend to get about 2000ft climbing between work and back. The breaks are not intended for more than cruising around town. Certainly not for gravel riding as it's intended. I've had significant issues with the brakes. I was sceptical anyway but found that shipping to Performance Bicycles to have assembled was a joke. I had to take it to my bike shop to have everything redone. Avoid this option. They don't care and their mechanics are terrible. Then the paint job. It looks cool, at first. I have a dozen "big brand" bicycles and mostly high end $3000 -$6000 but even of the cheaper more casual rides I have the paint has never chipped or come off like this. The Tommaso's paint may be it's worst drawback. It's so bad a brake cable near the hesdtube has literally worn the paint to the metal just from turning the handlebars.

At the end of the day this bike is not a great value. You're not going to get the support of buying a new bike from your FLBS and you can do more with $1000. I was curious and took a chance but do regret not investing the money in something else.

Review:
The positives are: I don't think you can find a better 20" bike for your girl. The aluminum frame makes it light weight, and the gear shifter offers 6 speeds. It also has front suspension.

The negatives are with the gear shifter, wheels, and brakes.

I don't think either brakes or gear shifter are well suited for young riders. Our six your old struggles to have enough strength to change gears. In fact I've only seen her succeed in shifting gears once or twice. The brakes are another big problem. The levers extend way too far away from the handle bar. For me, this is a huge problem because it's safety related. She has to take her hand off the handle bar in order to grab the brake. I've tried adjusting the brakes so the levers are closer to the handle bar, but they are still far from ideal.

Finally, assembly of the bike was pretty easy but I still had to spend two nights doing it. This is because the wheels were horribly out of true. I had to make large adjustments to the spokes in order to true the wheels. It was also kind of tedious because I don't have a trueing stand.

My recommendation is to avoid this bike unless you have kids with large strong hands and you have a bunch of time to tinker.

Review:
I purchased this bike in November 2020 (arrived in December). Everything on the bike is worn out today, 6 months later. The crank bearing is wiggling and popping, brake pads needed to be changed a few weeks ago already, chain and sprocket are worn out and most importantly, the suspension joint is out of whack, too (it developed a lateral play which makes riding the bike a very uncomfortable experience). I put mere 930 miles into it.

I contacted Schwinn about replacement parts TWICE, to no avail (I would even cover the cost and shipping for the joint bolt, even though the bike must still be under warranty), nobody got back to me. No bike shop in the world will have this specific suspension joint bolt, it's a proprietary Schwinn part and their message is clear: why would they give me a replacement part for a few dozen bucks if they can milk me for another $400 for a new bike?

This is clearly a low quality bike with a high price tag, but the manufacturer cut corners wherever possible. Stay away if you bike more than an occasional, recreational amount of miles, otherwise you will need a new one in probably less than a year.

Review:
I was pleasantly surprised by the Schwinn Bonafied. First off assembly was simple and only took about an hour even though I did not find the instructions until I was almost finished. Unfortunately the instructions aren’t very good due to Schwinn/Pacific Cycle supplying a generic bicycle assembly manual rather than a model specific assembly/owner’s manual. That led to a bit of confusion since out of the box the disc brakes needed a bit of adjustment to eliminate drag when not engaged and the adjustment instructions were about as clear as mud. My only other complaint is that a reflector was broken and rattling around inside the box when the bike arrived. Minus one star for the manual and broken reflector.

Speaking of brakes, the Bonafied’s build quality and included components seem quite good. The brakes, once adjusted, are very smooth with lots of stop without feeling grabby and instill confidence. The thumb shifters and derailers are quite good for a bike this inexpensive and the ride is pretty comfortable. The bottom line is that yes I would recommend this bike to friends looking for a mountain bike at this price.

Review:
Pros
+Has 4 modes of pedal assist including motor only.
+Came with a light and horn powered by the same battery.
+Water resistant battery, went through heavy storm rain 4 times and it's still functional even when prongs got wet.
+Disk brakes
+Cheap at 600$

Cons
-Horn and light no longer functioning after 2 months of use.
-Very hefty bike, it's very tough to pedal only.
-Seat is uncomfortable after 20 minutes of riding
-Chain makes this annoying clicking sound when you pedal
-No replacement parts, even contacted seller about this.

It's the first electrical bike I've owned so I can't give a comparison to other bikes but it definitely beats having to pedal if your feet are tired after a long day at work. However not having replacement parts does not makes this a good long term bike, I'll have to switch to another brand of electrical bike eventually due to this. If your paranoid like I am plug the battery in for charging were you can keep an eye on it, the adapter can get pretty hot after 1 or 2 hours of charging and I usually unplug it to cool down before charging again just in case but that's just me. I recommend you oil the screw that holds flash light in place, it started to rust after getting rained on only once. I thought the light broke cause bike got rained on but it was still working 2 weeks after all that and I would always wipe the bike down after going through rain, I have not rode over rough terrain just barely and all the connections are intact. Good thing I carry my spare bike light especially since it was 4am and while I was on my way to work, since the horn doesn't work either it's back to shouting at pedestrians on the sidewalk.

Update 5/21/2017: a little clip that secured the battery in place fell off so now I have to use a Velcro strap to keep it from bouncing up and down while riding but worst of all the motor box is now making this screeching noise when I twist the throttle and it happens when it starts to accelerate.

Review: Great, sturdy bike with some adjustments. It's hard to find a high quality kids bike in a 12" size in the $100 range, but we weren't willing to spend hundreds on a higher end lightweight bike for our 3 year old. If you are like us, this bike is a decent option. It's heavy, yes, but lighter than any others we found that were not twice or three times the price. Our daughter is about 37" tall, and the 12" bike was still a little tall for her inseam the way it came at the lowest seat setting, but we were able to remove the rear reflector, buying us an extra couple of inches to lower the seat, making it a great fit. It was also a little too tight to begin with in the chain/gears but we fiddled with it and loosened it up a bit and now it pedals much more easily. We have been using the training wheels (though we were hoping to skip this phase because she had gotten pretty good at her balance bike) but it seemed necessary while she got the hang of propelling herself quickly enough to not fall over while pedaling. After a couple of weeks we tweaked the training wheels so they sit higher and she isn't completely relying on them... When she pedals fast enough she is just riding a 2 wheeler, but when she slows down, they are there. Overall worth the price, adorable, and recommended!

Review:
We don’t have a ton of time on this bike but here are my impressions so far. Assembly was extremely straight forward, I didn’t even have to go into the garage for tools(until later) For the reviews saying even with 72 years of bike building experience it still took them 2 hours, they are either full of it, extremely incompetent, or got a defective product. I have assembled 0 bikes before this one and it took me roughly 45 minutes, including the youtube video I had to watch to adjust the brakes. If I didn’t have to adjust the brake levers so my son could use them easier it would have been 30 minutes. Adjusting the levers in threw the brake pads out of wack and I tried staring at it for 5 minutes before I admitted I had no idea and looked up a youtube video. I had to pull some slack out of the brake cable and adjust the tension on the pads(had to go grab a philips screwdriver for this) After that it was ready to rock.

Saying it fits a 44” child is being EXTREMELY generous. First off, this thing is heavy. Noticeably heavier than my 650b mountain bike. The price tag and materials are definitely the reason for that, but I figured the smaller size would even it out. My son is 47” and even with the seat all the way down he was on the struggle bus. It can’t go all the way down, the seat post hits the frame before that happens. He could only wobble from one foot to the other, trying desperately to find a balance. I grabbed the seat and post off his old bike which allowed it to sit lower, and he was ok after that. Note that this does affect the pedaling motion for the worse, his knees come up too high, but it is manageable until he grows into it. He also has a rough time shifting down with his thumb. He ends up moving the handlebars to the left trying to press it in, but he’s only almost 6 and I had an idea he may have some growing pains moving from a single speed bike. Shifting up you pull towards you with the pointer finger, and there is much less resistance, so that’s not an issue. Brake levers are a drastic change from the pedal brake he was used to. It took a couple rides for him to get used to it.

With all that being said he has not asked to go back to his old bike, which he can handle much easier. He loves that we can ride up the hills on the bike path instead of having to get off and walk like we have been doing. For the price of a nice single speed I think this was a great choice. Only time will tell how well it holds up, but so far so good. I wish the height recommendation was a little more realistic and it was lighter, if it wasn’t for that it would definitely be 5 stars.

Review:
Update:
I received the new display and it has the same issue as the original.
The factory has disabled the settings mode so just be aware that you will not be able to clear out you trip mileage and your battery meter on the display will not give you accurate indication of battery condition.
Communication with the factory is typical useless as they were not at all helpful in solving these extremely simple issues.
Still not a bad bike for the money.

Update; I emailed customer service and they answered within 24 hours.
Their response confirmed my suspicion that the display is locked at the factory.
They said they disable the ability to enter settings mode because they do not want customers changing settings and causing warranty issues.
I told them that for me, a functioning trip meter and battery monitor was essential so they said they would ship a new display to me.
So if you buy this great performing ebike just keep in mind you will not be able to reset your trip miles and if you choose the 48V model you will have a non functional battery meter.
I will update again after I receive the new display.

Bike was delivered on time and packaged very well.
Assembly was easy but I have worked on bikes all my life so easy for me may not fit your situation.
Performance and battery life exceed my expectations. frame quality is good.
Ride quality is good.
Holds speed very well on hills.
Checked speed with GPS and its within 1/2 mph.
The bike will do 20 mph.
Issues:
Front shock is only good for looks, its just a simple spring in a tube so don't expect much.
Front brake was dragging on the brake disc and I had to make spacers for the caliper to get it centered properly on the brake disc.
Display is functional but you cannot reset the trip miles. Display will not go into settings mode so no way to rested trip mileage. I think the display is locked by the manufacturer.
Battery meter is not accurate at all, I suspect its set for 36V not the 48V for this bike.
I have emailed Ancheer and will report back if I hear from them.

Review:
I've recently purchased this mountain bike and overall, I'm very happy with my purchase. The instructions were very confusing to understand and seemed to go with a different bike altogether. However I put it together just fine without the instructions. The bike handles well on various terrains, provides a comfortable ride, and its frame is expertly designed and constructed with high-quality materials. The seat does cause a good deal of discomfort after riding for some time. I appreciate the smooth gear shifts and the overall performance of the bike.

However, I did notice a few drawbacks that could improve my cycling experience. Firstly, I feel like the bike could have been a bit taller. As someone who is taller than average, I experienced some discomfort while riding this bike for long periods of time. Secondly, the gear guard could have been slightly shorter as it gets in my way sometimes. Lastly, the kickstand could use a bit of improvement as it doesn't always hold the bike in place securely, often making scratches and dents from falling.

Despite these minor drawbacks, I am still very pleased with my purchase. This mountain bike offers a thrilling riding experience and performs well in different terrains. It's the perfect bike for those looking for an exciting and challenging ride, but I would recommend it to people who have to use it in busy street areas.

Review:
The Chillafish balance bike stands out compared to other balance bikes for young children because it's almost entirely hard plastic and fiberglass. There's very little metal at all. This makes the bike lightweight and, from what I can tell, actually more durable instead of less. Ignoring the possibly fragility of the footrest, the rest of the bike looks like it will last longer than a metal bike.

Let's face, kids leave their bikes around outside. This thing is going to sit in the rain. In some cases it might sit out in the snow all winter if no one remembers to bring it inside once the flakes fly. This is a balance bike I could see lasting through its intended age range of 2 to 5 years old. Much less sure about the mostly metal bikes that are in this price range.

The only caveat I have is that, like most balance bikes on the market, this doesn't have a hand brake. Because of that I'm less sure of its usefulness at the older end of its age range, especially say a 5 year old boy. Some kids are going to push their physical limits and once they get their balance will take a bike like this down a hill . . . and will only have their feet to stop the bike. This won't be most kids for sure, but for some this will be an issue. So with that in mind I'd recommend this bike for age 2-4 and an asterisk for adventurous 4 to 5 year olds.

The Chillafish was very easy to assemble. Basically just putting together three difference pieces and that was it.

About the only concern I truly have is if and when grit gets between the moving parts of the wheels and handlebars. On a metal bike this would be a non-issue. With this bike the moving parts are a bit tighter. So the bike might need a power wash sometimes to get this grit out of the tight spots.

Overall, very happy with the bike

Review:
I purchased a previous version (different color) in February of 2021 here on Amazon -- listing is gone now so I can't review it there, but this appears to be identical to the bike I bought.

My wife and I are REALLY hard on our folding bikes because we keep them in our marina (or on our boat) in humid, salt-water environments. We've owned six different brands of aluminum frame folding bikes including a Dahon Mariner, but this one has been the best. It rides nicely, shifts smoothly and uses high-quality components throughout.

Of course, not ALL of the components are stainless or aluminum, but I've never seen a folding bike that does not have some steel parts somewhere, even including my 2019 Dahon Mariner.

Note: if you don't know how to adjust cantilever brakes (adjusting them takes practice and skill), get to a good bike shop and have them do it for you after you've ridden the bike for 10-20 miles. All bikes with brakes of this type will need to be re-adjusted after break-in. Once adjusted correctly, the brakes on the ZiZZO are excellent.

One REALLY nice touch by ZiZZO; the corrosion resistant chain is awesome. I have previously had to replace all our bike-chains with these 'miracle' corrosion resistant chains, but I was delighted when I opened up the box and found that ZiZZO had installed this important upgrade at the factory! Awesome!!

THANK YOU ZiZZO!

I called Kazam's customer service and Elizabeth returned my call the same day and it turns out that spare parts are available and the bike can be fixed easily. Although the “cons” paragraph length is longer than the “pro” this is a wonderful bike. And I forgot to mention the value factor. This feels like a $3500 e-bike.

Review:
First, here are some stats:
The bike weighs 37 pounds
The seatpost height is 25 inches
The maximum (safe) pedal to seat height is 31.5 inches (which means if your inseam is 33" or taller, don't get this bike)

First things first--you SHOULD/NEED to get this bike professionally tuned/built when it arrives if you have NO experience working on bikes [.............]. I have worked in a bike shop for many years, so I know my way around a bike, and if you want this bike to work like a bike should, you need someone who knows what they're doing to tune/build it. (If you're wondering why I didn't get a bike at my old shop, it's because I moved across the country--don't work in a shop anymore).

Anyway, I have plenty of high-dollar bikes hanging in my garage, but I needed an upright ride because my back is going out on me. If you can tune/build/true your own bike or have a buddy who will do it for you, this is a great deal. Otherwise, you'll probably be better off going to your local bike shop and buying a bottom of the line brand name cruiser that has been properly built.

This bike was double-boxed and in pretty decent shape when it arrived with FedEx. There was a large paint gouge on the rear down-tube, but only superficial. The brake/derailleur cables were frayed because they didn't put cable fairings on the end. The wheels were rideable but out-of-true, and if you don't get them trued you will incur permanent damage and end up paying a whole lot more for new wheels.

All the bolts were there, except one for the rear fender (I just used a zip tie to secure it). It does not have holes in the downtubes to attach a rear rack, so you'll have to gerry-rig it. If I keep this bike, I'll throw a new rear derailleur on, but the one it came with will get you where you need to go. In my opinion this a great bike for the money. If you have a bike mechanic friend, buy him a six pack and you're set. If you are on your own, I'd suggest getting a cheap but well-put together bike at your local bike shop (NOT Target or Walmart--they don't have mechanics putting their bikes together).

My one BIG complaint about this bike is the size. I'm 6'1", and I figured it would be semi-small since it's one-size-fits-all, but it's actually quite a petite bike. I'm stretching the seat-tube past the recommended limit until I can buy a longer one. You don't want to pay [.............] for knee surgery to save [.............] on a bike, so if you're 5'11" or taller, find something cheap that comes in a large size.

Like I said, having worked in a shop, the frame and the parts are more or less on par with a low-end brand name cruiser bike, it's the labor that makes the price difference.

Review:
My bicycle is an old mountain bike from the late 80s and I've gone through a head light about every 4-5 years. These lights are cheaper and better than any that I've bought before. I'm impressed at their thoughtful design. The silicone strap mounts are tool-free and wrap on in seconds, and the lights are small cubes that twist and then pull off of the mounts so you can pop them in your bag or pocket.

The headlight and tail light both have two sets of lights, and a control button for each. The headlight has an upper and lower set of LEDs, and one illuminates a wide area and the other is more of a spotlight. You can adjust the brightness of each to match your riding needs. The tail light has an inner set and outer set of red LEDs, and you can adjust the brightness and flashing pattern (or not) of each.

They're rechargeable with a clever dual-wire USB cable, and have lasted longer than my longest night time ride. They also were not affected by our recent rainy weather. With all of these clever features, quality, and reasonable price these are a good value.

I'll update this review if there are any issues long term.

Review:
I visited a friend a month ago or so after he helped my family move and while we were there, he asked if I would be interested in going on a ride in one of his e-bikes. "Absolutely", I told him. Little did I know that would immediately get my inner-gears moving and plotting so I could come up with a way to get one of my own.

Then I began to price them and my dreams were kind of dashed. ALL the really decent ones started at $899...a little out of my meager budget. It seemed that most of the truly economical bikes were considered trash and a waste of money. The popularity of even the cheap ones have gone up by a minimum of $150 to $300...oh crap. If I can't afford a cheaply manufactured e-bike, how in the world am I going to buy a decent one? As I continued to do my research, I had a few names pop-up here and there and one in particular was Ancheer. Clearly a Chinese bicycle company. I personally believe Chinese products get a bad rap - much is earned, but all? Not hardly. So much of what we have is made in China that to make a blanket statement that it's all garbage is simply ignorant. Your iPhone is made there, and so is that Samsung Galaxy S22 Ultra (along with Viet Nam and India among others). What truly IS important though, is the quality control ON SITE. If that is good, then you have a better chance of getting a well-made item for a lot less.

First off...know what you're getting. This is NOT an off-road bike - nor was it ever intended to be. It's an intro e-bike and depending on what you truly need it for, that can be a good or bad thing. So before you buy, do yourself a favor and be totally honest for what you plan to use your bike...that way, you'll either be very satisfied or disappointed if you go ahead and buy it.

I chose this particular model mostly because it has step-thru and the battery is so well hidden in the rack behind the seat. Sure you can tell what it is if you understand what you're looking at, but most will simply think it's a bike rack to bungee stuff to. The packaging was well done and the included tools can help with your assembly such that you probably won't need any others...but if you have them, get them out and ready just in case. Better to have it and not need it rather than need it and not have it, right? Assembly is pretty straight-forward and I might add this is my very first bicycle assembly. I watched the Ancheer assembly video on YouTube multiple times which made a HUGE difference. I see many who complained about the included instructions and I actually thought they weren't bad at all (especially considering some of the instructions I've seen from other Chinese manufacturers). The only issue I had was the video still showed the front wheel assembly from the older version and not the quick-release model that now you now get. It seemed a bit puzzling to me, but I figured it out.

The bike looks pretty much as it does in the pics. Like EXACTLY. Pretty happy about that. Yes, some parts are cheap (like the fenders) but I could probably change them out if I really want to. The tires are about the minimum you can find, but they work. My battery came fairly well charged, which was nice. I took it out first when I unboxed everything and immediately plugged it in before doing anything else...after all, once you get the bike assembled you REALLY want to take it out for a spin, right? I know I sure did. By the time I finished, it had taken me roughly 50 minutes and keep in mind, I have never assembled a bicycle before. I think those who are clearly experienced could shave that time easily in half. By the time I got it all done, the battery registered almost full. I can't speak for how charged your battery may be, however.

After sliding the battery into place and tightening the charging box it plugs into, I turned it on and was pleasantly surprised that it has a rear light (red) that I was not expecting. Nice touch I have to say. After that, you press the center power switch on the handlebar and power the entire bike on. Keep in mind the battery fits into a groove so it stays secure, so be careful to make sure that it goes in properly. To be entirely honest, I was quite surprised that after my 50 minute assembly ordeal which may or may not have included a few improper phrases that ought not be said in front of children, I was downright gleeful that the bike operated flawlessly on my first try. Granted, once I sat down I made a few adjustments to the handlebars and the seat so it would sit correctly for me in particular. This is something I think is very important for everyone to ensure a better riding experience. I also admonish you to tighten all screws and not just the parts you assembled.

As far as how it rides...I think it does exactly what it says and that's about it - and that's okay. I DO wish that it had more gears. Because of so few gears to switch to, anything more than pedal-assist 2 is kind of overkill. You have to pedal SO fast in order to catch up to it that you may as well just ride a regular bike. This of course may change going up a steeper hill (none of my commute so far has involved any real inclines to test this - but rest assured I will be doing so and including it in a future update.

The gears shift with ease and is easily done with your right thumb. I've seen a few people complain wishing the bike included a different shifting mechanism, but honestly, I'm happy with the one it came with. The power control operation on the left side may need to be adjusted after you position your handlebars as I had to do, and that is the ONE tool that I needed to adjust that was not included. It's an Allen Wrench but it's smaller than the 3 that come in the makeshift tool bag that comes with the bike for assembly. It was an easy correction since I already own my own set of wrenches. The power bank is pretty straight-forward. Press the middle button (which conveniently has a power symbol) and use the right and left buttons to switch between pedal-assist and full throttle. Easy peasy.

Once again, keep in mind that this 250 W motor will not be able to get you up to 20mph in throttle only (even on flat ground - unless perhaps you weigh next to nothing) and especially not going uphill. I wouldn't recommend that at all actually. Going uphill, use pedal assist and you ought to be fine. After I put the bike through it's paces, I'll do an update to bring to light more detail regarding this. In throttle only I think I was able to do around 12-15 mph on flat ground. That may be a little exaggerated however. Again, I did not purchase my bike in order for it to act like an electric scooter, I got it to supplement my exercise routine and to be able to explore the surrounding city in which I live. To each their own, right?

The included headlight is also another nice touch. I love that it helps on my morning commute to work since I leave a little after 4am and even in the summertime, the sun isn't up yet at that hour. I wouldn't say it's a stunning light, but sufficient for what I need it to do and for that, I am grateful. Any time you can be more visible while riding - especially at night - is a good thing.

I'm quite interested in how far I'll be able to go on a full charge, and while I will most likely not allow the bike to get too low on battery before plugging it in again, I'm interested to see how far I can get before I decide to head home and recharge.

All in all I am impressed with what Ancheer has managed to pull off here for the price. The most critical part of your purchase is in knowing what you'll be getting before you pay so you won't have unnecessary expectations that the bike simply cannot live up to. For what it is, this e-bike really is a great buy and does exactly what it was designed to do.

***UPDATE***

So, after some extra testing, I have a few things I want to add. First off, the seat isn't uncomfortable - at least not for me. I was expecting it to be horrible based on several reviews and was pleasantly surprised that it wasn't an issue for me at all. Another nice surprise.

Brakes are okay. When they're wet, boy do they squeal. Yes, they work, but heaven help you if you need to make a very abrupt stop. These will definitely do the job, but nowhere as well as brakes that come on much higher end bicycles and e-bikes. Keep that in mind.

The front forks are kind of hit-or-miss for me. Sometimes they absorb bumps, but more often than not they don't. I think they were almost an afterthought as they were designing the bike. Oh, and they cannot lock, either - not that it'd be much of a difference...but I thought it important to mention anyway.

As far as battery life goes, I still haven't discovered just how far you can ride before the e-bike becomes a regular bike when your power dies. Mind you I haven't really tried too hard, so do keep that in mind as well. I've been trying to tax the battery by using throttle only in order to speed up the battery usage, and from what I've been able to do, I think you can say under throttle only, you can safely do around 12-17 miles before totally exhausting the battery. It is entirely possible, as with most rechargeable batteries, the further along they go before charging, the quicker the charge disappears, but even with that factored in, I still think I could do at least 10 miles comfortably in throttle only. If you did peddle assist level 2, I truly believe that would bump up to close to 20 miles minimum. Your mileage may vary. Of course that will go up if you have it on level 1.

With even more miles under my wheels, I am still happy with my Ancheer bike and NO, I am NOT being compensated for my review. I bought my bike and paid for it in full myself and received nothing in exchange for what I've written.

***UPDATE #2***

The advertising says this bike will do 19mph...but that will only happen going downhill. Possibly if you peddle your brains out in pedal-assist level 2 or 3...but otherwise, I'm crying foul on that claim. The best I could do was an average top speed of 15mph...which isn't bad considering the motor you receive. I suppose if you are a very petite woman who weighs in at 100lbs that 19 is possible, but other than that, I doubt it highly.

I'm going to reassess my guesstimate on how far you can go on a full charge as well. In throttle only, I think the best you are going to get is maybe 8 miles (depending on your weight and the terrain you travel on) and in peddle-assist #2 maybe 15 miles.

Over this past weekend I rode my bike up a fairly steep hill in assist #3 and in the lowest gear and it was a challenge, but one I doubt I would've been able to do in my current physical shape without that assist on high. I came close to pulling over and resting, but never did. Riding back down was pure heaven doing 35mph or so on that steep grade. I kind of felt that the bike frame was about at it's limit though at that point even though I was just merely coasting...so no need to test that theory too much because the resulting crash could be catastrophic (at least for me). I may do that hill again, but I will definitely slow down a bit on my way back just to be careful.

Still happy with my bike.
