Review: I was very excited to have the opportunity to give this bike a try. I had been researching Cruiser style bikes lately and really wanted to get one. I have a basic aluminum frame Schwinn frame 12 speed "mountain" bike that I bought from Walmart with Shimano brakes and gears. It is a fine bike, but I would like to have a more upright posture and higher handle bars. The handle bars on my current bike will not raise up further. This bike arrived well packaged and double boxed. Thank goodness that it was "mostly" assembled because it still took my husband and I over two hours to unpack the bike and finish assembling it. To get the seat on its' rest and at the right angle for sitting took several tedious adjustments. A fender arrived with a thumbprint sized dent in it. The other fender arrived rather twisted and caused some rubbing on the tire. I was very concerned that the bike would be too large for me because the listing info stated that it was for taller adults, specifically 5' 10" and up. I am only 5' 3". This bike fits me just fine and does not feel big for me at all. I don't understand where they are coming up with that size/height recommendation. I don't even need the seat in a very low position, nor the handle bars. I was a bit concerned about the smart braking system that engaged the front and rear brakes together!? It does work and I do not feel like I am going to be thrown over the handle bars! So, all sounds pretty darn good so far, right? Here are the parts I do not like and they are deal breakers. :( unless I have only my toes on the pedals, my feet rub on the front tire when I turn the bike. The first couple times it happened it startled me and then I realized that each time I turned the wheel my foot hit the tire and that this could be dangerous! The second disappointment I have with this bike is that even in first gear the bike is remarkably difficult to ascend any inclines. I know this is a cruiser bike and is meant to mainly be ridden on flat surfaces, but come on. Surely, Huffy who has made bikes for at least 40 years that I know of ought to be able to make a 7 speed cruiser bike that someone could ascend a small grade in first gear without feeling like they were towing a semi!!! Review: My 5 year old son has had this bike for about 9 months now.  The bike seems to be well built, and I imagined would be quite durable.  We did have a problem with the tires wearing pre-maturely....cord began to show on the rear tire within the first month, and after only 2 or 3 short skids.  It has since been replaced, and replacement tires have fared much better.  We however are VERY VERY DISAPPOINTED with Raleigh customer service.  While transporting the bike we lost one of the two nuts that screw onto the axle and hold the wheel on the front fork of the bike.  I first went to three very large hardware stores and had multiple exployees trying to help find a nut that fit.  None of the stores carried a nut that would fit, it seems to be a specialty nut with an irregular thread size.  I then contacted customer service to ask if we could purchase a replacement nut, or, if not, if they could tell me what size and thread count the particular nut was.  Customer service could only give the diameter of the nut, and they advised I go to a bike store to ask for help.  After visiting two bike stores, no replacement could be found.  I emailed customer service again, and asked them to please find the thread count, so that I could special order a nut online.  they said that they could not tell me, that thier diagrams didn't specify a thread number.  Now I am left with a 9 month old $170 bike that is worthless.  I find it impossible to believe Raleigh doesn't know what part sizes their bikes are built with, unless they are not actually built by Raleigh and just imported from a generic overseas manufacturer.

Review:
Bought this bike for my 10 year old daughter with an inseam of 29". She rides the bike well with only rare taking-off-up-hill issues.

I ordered from Amazon and not a third-party retailer. The box arrived in perfect shape. No issue with the bike being damaged or scratched in any way. The bike arrives mostly assembled. Be careful unpacking the front wheel. If you need to, rotate the pedal arms until the front wheel is relatively free. If not, you'll likely bend the spokes and damage the wheel.

Assembly is the biggest thing you'll need to consider before purchasing a bike. The assembly itself is easy. The somewhat tricky part is adjusting the brakes and derailleur system. I'm the type of person who can never bring myself to pay some one to replace a light fixture, work on my lawn mower, replace brake pads on my vehicle, or repair a leaky faucet. If something breaks, I fix it myself. Still, having not assembled a bike in roughly 20 years, I had to take my time and exercise patience in adjusting the brakes until they were "just right." The rear derailleur adjustment isn't too complicated, but it is tricky if you've never done it before. I recommend looking up a YouTube video if you encounter any frustrations with it. Once I did this, I breezed through the adjustment of the rear derailleur. It helps to have a bike stand or some assistance when checking the adjustments as you're making them. However, I was able to adjust the derailleur solo, and I got the shifting down until it was smooth through all gears.

I have since put together another similiar bike (a 20", 7 speed) for my 8 year son. I made quick work of getting it ready to ride with the experience of already assembling this one.

I also purchased a third bike for my girlfriend's 9 year old son. I purchased it from Wal-Mart, PRE-ASSEMBLED. I couldn't find a 24" boys bike on Amazon with reasonable shipping. The pre-assembled bike had the front tire on backwards and required brake and derailleur adjustments. So, basically, although the third bike was fully assembled when I got it, I had to go back over all the finer points of assembling a boxed bike.

Last comment. If you don't feel like you can adjust the bike properly, I recommend taking any bike, pre-assembled or not, to a bike shop. I have always heard other people say that, and the experience I had with these bikes reaffirmed that belief. We definitely want our kids as safe as possible.

Review:
I was looking for a sturdy, well-made bike for my daughter's first bike. She's only three, but she's 40 inches tall, and I figured a 16 inch bike would be the best bet. The options at the box stores grabbed her attention with all the pretty princess and disney themes, but I was skeptical about the quality. The plastic baby carriers and other accessories looked flimsy and weakly attached, etc.

I purchased the pink Bikestar, mainly due to it's stellar reviews on Amazon, but also because it has a sturdy metal rear rack (for her babies), a usable basket (not a silly plastic case-type thing), and a chain guard that covers the entire chain (you'd be suprised how many kids' bikes only have a partial chain guard).

Shipping from Dakota Cycle was super fast! Took only 3 days, which was good since I was only a week away from her birthday! The bike is easily assembled with basic tools and the instructions are very clear. I put it together on my lunch break.

As for the bike itself, it is a quality bike for sure. Well built, but not too heavy either. I couldn't be happier. Dakota Cycle will get my business again when my son is ready for one.

If I had to pick out a negative, it's the front reflector. The bracket is plastic, and will most likely break in the future, but it's not like my three-year-old will be doing any night rides, not to mention, the basket partially obscures it anyway.

It is significantly more expensive than the big box store bikes, and you'll have to shell out extra for training wheels, but the quality in this bike is well worth it. If you're like me, you don't mind paying more for quality, if it saves money in the long run. You won't regret buying this bike. Slap some princess stickers on it if you have to!

Review:
Item arrived in great shape with the bike and shipping box undamaged. Results will always vary depending on who handles one of these during shipment. Was purchased for a 10 year old boy who is 53-54" tall.

Upon final assembly, the brakes and rear derailleur needed to be tuned. No surprise there. Rims were mostly straight with only 10-15 mins of time spent truing them. Did spend longer than should have adjusting the handle bar. Most of the bike is of decent quality but felt the stem/bar assembly could be a better design for this price point. The steel frame seems durable of average weight with a nice painted finish. Took about 2 hours to unbox, assemble, and perform initial tune before first ride.

Overall handling is good. My boy thoroughly enjoys riding this bike. The Cobra is one of the few youth bikes of this size, for a boy, that has a top tube low enough for smaller riders. Besides the stem/bar assembly, the only issue I have with this bike is the fork. The fork is very stiff with limited travel and will be mostly useless for a 80-90 lb rider except for the most extreme trails. Perhaps it will "loosen" over time. He had a little trouble working through this bike's 21 speeds initially but is becoming easier to shift with each ride. This is his first trail-worthy bike and will be a nice transition into something bigger and better as he grows.

Has been in use for less than 2 weeks with no problems. Is a good value at the $168 price point especially if you can assemble and tune it yourself. Otherwise, consider taking this to a bike shop for assembly and setup. Properly tuned it will hold its own with bikes costing 3x more. Happy with the purchase but if more info had been available at the time, I would have purchased the Diamondback Octane 24 instead. At $245 currently, the Octane offers a lighter aluminum frame, more aggressive tires, replaceable hanger, and upgrades to the shifters, stem, seat and rear derailleur.

Purchased for $168 from Amazon in September 2015. Made in China.

UPDATE: 5 weeks since assembly, the stem bolt has needed tightened twice as it has worked loose. No other issues and overall quite happy with the purchase.

Review: We got this bike for our son for Christmas.  He still loves his other Mongoose bike, but it spends so much time at his grandma and grandpa's house that we decided to keep it there and get him another one for home.  His other bike has reverse pedal brake system whereas this one employs the single hand break.  We were interested to see how he would adjust to that and he figured it out very quickly!  The brake works well, though I will say it's still a little more challenging for him to operate with small hands, especially in the winter when he's wearing gloves.  The bike itself feels very sturdy and well made and I love the sleek BMX look of it.  The tires are super thick and heavy duty.  It took me about 45 minutes to assemble by myself.  The only challenge is that the instructions apply to many different models/styles of the same bike, so it takes some time to sort through it to find which directions apply to your specific model.  It would be really nice if they would send exact directions for whatever model you end up buying.  I will say my son does not find this seat to be very comfortable, or as comfortable as his other bike, but I guess we could always get a different seat.  All in all this is a great bike for kids who are interested in BMX, want to try hand brakes as opposed to reverse pedal and have the reverse pedal free fly thing.  I can definitely recommend!  Also, as a side note, his other Mongoose bike had a pedal come off and it turned out there was a problem with the entire pedal assembly.  Pacific cycle (parent company of Mongoose) has wonderful customer service and sent us out new pedals and a new pedal assembly at no charge when I contacted them about it!  The bike is working great again!  Such good customer service earns MAJOR points with me!

Review: I did a lot of research before buying the Diamondback Overdrive.  Most of the reviews were positive and I found it highly recommended as an entry level Mountain bike on various web sites.  At 48 years old, I started riding again last summer to get some exercise.  I was using an old 26" bike that belonged to my son and thought I should upgrade to get the most out of the hobby.  Because the old bike was really basic (10 speeds, no front suspension, big box store quality) I wanted to upgrade to a quality bike without breaking the bank and the Overdrive seemed to meet all the requirements.  I knew the frame on the old bike was on the smaller side so not only was I looking forward to the 29" tires, but the larger frame as well.  I'm 6'-1" tall and 210 lbs which put me right between sizes for Diamondback frames.  I opted for the XL frame size which works well for me.  I have the seat in the lower 1/3 of the adjustment range.  I'm sure the LG frame would have been ok too in my case, but it's definitely a large bike so trust the sizing charts.  Compared to my old bike, the breaks and mechanicals are top notch.  For someone not accustomed to 24 gears, the low end power is amazing.  This is the "Ready Ride" version, and just like they said, you only need to install the pedals, seat and front wheel.  Very simple.  The breaks and derailers were factory adjusted perfectly.  I did go over the whole bike to make sure everything was tight and did find a couple bolts that needed an extra turn.  The bike arrived in a giant box and was well packaged.  Absolutely no scuffs or dings (not even the box).  The only problem is figuring out what to do with the huge box.  After a quick test ride in the driveway, the warnings from all the other diamondback reviewers were confirmed:  the seat really sucks.  Buy the larger seat and don't waste your time installing the factory seat.  I took it out on the trail today and it is truly a pleasure to ride and it looks amazing.  The front suspension and 29" tires make most of the bumps disappear.  Solid ride with excellent paint and graphics.  I highly recommend this bike!

Review:
This bike has excellent value and good quality. It has adequate range for 20-40minute workouts on hilly roads by this older adult. The pedal assist and throttle are a joy to use and along with judicious shifting, the ride up steep slopes are comfortable. The steel frame makes it a bit heavy but sturdy. It can fold comfortably in the back of most vehicles.
Packing: Cardboard box held up well to shipping but had one small hole and small tears. All the contents were well protected.
Assembly: Using both the paper manual and the online assembly video, assembly took several hours with no issues. Supplied tools were adequate but some steps needed two allan keys or wrenches. Most of the time was spent tightening and inspecting. Front wheel bearing was a bit stiff and needed oiling. The chain at pedal hub needed to be pulled onto pedal sprocket. Front Brake lever had too little play resulting in grabbiness; needed to increase cable length slightly.
Charging: Initial charging took about three hours. The 48V battery pack consists of 13x3 2600mAh 18650 LiPO cells in a sealed plastic box. There is a small power switch on the cable end which needs to be turned on to provide power. Electrical data from DVM and power meter checks infer that an industry standard BMS (Battery Management System) is probably integrated in the pack. This is required to maintain cell balancing, protect the battery, and optimize charging. Charge time consists of two parts. First half is in 'Constant Current' mode where the charge LED is RED. When the LED becomes GREEN, the battery is near full charge. The 2nd part of the charge cycle is probably the 'Cell Balancing' and 'Constant Voltage' Charging which is ensures full capacity and long life. The manual recommends charging for 4 to 6 hours (well past the switch from RED to GREEN).
Road Performance and Ride: The only ride complaint about the bike as shipped is that the seat is uncomfortable. Amazon's recommended 'Giddy Up' seat option is much better. A power meter was plugged in between the battery connector and power cable to monitor Electrical performance while riding. A 20 minute 3 mile ride up and down a hilly route (2.3% average grade) showed that the bike provided comfortable uphills with pedal assist and gear shifting. Bike was stable and comfortable at 35mph downhill coasts. Peak power consumption was 750 watts and the ride used about 20% of the battery capacity (1.5 Ahr). The estimated range is about 15 to 20 miles on hilly terrain depending on the power setting used. The manual recommends limiting the battery use to 30-50% levels (2 of 5 Bars Left) for optimum battery pack life.
Maintenance: I would add bike chain dry lubricant to the chain. The manual recommends oiling the moving parts periodically. Check all the bolts and screws for tightness during the first few rides.

25 Ride/125mile Update: The bicycle performed well for 25 4-6mile rides on hilly roads.
-Battery/Charging: Charging generally took about 1hr to 'Green Light' but full battery voltage took at least another 2-3 hours. Charging to 24 hours did not cause any harm (smart charger kept battery charged at maximum with Float Voltage of about 4.2V/Cell.
-For riders taller than 5foot 9inches, one needs a longer seat post.
-In 'Low' pedal assist mode, the bike gives you a good workout on a hilly 5 mile course. In 'High' mode, you can power through the course with less effort and higher speed.

50 Trip/210mile Update: The bicycle was very reliable but had a few minor issues:
1. Rear Wheel Reflector fell off. Replaced with Amazon generic.
2. 3-Gear Shifter cap and screw fell out. Cap/screw replaced from a inexpensive Amazon replacement.
Wear Items:
1. Rear tire Center Tread has signs of initial wear (see photo).
2. Chain/Gear mechanism needed readjustment and lubrication.
3. No signs of Battery Degradation! Battery does need 5 to 6 hours to rebalance the individual cells. (Per cell voltage goes from 4.16V to 4.20 V during the last 4-5 hours of charging).
Enhancements:
1. Left Handlebar Rear View Mirror aides greatly with keeping track of overtaking cars. (See Photo)
2. GiddyUp seat with Rear Lights improve bike visibility. (See Photo)
3. Mount the battery in the soft case with the Cables and On/Off Switch right-side-up. (See Photo). This gives you access to the On/Off Switch.

Review:
I am very happy with the Schwinn Phocus 1600. Here are my observations and reasons I gave it 5 stars. I highly recommend this bike to anyone looking for a high-quality road bike that is priced right.

1. The bike came well packed with several cardboard tubes around the frame, bubble wrap around the handlebars, and many tie wraps to hold all the parts snugly together.

2. The tires are high quality and the Schrader valves are secured to the wheels, so they don’t push in when you fill the tires with air. In my opinion the 700X23 tires look good on this bike, I was thinking about getting bigger 700X28 tires, but the factory tires look to be very good quality and adequate size. And the smaller tires allow for easier removal of the wheels through the brake pads.

3. It has a handy kickstand.

4. Both front and rear wheels are quick release.

5. I put the chain on and both front and rear shifters were adjusted properly, and bike shifted through all gears nicely without any further adjustments.

6. Easy to assemble, it took me about the same amount of time to assemble as it did for me to unwrap it out of all the protective packaging. Bike comes with a very nice user manual. The user manual has assembly instructions and instructions for other adjustments, like adjusting the brakes.

7. Factory seat has memory foam feel and a good amount of cushion.

8. There are brake quick disconnects that allow the brakes to be released to remove the wheels more easily.

9. The break and shifter handle horn make a very nice grip to rest your hands on while riding and it is very well padded.

10. The machined rims look very nice and provide a smooth braking surface.

11. Colors were much brighter in real life than what is pictured on website. It is a very attractive paint scheme...with carbon coat and lime green accents on the frame and on the seat.

Review:
This bike earned five stars from our household because it's a good-looking, thoughtfully designed option for small kids who want a big-kid ride. Here's what we liked about it:

-- This bike looks more like a mountain bike. There are no plastic shields with cartoon characters and no silly padded bars.
-- It has a clever space for a water bottle, something few bikes this size offer. Our son loves it. He feels his bike looks like his older brother's model.
-- It comes in a hard-to-find 14" size, which is just right for many riders ages 4 and 5. This bike was a present for our son's fifth birthday. He was riding a 16" bike with training wheels, and although he had developed the balance necessary to ride without trainers, he was afraid to ride without them as his feet couldn't touch the ground. With this bike, he was riding without trainers within minutes.
-- Our son loves the bell, and we like the handle on the back of the seat. It makes the bike easier to load into the truck for family bike rides.
-- The seat is more comfortable than those typically found on small children's bikes. That helps-- our son rode nine miles on it one day. It's also a bit lighter than some.

We had one complaint: one of the pedals cracked after a few weeks. They were of a standard size, so we substituted one from an old bike. Overall, build quality on this bike is good.

So, if you're looking for a nice-looking "big kid" bike for your little rider, the Royalbaby is a fine choice.

I bought this bike in October of 2012 and wanted to break it in before writing a review so here we go. I have so far put hundreds of miles on this bike commuting every day to and from work while also using it as my main source of transportation since I have no car. I opted to use the fixed side of the hub and I immediately fell in love with it since it gives you such a greater sense of control when weaving through Chicago rush hour traffic. I recently replaced the seatpost for the Avenir 200 series 27.2mm, which is not only lighter but actually holds its position unlike the the stock seatpost. For them I chose to go with the Fyxation Gates BMX pedals paired with the Origin8 Pro-Grip Double Toe Straps. I also replaced the drop bars(which add a lot of unnecessary weight to the bike) with aluminium bull horn style bars.

Overall I recommend this bike to any city commuter, but my only advice is to change some components to not only improve it but also make it your own :-)

Review: I ordered the Six Three Zero bike for my wife. She's 5'7" and fits on this bike very well. It takes about a week from the order for delivery of the bike and I live on the East coast. The bike is pretty easy to put together. Very easy for someone who is handy. However, the company advertises that they will reimburse you for professional assembly so I took them up on that. You receive the bike about 75% put together and have to finish installing the seat, handlebars, front tire, front brake, and fender. This should only take you about 30-45 to assemble yourself, but calibrating the brakes can be a bit of a challenge so I would just go with the pro assembly. The bike functions as advertised. I got the battery-powered, 7-speed bike in Navy/Teal combo and it is awesome! You could easily ride without the battery. The battery only mode/throttle will take you up to 13mph on a slight grade hill, without peddling. Top speed with battery only has been about 16-17mph however. Great for cruising and flattening hills with the rider assist mode (1-5). This would be an excellent commuter bike or leisure bike. We have enjoyed for about a week now and look forward to updating after putting some extra miles on this one.

Review:
Professional Setup Mandatory! I say this because there WILL be additional repairs needed right out of the box. Mine arrived with destroyed upper headset bearings. After removing the battery, 10 of the bearings from the precision ring went everywhere. The brake calipers, though tight , were misaligned, and had to be totally reset. The main hinge is way too stiff, with no way to adjust it. The brake rotors do not have a uniform surface, and as a result , the brakes pulse, rather than provide even resistance when applied. Some bike shops will shy away from electric bikes, due to their insurance policies. Now that the nasty is out of the way, whats good about it?
PRO: The motor is very quick, and fast right off the line It is clearly marked for 36volt, 250watt, 20" wheels, just in case some cop wants to give you a bad time. The pedal assist modes are not lame, but quickly get you up to 14.5, 17, and 19mph with no trouble. The ride is stable, and fairly comfy. The seat and handlebars adjust easily for a tall rider, and still feels stable.
Included toolkit is adequate for basic maintenance, and doesnt feel flimsy.
CONS: Poor Quality assurance from factory, the bike is NOT ready to ride directly from carton. CADENCE SENSOR is very flimsy affair! If pedal assist isnt smooth, look at your crankset for a lopsided disc, its not bolted in place, just push it square and close to the sensor and boom, you have pedal assist.
In folded configuration, the battery connectors for the controller are unprotected, and there is no secure "home" position for the latch when the frame is opened.
Although this bike folds, it is still a cumbersome presentation, with no real nesting place for the handlebars it may be smaller than unfolded, but its still not what I would consider an ideal traveller.
Despite the affordable purchase price, the potential is there for needing to upgrade components in order to really be satisfied with the bike long term, or to cover damage from poor assembly at the factory. Is this bike suitable as a daily driver? I cant speak to that yet.

Review:
This review is for the Schwinn Huron 26 inch cruiser bike (one-speed and coaster brakes).

Great bike. Easily put together. Just a couple of minor problems that cost this review one star.

First, the kick stand is about 1/4 inch too long. With the kick stand down and engaged, the bike stands almost perfectly straight up instead of at a very slight lean. This makes it real easy to topple it. In fact, I stopped along the road to remove some debris from the road. A car passing by caused a burst of air and toppled my bike. The bike now has a few scratches that it should not have.

Second, within a few days I was having to air up the tires. At first, it was every other day and then became every day. I took the tires off and replaced the tubes. I put the old tubes in the bathtub with enough air in them to inflate them some. There were no outright holes, but you could see air bubbles all over the surface of the tubes. The rubber was just that weak.

Even with these two flaws, I love this bike.

Review:
I had been wanting to get back into cycling or a while, but couldn't justify spending [...]+ on something I might find I didn't enjoy. I was able to get this bike free through a rewards program at work. It has definetly been a great starter bike. I've got about 500 miles on it so far. I've had to replace one tire tube, but that was due to riding through glass on the road.

It was easy enough to put together, but having it tuned by some one who knows what they are doing will make a big difference.

I would also recommend the Schwinn cycling computer as a great add on (http://www.amazon.com/Schwinn-SW654-17-Function-Bike-Computer/dp/B000DZD3FQ/ref=pd_sim_t_1)

Only complaints are the bar mounted shifters which can hamper add-ons like aero bars and computers (I moved the left shift over to fit the computer). The front derailure leaves a little to be disired. Got it tuned by a friend who is a serious cyclist and it has stopped the chain from jumping off. But still doesn't shift very quickly. The back derailure by comparison shifts VERY quickly.

All and All a VERY goot starter bike and well worth the investment. Once you put a couple of thousand miles on it, then look to upgrade.

Overall I am very pleased with this purchase and have told a few neighbors about my sweet new ride. My only wish is that I had a basket or water bottle holder attached already, this bike is just bike-no bells or baskets, so those will have to be added on.

Review:
We were excited for the arrival of my daughter’s new bike. She has been practicing on her balance bike for the past year and was overdue for a real pedal bike. After researching some of the more expensive name brands for kids bikes, we decided to save about $50 and gamble on this lesser known brand. There also is an industry wide bike shortage during the pandemic so options were really limited for the timeframe we needed it in. Our daughter is a tall 4.5 year old so for a 16 inch bike we only anticipate getting about 1.5 years out of it considering the seat post is already adjusted half way up for her height. We ran into a snag with assembly which really had us disappointed. The crank arm on one side was not tapped with threads to screw in the pedal. Manufacturing defect and/or quality control issue that slipped through. So we were unable to attach pedal and unable to ride that day. Bummer. The manufacturer A11N Sports was very responsive to our issue and promptly sourced a new crank arm with threads to ship to us. It did take a few days for them to get the part from their supplier before they could send it to us. Due to the added complexity of removing the crank, risk of damaging something, and timeframe before I can actually get the new part in hand, I decided to return the bike to Amazon instead and get a replacement bike. We received the replacement bike, crank arm has threads, but the frame had a minor chip in the paint from shipping. I will say that on both accounts the bike was packaged very well, just this time a metal part rubbed against the frame chipping the paint. Sigh... I decided to accept this small blemish and assemble the new bike successfully. Assembly is straight forward as there is only the handlebars, front wheel, pedals, seat, and two reflectors to attach. It also came with a guard for the belt, but I don’t see the need to attach because the belt is mostly out of the way and is not greasy. As for her first ride, she was pedaling on her own with little assistance within the first 45 minutes. Again, she was well practiced on a balance bike before. As for the bike itself here are the pros and cons.
Pros: Lightweight frame and quality components (brakes, levers, handle bars.) Brakes work well and require just the right amount of effort on the levers to come to a predictable controlled stop. The belt drive seems to be a plus, no maintenance and no dirty grease to rub your leg up against. We’ll see how it works over time, but I cannot foresee any issues with it. It’s easy to pedal and has a freewheel just like any other bike. Paint schemes are nice and have a premium look, but I suppose this is subjective.
Cons: The the bike requires a slight lean forward to hold the handlebar grips. It is not as upright as some of its competitors, think seating position of a mountain bike. For a smaller child this may be a little uncomfortable. Our daughter had no issues however, again she is on the taller side at 46 inches with sneakers on. Also, the bike does not come with a kickstand, why not? We will be adding one.

Bottom line. Aside from the defect we ran into, and hassles of returns, assembly and disassembly, this is an excellent bike to learn good skills on. No coaster brake and no training wheels, both of which are unneeded distractions from learning proper techniques.

Review:
This is a solid starter road bike. It's very simple to assemble, but you should take it to a bike shop to have it tuned by a professional. The rear cassette on my bike was completely out of whack when I got the bike. It could barley shift gears. Once I took it too my local bike shop they got it working well for $20. I may swap out the cassette in future so the shifting is perfect, but for now it's okay. For $140 you may get some defective parts, but none of them are that expensive to replace or fix.

There have been a lot of complaints about flat tires and poor breaks. I have had no flat tires, and the tires are great. They are more akin to tires you will find on a Hybrid bike, but I like having the extra tread. The brakes are fine. My bike stops well. Again have them adjusted by a professional.

It's fast, fun, and looks great. I suggest taking off all of the stickers including the GMC logo on the front. The bike looks great without them. My local bike shop said for the price this was a very good bike. I would have given it five stars, but the defective cassette has knocked it down a peg.

Happy riding!

Review: I recently purchased this bicycle for my wife after she had taken my 15 year old Dahon and would not give back to me.  The Mariner D8 is a lightweight, solid bicycle that folds down to a reasonably small package, making it a great commuter bicycle.  In addition, the fenders do a good job of keeping the mud streaks off of clothing and gear.  The gear ratio settings are about right, allowing for relatively steep grades to be climbed despite having a single chain ring in the front.  The bike tops out at around 25 mph with respect to gearing, but 30 mph can be achieved going downhill.  The 20" wheels are large enough to avoid flipping or getting caught in potholes, while still being small enough for the bike to fold into a tight enough package for traveling.  The fold down handlebar stem is much improved over my older generation of Dahon.  The Mariner D8 has also proven to have the same dependable reliability and high quality I have come to expect from Dahon.  I have looked at Brompton's and they are nice bikes, but it is hard to justify double the price difference for something that is of equal performance.  Kudos to Dahon for making great bicycles and continuing to iterate on solid engineering and building on quality.

Review:
Pros:
- When unfolded the bike is actually a nice size even for an adult that is 5' 11". I'd imagine it would be a good fit for folks up to probably 6' 3".
- When folded, it is pretty compact.
- The folding mechanism on the frame is actually pretty sturdy.
- The bike was greased up properly.
- Derailleur was set up well and needed minimal adjustment.
- The rear break was set up well and needed no adjustment.

Cons:
- Steering column wobbles. Not a lot, but enough to make me feel unsafe. The folding mechanism on the steering column causes the column to wobble at the hinge (not the folding lock). I've adjusted the tension on the lock to be even tighter to the point where considerable force is needed to lock it in place but the wobble remains and the steering column feels unsafe. Its not that the steering column is loose in any way, its just that the folding mechanism has a bit of play even when hinge lock is tightened really well.
- Packaging: the Styrofoam inside is not planned well and does not support the bike well as it got dinged up during shipping. The rear mudguard came assembled but due to poor packaging it was twisted out of shape and digging into the tire.
- Quality control: The screw heads of the screws holding the rear mudguard were ALL stripped, someone at the factory really cranked up on them.

Review:
After messing around with Off-Road bikes and Hybrids for my 10 mile-a-day commute to work, I finally decided on this bike after some research and intense review searching. Much like most owners have already posted, this is a great mass produced bike for the money, although there are a few components that need to be adressed right out of the box, before you even assemble it.

Get yourself a set of new brakepads for the front and rear. The ones included do not have much grip and stopping can be a little slower than you might be comfortable with. Also, the hardware that holds the brakepads in place is flimsy and after two rides, my front brakepads had unscrewed themselves and became lost.

Replace the tire tubes before you even assemble the bike (700x28), otherwise you will most likely dealing with valve stem problems and flats that are very easy to get (if you are new to road bikes, this is common. If you can find those solid foam tire tubes that require no air, those would be ideal, since the bike itself is still pretty light weight.

Again, if you are new to road bikes in general, you might want to get yourself a new saddle. Specifically, one that can at least pivot forward since the included saddle only has one position, which can be uncomfortable for some.

Not too much of a big deal, but I also replaced the griptape around the handlebars in exchange for something with a little more cushion.

Assembly is quite an easy task for anyone, only needing a few different size allen wrenches and standard wrenches. More tools may be required if you want to tweak and tighten up a few other components, such as the cables, shifters, and gear mechanism (as the #3 gear on the left handlebar tends to slip off the chain and doesn't effectively do much).

I know that this all may sound not worth it, but once you work out all the little (inexpensive) kinks, this is a wonderful bike that will provide a lot of riding miles on it. Plus, for the price, it really is a good move. Great entry level bike for commuting or recreational riding! Also, I'm 5'10", and the large frame is ALMOST a little too large, so if you are any shorter than that, maybe see what a medium size frame does for you. I will be purchasing another bike for my girlfriend and since I don't believe in spending over $700 for a bicycle, I will probably buy another one if it needs replacing.

Review:
This is a very nice bike. When I originally ordered it it was advertised as a hybrid - it even says "hybridbike" on the frame - but I would say that it's really a classic road bike. That said, I like it a lot; it's fast, smooth, and great looking. It isn't much good on rough road, but I don't really go off-road when I ride. I now see that the ad has been changed and that the seller is more accurately calling it a road or commuter bike.

It comes with tools you need to put it together, which I ignored in favor of better ones in my tool bag , and a little hand pump. I tested the hand pump by getting one of the tires pinch-firm, then I put my electric bike pump on both tires to get them to proper pressure.

Assembly was not at all difficult. The video on the Amazon ad was a bit of help figuring out where the reflectors and so forth are supposed to go. The instruction booklet is nicely printed but poorly translated - I have no idea what the booklet is trying to explain about how to adjust the rear derailleur. Strangely, the front shifter is marked just "L" and "H", even though there are three front gears. This is not a big deal, I can tell where the shift lever is.

The overall quality of the bike I received seems very good, although the front wheel was not "true" when I first put it on - it wobbled a lot. I had the spokes professionally tuned, and while they made it much better, the tech informed me that the wheel was dented and looked like it had been damaged in shipping. The seller stood behind their product very well and immediately replaced the wheel.

Bottom line, this bike seems sturdy, everything works fine, and the ride is surprisingly good. I intend to put some miles on it.

Review:
This is my first E-Bike, but I have owned many bikes in my life so I can speak to that and the new experience of the electric motor.

At first I did not know what to expect but before I get into my experience, let me highlight my like and dislikes.

+ Design (looks like a motorcycle)
+ Powerful Motor
+ Good Range on 100% motor assist.
+ Integrated LED headlight, taillight, and turn signals.
+ SUPER EASY ASSEMBLY. (Took 5 Min)
+ Twist throttle (better than thumb lever IMO)
+ 28MPH top speed
+ Carries well uphill
+ Saddle leather and color
+ Bike color and finish
- Seat is not comfortable on long rides. (Anything more that 40min)
- No saddle bag or storage (unless you upgrade to the R750)
- No mirrors (must have when commuting on busy streets)
- Small rear taillight/turn signal

This little bicycle is good for leisurely cruises, short commutes, or to ride around the RV / Campsite. I got it to get back into riding bikes to help increase strength in my legs since I have joint problems so long walks are not an option. So this helps me get around and ride longer than I would normally with the electric motor assist modes. The assist has 5 levels, however once you go over 13-15mph, peddling is useless so at that point your just along for the ride.

This bike has good torque, I weigh 225lbs and it pulls me along just fine, the big fat tires does help give the ride more cushion for comfort and load. The seating position is comfortable as long at it is not for too long, but it is tolerable.

COMMUTING:
I used this bike for a few weeks to commute to work. I work about 10 miles from work. On the way to work it half flat with the other half being small to very steep uphill grades. On the return trip the up hills are down hills.
NOTHING BUT MOTOR!! So I wanted to test out the range to work and back, I was able to ride 5 miles around my neighborhood, then ride 11miles to work and back for a total of 27miles and still have 2 bars left on the battery. It took me 34min to get to work (up hills) and 15min to get home (down hills). The commute is mostly long straight roads with some parts being curvy so going full speed was not a problem.

AROUND TOWN:
If you are using this in your local park or streets with other bicyclist and pedestrians learn this bicycle before you get out there. It has fat tires and the turning radius does not make it nimble and if you don't know how to control the throttle or have the proper setting (such as cruise control) things can get dangerous quickly. It is basically an electric motorcycle and can get to 28mph really quickly so it is not a beginners bike for sure.

CLASS 3: E-Bike. (Check your local laws)

GET SAFETY GEAR:

I got myself a great E-bike helmet and some gloves incase I had a mishap. I recommend a good helmet, even a entry level motorcycle helmet is a good option, going 28 mph and being able to reach speeds of up to 40mph down hill, I would protect my head and wear some good jeans and a jacket. I ride at night too so I added flashing rear light to increase visibility.

All in all it is a great little E-bike, the convenience of 5 min assembly out the box and the on demand power with most of the bells and whistle make this a good option for a bike rider that is getting into the electric vehicle world.

Hope this was helpful!

Review:

Nonetheless, I was able to put it together looking at the wrong manual, which should be encouraging to any prospective buyer, especially because I had absolutely no prior knowledge about bikes. I took my bike out for the first time today, and I had the time of my life. (I'm an adult, by the way. Anyone can have a blast with this thing.) I'd recommend setting your bike to the lowest torque mode if you've never ridden a dirt bike before. My bike was originally on race mode, and I crashed pretty badly. The handlebars got scuffed up and the bike wouldn't run for about 15 minutes. But the bike worked perfectly after that period. It is very durable.

Riding on the beginner's mode, I quickly became used to rate of acceleration. I felt in control. The bike was incredibly responsive while turning and braking. The bike went over cracks, curbs, grass, hills, etc. like it was nothing. Today, I rode for about 45 minutes straight (probably 15 of those minutes at top speed), and the bike is still more than halfway charged. Its range is incredible.

I'm very happy with my purchase. I'd recommend this bike for anyone: kids, adults, experienced and non-experienced riders-- anyone looking for a good time.

Review:
[[VIDEOID:f1872989bfa1adc05eff7ccc700cb2bd]] 5/19/21 Edit: WOW, the price has increased by $100 or 62.5% in under a year. Granted, that is for the "updated" model but there is no description what that means. The standard model is not available. Not cool.

Note: I paid the full $160 for this bike, it was not a free promotion in exchange for a glowing review. I just love bikes and I think this is a great niche item for some people. Amazon initially said 1-2 months for delivery (?) but happily it only took 5 days. It's out of stock at idshomeusa website, so Amazon may be the only source for these bikes now.

This bike just came yesterday, so I can't comment about reliability yet. But first impression is: I REALLY like this bike, it's an amazing value! This makes 15 bikes in my garage, including numerous other folding bikes (AKA "Folders"), some are Dahons. This "unYOUsual" bike has a curved main tube and is styled much like my $700 Dahon Mu P24, and I think it looks great. The bike is painted black with green trim stickers. Out in the sun, you can see that there are actually green metal flakes in the black paint. Really nice touch!

Of course it's NOT a Dahon, and I knew that going in. This is a no-name steel Chinese clone bike and I had low expectations. Very likely, quality varies between bikes. I do all my own bike repairs and adjustments, so I expected some work would be required to dial the bike in, and I was right. This bike "is what it is". It's mostly steel and will eventually rust, faster or slower depending on your environment and care. This bike would be a poor choice to leave outside or keep on a sailboat. Most parts are either not upgradeable or not worth upgrading. I'd call this a great kid's bike, or for an adult, a fine weekend bike or one to keep in the trunk of your car for fair weather use, moreso than a hardcore commuter bike through daily rain and mud. From my experience, it pays to manage your expectations up front. Folding bikes bring pluses and minuses to the table.

UPS delivered my bike-in-a-box in good shape, with just a few minor paint scrapes. The cardboard shipping box had a split in it, so the paint damage could have been much worse, not worth returning IMO since there were no dents, damage and everything else was fine. Both wheels spun nice and true (no wobbling). Aluminum rims good, plain steel spokes bad (you want stainless). Plain steel spokes will rust/corrode eventually. (My single-speed Dahon boardwalk also has plain steel spokes, which have light oxidation after 12 years, always stored in the garage.) The "Wanda" tires look good and are supple and grippy. Tires say "Inflate to 43 PSI", which is a very low pressure rating. Better tires can handle higher pressure for less rolling resistance.

Both brakes and shifters required adjustment for proper function. Easy enough for me, but probably not for everyone. No special bike tools are required, just an adjustable wrench (or socket set), a Phillips screwdriver and a metric Allen wrench set. After centering via adjustment screws, the brakes work great, don't rub and are very quiet. Brake levers (handles) are aluminum, while the V-brake arms are steel. 6-speed Shimano grip shifter & derailleur work fine after careful adjustment. The 14-28 freewheel (not cassette) gives a nice gear range and replacements are widely available. The chain was clean and came with a light layer of lube on it. High gear on this bike matches pedal cadence & bike speed of a single-speed beach cruiser. Low gear is exactly half of that, suitable for some moderate hill climbing. Steel crank arms are 170 mm long. The non-folding pedals are easy to install if you know that the left pedal uses a left hand thread. The pedals do not spin freely (yet), they feel like the cheapest part on the bike.

The handlebar is a welded aluminum T: you can't change it but it works fine. It telescopes to adjust the height into a steel tube which hinges to fold down. The locking mechanism feels very solid, and lock tension is adjustable using two small Allen wrenches (photo). My headset (steering shaft bearings) came adjusted properly, no binding and no excess play. I rotated both brake levers and the grip shifter on the bar to my ergonomic preference using an Allen wrench. A bell and front reflector are included to mount on the handlebar using a Phillips screwdriver.

The seat is comfortable and came pre-mounted at the right angle on the long and large diameter steel seatpost. A red rear reflector for the seatpost is included. The seatpost has a diamond knurling pattern for the quick release adjuster to grip. There is a "Maximum height" line engraved. I'm 6'-0" and the right seat height is a half-inch above the max line. In my experience there no danger, there is plenty of seatpost left in there. I weigh 190 pounds and the bike feels solid and responsive, quite similar to my more expensive Dahons.

No tools are required to fold the bike or adjust seat and handlebar height. Both adjusters and both folding mechanisms (main tube and handlebar) feel solid. The wide range of height adjustments allows a folder like this to fit nearly anyone, I'd say from 4-1/2 feet to my 6 feet. My 11 year old is 5 feet tall and she has a blast riding this bike. Folding this bike is straightforward if a bit clunkier than my Dahon. The folded size is similar, except the pedals don't fold. It weighs ~32 pounds, 8 pounds more than the Dahon Mu (steel vs aluminum).
A few nice surprises: The rear-mounted kickstand has a locking feature to it, you kick the middle black lever, not the bottom of the kickstand. The bike has dropouts (mounting holes) for an optional rear rack.

Riding the bike is delightful and fun. It is smooth and responsive in all ways. Brakes and shifter (after adjustment) work great to deliver a pleasant experience. Note that 20" wheel bikes (Dahons included) have a different feel and more rolling resistance than bikes with larger wheels, say 26" or 700C. More pedaling effort is required, and the small wheels may feel twitchy to first timers. After some practice, "twitchy" becomes nimble and maneuverable.

Review:
[[VIDEOID:de50a126c97664e282301b389d327144]] This is the first bike I have bought for myself since 1986. My previous bike disappeared about 15 years ago and I have been without one since. I've gotten lazy and out of shape during the COVID era and decided I needed to do something to get active again before it's too late. I looked at a lot of bikes on Amazon trying to decide what to get, but this one managed to rise to the top based on features and price. I'm not going to be racing offroad or anything. I just wanted a basic bike suitable for riding around the neighborhood with perhaps the occasional excursion down a dirt road or path.

The features that attracted me were the aluminum frame with lighter claimed weight than most of the competition in this category, disk brakes front and rear, and front suspension. I would have liked both front and rear suspension, but they tend to get heavier in that category. I also figured the Schwinn name has been around for a long time, so that might be worth something. (Though it seems like most reputable brands of our youth have become just marketing tools for the same offshore goods as every other brand)

The box arrived in good condition within 4 days after I placed the order, including a weekend. There were few dings and scrapes on the outside, but nothing that appeared to penetrate to the bike itself. Unboxing was super easy: Opened the top flap and lifted the whole thing out.

Assembly was typical for any bike. Just attach the pedals, handlebars, seat, and front wheel. The pedals took a 15mm box wrench. Most of the rest of the bolts took a 5mm hex key. Some of the adjustments required a Phillips (or JIS) screwdriver. The quick releases on the seat post and front wheel work well and hold securely when properly adjusted. The handlebars were the trickiest part mainly due to having to get all the cables routed properly. It took some fiddling and I'm still not 100% sure I have them routed the best way.

At 5'7" with a 30" inseam, the height of this 27.5" version fits me just about perfect. I can stand over the bar flat footed and I just barely touch it. I wasn’t thrilled that yellow was the only color available for this model, but now that it’s here, I can say I kind of like the color. Certainly, easy to spot in a crowd. 😊

As you would expect with any new bike that you are assembling yourself, there were some adjustments that had to be made. The rear brake did nothing out of the box. Easy enough to fix by taking some slack out of the cable. The front brake kind of worked, but also needed to take out a little slack. The front caliper was not centered on the disk and pulled it sideways when applied. Even though this is my first bike with disk brakes, it didn't take long to figure out how to adjust it even without looking at the manual. I moved the brake levers around a little for comfort. The front and rear derailers both required some minor adjustment in order to get them to move through all the gear combinations smoothly.

All in all, I doubt it took more than an hour to get it assembled, adjusted, and road tested.

First impressions are that it is a decent bike for the money. There are a couple issues though. One minor, and one that I am debating contacting the manufacturer about. The minor issue is that the seat is crooked. It tilts to the side quite noticeably. (see photo) I tried loosening the adjustment/mounting bolt on the bottom to see if I could twist it straight, but no avail. I don't notice that much while riding (the seat isn't very comfortable no matter what), but it does make the bike look cheap or damaged.

The bigger issue is the forks. They have what seems to me like way more "slop" than they should have. Maybe it's because I am used to motorcycle forks which are pretty tight in comparison, but these have a huge amount of play in them. If I had to make a diagnosis, I’d say the bushings are either the wrong size or totally worn out. You can really notice by holding the front brake and rocking the bike forward and back. (see video) The whole fork twists due to the disk applying side torque. On top of that, the fork travel is quite stiff and they don't seem to do much other than make the front end feel looser. I am not impressed. Maybe that's typical of bicycle forks, I don't know. I haven't had much experience with modern bike suspension. I don’t notice it while riding though.

Others have complained about the manual not being specific to the bike, but honestly, I've never bought a bike that didn't just have a generic manual. (I haven't bought myself many bikes, but I have assembled a LOT of them for others over the years). I didn't actually even read the manual until after I had everything assembled, and it didn't really tell me anything I couldn't figure out on my own. If you are experienced with bicycle maintenance, you won’t have any problems.

Overall, it seems like a decent bike for the money. Pretty typical of others I have assembled. I haven't ridden more than a few miles yet, but it seemed to ride fine for the short tests around the neighborhood. My only real issue is with the forks. I may try taking them apart to see if there is anything that can be done, or contact the manufacturer to see if it’s normal.

I also just noticed the price jumped up by about sixty dollars since I ordered mine. Glad I got it when I did. I might have chosen a different one had it been at the new price when I was comparing.

Review:
I don't know how I should rate this. I enjoy the bike but it has a lot of kinks that the general consumer may not have the problem solving skills, mechanical ability or knowledge to correct.

I am a hobbyist both with cars and bicycles, so I would like to think I have a jump on the average consumer.

First off. This is a cheap, Chinese made bike. They have issues with how they package them adequately enough in some areas and not enough in others. There are machine work issues that need attention at the factory level. There is an overwhelming lack of attention to detail. Most of the bearings were dry. People comment on coaster brake and rear hub issues, this is because the thin layer of grease they apply isn't adequate for any kind of real friction and burns up fast. I repacked my rear hub with 800° Shell Gadus grease and haven't had issue since. The headset bearings were dry, there were metal shavings and burrs in the steer tube threads that needed to be filed down, and this was a small headache but nothing I've never done before. Threads cleaned, headset greased and set with proper preload. Crank was dry, greased that. Front hub was dry, greased that.

When I pulled it out of the box, front tube had a hole in it, front fender had a moderate dent in it, handlebar had gouge into the metal.

After a phone call to Pacific cycles in Olney IL by the number on the frame, I had a new tube, fender, handlebar, and groups in my possession.

When the fender arrived, it was the wrong color. After submitting photos of the bike to Pacific cycle via email, they sent me the proper color and let me keep the old one.

After the roads were clear enough here in northern Chicagoland, I took it for the first ride. Hit a bump and the rear fender mount at the chain stay broke. This caused the fender to drop onto the tire and then go under the bike's rear tire. I somehow didn't crash.

Phone call to Pacific cycle, they asked me why I am having so many issues. This is a brand new bike less than a month old. I ordered it on new years day. This is the 26th of January now. I was then accused of fraudulent warranty activity because I buy a lot of Pacific cycle products, mostly Schwinn. They always have issues and I use that warranty to make sure my stuff is perfect.. But they agreed to send me a rear fender in "good faith". That fender arrived bent. After straightening it out with a heat gun as to not crack the paint work, I installed it.

Currently there have been no other issues. Bike rides great after all of this but chances are other consumers will write it off after one or two small issues.

If you buy this, understand you will be fixing some things before you ride it. Take advantage of your warranty and fix the issues.

Review: I ordered this about 2 weeks ago for my daughters birthday. I opened the box today to assemble the bike and there was, to my surprise, a red bicycle inside the box. For some reason, Amazon sent me a Diamondback Octane 24" bike instead of the blue Lustre 24" girls bike. My daughter wanted a yellow bike, but they are very rare, so since this Octane is virtually the same as the Lustre, and it has yellow on it, I've decided to keep it and give it too her. Assembling the bike was pretty simple, taking just over 2 hours to complete. I spent some time adjusting the brake shoes, but the derailleurs seemed to be working fine out of the box. It comes with the tools needed to assemble but you'll need some sturdy scissors, or wire cutters to cut through all the cable zip-ties that are used for shipping. The red, rear reflector that mounts to the seat post is not large enough for the seat post. I had to swap in a longer screw in order to make it fit. Otherwise, I'm pretty pleased with the middle-of-the-road quality. I was able to buy a decent Mt. bike with an aluminum frame for about $250. Due to the couple of tiny issues with assembly, and the huge oops on Amazon's part. I'm rating this as a 3. Most buyers would have sent this back due to the wrong bike being shipped.

Review: This review is for the Eurobike S7 with the normal spoke wheels not the 3 spoke wheel. I have only ridden this bike for 20 miles but at this point I am happy with this purchase. I wanted a bike for light trails and pavement riding that was better than the department store bikes without breaking the bank. This one has met my expectations. It rolls easy and is smooth ridding with no binding or dragging. After very minor derailleur adjustments the gears shift smoothly and precisely. Assembly was easy and I found all nuts and bolts on the pre assembled parts were tight from the factory.  The weight of the bike, ready to ride, was 40.6 lbs which isn't bad for a steel frame. The tires are a small knobby design, perfect for my usage. The handle bars are 31.8mm at the stem. I have replaced the stem and handlebars so I can ride in a more upright position. The only negatives I have found was slight damage to one of the decals on the bike upon arrival and the brakes are not very good. How well it holds up over time remains to be seen but for less than $300.00 I am quite pleased.

Review: I purchased this bike with pretty high hopes, being my first purchase online to purchase for myself. Unfortunately in the first week the 3 speed control broke. I made an appointment at our local shop and had it repaired. Once repaired I went out for a ride. The bike was riding pretty good, then came a noise in the rear axle area. I contacted the shop again, the rear axle had bent. Sounds pretty crazy. I weigh 185, the rear rim was not damaged nor had a damaged tire. So I had it repaired and contacted Omibike. I supplied the receipts and was given a refund for the repairs made. Next I went for another ride. Another noise from the rear axle area. This was from bushing on the lower right side in the rear frame area, this is second hand information, but, my local shop said "make sure you are sitting while riding, the bike is not designed to stand while riding." This is something I've never been told by anyone.  Upon contacting the seller we had issues with understanding what parts were needed, this was very frustrating, no parts can be found in the U.S. The bolt was found not properly tightened at the shop (3rd visit). It still has a noise. The rear axle is popping. The ball bearings have been changed, spokes checked, axle checked, rim balance checked. All is okay, No charge from the shop on the 3rd visit.  With the virus outbreak this has taken over a month for this whole ordeal, which is very acceptable. I'm currently asking for a complete refund. With approximately 20 miles or less this has been a complete nightmare.

Review:
Love, love, love this bike. Our toddler young'un received this as a gift from his very generous grandparents, and this has quickly put all his other toys to shame. He now tools around the driveway over and over and over, while we try to catch up with him so that he doesn't fall.

1) Does the young'un fall all the time?

Yes and no. At first, yes, all the time, because he didn't know how to maintain his balance. Now, he doesn't fall as much because his balance is better, but he also keeps his feet on the ground more because he is more scared of falling. So, I think he's getting better and learning his balance more, and that's the cool part of this bike--he's really getting his confidence up. In fact, when he was riding a friend's tricycle at the park the other day, he somehow knew exactly what to do even though the tricycle is obviously different with its pedals and third wheel.

Given that falls do happen, we use close supervision and a helmet, and all the running to catch up with him is quite the workout.

2) Is the bike sturdy?

It's very sturdy--that might be the most impressive part of this little bike. I can't believe how well made it is for a tiny balance bike. (In fact, I hadn't even heard of balance bikes until we received this.) The wheels are rubber and are thick, which is great for durability and also balance. I believe this will last for as long as our young'un can use it.

3) Will the kid want to/be able to use it for a while?

This is a trickier question, but I think so. Our young'un is tall, but he's all legs, so this fits him for now at approx. 2 1/2 years old. But, we have the seat very low. I do not believe a small 2 1/2 year old would be able to use this, but that's just my own impression.

As for how long he can use this, the seat goes up quite a bit, so we're hoping that he can use this at least for a couple more years. By then, we think he'll be balancing well enough for a real bike... maybe?

If we hadn't received this as a gift and if I had known what a balance bike was, we absolutely would have bought this for our young'un. It's a really nifty idea, and Schwinn has executed it extraordinary well. Highly recommended!

Review:
At first, the power and quickness of the electrical assist surprised me, but I got comfortable in no time. I hate to admit it, but I find myself using the electric mode (which requires no pedaling) more and more everyday. It is much like a scooter and very fun. Overall the bike handles like a cruiser and pedals with ease given 7 gear options. The shifter works well and does not get hung up too often. 

The bike is heavier with the battery but it is not that noticeable. The battery can also be removed when you know you won't be using it, or when you're charging it. It is GREAT that this has disc brakes to slow down the power of an electric bike. I kept track and for over one week, 1 hr/day, ~15 miles using assist and electric only mode, the battery indicator shows half life. It folds down to a more compact size, which is so useful if you want to transport it in your car, or fold it up for storage.  We got this initially for grandma to be able to keep up with the kids.  It turned out to be too high for her legs at the lowest setting (she's 5'1"").  My wife (5'4"") has no issues and loves using this bike with the kids. Overall, we never expected to be so won over by electric bikes; this is a great ride, and at a great price for what you're getting. There are other 'bells and whistles' included like a very loud alarm and front/back lights/accessories that add to overall biking safety and comfort.

Unboxing the bicycle was easy and the package included all the tools needed for assembly. It took about 30 minutes to put together (handle bars, seat, front wheel, fenders, pedals, air pumped into tires). There was no instruction manual in the box and the QR code led to nowhere, but I was able to find how-to videos on other similar models.  I also later found that they emailed me some tips, which were helpful.  Speaking of the seller, we contacted them because of an issue with the battery and they resolved it quickly; we were happy to see that they offered good customer service. Would be a perfect commuter's bike.

Review: I want to first start by saying I've been an avid bike rider for decades and actually know few people who even ride as much/often as I do. I switched to a full suspension (MTB) bike many years ago not because I ride on trails but because the roads in California suck. Yes, they're more expensive but the ride is much better. So I spent months researching the type of electric bikes out there including batteries, location of the drive motor, brakes, price and availability because of the shipping problems. Most experts will tell you that the mid drive "Defang" motor is the best way to go. This bike has it. The 17.5Ah battery is one of the best only beat by the 20Ah but very few bikes have it now and they are in short supply because the Chinese are using most batteries for the car manufacture. I took my first long ride, 15 miles, and only used about 25% of the battery charge riding using full power only...amazing. The best brakes: hydraulic disc brakes front and back which brings this fast bike to a stop quickly and I'm 6'3", 250 lbs. Which brings up the 27.5 wheels on this very well-made bike. I had a 26" bike before and looks like a child's bike compared to this bike. The bike arrived very quickly and unlike other bikes it arrived mostly assembled unlike many others that require an hour or more to put together. Now let's talk about an issue that many are fearful of dealing with...customer service. I had a problem installing the kick stand which was totally my misunderstanding and contacted them (Yvonne). She quickly responded with details and pictures to help me understand the way it goes on. I had another small issue that she also immediately solved for me with kindness and understanding. In every aspect starting with the well-made manufacture of the bike with parts that make this a great bike for anyone. If you're considering an electric bike, I highly recommend this bike and company.

The display is clear and sharp, and the orange color bike is good looking. The bike was fast, but it also had great great brakes to slow and stop. I did not have to adjust anything on the bike. It even has a setting for intensity of the front and back lights. Stay tuned after I get pedals and try the assist levels I set from 1 to5, although you can also set it 1 to 3 if you want.

Review:
Full disclosure, I have never owned an electric bike before, have not had to deal with customer service and am pretty easy to please. All that being said, this bike is AWESOME! For starters it came much quicker than expected. The instructions are decent, could be better though. It took a while to figure out that there is a protective disc to be removed and discarded from each side of the motor near the wheel. I also put a couple of parts on backwards and then was like 'huh, well that doesnt seem right". After staring at the instructions for a few minutes (per section) I got it. Again, full disclosure, I was super high when I put my bike together. That being said, I was able to put this bike together with pretty much no problems despite being high, it shouldn't be a problem for you.

Now for the meat and potatoes of the review, performance. I have been riding and playing around for a couple of days now and I am very impresesed. I wouldn't say that I "stress tested" the bike, but have put it through various normal conditions such as sidewalks, grass, dirt roads, gravel and.... well, some puddles. Nothing crazy, but all things faced buy average commuters. I found no immediate sign of air loss in the tires, no bending or shifting of the alignment and no loose electrical componants.

The interface is easy to understand and you can easily feel the motor kick on once the bike chain has been engaged for about one second. I actually looked at the controls and thought "easy peasy, no need to read how to use them" and spent the first day using only peddle assist mode. I figured the setting below "low" was basically "still on but no power to the motor", for just the light and horn or something. I was mistaken. I realized just as I got home from my first day of trying out my new bike that there is an *actual*, twisting throttle EXPERTLY blended into the inner part of the handle bar. I just so happen to grip my handles super close to the edge AND DIDN'T EVEN NOTICE THE THROTTLE. I had a good laugh about that. The mode beneath "low" would be "throttle only". Womp womp.

So yeah, super great bike. Goes pretty fast, handles well, looks nice, read the instructions.

Edit 1: I do have one tiny complaint. Merely aesthetic but it says "ELECTRIC VEHICLE" in small but bold text on each side. I actually taped over that with black electrical tape. Every time I went to lock up the bike I saw the words thought to myself "Well, glad they know which bike to steal." I have had a $40 bike lock cut with bolt cutters for a $100 Walmart bike. Again, a very small complaint but the advertisement seems like extra incentive to would be thieves and it terrifies me 😱

Review:
Don't let the "low" price fool you - this bike is insane value.

I have never owned a Schwinn bike before, and I'm super impressed with what I have seen so far. Prior to getting this bike I had a really old Raleigh road bike and new Diamond Back mountain bike. For the last year, I spent the first 6 months on the road bike and the last 6 months on the mountain bike as I would navigate the 10 mile trails around the lake.

I have been using a Suunto Spartan Trainer watch and the Under Armor 'Map My Ride' app on my phone to monitor my stats. Because I have done the trail so often I know where I should be at 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 35, and 40 minutes. Typically, it takes me right at 50 minutes to do the 10 miles, but the last few times on the new Schwinn, I am coming in at 48 minutes. The bike is super light which is so refreshing, but its weight is not light enough to take two minutes off my ride

It is an interesting discovery to see that the features that many people use to determine a purchase decision may not actually be the right selection criteria. I say that to say that my mountain bike has 24 gears and the new Schwinn only has 14. Initially I thought that this would be a step down - boy was I wrong...

The crazy thing is that I go faster with less effort on this bike. I know its all about ratios, but I don't understand going faster with less effort - that boggles the mind. I know the places on the trail where I would have to rise up off the seat to deliver more power to keep up with my son, but with the Schwinn I don't have to.

The seat is super comfortable, and without making any fit modifications other than seat height, this is perfection for my leg length etc.

I absolutely love the gear shift mechanism with the brake leaver integration, although it is a little difficult to brake and downshift at the same time. Not really an issue for me since I know the trails well and can plan in advance.

The only thing I am not too terribly keen on is that the brakes are no match for the disc brakes on my mountain bike, but A. That is an unfair comparison and B. I can always get different pads.

In closing - for the money this bike delivers huge value, and when I mentioned earlier about the low price, I am saying that in reference to the fact that you could spend that money on a range of other bikes even from lower price outlets like Walmart and never find your self enjoying this level of quality and craftsmanship.

If you are reading this review - go ahead and get yourself one :)

Review:
I bought this for my son to get him started in road racing at an entry level. I was prepared to pay $1,500 to $2,000 for a quality entry level bike that he could get started on. I have been racing and competing in events for over 30 years so I know what i know about bikes. I picked up the bike and took it for a spin, after having a set of Look pedals installed, and OMG is this a screaming value. After 10 miles, if you had told me this bike was $1,600-$1,900, I would have said that its was a great value. Right after did another 10 miles on the exact route on my CF Pinarello for comparison. The geometry of this bike is more relaxed (comfortable to ride).
The only thing to do is select your pedals and put some good tires on it. To be honest, in my opinion, the tires are virtual garbage. I am going to leave them on the bike for for now so he gets some experience fixing the inevitable flat tires coming is way on the road. Every rider needs to learn this anyways. The sooner the better. The handle bars and seat are great. The 4700 banged off shifts virtually as well as previous generation Dura Ace and now the cables run under the wraps...progress. The brakes were strong and felt smooth right away. I bedded the pads for him. The larger hoods are amazing. All in all and at this price point, with a carbon fork, add a few bucks for your favorite skins, put on some quality pedals and ride it for years. This is a SCREAMING Value. I am taking it for a longer ride tomorrow and really pound on it but I do not foresee anything short of a unexpected failure to change my mind after my quick spin today. Feel free to ask any questions of me and I will share my opinions.

Review:
[[VIDEOID:3c004b4915fc69f9dffa751eff71206c]] Short Version: Super light weight and so smooth with the Grease-free, maintenance-free belt.

Long Version:
As beautiful as it is functional, it is a modern marvel of bicycle engineering in a pint-size package. From its hand-brushed and lacquered aluminum frame to its belt drive, it leaves kids begging for more while their parents simply stare in awe. Fully loaded with 16″ air tires on aluminum rims, front and rear V-pull brakes (no coaster brake!) and a remarkable grease-free, maintenance-free belt drive, weighs in at about 13.7 lbs., about 10 lbs. less than most lower-end 16″ bikes. With a minimum seat height of 19″, is the perfect first pedal bike for any balance bike graduate.
While the brushed aluminum frame steals the show, the belt drive is the icing on the cake. In addition to being grease and maintenance-free, the belt essentially prevents any snags on clothing and, unlike a chain, will not stretch or easily slip off the gears.
Another main draw is the lack of a coaster brake. Required by the CPSC on all bikes with a minimum seat height of less than 16″, coaster brake often cause more harm than good for graduates of balance bikes who are accustom to hand brakes. While learning to pedal, many kids accidentally pedal backwards. On a bike with a coaster brake, pedaling backwards causes the bike to stop abruptly, causes them to lose all their momentum, while a bike without a coaster brake is much more forgiving and keeps moving forward. Instead of a coaster brake, it has two short-reach, front and rear, V-pull brakes.

My 4yrs old is riding it an hr daily. I will continue to update this review.

Update 5/18/20: I've a chance to compare it to a Woom 3. The gear ratio on the Woom is very low. Kids can't go fast on the Woom. The components on the Woom are much smaller and lower quality, the chain is noisy and always rubbing the guard, the guard is flimsy, thin, moving around as you ride, it's not screwed to the frame, just sitting on the chain. While 2lb lighter is great, until I realize where they cut corners and use cheap parts. All the parts on the Belsize are from brand names; Continental belt, Kenda tires, Tektro brakes. The Flat handlebar on the Belsize is probably too aggressive for kids under 5, it forces the kid to lean forward too much. The good news is that it's a 1" standard size, and you can replace it for around $20. Kids sit a lot more upright on the Woom 3.

Update July 2021: It looks like they addressed the biggest flaw. I see that the 2021 version has a Riser Handlebar, much better than the Flat one, but still on the aggressive side, perfect as a mountain bike though. Too bad, my son won't have this new version to test because he is now super happy with a Woom 3. He stopped using the 2020 version since last summer because of the bad Flat handlebar design.

Review:
I researched mountain bikes in the $300-400 price range for a couple months, and determined that the Diamondback Response line were the best ones available at this price. When it suddenly dropped under $275, I bought it immediately.

Assembly was quick and easy. The only part for which you might actually need the instructions is the front brake assembly. Everything else is dead obvious.
