Review:
This bike far exceeded my expectations. I am primarily an in-city and path rider and have appreciated the performance on poorly maintained streets. I've purchased bikes locally for several hundred dollars more than this that have had worse components than this beauty. I followed the advice of several reviews and went ahead and purchased a more comfortable seat. At first the double clamp mount didn't seem large enough for the seat, but as I started to tighten the clamp onto the rails of the seat, the rails just seemed to fit themselves in. I also added a 9" kryptonite lock that fits comfortably mounted high on the inside of the frame toward the front tire without getting in the way (on a 20"/XL bike). The assembly instructions are not sequential, but are part-by-part and are all over the place. I chose the order to do things pretty incorrectly and often had to back-track. However, even without prior building or tuning experience, the documentation was sufficient to help me figure out how to build the bike and tune it to my exact preference. The brakes, derailleurs, and position of everything feel perfect now, which I have never done for myself before. In the end, the experience of working through the build was a definite gain in terms of my confidence to keep my own bike tuned.

All of the parts feel very solid, and the gears shift cleanly and without much noise (none from the rear derailleur, and a little from the front (Shimano) derailleur going up from first to second gear). The derailleurs most definitely need to be adjusted in order to work cleanly, so expect this extra slightly involved task. Also, the handles seem perhaps on the firmer side and my hands feel sore after a long ride even with heavy gloves on. I might go in for some soft handles come springtime.

Also, the snow can be pretty heavy here in Boston during the winter, and my road bike has an impossible time after a storm, but this bike handles moderate snow and ice well.

In all, I love this purchase and definitely trust this company! Also, to respond to a previous complaint I read about parts coming poorly packaged to where they were bent and/or scratched, I'd say that the manufacturer has perhaps responded the this because the packaging seemed sufficient and nothing came blemished (more than expected) or bent (at all).

Definitely purchase this bike if it's in your budget range.

Review: This is a great bike for the price. It came mostly already assembled which was a nice surprise. I saved alot of money assembling the rest myself. I am a rough rider so I have already bent and damaged the derailleur which I think should have been better designed. This is a great bike to build on and tweak yourself. It really goes over all the bumps and does what other bikes can not do. I am very impressed with this bike. It is a very fast bike and I have had a tone of fun riding it. I wish the seat was a bit taller and the crank shafts a bit longer as I am 6'3" and around 230 pounds. You really can't beat the price. A similar bike that is swift and has a full suspension like this goes for around $700-$800 so I feel like I got a great deal. I saved on not having it assembled and that was a huge plus. Really the only downside was that its made in China so everything is delicate and the quality of the metal is not as good as it should be. Mongoose has always made the best bikes in my opinion and its just wonderful that they offer a full suspension 29er at this price.  The bike is delicate and its noticeable how things made in China are done with the minimal amount of material in order to save money so things on this bike bend and break easily when you really bang it through trails like I do. I suppose it would be fine if you ride on the streets mostly.  I just wish it was built stronger and built to last longer without needed frequent maintenance and repairs. I understand how the manual recommends frequent preventative maintenance but I just don't have the time to do all that and I ride really hard. If I could have afforded a really expensive bike I would have still chosen a Mongoose. I plan on adding to this bike so its more durable and can take the abuse I give it when I ride it in the woods and up and down mountains. I like to go over rocks with it. I will be a loyal customer of Mongoose for life. The feel of a real Mongoose once you get to riding is unlike any other bike. You just can't find a great bike like this for a better price.

Some changes that they seem to have made since they released this,

Rear derailer is now the shimano claris, and it shifts very well.
The front derailur is not the claris model (atleast the one I received) and is not all that great.
The crank set is the same as pictured.
The shifters are also the claris model and no longer have the thumb shift (good thing). They are now integrated in the brake handle so that you are able to shift while in the drops.

The wheels came with schrader valve tubes instead of presta. I believe this to be a mistake since it is quite difficult to remove and replace the tubes when I went took them apart to true the wheels- took me taps of the hammer to get the schrader valve out of the rim.

Overall its a good bike for the price (purchased at $350), I was hard pressed to find anything similar within the price range. Will update as I put some more miles on it.

Review:
First impressions of the bike out of the box was very good.

Assembly was straight forward and fairly easy for anyone familiar with hand tools. I made sure the bike was assembled as perfectly as possible.

After taking the bike for a few local rides on some dirt\paved trails, totaling approx. 40 miles, I decided to take it for a solo over-night bike-packing trip up at Grand Island, Michigan.

My trip was to be approx. 28 miles of dirt trails, with an elevation gain of approx. 1,900 feet. Some of the sections of the trail were fairly rough with fallen branches, etc.

My gear consisted of a standard backpack with sleeping bag, clothes, food, water, and misc. camping stuff, while my 6 lb. tent was strapped to the rear bike rack.

This is how my trip went on this new 3-speed cruiser:

After approx. 8 miles I noticed a lot of rattling on the back rack. Unfortunately, one of the upper mounting bolts had completely unthreaded and fallen out. Fortunately, I had some extra small bungee cords and was able to support it enough to continue on.

After approx. 20 miles, my handlebars became loose and starting tilting up and down. Shame on me for not carrying the proper tools to tighten it up. I managed to ride this way for the final stretch to the end.

Overall, it was a GREAT experience with the bike.

After I got home, I evaluated the bolts that hold the back rack on and concluded that the stock bolts are too cheap, so I replaced them with automotive grade bolts.

I realize that this bike was not designed for this type of use, hence the reason I am impressed with how it performed overall.

I really loved sitting upright and enjoying the sights while pedaling the backcountry of Grand Island. In my opinion, although I had to walk it up several hills, it was much more enjoyable and comfortable than riding a mountainbike.

I intend to take it on more bike-packing trips with the addition of some panniers.

I LOVE the bike. The reason for deducting a star is because I feel the back rack and bolts are a bit on the cheap side. I am doubting the strength of the rack to support my next load of approx. 30 lbs., but we'll see.

And the 3 speeds were GREAT. There were several small hills that I was able to peddle, which I would not have been able to with a single speed cruiser. Shifting was spot on.

At this price, it's a great bike for the money.

Review: To all of you considering an electric bike and anticipate using it as an economic form of transportation between say home and work within a 10 mile range - consider this one.  I find that it was relatively easy to put together given that they supply you with the necessary tools.  After a month of back and forth daily use I find it very easy to use and robust.  I especially enjoy the power assist being a real boost bonus to get you off a quick start - especially at street intersections.  This really helps when you have not cycled for a number of years like me.  It also puts you ahead of other cyclists - even though it may seem like you are cheating.  I have to go over some pretty raunchy road surfaces on my route and the suspension really comes into its own at this time - without which the ride would be horrific.  Bear in mind that the battery does not get charged by your pedaling and that you will have to recharge it every other day if you travel around 5-10 miles round trip per day.  The headlamp works great during nighttime or earlier morning hours, but be sure to get a rear light or one on your helmet for nighttime travel.  Highly recommended for those returning back to the world of cycling.

Review: Ok, I looked for the right bike for months and ended up getting this one. I love it now but I had to make some adjustments. First, this is the worst bike seat that I have ever experienced. It tilts upward into your pelvic bone making you feel both violated and bruised after every bump in the road. I ended up replacing the seat after two rides. Second, this bike will not propel you on its own, you need to do some pedaling, even if it is light. The motor disengages if you do not pedal. This isn't a big deal, just know that this is not a true electric moped. Third, it is pretty hard to find a basket for this bike. You can't really attach one to the front (the bag you see is where the battery is stored) so you have to do diligent measuring to get one for the back. Lastly, I recommend that you add some accessories to make this bike usable beyond just riding around your neighborhood. If you want to use this for any kind of commute you will want to get a flexible rea-view mirror, a rear light, and something to hold your stuff. Also, make sure that you have a medium-wide water bottle because regular bottles will fly out of the holder.

Review:
(Bought this bike on a different family account for free shipping at the time. )

What to say you know? Two years of experiences with this bike. There are other options on Amazon for this price point now but I doubt any of them could beat out the experience I had with this model. The bike I received was never lacking in performance and has taken an enormous amount of abuse from an inexperienced biker. This was my main method of transportation daily off and on for a period of two years plus. At the height of use I was riding this bike 100 miles a week for a period of about three months straight. I may be looking to upgrade soon but it is still effective and I will continue to ride it till I upgrade.

-It has comparable weight for other bikes of this price point.
-The 21 speed felt effective to me. Eventually I was biking at the top/hardest gear with continued effort but that was after a year plus heavy riding.
-The brakes got a bit wonky 1 year+ into it. Needed to replace the brake pads at a reasonable point but could never quite calibrate them perfectly after that, resulting in a reduction of efficiency. This is most likely due to me riding the bike hard but not cleaning any mechanical components relating to the brake mechanisms.
-Had many flats on the back tire but now I suspect this is due to me riding way under the recommended PSI constantly. In addition to that I frequent freeways with excess debris.
-Never replaced the chain and the crank/pedal assembly seems loose. It kinda wobbles a bit when peddling. This has led to the wear and tear of the protective metal pieces on the outside of the chain/pedal area breaking and me subsequently cutting of the remaining metal. This could probably be avoided by getting proper maintenance on the bike at a local shop, which I never did.
-T-Boned one big truck at about 15MPH and got away without anything breaking.
-Rainy seasons are common where I live. Never had a slip with this bike (though I do ride cautiously). Eventually I did begin to oil the chain during the rainy season to prevent speedy degradation.

For around $200 I put this bike through everything a beginning biker would throw at it. Due to price point I would assume that some delivered bikes could and would have deficient aspects but that was definitely not the case here.

Review:
I got this bike at a local K-Mart for $86 yesterday, plus $13 for two years of no-cost repairs and replacement (if it totally breaks) warranty. The bike was already assembled by them, so all I had to do was take it home. The brake feel is fine, but it is a little squeaky sounding. What I did notice is this bike is heavy as hell. When lifting the woman's Pacific Stratus, which I also purchased ($59, assembled), it felt like it was almost a 1/3 lighter than the Chromium.

The bike has no manuals that came with it, so I guess I am on my own with figuring out how to work the gears on it. This is the first bike with gears I ever had, well the first bike I have had since I was in my early teens back in the early to mid 90s.

I found it hard to move up hills (a lot of work), but I noticed adjusting the gears it was a bit easier. Maybe it's a combo of not being familiar with how the gears work, and also that I am out of shape.

I do like the look and feel of the bike. So tentatively I am giving it 4 stars, as I can not ding it for being a novice newbie. I took it out on a small trail yesterday very briefly, and it felt just OK.

I will update this review in the near future, as I get back in shape with it, and I learn how to properly use it. If anything seems amiss with it, I will drop it off at K-mart to be sent out for new parts and adjustments for free.

So 4 stars because I was on a hyper shoestring budget, the bike looks nice, got 2 years of repairs and/or replacement warranty, and it seems like a decent fit for me. I will include some pics of this bikes in my review.

Update -> August 3, 2011:

Added more pictures of the bike for close ups of the bike. Since the last review, I took it out a few times on some simple trails, rides fine. I adjusted the brakes, and they don't squeak as much (they still squeak).

Things I do not like: The seat is extremely uncomfortable and will soon be picking up a gel seat cushion. After an hour ride, my rear will hurt for a day or so from the seat. The pedals don't freely spin, which sucks when your foot slips off the pedal. The bike weighs a ton, and that added weight makes hills even harder. I had less trouble up hilly terrain on the Pacific Stratus, than on this Chromium. On the upside, it is giving my legs a killer workout.

Review:
To my pleasant surprise,this bike actually arrived way earlier than I expected. I have assembled many bikes before this one but this one was about 75% pre assembled already so it only took me about 45 minutes to finish it. I test rode it the next day and it rides very well. Its my first e-bike and I thought for sure I could just press a button and ride without pedaling. Nope, no such luck. But it assists as you pedal.The more you pedal, the more the motor kicks in.When you stop, it stops. Pretty cool concept insures you still get a workout.Its a nice looking bike too with thick tires, spring shock forks,disc brakes and speed shifter gears. It even comes with a night headlight and push button horn. The kickstand is nice too. The seat is basic and entry level.You wont stay on it all day but comfortable enough. I will warn you. This sucker is heavy with the motor and rear wheel so if you live on the above the first floor, you may want to leave it downstairs chained up. The battery has a theft proof lock with keys so you can take it with you .

A great bike for a great price with a ton of bells and whistles. Don`t pass this one up. Happy Trails!

*********************UPDATE**********************

Yes you can ride it like a scooter without pedaling!!!
After a little online research I found out all you have to do is hold the “-“ button after the initial turn on. When the “low speed “ light goes out, you are in Cruise Mode and when you twist the throttle and the bike will take off without you pedaling. The downside to this is that your battery life will be shortened probably to about 2 hours off a full charge. Enjoy the ride!!!!

Review: I have been cycling as my major form of transportation for over 30 years. I've had $300 bikes up to $1000 bikes so while researching E-bikes in this ballpark, believe me I took like 2 months to make a decision, I decided on this Hittroad Safari E-Bike. The assembly was pretty easy, Gratefully Hittroad has a YouTube channel with 2 videos on how to assemble. This works out way better than the hand drawn manual that comes with the bike as well as more information that is not included with the bike. All parts were included (And then some. I was baffled by the fact that I had several parts that are never mentioned in the manual or videos) It is an absolutely good looking E-bike (I purchased thee white/blue with the black trim), the qualtiy of the bike is also great. The bike seems very sturdy.  I will admit that I had some issues but every time I contacted Customer Service they were very quick to follow up and ship 2 parts that I needed- A+ for their customer service. I took it for a test ride just out on my street, which is pretty flat, but has two streets that are long hills (My older bones can't make it up the hills anymore - haha)  I live in PA and it's still NOT spring here so I haven't been able to do a long run just yet.  However, after practicing, I found the ride to be great, the front shocks work great as do the disc brakes. The LED works very well as does the modes switching from bike only, hybrid of peddling and electric and the pick up on the electric only is great! One attribute I really love is being able to adjust the handlebars and move them up it made the ride very comfortable! The padded seat isn't bad, but I have a feeling it's going to hurt on long rides. I just purchased a more ergonomical seat. The battery was fully charged upon receiving but I have some colorblindness and the battery indicators always look red to me. Customer service asked for a photo then helped me out.  The battery was very easy to remove and charge. On my test rides I got up to close to 22mph on pedal assist, 20mph on full throttle, and then I just rode it without the use of the motor. I made it up the hills very easily. That's what really sold me on it. I will not be using this on trails so I can't speak to that. I purchased this on Amazon and it was $250 less than on Hittroad's website. So far I'm happy! I really recommend this E-Bike!

Review: I've had this bike for a few months now and feel like I can give a good honest review.  Pro's:  Was easy to assemble.  The front brake will likely need to be adjusted.  Use the micro adjuster on the brake lever - no problem.  The bike is not that heavy for an e-bike and looks for the most part like a regular mountain bike.  I like this because I don't look like some lazy slob out on the road and in reality I tend to ride the bike without the e on a lot of the time.  The bike rides conventional enough to basically ride it like a normal bike - which is what I was looking for.  The controls are very simple and bare bones.  No KW meter or any of the tech you will see on more expensive bikes.  The battery seems to be very good and easy to charge.  I have yet to worry about running out of juice.  Con's:  I have really only had one issue with this bike.  It will not engage in 6th gear.  The chain just bounces between 5,6 and 7.  So I'm down like 14% of total gears available which is a bit of a bummer.  I get the feeling it's probably a bad sprocket and not the derailer as the other gears are flawless.  Overall - I really enjoy riding this bike and would recommend if looking for a simple bare bones e-bike that rides very much like a conventional mountain bike.

Review:
I've had this bike for a couple weeks and I've taken it out quite a few times and pulled a child's trailer behind it, here are my thoughts:

1) Packaging: Not the best. My rear rim was bent slightly, but nothing terrible. Adjusting the spokes will bring it inline.

2) Assembly: No instructions. Not the hardest thing I've ever assembled, but not the easiest. The little things like adjusting the brakes and gears is the hardest part. I still don't have the gears right, I will be taking it to a bike shop and having everything adjusted.

3) Look: The bike that arrived was blue/purple, not the cooler greenish color on the website The fenders didn't initially fit over the wheels very well, but a little adjusting and they were fine. Still, a nice looking bike. No kickstand though.

4) Comfort: Very comfortable, and easy to ride. Many of the gears overlap, this probably has 10 true gears. I am 5'11" and the 20" bike is a good size for me, but it was too tall for my friend that is the same height, I have longer legs and a shorter torso than he does.

5) Final thoughts: Well, the bike is a good buy, but figure in an extra $50 and have a bike shop either assemble or tune up your assembly, you'll be glad you did. I tow the Instep Quick N EZ kids trailer behind it and the two of them work together well.

I would recommend this bike, and if the ladies version goes on sale, I will probably but it for my wife. I really recommend having someone that knows bikes either look yours over after you put it together or take it to a shop and let them do it.

Review:
To start, I bought this bike about 2 years ago after my old bike was stolen, so maybe some of this stuff has been fixed.

This bike has tried to kill me on a regular basis. It started off ok, and then after about half a year, the bottom bracket gave out. This means that now, when I put too much force into pedaling (usually at high speeds) the pedals will "skip", causing my feet to slip off, resulting in... pain.

In terms of general maintenance, the rims of mine must have been defective because there are all sorts of sharp edges within them, which pop your tires. After I filed them down, things have gotten better though.

Additionally, none of the screws seem to stay tight.
Brake lever attachment screws: No matter how much I tighten them, which involves disconnecting it from the brake itself, they always loosen up and fall off in about a week.
Handlebar screws: Remember how I said the bottom bracket broke, well when it does throw me off, the handle bars rotate in their attachment, because the screws loosen themselves, and without a something to brace myself on, I get thrown over the handlebars.

Also, if you get this bike, be sure to swap out the brake pads. Something about them will make it so they are unable to grip the rim of your tire well.

Finally, the derailleur are absolute crap. the front one doesnt have the range to effectively reach all 3 gears, so I have had to adjust it to just work on the first 2, and the back one only works for the middle range of gears.Because the bottom bracket is broken my bike pedals wobble when i pedal, and this wobble can shift the front gears for me in between strokes, which causes more skipping.

Well, that covers just about every component on the bike that I've had trouble with, and just about every component on the bike as well.

DONT BUY THIS BIKE!

Review:
I rode bicycles and hauled them off to rail trails and rode them around the neighborhood for years. Then I got into motor scooters about 13 years ago. I always like the little ones that carried me out into the rural backroads, but they had their limitations for distance. It took all day to get out into the country on one and just was not feasible. I looked into hitches and racks but the cost was ridiculous. Eventually I got bored with it all after many years and went back to bicycling after a long hiatus. But the back roads and hills beckoned and I longed to ride them again but at my age and fitness level I knew it was not possible. I got a folder bike and loved throwing it in the car and driving out places to ride. Then one day I saw ads for electric bikes and started reading. And discovered folder electric bikes! And then I discovered this EB5. Wow! I am a short woman who is pretty fit but I had to be able to lift my bike up into my truck to get it out to those lively rural areas. I can handle this bike's weight. 15mph on a bike feels like 70 in a car! Plenty fast for me. The range on a 250 watt battery is 15 miles but after riding I can already see that my range may be further because the little bike just flies along just peddling with the power off. I took a 5 mile maiden voyage and learned that this thing flies if you hit power while peddling. It is an unnatural pedal rhythm so I am reserving it for hills. Human legs cannot maintain that pedal action on flat ground. So do not pedal. Keep your legs still and hit the throttle to ride it like a scooter. Or turn it off and ride it like a bike. Those are your options. I charged it for 5 hours on set up and pumped the tires to 45psi with a gauged bike tire floor pump. This is the recommended psi on the side of these tires. My valve stems looked strong so I hope I don't blow a tire as others have reported. Keep your tires properly inflated otherwise you will get what they call a "pinch flat" where the tire is under inflated and the inner tube inside gets pinched by the rim. Don't look at the tire and assume it is properly inflated. Get that tire pump gauge on it. Decent pumps are about $25 and get a floor model. Check your tire pressure each time before you ride. I have made an acquaintance already with a bike shop that sells and services electric bikes because changing a rear tire on these takes know how. I also am going to baby this battery and only charge it until the green light comes on. I know with cell phones if you overcharge then the battery gets drained. I wanted to purchase a spare battery to extend my range but haven't found them for sale yet. I did buy a 12v/AC adaptor so I can charge the bike in the car on trips. Sold on Amazon.

Bottom line: love this bike and done with gas scooters and their gas and license costs and insurance costs and stink and noise. I have regular bikes, electric kick scooters, and this electric EB5 and it gets me where I want to go. It is a single speed but you won't care. It is so much fun!

Get some strong bicycle lighting front and rear and some handlebar bags and some bags for your rear rack. All found at your local bike shop. I added a regular bike rack as they are out of the Swagtron model.

Eventually I may upgrade my tires and tubes but only when necessary. Put on a rear bike mirror and a better bell maybe and some water bottle carrying devices and spend some hours grinning.

I do not worry about breaking down because-hey!-it's a bicycle and you can ride it home or call an uber or a friend and it will fit in their cars and you will be ok no matter what. I got the matte black and feel like Bat Woman cruising the streets! Love love love this little thing! A life changer. Can't wait to explore on it. SO MUCH FUN! 🚴

UPDATE: I did find a replaceable battery on the Swagtron website for $129, so it is on the way. This will double my range for those rural excursions. I am also pulling out my hi viz jacket from my motor scootering days and looking at a helmet that offers more protection than a bicycle helmet but not as heavy as a motorcycle helmet.

UPDATE 2021: Well, my first black bike got stolen so I recently acquired a black with yellow trim version with a removable battery. I blacked out most of the yellow decals to get back to simplicity. Put it together tonight and I have to say that the box was crisply intact and its contents expertly wrapped. So far, so good. I am looking forward to taking it out on a spin tomorrow if the weather cooperates.

If you want a quality electric bike I think that this model is great as it is a folding smaller model that is easy to transport and keep in a small apartment. Though small, it rides like a regular bike.

As an older small woman I could not begin to heft a larger bike into my suv to ride anywhere besides right around my area. Too heavy for me. So keep that in mind too. This bike size is manageable for that.

If you get this model be sure to get the removable battery model in case you need to swap out or want to extend your range.

Review: I am not a cyclist or bike expert by any means, but I've taken this bike out for a quick ride for the first time today and I'm pretty happy with it. I rode about 3 miles, so not much, just to get a feel for it. The seat is uncomfortable, and I expected that honestly. I've never sat on a bike seat that was comfortable. I've got padded bike shorts on the way to solve that problem. The grips are basic, so I ordered some ergonomic ones. I wasn't sure if it would come with pedals, so I bought some nice ones. It did in fact come with pedals, pretty standard ones, so I think I would have bought nicer ones any way. Now I have spares if I ever need them. For my height (I'm 6'2), I think the handle bars sit a little low, so I've ordered risers. The front disc brake made a scratchy sound the whole time I rode, but that may be a minor adjustment I need to make, not sure. The ride was smooth overall. For someone like me who's just getting into biking, the price was right for what I'm getting. This is not a thousand dollar bike, so having to make some minor tweaks here and there was expected. As I learn more and become a better biker, I'm sure I'll start to notice stuff here and there, but I knew that going in. I totally recommend this bike if you want to start riding and just begin to understand the sport better. If you're already a pro, get a pro bike. For a beginner, you get a lot of nice stuff for the price compared to other "cheap" bikes. It folds, has disc brakes, 27.5 inch wheels, and what looks to be pretty decent gear shifters and derailleurs. Not a bad purchase at all.

Review: I have no complaints about this bike. It’s a very capable light trail and paved trail e-bike. I have added accessories such as a wide saddle seat, a phone mount, a stem extension for the handlebars,(I’m very tall 6’6”) front/rear mud guards, and a cargo rack made for a full suspension bike.I also upgraded the front and rear shocks to air shocks. I had to modify the rear shock mount and fabricate my own bracket so I could put a 190x50mm rear shock on it. The rear shock that comes with it is 170x60mm which is nearly impossible to find in a replacement air shock.(Paselec if you read this you need to put a more common sized rear shock on this bike so people can upgrade to better shocks without a hassle) JUST WANT TO MAKE CLEAR THE SHOCKS IN MY PICTURE ARE NOT WHAT COME WITH IT, AND THE REAR SHOCK MOUNT BRACKET WAS FABRICATED BY ME. The shocks that come with the bike are perfectly fine, but I wanted a more plush comfortable ride so that’s why I upgraded to air shocks. I also added a voltage/capacity digital display so I know exactly how long I have with the battery when I’m out riding. The display that it comes with only has battery on it with dashes to let you know where the battery level is, it doesn’t show how many volts/percentage is left. I’ve had this bike since November 2022 and no issues so far. Also customer service is actually really good. They do whatever they can to help you, which isn’t very common these days.

Review:
I like this bike!
I drive trucks for a living (27 years now,) and being stuck at a Truck Stop (TS) really sucks. The food is repetitive, the environment is crap, and the socialization is awful (mostly drivers griping about their company, or inconsiderate civilian traffic.)
I've always tried to keep some form of alternative transportation onboard, just to get away from the TS, be it Roller Skates, Skateboard, Scooter, or a Bicycle, just SOMETHING! The problem with a regular bike is that you have to strap it onto the deck behind the cap, where it is exposed to tje weather and collects road grime. This is REALLY hard on electronics and shifting mechanisms. So I thought about a folding bike, to keep in the cab. Well, that didn't work with this one either.
I digress...
The bike is awesome and really fun to ride!
It fits well into a 20" bike bag, I bought another "bulk sand bag" (a tarp bag for transporting bulk sand in, like 2000 lbs worth) to wrap around the nice bike bag. No road grime here!
Ok, so I have put about 50 miles on my bike and she runs great! The long stalk on the handle bars takes a bit to get used to, may be that is why it is called an "Ostrich?"
There are 5 different setting for pedal assist (PAS) which actually give you a better top-end speed than the throttle does. There isnt ANY specific setting for "Throttle Only" just the PAS settings, BUT the throttle is a thumb switch, similar to some 4x4 ATVs, when activated, overrides the PAS setting. It is VERY easy to go from full electric to PAS and back again, just press or release the throttle.
Top-end speed on full electeic is 20mph, maybe, PAS about 23/ 25mph. I am a fatty, 6'0" 240lbs, so I am not getting her full potential.
I cannot comment on the range yet, as I have not had the opportunity to test that aspect of the bike.
The "control display" is not truly waterproof, after being left outside in one rain, there is moisture on the inside of the display. If you leave your bike outside, wrap it up or seal it with some clear silicone caulk.
I am goimg to replace the seat, I always do on my bicycles, not because it is bad, I just like comfort. It is ok, but there are better.
I am also going to replace the folding pedals, the stock ones are made of plastic and feel "spongey" (is that a word?) when you really try to power-pedal... they feel like they want to collaspe under pressure. So, I found some aluminum ones on Amazon.
All, in all. This is a great beginner e-bike that won't kill your budget. 20mph is a good speed for a Bicycle, considering it is 5 times faster than walking... and if you NEED to go faster because you're late, START EARLIER!!! (This goes for your car also...)

I will update this review later after the new seat and pedals are installed, and as I stress her out.

If you're just getting into e-bikes, I would recommend this one. She's solidly built, good gearing, good speed, good brakes.

I have not found a way to hack the electronics for better speed yet, I am not that smart, but I'm studying. The "average" hacks on YouTube will not work on this bike. The speed sensor is on the front tire, and there is no "pot" screw on the controller.

Review:
This is my first entry into ebikes. I have nothing else to compare it to other than my standard mountain bike. Here's what I think:
Pros:
1. It's very fast. I've gotten it up to 32 mph on a flat paved trail
2. It's mostly assembled. You just have to install handlebars, seat post, fenders and pedals
3. The range is decent. I went for a long ride totaling 20+ miles, with 2 bars left on the battery meter
4. Comfortable to ride. Takes bumps very well
5. Has rear rack, head light and tail light included
6. Is actually okay to pedal like a regular bike without using the motor (good if you drain the battery)
7. Folding option allows additional flexibility for storage
8. It looks really cool. I've had random people comment on the looks
9. Price is very good for the quality and features
10. The disk brakes stop the bike well
11. The battery locks inside the frame making the battery harder to steal off the bike
Cons:
1. The bike is bigger than I expected. This is not a small compact commuter bike! (See photo comparing size against a standard mtn bike with 26" tires)
2. It's hefty! Like 65 pounds. Even with the battery removed, it's still heavy
3. The handlebar riser stem is insanely tall for some reason. And it can telescope even taller! Did they make this bike for Shaq? I cut 2" off to bring it down to a more normal height
4. Folding it in half is an awkward effort. Don't expect to do this quickly
5. On hills, you are pedaling to help the motor rather than the motor helping you to pedal. The motor isn't strong enough to take you up hills under battery only, so you must pedal to get up steep hills
6. The computer settings aren't the most apparent, and the instructions don't do a great job of explaining what each setting does or how it affects the ride
Overall, I'm very happy with this bike. The cons above aren't really anything bad, just some things to keep in mind or get used to. And yes, I've ridden this bike without using any electric assist just to see how hard it is to pedal. Surprisingly, it's not hard to pedal at all! I even pedaled up some medium hills without too much effort. Steep hills are another matter- it only has 1 front ring gear so you wouldn't want to pedal up a steep hill without the motor helping you. One thing I recommend is adding a smartphone holder and rearview mirror to the handlebar. In all, you will not be disappointed with this bike!

Review:
Just received the Giordano Libero 1.6 - 56cm today (it's for my daughter, she's 5'9") and I must admit I am impressed, and my daughter absolutely LOVES IT! The pictures do not do justice to this bike, it truly is a nice piece of equipment.

Some of the details that are not easily seen in the pictures are, for example, the handlebar tape, it looks like typical white tape but it's a nice soft and textured tape with a tropical (floral) pattern on it, as it gets dirty the pattern slowly appears. The "o's" of the name (Giordano) have the Italian flag colors in them. Between the red and white parts of the bike there's a section in black with the word "libero" repeated several times in a discreet and elegant pattern. The Velo seat is actually fairly comfortable, and has a very nice race design to it, including a measurement scale on the rails to fine tune your seat placement (forward or backwards). The crankarms are white as well (really nice touch!).

In all aspects this bike looks and feels like a much more expensive bike than it is. The integrated shifters/brakes (or brifters) are quite nice and work exceptionally well after being properly adjusted and the shift indicators are easy tho see since they are bright pink/red on the black background. The Shimano 2300 front and rear derailleurs are good entry level quality (lowest level for Shimano, but still good overall) for anyone trying out road cycling and the wheels, albeit heavy, are strong enough for the typical beginner rider to work with. The 6061 aluminum frame is strong and yielding enough for recreational purposes (I believe it's the same frame as the GMC Denali Pro, same angles and material but the Libero is MUCH MUCH nicer in appearance due to the Italian inspired racing designs - both bikes are made by Kent Cycles).

Assembly was quite easy for anyone with some experience with bikes but if you want this bike to perform at its best (and you're not familiar with fine tuning) you should take it to your local bike shop to get it running perfectly. I was amazed that the wheels were true right out of the box (but surprised that they were Schrader valves instead of Presta) Also, this is the type of bike that if you start getting serious about putting lots of miles on it you can slowly upgrade components with the help and advice of any decent bike shop out there.

Now, there's three items that are somewhat disappointing about this bike, but they're not that big of a deal. First, the pedals, they are heavy and the typical steel "shin scrappers", with no toe clips or straps, which was weird because the GMC Denali Pro (which is in all ways is a cheaper bike) has decent clip/strap pedals included (entry level stuff). The second thing are the brakes, they work fine but they are "ugly" and clunky and don't have a quick release system on them, it's something odd since everything else on this bike is quite nice and clean, another oddity about the Libero is that the GMC Denali Pro has actually "nice" Tektro brakes (entry level stuff) with quick releases... If Kent only had put the pedals and brakes of the Denali Pro on the Libero 1.6 this Giordano bike would've been as close to a perfect buy/value/performance as you could get. Lastly, the fork, even though it's an all aluminum frame, the fork is made out of steel, it's strong but heavier, unfortunately this is a little costlier to upgrade, but all in all it functions as it should.

So, if you want to do some immediate, and somewhat inexpensive upgrades, to make this bike feel and ride like an excellent road bike I would recommend upgrading the brakes (something less clunky and lighter) and pedals to toe clips/straps or even better go with clipless (this will cost a little more due to the purchase of the pedals and specific shoes) also take off the kickstand to shave a little weight off too and of course, talk to your local shop.

The Giordano Libero 1.6 will not disappoint the entry level road cyclist.

Review:
I am a road warrior and I bought this bike to help me get exercise on the road as there are bike paths near where I work. My main complaint about the bike is that "as is" the seat isn't quite as high as I would prefer and I am 5' 9". I have the seat and handlebars slightly past the max lines and I purchased a Schwinn seat with more padding from Walmart and that helped make it as high as I want. Know that this bike, while it looks like a mountain bike, is not meant for much off-road riding and it comes with a sticker that tells you that. But it will take reasonable bumps and has decent bump-reducing suspension.

So I would buy it again, especially since I don't see anything quite like it out there. And I did have a question via email that was answered quickly.

That question was regarding a bracket that is meant to protect the rear derailer. Well, in my case, it did its job and got bent in the process. Because of that, my rear derailer wouldn't go to the smaller, faster, cogs. Once I removed that bracket, everything was fine.

I also purchased the bike bag for taking it on a plane, but I keep the bike at the hotel on the weekends (I am long-term at the same hotel). I have not yet needed to put it on a plane and am slightly concerned about that.

Review:
Best bike I've owned so far. Love it, switches gears pretty easily. Goes great on trails or cement. Can't wait to try it on sand.

First of all, this isnt some $1000+, lightweight fancy pants bike. If that is what you are looking for then why are you looking at a $300 bike in the first place. This bike is sturdy and tough, it has some weight to it but that is because its solid.

Talk about shipping，The box had some dings and small tears but nothng that worried me. Taking the bike out of the box, it was nice to see is was very secure. No loose pieces, everything tied and wrapped together. Assemble was a breeze. The rear wheel is already on. Installing the front wheel was easy but take your time. The handle bars just needed aligning and tightening, pedals were properly marked with arrows indication which side and way to tighten, and the seat was quickly installed.

FINE TUNING. The rear gears and rear brake were already perfect and didnt need any adjustment. The front wheel did. The rotor was dragging against the pads big time. It took me about 5 minutes to loosen and adjust the front caliper to get the proper alignment and then adjust the pads so the wheel turned freely.

FIRST RIDE. The brakes will need some adjusting afterwards. Just because they are disc brakes.

It gear shift works great, uphill climbs are a piece of cake in high gear, mid range is great for cruising. Easily completed 5 mile trip first time out with no issues.

Ride it and have fun, like it was designed for.

Review:
NOTE: Basket and bell not included.
I bought this directly from Sixthreezero in the 24” 3-speed model in to replace a 26” 3-speed bike that was just too big for me. I am 5’2” and older, so my legs and feet aren’t as flexible as when I was younger. Although Sixthreezero recommended the 26” for my height, I noticed the relatively large frame size of this model and chose the shorter bike to be sure I could plant my feet flat when seated on the bike. It’s been several years since I rode a bike, and I feel more comfortable starting and stopping on this bike than on my previous bike. The 26” in this model would have been too big for me.
Assembly of this bike isn’t difficult. The instruction book is just okay, but the company has many videos on how to assemble their bikes and adjust components. The multi tool and hex wrenches provided are barely adequate; you will probably do better with your own tools, if you have them. With the help of the company’s videos and instructions, I was able to complete most of the assembly myself. I needed my boyfriend’s strength and his crescent and socket wrenches to get the seat tightly attached to the seat post. I also needed his help to close the seat post quick release clamp. I had trouble getting the handbrake adjusted to where it was usable, and the company’s video for this wasn’t very helpful, but I found other videos and articles that I pored over, and I spent a few hours tinkering and tweaking and finally got it working.
The bike rides and shifts smoothly, and the coaster brake and handbrake work as expected. A nice advantage of the 3-speed is that it’s fairly low maintenance, since the gears are inside the rear hub. The riding position is upright and comfortable. Because of my need to have the seat so low, I don’t quite get full leg extension while pedaling, but I expected that. I’m still getting used to the overall feel of the bike, but the seat seems comfortable enough. This bike is a beach cruiser style with a rather heavy steel frame and wide tires, meant for relatively short, casual rides, best for paved roads and maybe some easy rail trails.

Review:
Great bike. I was looking for a bike to ride on the ride across Iowa this summer (RAGBRAI) - around 400 miles in 7 days. My wife has a nice hybrid and my mountain bike wasn't going to do the trick even though I had changed the tires and tricked it out for road.

I found this bike at a fantastic price so I bought it.

I put it together in about 30 min. No problem. I did take it out and ride a couple of times before having it tuned. It does need to be tuned!!! After the tune up, it is a great ride! Shifts smooth, great handling.

I am a 250lb man. I put a gel seat cover on it to help the "break in". I now have well over 500 miles on it. It still rides nice.

My mountain bike had shocks in the forks and under the seat. I do miss the shocks as this bike definitely transmits all of the city road bumps right to your buttock area!

We have 2 - 50 mile rides under our belt now. Somewhere around the 40 mile mark I start to get uncomfortable. Perhaps loosing the 50+ pounds as I plan to do riding this bike will help that out.

I am 5'10" and opted for the 57cm frame. Glad I did. It seems big and when straddling it flat footed I have to take care to not crunch the family jewels, but it rides comfortably. I am a fairly large man at 250lbs and wanted enough room on the bike. My mountain bike felt a bit small.

The only thing I think I would change is the gearing. I wish it had a touch higher gearing. Granny gear (all to the left or low gear) is very, very low! It is great for climbing those steep hills, but while it is easy to pedal, you are barely moving fast enough to keep the bike upright! Side by side riding with my wife at 18mph, she is pedaling slower than I am. I think I have enough power to have a higher gear ratio and still be comfortable.

I do love the bike including the looks. It is flat black with silver/white lettering. It isn't flashy like a lot of the fluorescent framed bikes out there now. The breaks work VERY well! I have to be careful not to squeeze too hard like my old bike with rim brakes. I have slid the tires several times on dry pavement when stopping - and that's not easy with a 250lb man sitting on a bike. We got caught in a 20 minute shower last week while riding. No affect on the braking at all! Still stopped very quickly and easily. My wifes wet rims and rubber pads were a different story!

Review:
Let me start by saying the delivery was really fast on this bike. I ordered it on a Thursday and it was delivered on Sunday at lunch by FedEx! It took me about 45 minutes to unpack it and assemble it. The assembly was easy and the included instructions was fairly clear and helpful. A helpful note on assembly is the forks are turned around backwards in the box so spin them around forward before you install the handle bars !
I’ve put over 50 miles on this bike in the one month I’ve had it . I’ve road it on asphalt, dirt, gravel , and grass. I’m very pleased with how long the battery stays charged and I’m impressed with how easy it goes up hill on the trail when using the peddle assist.
Now for the bad . I read other reviews before I bought this bike stating the back tire went flat quickly. Well mine did too! The tube in the rear tire got a hole in it after the first 25 miles , I got a replacement at Walmart that had slime in it and now 25 miles later it’s doing fine still. My only other complaint is it does seem like the handle bars are sort of low and I feel like I’m humping over some riding it , but it’s no huge deal
Overall So far I’m very pleased with this bike . Ive took it on two twelve mile gravel and dirt trails at mammoth cave National Park and it did great ! The battery seems to last about 20 miles which is more than I’m ever gonna ride in one day. I installed a inexpensive speedometer as soon as I got it and it seems to top out at 18 mph with myself weighing almost 180 pounds . The brakes could be a little better ! Sometimes it’s a little slow to get stopped when your doing 18 mph! I gave this bike 5 stars for the following five reasons: how fast it was delivered,how easy it was to assembly, how powerful it is, how easy it is to operate and how long the battery holds charge !

Review:
It's been 11 months since we bought this tandem monster bike and it's been almost a year of extreme pleasure for us. We love it so much that just like with our dog, we buy little gifts for it. And it's no longer an ''it'' because she is now Agnesaurus rex (Tyranosaurus agnes didn't sit well with the stoker). We bought the bike knowing it's a beach cruiser pumped up with 21-speed gearing so there were no surprises. We knew she won't be a Colorado made-to-fit titanium creation. Anyway, here's our experience with Rexie:

1. Delivery was advanced. It was the biggest and heaviest parcel I've ever received. Inside was a well-padded, partially assembled, gleaming red and black and chrome monstrosity.

2. Assembly was intuitive. I've assembled my growing family's succession of bikes over the years and I consider myself competent by now. I didn't even read the instructions until I read the incredulous reviews about it. I'm keeping the sheet for laughs.

3. I did go to a bike shop! I may be mechanically inclined with all the right tools, but three things I want a pro to do for us: (a) shifting system fine tuning, (b) brake adjustments, and (c) truing the rims. I cannot compromise on these three things because for the very first time, I will be responsible for the safety of a VIP stoker. Read #5 below to see just how VIP she is!

4. Before riding, we researched everything about tandem bikes. We learned in theory how to mount, dismount, how to tackle different terrain and surfaces, and the *very important* communications skills between captain, stoker as well as with other road and trail users. Then, with helmets on, we put theory to practice. One thing I modified from the online instructions: Extra stability and safety during mounting and dismounting are attained if I sit on my crossbar, feet firmly planted to the ground, and brakes applied while the stoker does her preflight ritual, such as plunking herself on her saddle and putting both feet on her pedals..

5. Upgrades and additional accessories: (a) Rear view mirror for stoker - that's her job. (b) Ibera system of rack, saddlebags and trunk. (c) Sunlite front rack. (d) Voyager low riders. (e) Shock absorbing seat post for stoker. (f) Vitesse wider saddle for stoker. (g) Sunlite swing back handlebar for stoker with ergonomic handles. (h) Sunlite adjustable handlebar post for stoker. (i) Schwalbe 1.5''road tires that added speed and reliability. And (j) a brass ding-dong bell for me!!! So you might have surmised by now that we ride the enhanced and upgraded Rexie to other zip codes, camping along the way. Not a single breakdown, not a flat, no incidents at all.

Yes, we're not just happy with out tandem bike, but we have already placed our lives and safety in her hands, I mean, handlebars, and in our Garmin GPS. We highly recommend this bike. It's not the very best money can buy but we're not into competitive or off road riding.

Review: I bought the Framed Minnesota 2.0 from my lbs and love it!  This beast rolls over everything I throw at it. I ride a Trek 29er with 2.25 tires and the difference is night and day. With the 29er I have to pick a line and avoid bigger rocks and roots; with the 2.0 I just run them over. I had a 26" full suspension a few years ago and the 2.0 kind of reminds me of it with the ride quality. I haven't even noticed the 2.0 has a rigid fork as the front tire absorbs a lot of the impact. I had no plan on getting a front suspension fork, so I got the 2.0. If you want a tapered head tube so you can switch from the standard fork to a EFBE approved carbon fork or Rock Shox Bluto, you'll want to get the 2.2. Awesome bike at at awesome price. Why spend twice the money for a name brand bike; the Framed Minnesota 2.0 is just as good imho!

Review:
Bought this as an XMAS gift for you 5 year old:

PROS: looks good, comes with water bottle
CONS: SO DARNED HEAVY EVEN I HAD A HARD TIME GETTING TO GOING - I mean, this is heavy and the crank shaft for the pedal was low quality. The most important part of pedaling a bike is the transition from 11 O'clock to 1 O'clock - that moment when your foot gets over the hump. On the bike we got, the gear had about 2 inches of give at the top. This meant that when my kid, (or myself) was trying to "turn the pedal over" it would often just fall backwards. Did I make that complicated enough? Simply stated, the bike is HEAVY AND difficult to pedal resulting in a negative feedback loop. If you are ok with slowly getting to a maximum speed of about 2 mile per hour, then this is the right bike for you

Review:
I'm really enjoying this bike! I've had it for about a month and a half and it's been great to ride around town. My mountain bike wasn't getting much use because of back pain issues, but I missed biking around and I figured a cruiser bike's upright seating might be more comfortable... and it is (for me). It was a little adjustment to learn how to use the coaster brakes, but I've got it now and I'm comfortably cruising around town and by the beach.

I love the sea foam green color with the black hardware details and kick-ass whitewalls. One of the things that initially attracted me to the bike was the clean look, without flowery decals and stuff like that which really aren't my thing. It came mostly assembled and the rest was very easy to put together. The trickiest part was finding the correct height for the handlebars and seat. :)

It did come with a little scratch and finger print in the paint on the frame... but it was under in a part I wouldn't normally look at, so that's not a huge issue. It was missing a nut and bolt to attach the front reflector. I emailed and a replacement was sent very quickly, free of charge.

Regarding the size of the bike: I'm 5'10" with long legs and I fit, but I wouldn't push the height much more than that. It's good for me, but I'm not sure how tall you could be and still ride comfortably.

If you're looking for a nice helmet to go with it, I'd recommend the Nutcase helmet in Cherry Blossom: it's comfy, has good specs, and looks cute with the bike (nearly the same color: very slightly bluer).

Review:
I just recieved my new Mongoose Impasse 29" mountain bike.

I haven't riden it yet due to illnes but I'm anxious. It was easy to assemble for someone handy with a few tools. It required a metric wrench, metric hex "allen" wrench and a screwdriver.

There was no recomended rider height for this bike so I took a chance. If your jeans are over 32" long you'll be OK, I'm a little short but I'm keeping it. All the faux componants are there for a $1000+ bike and they look good.

I'm a little sensitive to false advertising so when I noticed the "ALUMINUM MONGOOSE" sticker attached to the "STEEL" rear suspension fork it set off my BS detector. The front fork is also steel which might be understandable for strength. The steel kickstand should have been aluminum even though it's not part of the frame. The seatpost,rims, stem and handlebars are aluminum. Overall my bike and I could stand to loose a few pounds but we'll work on that together. One of the plastic brake lever adjusting screw holes is striped out and I'm in the process of trying to get them to send me a replacement. So far no responce. I'm not sending the bike back for a minor defect.

I'll let you know how it works as soon as I get back on the road.

Carl

Update
After a few miles on the Mongoose Impasse I'm satisfied with the value. Everything works as advertised.It has a very soft smooth ride and at the same time it feels tight and responsive. My previous suspension bike had a tendancy to bounce and jiggle around a little. This one just absorbs the bump and dosen't oscillate. I didn't even try the seat it came with. I bought a split gel padded one while I was waiting for shipping. Customer service at Mongoose was very good to deal with. They are sending me a replacement brake lever no charge and offered to take the bike back if I felt it was too tall. I declined. The frame it self isn't a tall frame but the 29" tires make it stand high. With that in mind I'm changing the steering stem to a adjustable one that will help my riding position. Also the kick stand is just wrong for the bike, It's too short and easily bent backwards alowing the bike to fall over if it's bumped or not parked just right. I understand "REAL" mountain bikes don't use kickstands and they are just thrown down anyplace by "REAL" mountain bikers. For the rest of us civilized city folks who eat with a knife and fork the kickstand is a usefull accessory. I may call Mongoose and ask for a replacement.

Review:
I went with Diamondback because a co-worker, who is a very serious bike rider, and who has a few kids, told me that it would be a great bike for the money. I read several reviews on this bike about bad packaging and missing parts, so I was a little apprehensive, but mine came well packaged, with lots of protective pieces. All the parts were there and I got the color I ordered. The only explanation I can think of is that I ordered the size and color combo offered with Prime shipping, but the other color and size combos are only offered by third party sellers Maybe one of those sellers isn't so good. I initially thought some bolts were missing, but they were just lodged inside the white plastic protective caps that had been on the wheels. If you thing you are missing bolts for the wheels, check those caps.
Anyway...
It was easy to assemble. The instructions are not great, but it isn't hard to figure out. The rear tire, brake, and chain are already assembled, so the most complicated parts are done for you. I used the included tools. They are cheap, but perfectly adequate for the task. I have a garage full of mechanics tools that I didn't bother to go get.
This bike is definitely a big step up in quality from the huffy My son had been riding. It's a bit heavy, but that's not a huge deal. His old 14 inch bike was almost as heavy and much smaller.

I like the smooth ride, brakes (once adjusted), lights, kickstand, rack, smooth shifting and look of the bike. Review:
After reading many of the negative reviews on the Discover model, I wanted to weigh in as a long time owner. I have owned a Solitaire model since 2013, and have ridden it 2800+ miles. The Solitaire was WalMart's private labeled version of the Discover, with steel fenders, and trigger shifters, for which I paid $200. Comparable bike shop models were selling in the $4-500 range at the time. I rate it a solid 4 stars, and believe you would have to pay +$1K today for a 5* model.
My bike was assembled at the local store where I purchased it, so I expected having to tune it (wheel trueing, spoke tightening, brake, shifter, seat, and handlebar adjustments). Since it's initial tune I have had to adjust the derailers/shifters, and brakes as the cables stretched, and the pads wore (I have replaced the front and rear brake pads once). I have also replaced tires, and tubes, which brings me to the most common complaint of the negative reviews (tire/tube failures). I experienced two flat tires within the first 100 miles. What I discovered as the cause, were inconsistent spoke lengths, with a couple protruding through the spoke nuts, wearing through the rim band, and puncturing the inner tube. The recommended 60PSI tire pressures contributed to the failures. I doubt lower PSI ratings of balloon type tires would have resulted in failures (as quickly). The OEM tires and tubes are inexpensive, low quality (which is where most manufacturer cut costs). I have not experienced any peddle/crank issues, and have dis/reassembled them for routine bearing cleaning, and lubrication (as well as the axles and steering head). I always use threadlock compound on all fasteners. Other than typical wear item adjustments/replacements, routine maintenance, and cleaning, the bike has performed flawlessly.
This thing is built like a tank, and as others have stated is heavy in comparison to other hybrid/commuter models (which I attribute to its durability). It is also a tall bike (18" frame, with 700 series tires). I am 5'7" with a 31" inseam, and admittedly this bike is too tall for me. That said; it is comfortable, and enjoyable for me to ride. My riding style is recreational (exercise, exploring, and enjoyment), on secondary roads, and paved trails. My mid-season average speed is 10mph, which is plenty fast (at 63, and 190 lbs. I have no delusions of participating in the Tour`de Farce). As a "hybrid" it is a compromise between a commuter, and mountain bike. IMO it is a better commuter due to tire size, and hardtail frame (the spring in the seat post is no substitute for rear suspension for off road riding).
The only modifications I have made are upgrading to a more comfortable seat, and installation of mirrors, and lights. The only additional options on my wish list would be disc brakes, and a Bafang electric drive conversion, but I'll survive without them (I hope)!
I expect this bike will last me for the remainder of my riding years, and highly recommend it to others with similar expectations.

Review:
The Retrospec Beaumont is an adult bike shrunk to toddler size. It has the build quality and look of an adult bike (sans pedals). The colors (I got the eggshell white) look very stylish, and the lines are classic retro cool. The front basket and working bell are a nice touch.

The bike comes in just four pieces—take the frame, attach the seat, handlebars, and front tire, and you (and your child) are in business. It even comes with the necessary Allen wrench and multi-tool wrench. You supply a Phillips screwdriver and some scissors (to cut the zip ties).

The bike is marketed at 2 to 3 year olds. But even with the seat flat against the frame, it may stretch smaller children. Still, they will enjoy being pushed around while they grow (which they do every night). And with both the seat and handlebars adjustable, they can ride this bike for a long time.

But no matter how long they ride it, this bike will last—and be ready to be handed down to the next toddler. It is a solid piece of transportation! And while there are other, similar bikes for less, this one just exudes quality, so you will enjoy looking at it as much as your child enjoys riding it.

Review:
IF YOU ENCOUNTER ISSUES WITH THE FRONT WHEEL HITTING YOUR FOOT OR PEDAL, YOU HAVE THE FORKS BACKWARDS!

When I unpacked and assembled this bike, I saw that I was encountering the issue where the front wheel was extremely close and hitting my foot. After a little investigation, I saw that this was because the forks are reversed when shipping. Turn the forks around so the large U bracket is facing forward, and this solves the issue and is how the bike is intended to be assembled as per the manufacturer's pictures.

Outside of this, I am a big guy, 6'2" and 330lbs so far this bike holds me up quite well, I am yet to do any major riding, but I wanted to make a note for everyone since I have seen multiple reviews showing that, but no one has posted a how-to correct this. Simple fix when assembled correctly; it's just not documented by the manufacturer. For the record, I wear a size 14 shoe and have ample space between my foot and the front wheel when turning, simple fix, to poor documentation.

Review:
First off, FAST shipping. Very, very impressed with that. And, the bike...oh my gosh. Even at the lowest pedal assist (PAS 1), this thing pulls HARD, and I weigh about 280 pounds. I've only experimented with Peddle Assist up to level 3 (of 5), and that's scary enough...LOL

Also, there used to be hills where I live. Somehow, everything feels like level ground now. :D This bike has plenty of power, is VERY solid, and is great quality. You could spend a LOT more, and get a lot less.

One thing to add, though...this is a big bike. It's NOT for shorter or smaller frame people. My daughter is 5'4", and she can't get on it with the seat in the lowest position. I'm 5'10", and I think that's probably near the minimum height for this bike.

But man-oh-MAN is this bike fun to ride!! If you're on the fence, take it from me, you will NOT be disappointed!!

Review: This is not a Wally World special with shoddy build quality and low end components. Even though this is one of their lower priced bikes...K2 doesn't make junk and this is no exception. Everything about this bike screams quality..from the frame welds and paint quality to the ease with which the unassembled components go together. The front suspension and suspended seat truly work to smooth out road ripples and imperfections, while never feeling "floaty" or affecting control. With 21 speeds you will never be wanting for the perfect amount of pedaling effort...but the twist shifter sometimes doesn't click in positively..I will take it to a bike shop and have it adjusted at some point. The brakes are the usual friction on wheel rim type..but high quality and provide a progressive amount of stopping force without the slipping/grabbing you get with cheaper setups. This is truly the most comfortable and solid bike I have ever ridden.. the suspended, well padded (but firm) seat and upright handlebars are great if you have back trouble...when going along a smooth street the only sound you will hear is a faint hum from the tires and your own heavy breathing. Well worth the price...just make sure you get the right size frame. (I'm 6'1 170 and ordered large)

Review:
For over a year, since the beginning of the pandemic I’d been looking for a bike. First there was a mad rush, and nothing was available, online or big box stores. So, I waited. A year later, bikes started showing up as available for purchase. I wanted to get a bike for my wife, with casual rides for less than five miles.

SixThreeZero came up as available and I was unsure about the brand. I’m somewhat old school looking for brands that I knew from the 70s but had not realized the explosion of brands since then. From custom generics from China to high performance competition style bikes that cost thousands.

This brand was the best bang for the buck. When it became available, I bought it, fingers-crossed that this would not disappoint. When I received the bike, it was quick to assemble and adjust. No issues with assembly and my wife is happy with the results and color. I bought the blue one, not likely coral or teal, but that was my choice.

I would recommend this bike for casual riding around on flat surfaces, even though I bought the 7-speed. My wife will probably buy more accessories, which is a good indication that this was worth it.

The fenders, rack, and frame seem to be well-made. The tires have the perfect width and amount of tread to ride comfortably on our potholed streets. It could be improved with a quick release for the seatpost, kids being the type to grow often and unexpectedly. ;)

There were a few chips and scratches in the paint that seem to have occurred during the shipping process. The bike was packaged with several metal parts directly against one another with little or no padding in between. I've rarely had a bike come without a little damage, so this in and of itself really wasn't a huge issue.

The largest problem that I'm having is that the basket was missing from the box. The plate for it came pre-attached to the stem of the fork, but the bracket arms and basket itself are missing. I've contacted both Honest Traders and Columbia and am waiting for this to be resolved through one or the other. Honestly, my daughter loves the bike so much that, had the basket come like it should have, this would have been a five-star review, even with the damaged paint. We have four children, and our bikes get handed down. I think this will be a lovely addition to our family's little collection.

i highly recommend this bike

Review:
We are replacing an old wooden balance bike, and one of the things that always bothered us about it was that every time I'd take it out after not using it for a while, the tires would be flat. I was so happy to see that the tires on this one are airless. Everything seems to be made of high quality materials, and assembly was quick and easy. Just slide in handlebars, fork, and front wheel, then tighten a couple of bolts with the included allen wrench. Seat adjustment does not require tools.

The sizing is similar to the three other balance bikes I have reviewed or bought at yard sales over the years (none brands you have heard of), so no surprises there in terms of heights of child who can ride this - seems pretty standard across the board. I like the curved seat which is super comfortable. I actually sat on it myself and it felt very comfortable despite my being an adult on a child's bike. Just the sort of effortless comfort I remember from my BMX bike when I was a child.

I know so many people recommend balance bikes over bikes with training wheels because it makes the transition to riding a proper bike so much easier, and it's easier to learn to balance on a pedal-less bike that you scoot on and direct with your feet. This is the nicest we have tried and I really like the steering, comfort of the seat, aforementioned airless tires, and the proportions.

Review:
The account owner here is who I received the bike from. I am a 22 year old male writing this review (approx. 180 pounds and roughly 6'). I am overall extremely impressed with the product. I wish there was a 4.5 stars options, but I ended up deciding on 4.

The bike is excellent for urban, as well as suburban, environments. I have ridden the Nakto in both of these types of settings. In Norfolk, Virginia (where I ride it the most) the conditions are sometimes less than ideal, but the Nakto powers through every time. From my experience the battery will last quite a while unless you plan on riding it until the battery is completely dead each time.

I typically on a full charge will ride this bike approx 15 miles (possibly more) broken up into about 4 or 5 rides over the course of a week. On a full charge, and pedaling to assist it, I'd estimate it reaches speeds close to 25 mph (maybe you could hit 30 going downhill).

The bike folds ups quite small and fits well into the back seat of my 08' Honda Accord. (only takes up one seat) This is great for transportation and storage.

Overall: Great little folding commuter electric bike which reaches speeds of 25mph on a flat grade (estimated). Gets nearly 20 miles per full charge on the lithium battery. I don't ever use its headlight though... I have one on there that I bought from a store to save battery for riding

One more thing to add, I do NOT work for this company. This a real review despite the countless fake reviews on this site

My biggest issue is that a replacement part is almost impossible to find. My seat was stolen, and the company does not sell replacement seats for this model. There is a similar model (RJ26A) with a 1mm wider seat post for which the company sells replacement seat posts. If you live in a city, consider the RJ26A model, not this model. Review:
I bought this bike as a replacement for another that arrived damaged (rear wheel rim was bent and was cracked open). For some reason the protection in the packaging for most bikes seems to be insufficient, so that many times you need to count on your luck to get a perfectly looking bike. This bike also had a few minor scratches, just as some reviewers have mentioned it here, but nothing serious. If it is your first bike ever, since both wheels are quick-release with disk brakes, you will need to spend some time practicing fitting the rear wheel with all the cogs and brake disks perfectly in position to fix it. Sometimes brakes' and front derailleur tuning can become tiresome, but this is something you would probably need to do to ensure safe and smooth ride. Some people say it is heavy... well, yes, but it's not that other entry level bikes of this class weigh much less, if actually not more. Just make sure your tires are properly inflated and your ride will be fun.

One drawback that I find rather annoying and, to some extent hazardous, is the short frame which sometimes makes the pedals trip over the front wheel when turning. The first time I rode it, I was lucky there was no traffic on the road because I was forced to move forward on the turn since my foot on the pedal didn't allow the front wheel to turn sideways as intended. So beware of that as this may cause you injury!!!

Review:
If you are shopping for a nice road bicycle right now, like I, you have probably found out how hard they are to come by. I decided to buy this one because it was the best I could find for around $1,000.00.

The bike came quickly and as promised. No problem putting it together, although there were no instructions included in the box. Perhaps this is because they want you to take it to a bike shop? I'm not sure. Anyways, it took about an hour for me to put it together. Everything came wrapped in foam and the major sections were semi-pre assembled in the box.

Riding it I found that it is lighter feeling than my steel trail bike, but heavier than a full carbon-framed bike like a Trek. The frame is quite stiff too, so you won't want to ride this one on anything but a smooth road surface. I replaced the stock seat right out of the box with a more cushioned one I found here on Amazon. I also added a rear pouch, electric horn, two water bottles and some strapless toe clips, which were a huge safety and performance improvement.

The bike is very nimble too. It took some short test rides and adjusting to get the seat and handlebars where I wanted them. That was to be expected. The front deraillleur was a bit out of adjustment though, so it took some fiddling with to get it right and to get all of the gears indexed. The rear wheel was also out of true so I had to work on the spoke tensions a bit as well. The rear wheel also needed some oil on the rear hub assembly, as it was squeaking when it turned. I also oiled both of the deraillieurs and the chain.

All-in-all I'd say this bike is a 3 out of 5 stars. It is certainly as stated in the ad, but a little disappointing for the price tag. A good bike for someone who is just starting out on a true road bike though, not wanting to spend $2-5k. I can see that in a couple of years time that I might want to move up to something a little better though.

Review:
These days, it's cheaper to buy a complete bike than to buy a frame & fork set. So... I set out looking for a bike that had what I wanted at the cheapest price. I'm a hefty guy, so no carbon fiber for me... which means Throne and Cyborg are a no go. I wanted a thicker styled frame in 100% 6061 alloy. It came down to Golden and 6ku. 6ku has a decent reputation at this price point, 6ku won.

HINT HINT: I strongly urge you to check out 6ku's website to see additional tech specs before purchasing.

Fast forward, the bike arrives, well packaged. The frame is well built, the tires are blah... standard issue Kenda tires with Kenda tubes. The Tektro breaks, again blah... actually my front break had a bad paint job, but all else was functional. The rims are OK, but the back rim is slightly out-of-round, which my local shop fixed up for me. Also the bearing nuts were way too tight out-of-box and not adequately lubricated. I fixed that... The chain is cheap and will stretch quickly (see above where I mention that I'm hefty) but it will do for now. 1 of the hex nuts for the rear wheel was stripped, out of box; luckily I had spares. The plastic pedals are a joke, I bought some really nice platform pedals on Amazon with spd clips on 1 side.

I bought this bike for the frame, mostly, so for that It did it's job. I replaced the tires and tubes (Vittoria hybrid road tires, 700x28c pictured), but kept the rims. I replaced the tektro brake levers with TT break levers from Soma. I replaced their handle bars with a T2 wing bullhead handle bar, 44cm width, very nice.... I've kept the seat for now, but it's not great and I'll replace it eventually. Also, I've seen plenty of complaints about the BB breaking,so I'm gearing up to replace that as well.

That said, I'm happy with my purchase. All in all, I got what I wanted for about $600, which is a few hundred less than the starter Throne or CYBORG, which I would still have had to modify.

Review:
First off, I'm not a big review person but I feel I owe it to other buyers to offer a warning.

The directions provided were for a generic bike and not the one purchased. I should have known I was in trouble when step 1 referred to a set of handlebars I was not looking at in front of me.

Step 2 was the front tire. No mention of the trick pegs provided but the bolts and clamp washer washers to be removed. After getting on off and ruining some bolt threads trying to get the other off, I nearly lost my mind. I eventually figured out I did not need to remove the both and the pegs were supposed to hold on the front wheel.

Mind you, this is my 8 year old son's big Christmas present.

I'll spare you the nightmare of the front and rear brake lines running to the hand break triggers on the handle bars.

It was AWFUL.

After calling a friend and a couple of hours I will never get back, it is assembled.

We will see if this craftsmanship lasts. I have my concerns.

Buyer beware.
