Review:
I've been riding the bike around town to and from school, about 7 miles round trip. Also done a 12 mile and 20 mile trek.

This is my first time putting a bike together or doing any real work on one. Received the bike in 2 boxes, with everything wrapped in cardboard or some foamy wrapping stuff. The whole back end was Pre assembled which was nice. All I had to put together was front fender and handlebars. It took a little while, maybe 2.5 hours, cuz I didn't have the best tools and instructions were generic (not for that specific bike.) the derauler is set at the factory but I had to tweak mine a bit because it wouldn't go into third gear and the chain was rubbing on it.

I've been riding it a couple days and only complaint is the fenders. They're fairly cheap and if I hit a bump they start to rub on the tires which is annoying and kinda loud. Considering taking them off for the sake of not dealing with it any more. Also the seat isn't what was pictured. It has a white line on it but it's still a rather comfy schwinn.

Pros:
Back end was assembled and was fairly easy to put rest together.
Comfy ride for cruising around town.
Book rack is great for a backpack or other random stuff.
Was shipped wrapped and packaged fairly well.

Cons:
Fenders are annoying.
Had to readjust derauler before I could ride it.

Hope the review help :)

Review: I bought this wooden bike for a preschooler. In less than a year of use I have had to replace the bolts on the steering/front wheel assembly three times. We used a similar wooden running bike made in Germany to teach a whole neighborhood of children (more than 6 preschoolers in a single month) to ride a two wheeler and never had to repair a single piece. This bike has needed a special air pump because the intake gauge is so close to the spokes. Our other pump would not fit. We bought a box of the hex-metric bolts at Home Depot and needed everyone of them to replace ones that have been loss in use.  The blue knob on the handle broke off after repeated dropping on the sidewalk, when our little guy jumped off the bike to chase another thought. The seat is difficult to move up and down, requiring two hex drivers and disassembly of two bolts. This makes it difficult to share with different height children. I would pay more for a lower maintenance running bike with an easy seat adjustment, if I had it to do all over again.

I got it back from a professional putting it together, pushed it in and set it against the wall, went to watch TV and both tires went. I originally thought he might of put to much air in them so I took it to another shop and they told me that it was the rim tape. They put some more on and when I got back to the house the back was flat... again. I tried fixing it myself with some new tape but being no pro got the wrong one and went through two more tubes before I called the guy I took it to the first time who said bring it by. He redid my tape job, put in a new tube and it has been running great since so make sure you have that checked right off so you don't spend an extra hundred dollars on shop fees and tubes.

Review:
Bike is just great, 108 shipped.. What a excellent deal. Packing was excellent. Putting it together is very easy and ready to ride. Fine tuning the gear, although it is 80 percent ready to ride gear switchng wise.. It does still need to be fined tuned sometime down the road. As all new bikes do no matter what they cost. You well need a few tools to put er together.. Regarding the bad brakes a few have commented below.. They are not bad, there just far apart and need adjusting. Anyone can do it.. Just lossen the brake cable and then pull the brake cable till the brake pads are very close to the rim. Tighten the cable and that is it. After doing that, my brakes stop on a dime.. Frame is beautifully painted all joints are welded perfect. Seat is comfy. Bike weight is 25 pounds with kick stand. Funny.. the first thing I did to the bike is, took off all the stickers on it. Must of lighten the bike by two pounds.. Things I changed on it so far are. I reversed the brake levers. With the revo shifter, "which I love". The brake levers are way out of the picture on the handle bar. Mean you really have to reach down to use them. On a split sec, you be dead if you had to use them where they are now. I took off the grip. All it is is a tennis racket grip. Easy to remove and unwind. Loosen the levers and reversed them and put them a inch up more so up on the handle bar. Now the levers are facing me. Easy access to them as I'm shifting gears or just riding along. Reraping the grip is very easy. Now if I'm in a speed tucked position on a five mile stretch, which I'm not going to be thinking about brakes anyway, but if I do need them it is very easy to just reach up with one hand and stay tucked to use the back brake. Also, regarding the brake levers. The right works the back and the left works the front. I switched them so the left works the back. This way I can brake and still switch gears at the same time with my right hand. Plus it is more comfotable that way for me, using the left hand on the back brake. The revo shifter is great and works great.. Hey it is 108 dollar road bike. Put some lube on the chain. Have fun.. This bike for the price is a steal, it beautiful looking, easly put together, rides great. The frame 22.5 fits me perfect. 6' 200lbs.
Enjoy! Accessories I ordered for it below.
Road and Mountain Bikes(Expanded and Revised 5th Edition
Blackburn Quadrant and Mars 3.0 Combo Bicycle Light Set
Bell Ultra-Tool Multi-Function Bike Tool
Planet Bike SpeedEZ Road Front and Rear Bicycle Fender Set (Black, 35mm Wide)
Schwinn 17-Function Bike Computer

Review:
This bike is quite nice, and my 9 year old adores it. The only real issue is that it got a flat tire within a day. Investigation revealed that one of the spoke-heads had a burr on it, and had punctured the tire. A little filing, a drop of rubber cement, (and a new inner tube) and all is well.

The gears were adjusted pretty well for coming from the factory. Even my high end bike needed adjustment when I got it. (Of course, buying from REI, they did that for me... I had to do the final adjustment on this bike myself, but it isn't difficult, and it was only a tiny bit off.) Of course, if you aren't handy or have no clue about bikes, you might need assistance with this kind of thing. If so, you should buy from a shop. Note that stores like Toys-R-Us, Wally World, Target, or Sears never really do adjustments right anyway, you need a bike shop, or sporting goods specialty store. Or a handy friend. ;-)

Review:
I bought this bicycle to replace a hybrid bicycle I bought from Costco. I wanted a more upright sitting bicycle. This one suits that need perfectly. I’m not a bicycle enthusiast, just a middle-aged guy that likes to ride around on bicycles.

I would say this bike was built with about average parts. An average derailer, average hubs, wheels and tires, and average brakes. Pretty much average quality everything. Nothing special, but more than enough for my needs. There’s only two things I didn’t care for. One is the seat. It has a little lip on the aft end which was causing some irritation. I put my old seat on it and the problem is solved. The other thing is the gearing. I typically never make it past 3rd or 4th gear (out of 7). I wish the front sprocket was a little smaller. I don’t see me ever having a use for gears 6 & 7.

As far as the packaging for shipping goes, it was packed with more than enough protection to handle the expected bumps it will receive in transit. The final assembly, done by the buyer, it’s pretty much no different than any other bicycle. No special tools or knowledge is required. The instructions are more than enough so that the average person can put it together.

What I recommend bicycles from this company? Sure. They look great and the build quality is good.

Review:
I ordered this bike in Feb. The stated delivery was 5 to7 days, but mine came in 2 days. I had major surgery so this was going to be my help to get back in shape.
To begin, the whole package was very easy to unpack and set up for final assembly. Final assembly took less than 30mins. Very simple, but I also have a Lemond Zurich I made into a cross bike, and a Felt composite road bike, and I wrench on them myself. But really, assembly was simple. The front wheel was way out of true, so had to use a friend's truing stand to get it round and straight. Rear is also a little out but I didn't do that one.
I liked the bike from the start. The seat is bad, way to big and wide. I replaced it. Riding it the first time takes a little learning, but the power is very good.
I put on SPD peddles so it is easier to spin. So after tinkering with the setup I got it on the road.
I have now been riding the G9 and have about 130mi on it. It is a nice ride, and really helping me.
Now, there has been one big issue. At about 35mi the motor started to make a scraping sound about every half revolution. After several texts back and forth, service decided something had come loose internally. So Paselec said we will send a new wheel/motor assembly. They also told me to take it to a bike shop and they would pay me back. But I live in the mountains over sn hour to a bike shop. So Paselec service responds quickly to solve problems..
The new assembly arrived and I will install next week. But again to wheel needs truing.
Overall, very nice bike for a very reasonable price. So the battery runs very low at about 30-35 mi but every ride here is at least 2000ft of climbing. So in gentler terrain I think 50mi is reasonable.
Great bike for the buck if you are patient and can do a few fixes yourself.

Review:
I got the 12" white and blue version from Amazon Warehouse deals. It was in used condition, but the "used" looked like someone tried to put it together, got stuck, took things off, and sent it back.

What I like:
* The 12" size is a good fit for my almost 4 year old son, who is around 40" tall. He can comfortably reach the ground with the seat in the lowest position. This bike should last him for a year, or a little more, until he can get on his brother's 16" bike...
* It looks well made. It's solid. The colors work together well, and it looks way better without every possible cartoon character printed on it.
* It comes with the training wheels - a big plus, since you don't have to get them separately, and you know they'll fit. There also is a little bell in the box, the 3$ kind, but it's nice to have it.
* It has a hand break, and pedal break. Not always a case with 12" bikes. I'm glad it does! My son does better with hand break under stress - he did not grasp the "pedal backwards" to stop thing yet.

What I didn't like:
* It's HEAVY. Is it heavier than other kid size bikes? No. It's not a $400 aluminum frame bike, either. But I cannot believe kids bikes have to be that heavy. The frame's profile could be half the size and hold up...
* Hand break is a little flimsy, and adjusting the brake to actually stop the bike took some time and effort. It's there, tho, and it works now.
* The "Buttons" band is a little confusing. We got two of the same brown plaid, and not sure how to change the design. I think it's unnecessary. I'd trade it for the water bottle offered in the other RoyalBaby model in no time.
* The biggest problem I had with the bike was the rear axle, tho. It looks like the wheel was not in line with the frame. Not sure it was a manufacturing defect, or the first owner decided to adjust something, but it took a trip to a bike shop to figure it out. Amazon came through offering to take the bike back, or issue a partial refund. We kept the bike.

Overall it's a nice bike. If you cannot stand Cars, Planes, Ninja Turtles and My Little Pony plastered all over your kids stuff, this is a good choice. I think it's a little overpriced, that's why it has the 4 stars. If I was choosing again I think I would pick [[ASIN:B00HXGSBXC RoyalBaby Kids Freestyle Bike 12 inch, 14 inch, 16 inch BMX for Boys and Girls]], but we do like the bike, my son enjoys riding it, and since we fixed the rear wheel issue we've had no problems with it.

Review: I did not purchase this new so I cannot comment on ease of assembly or how clear instructions are but ... I can comment on how the bike holds up over time. The original owner had ridden it sparingly over the course of 18 months in So Cal and put about 200 or so miles on it. I've had it for about a month and it has held up well so far on about a dozen outings (150 miles or so) and has been really fun to ride. It has some old school components like side pull brakes and Schrader valves but the aluminum frame is a comparatively lightweight 26 lbs w/ pretty nice welding and the steel alloy fork softens the front end and adds stability. The shifters are some sort of in-house Shimano type (looks like Tourney) and both front and rear derailleurs shift smoothly w/ a sealed bracket and some resin platform pedals are included. The shifters themselves are thumb-style and on the top of the handle bars, the cabling is all external. The seat is okay, nothing unusually uncomfortable (I use padded shorts anyway). The 700 x 25 tires are an unknown brand but are actually pretty nice and you can ride them at about 80 psi no problem. The drop-bars are alloy and have decent wrapping on them. The brake levers are placed on both the drops and the top of the bars for convenience. The frame I have is the 56cm and just about fits me (I'm 5'9") - I think a taller rider would be more comfortable. If you're my height or shorter you might want to opt for the 53cm frame. The rims are alloy double-walled and both wheels did not need truing when I got it, so that's a good sign. One of the stellar features on this bike is the paint, I got the black & neon green. It's really an attractive bike despite the many, many times Trinx logos is displayed, I actually get more compliments for it than I do my expensive bikes! In closing I feel this is a very good entry-level road bike for the price. If you're handy at all you should be able to assemble it without issue (usually boxed bikes require mounting the front wheel, handlebar and pedals) but be sure to adjust the brakes and shifters, tighten the quick release axels and seat post, inflate the tires properly and get out on the road!

Review:
I'd been away from bikes for a decade but came to the decision that cycling is just too good a method of getting and staying physically fit, especially for those of us who are too old for jogging.

In doing my research on likely bikes, I narrowed it down to a K2 Easy Street, Schwinn Midmoor and this Schwinn Suburban. I wasn't looking for a long distance road bike or a mountain trail bike. I just wanted a decent quality comfortable cruiser for casual exercise and pleasure riding. When I compared the three models up close, my first impression was to go with the Midmoor. But after sitting on them, the Midmoor just didn't fit me as well as the Suburban. The K2 was nice, but I preferred the brakes on the Suburban.

Now, after riding it for a couple of weeks, I have no regrets or second thoughts. I was a little concerned about the twist-grip shifting, as I (being 60 years old) was accustomed to the Shimano frame-mounted lever shifters from the ancient past. No worries. The twist grip system gives quick, accurate, positive shifts. The riding position suits me very well as it's mostly upright which is what an old guy likes. The suspension on the front fork and seat stem makes a noticeable difference in smoothing the ride. It's light yet sturdy. Handles easily and steers accurately.

Bottom line: this bike is a good choice for anyone wanting an affordable, comfortable machine for casual riding.

Review:
Nowadays we find ourselves in the new generation of bikes known as electric bikes, the mass evolution of e-bikes are on the rise and what companies will dominate the electric bike industry will be determined by our fellow peers. I will be going into indeep analytical over view on the mechanism of Vivi E-bike components, speed, durability. So get ready for the E-Bike wars.
Speed
Now if you re-call bikes need you to pedal to go, well thanks to the electric bikes they come with a new form of pedaling, known as pedal assist. The pedal assist consist of three forms of speeds you have low, medium, and high, the option at starting high or medium is optional. Start at low and make your way up to high without pedaling the bike will go up to 25 mph respectfully, all depending on the terrain. Giving the bike assistance (pedaling) as u have the pedal assist, will boost u're speed to a max of 50+mph respectfully, all depending on the terrain. Without pedal assist, the bikes components will make it too heavy and difficult for you to ride, rendering u to reach speeds of 20 mph or less. Remember now this bike is meant for mountain purposes. It's considered to fast for commercial use.
Battery
Unlike other e-bikes the battery is located in a pouch hanging securely in the front part of the handle bar, made easily able to mount on, n detach, along the same area were the speed is located is the battery indicator, which light up red. The bikes battery last roughly 3-5 HR which is great from going to point a to point b if you're using vivi bike and you're constantly starting at speed high, you will drain out your batteries lifespan quicker rather than start at low speed, but my personal opinion this bike was meant to be used for though places, making the battery consume enough ohm's n volts to do it's Job, thus resulting in a bad battery. I recommend that u buy a spare battery.
Exterior
The frame is very thin, n it has the ability to bend in half for storing, n portability purposes. It's motor is found inside the back wheel area inside the rim. So if u bend the rim u will need to replace that, n that's we're the electric motor is located at, the gear's consist of 7, n in the pedal area it's three gear's. Under the seat there's a shock, n on the front area there are sealed suspension's all for making the ride smoother. We're the bike bends there's a secured lock to guarantee locked on in order to keep it from opening.
If I could get this bike again I will, it's a great bike n affordable. Compared to the bike's out there, n I'm talking about named brand's there nothing compared to the Vivi bike, it's well designed, n made for transferring the E-Bike we're ever u go. If I have to say I give this bike a rating of 5 stars.

Review:
I had this bike for under five months, and logged about 330 miles on it; most of the miles were road miles around my town with varying elevations doing between 15-20mph, usually no less than 10 miles at a time. I would not recommend this bike to anyone who wants to use it for anything more than leisurely, slow rides around a neighborhood for the following reasons:

The first few rides, I had a pedal fall off (almost crashed), and the handlebars come loose; those may have been an issue with assembly and not tightening up enough, but it's not like I left everything so loose that those things should have happened. It got to the point where I always carried tools with me on my rides in case those things occurred again.

The chain skipped for almost the entirety of the time I used it over 4-5 months, and it eventually got to the point where downshifting to gears under maybe 13th gear caused the chain to grind horribly against the gears, sometimes popping the chain off.

It rusted at many different points (possibly from me sweating on it, but come on).

The gears were always difficult to get exactly in the correct position, brake adjustments were often required and they were always squeaky

The kicker of all these downsides occurred on my last ride, where downshifting at the end of the ride caused the chain to pop off the gear. It popped off while I had my full weight on a down-pedal and this cause the chain to get stuck around the derailleur and literally tear it off, bending the gears, snapping the housing, and rendering the bike completely unusable. Thankfully I purchased the coverage plan where I was fully reimbursed for the bike, but that is besides the point.

This is a definitely a you-get-what-you-pay for product, but I shudder to think what would happen to someone who took this on actual mountain bike trails. I fear someone could be seriously injured because of the poor quality. Buy at your own risk.

Review:
This is my first e-bike. I got it because I dont have a car, so it's my transportation to work. I absolutely love it! It's pretty comfortable after a while, and has great battery life. I ride about 12 miles a day, sometimes more and I have not completely ran out of battery just yet.
However, about a month after I got it, the pedal assist quit working. I dont mind this but I bet to some people that would be pretty disappointing. I didn't assemble it myself- and this could be the place that put it together at fault, but the left pedal arm got completely stripped and was close to falling off. After it was replaced, the pedal assist started working again, but quit again not long after. Very occasionally I also have to "rev" the bike because for some reason it doesnt go the first time I pull back. As of right now, I've had this bike for about 3ish months.
Overall, this is a very nice bike and I am quite happy with my purchase. I've received many compliments on it too! Just be warned it may need some work done sooner than you'd think.

Review:
[[VIDEOID:1bc614bdf88ca77c57737c8d58eb2d72]] I chose this bike after some research and comparison with what was available in local shops & big-box stores.
I wanted a 14" for my 3yo son that has been riding a 12" balance bike for the last year or so and I felt he was ready to graduate. To my surprise - and everyone's I know - he was able to ride this bike by himself not later than 5 min after he got on it. We never fitted the training wheels that came in the box. Needless to say that parents and grandparents alike were all presently surprised and proud - as much as worried - but he pulled it off. I don't know why people claim the bike is hard to pedal.

Assembly for the bike was relatively easy for anyone that has done basic work on a bicycle before. Most of the bike is assembled from the factory: you need to install and adjust the handlebar that already has the front brake & bell fitted on it (perhaps rotate/adjust the position of those to your liking), add the kick-stand or the training wheels per preference, screw in the pedals and snap on the foam stem cap, perhaps adjust the seat height and you are "good to go".

Our bike had the front brake "working", but I did end up tuning it a bit more to be more to my liking. I am personally not a big fan of coaster brakes and I would rather have both front and rear brakes on hand-levers, but the coaster brake seems to be working OK and it is not too abrupt (hard to really evaluate something you cannot ride yourself). The little one seems to be getting the hang of the coaster brake after a week or so, as balancing became more natural.

Overall the built quality is above average and it is obvious for everyone that has ever inspected the typical big-box offerings. The bike does feel much better than $50 over what you can find there. The frame is super sturdy - perhaps a bit on the heavy side, weldings are not perfect but pretty clean by comparison to what you see in $150 big-box bikes for kids and the metallic paint job is pretty good.

I would rather have the brake lever not being plastic, but time will tell how sturdy it is. It is adjustable though and you can tune the reach to be more appropriate to your young rider's fingers.

The covered chain inspires confidence that cloths and fingers won't get hurt, but you do get some chain noise rubbing on it. The frame design is super-clean and the lack of a bottom-tube leaves lots of clearance and won't get any little legs stuck in case of a fall. Overall it speaks to a well-thought design.

1.5Y UPDATE: my now 4.5yo already transitioned to a larger bike as he is a bit tall for his age. The bike served him well for 1.5years and we had zero issues. All I had to do is oil the chain and check tire pressures. The ride is still quiet (other than the occasional chain rub on the plastic enclosure), brakes work like new. Minor chips on the paint from use and uneventful falls while riding or drops because of lack of attention, which are probably unavoidable on a first bike for a really young kid. Would probably buy again. The bike would be perfect if it was lacking a coaster rear brake, but as-is, is already better specced than most LBS kids bikes, some of which cost more. If you won't go for a Woom bike, seriously consider this.

Review: Purchased this bike straight from P3 Cycles and it was delivered in one day. I love the bike, its lightweight, easy to carry up stairs and even fits inside my Toyota Corolla (still need to purchase a bike rack). The design is perfect and I love the black color on it. However, I went with this bike because I assumed their sizing was correct, mind you I am barely getting into biking and don't know much about sizes and bike parts etc.. However after doing some shopping around I came across this bike, looked through their size chart and the 49cm size was suited for my height (4'11") and price was great.. however, once it arrived and assembled I realized this bike isn't fit for someone my height, it's just a tad bit too tall and I have the hardest time getting off my bike when cruising around, which has led to some funny falls. I have been riding this for a week and even getting on the bike is a tad hard but manageable, but I just cannot find myself comfortable enough to hop off the bike when I come to a stop. I'm no expert and maybe with some practice I could manage to find a way to get off my bike without difficulty, but I strongly believe they should maybe change the sizing info on their website because other sites have their 49cm bikes recommended for riders that are taller than 5' and it's quite misleading for newbies like me. Unfortunately, the roads I commute to work to and from are busy with semi trucks and I will sadly have to purchase a new bike more suited for my height which is a bummer because this bike is great! I highly recommend it to buyers though!

Closest bike shop is an hour away, so this was a day trip to get fixed. The bike shop reported cheap plastic tubes were used instead rubber and no puncture, but faulty work putting the tube in the tire. I’m actually holding off on a real star rating hoping this is a fluke and I actually do enjoy the bike, even though the first impression didn’t sit good.

About 60 days later, still having a great time! I'm obviously new to the bike community but it seems a lot of little stuff happens. Front tire blew, which I was expecting from the previous experience, headlight came off, & battery handle came off. All seem like small workmanship issues where parts could have been tightened or designed better. Or some better shocks or larger tires might help with the vibration and things coming loose. Truthfully I'm craving a smoother ride. Originally I was planning some dirt/gravel road travels but this is not the bike for that. This has improved my leg muscles and phsical fitness goals in a short time so all in all its a good purchase for me.

Review:
The bike is a great value. Made of 6061 Aluminum, it is both stronger and lighter than bikes made 10 or 15 years ago. The shifting group on the bike is entry level but more than sufficient for this level of bike. The brakes are wonderful, and the tires, although heavy, are quite acceptable. The front shock is pretty much crap but shouldn't cause you trouble (i.e. it is unlikely to break), only extra effort. Overall, though, it is hard to find as much bike as this at this price level. The big brand names cannot touch this bike at this price.

So, should you buy it?

To answer this question, you have to ask yourself, "What do I want out of riding?" There are many valid answers to this question, so think about it. Are you planning on riding the local gravel trail in town? Are you planning on short trips around with the kids? Would you prefer to ride actually two-track trails with roots, stumps, rocks and sand? Or are you looking to do distance riding with friends?

Answering these questions honestly will go a long way toward deciding whether this bike is for you. So what is this bike best used for? This bike is almost exclusively for riding gravel trails around town with the occasional off-road or two track. It is NOT meant for riding around town and definitely not for distance riding. Similarly, it shouldn't be bought if you plan on riding technical trails with drops and steep climbs.

Let me back up my unfounded statements. Gravel trails are easy to ride and you could easily get away with a touring bike (defined as thinner tires and a more upright, laid back riding style). But every so often, you will hit a patch that could puncture your tire and/or tube on a touring bike. The Kenda tires and soft shock will keep that from happening on the Response. Similarly, the low gear ratios on this bike will make riding up hills much easier, as long as the path is not too rough. If you are doing steep inclines on a bumpy road, the crap shock that comes with this bike will feel like a pogo-stick, causing the front tire to lose grip with the road/trail. This will most likely end with you walking the bike up. But in well maintained trails, you should excel.

If you are riding around town, you'll see lots of surburbanites riding their Treks and Specialized mountain bikes. You would fit right in with this bike. However, you would be better served by a touring (sometimes called "hybrid") bike. Something like this: [[ASIN:B002LFRT24 Diamondback Edgewood Men's Comfort Hybrid Bike (700c Wheels)]]

I know, it is not as cool looking. And looks should factor in since you want to feel good when riding around town. But that less cool looking will translate into higher speeds, less effort, and more comfort. If, on the other hand, you are planning on riding technical trails, you will need more gear. You could buy a better front fork (at a minimum, something like this: [[ASIN:B000NNSY2U RockShox Tora Coil 302 U-Turn Fork (Black Pl Rim/Disc, 85-130)]] ) but really, you'd be better off spending more on a lighter, more complete bike. You should expect to spend at least 50% more on an entry level bike for more serious downhill and technical trails. If you are over 5'10", you might even consider a 29-inch mountain bike.

Finally, if you want to do distance riding, the bike is wholly inappropriate. The riding stance and the tire thickness would make this bike (or any mountain bike) a poor choice for anyone other than the most fit athlete out there.

In summary, this is a GREAT value bike, oodles better than anything you find at your local discount market. Moreover, Giant, Specialized, and Trek cannot touch this price point and, although you must compromise on weight, front fork, and grouping, you will get a whole lot of bike for a low price.

By the way, yes, I both own and ride this bike, along with my Giant TCX Crossbike.

UPDATE: The day I wrote my review, the bike was on sale. Now that the price is back up, I would knock off a 1/2 a star, since the value is diminished. I'm not saying it's not worth Amazon's price, only that if I were you, I would consider waiting for a sale.

Review:
I have put almost 200 miles on the bike and have many good things to say, but a couple major complaints. First, the good:
PROS:
1. Great range! With practice and battery management skills, I'm sure you can get over 70 miles on a full charge. My first effort at 52 miles ended with still 2/5 bars showing (see photo). My latest trip was 43 miles with 3/5 bars left.
2. Great value. At $1,399, this is a lot of bike for the money.
3. Battery recharges on downhill rides! This is really fun, especially for me, an electric vehicle nerd. I love the way my EV recharges when coasting or braking. Now my bike does it, too. It also allows you to add to your workout. If you pedal while going downhill, it increases the charge rate while increasing your heart rate!
4. The battery has a USB outlet. No more dead cellphones!
5. Relatively light. Most large e-bikes can weigh 70 or 80 pounds. This one is under 50 with the battery removed. This is important for my bike carrier which is rated to 50 lbs.
6. Operates with throttle or peddle-assist. The throttle is really nice for starting up from a stoplight. It takes a couple cranks to get the assist going without it, so getting going by throttle really helps.
7. This bike can go fast on the flat. It cruises very comfortably with normal pedaling effort at 25 mph.
8. Tools needed for assembly were included... but no assembly instructions...which leads me to the....
CONS:
1. No paperwork included with the shipment. No assembly instructions, user manual, safety warnings, etc. Is that even legal? If you have never assembled a bike before, you'll probably need some help or at least find a good youtube video. There is an online user manual on the eahora website for the electric operation, but it is not particularly well written and contains some errors.
2. Some adjustments need to be made to make this comfortable. Not all the tools needed for those adjustments are included, so you need a good set of bike tools, especially allen wrenches. The gear shift thumb lever, for example, interferes with the grips, so I had to move it a couple inches to the left on the handlebar.
3. The motor has a mind of its own. It surges sporadically, sometimes when you don't want it. For example, I was turning sharply at a slow speed in a confined space when the motor kicked in, almost sending me crashing into a wall. THIS IS A SAFETY ISSUE THAT NEEDS TO BE CORRECTED. There are many adjustments that can be made on this bike, but the online instructions tell the user to contact customer support. I've done so. We'll see what they can do.
4. Because of the surging problem, this bike SHOULD NOT BE USED FOR MOUNTAIN TRAIL RIDING. MTB trails require careful control of speed, effort and direction. As this bike is sold to be used for trail riding, this is a major problem. If I had bought this bike for trail riding, I probably would have given it 1 star. I will use it primarily for pavement, gravel and dirt roads and flat trails.
5. The motor is not very powerful when going up hills. I am a pretty strong rider, so this is not a real issue with me, but it does take work getting up a hill. The power assist is certainly helpful, but underwhelming.

Would I buy this bike again, knowing what I know now? Probably not. But if customer service can correct the surge problem, I would change that to a yes.

August 1 UPDATE: Tech support was not very helpful. I sent them videos illustrating the problem but they said this is normal. So I decided to make some adjustments myself and was pleasantly surprised to find how easy the adjustments are. I was able to reduce but not eliminate the surging problem. I raised my rating to 4 stars, but if I could rate customer support separately, it would be 2 stars.

August 7 UPDATE: I discovered why there is so little help on the hills. For some reason, the pedal assist is limited to 6 amps, even at the highest setting. On the other hand, when you stop pedaling and use the throttle, the battery provides at least 15 amps and you can program it for more. I set mine so I now get 18 amps in throttle mode. However, as soon as you start pedaling, the assist level drops back to 6 amps max. I have alerted tech support to this so they can solve the problem. Hopefully they can help me reprogram the controller to either increase the level of amps in pedal assist mode, allow pedaling while using the throttle or send me a new controller that will allow me to tap into the power potential that is built into this bike.

Review:
Please be advised that the bike you're buying online is not assembled. Several local bicycle stores in my area had it in stock for $229 fully assembled and ready to ride.

The 2010 model is a world different from previous years, don't be confused when reading online reviews.

The bad:
- imprecise shifting mechanism
- terrible front suspension
- too weak for rugged mountain trails

The Good:
- aluminum and simple, very light
- fully serviceable, upgradeable and repairable, unlike bikes from WalMart and similar stores
- best entry level mixed terrain bike for the money

In my opinion, this is a very good city/commuter bike that can handle potholes, mud and snow, thus making it great for city riding. Don't try jumping over logs and boulders and it will serve you well for quite some time. There are very few aluminum bikes in that price range, and this one seem to be the best, so I bought one. Great for someone just learning or not willing to make a $500 commitment, which is an average price for a real rugged MTB. The front shock is so weak that it's completely pointless, and is likely to fail sooner than later. Keep it clean and lubricated to prolong it's service. If you're riding in the city, consider upgrading to a softer tire, as the original is very hard and noisy.

Good luck and ride safe!

Review:
If you are frustrated by putting things together yourself then you need to think carefully before purchasing this bike. There are videos that will help you if you do decide to get one. If you don't mind hiring someone or are confident you can do it yourself then this bike is a very, very good deal. It is rated the number one folding mountain bike on quite a few MTB review sites. The Number two bike cost almost a grand.
Now my review and I bought two white ones:
The bike is good-looking and gets a lot of compliments from other bike owners. It rides smooth and after a bit of adjustment, the disc brakes are firm and stop the bike quickly. I adjusted the front brakes on both bikes to slow more than stop the bike. The rear grabs hard. Shifting works well. I have found that I leave the front gear in second most of the time and shift the rear the most. The rear derailleur needed no adjustment out of the box for either bike, the rear brake the same. The front, which I had to install, took a bit of time to get right, which I expected. I used to build bikes when I was a teenager working in a hardware store so I have a bit of experience in that regard. The seat is good, but a bit hard, that can be changed if needed. Pedals are plastic but fold and appear to be very good for a non-metal pedal. Directions for installing parts were a bit confusing for the first bike, and then I watched the video and that ended the confusion over installing the fenders, which are a good feature of the bikes. The second bike was much easier to assemble. The including reflectors are screwed onto their brackets not slipped-on, but they work. Let me repeat, the ride is very smooth, the shock-absorbing suspension works very well over gravel, and fields with lots of dips and bumps. Let me say it again, this is a very good bike.

Update: after owning these bikes for a few weeks and putting just about 70 miles on the New River Trail we have discovered that we really like these bikes. We have had to adjust the derailleurs due to the cables stretching, as anyone who understands MTBs or any bike knows that happens with new bikes. That is not a hard procedure to do. We have added lights a bell and padded seat covers to each bike. I have learned to pad the frames when folded up in the back of my car to prevent scratches. Another bike owner said the bikes appear to be Xtra SPECial. I had not thought of the names like that but I have to agree with his sentiment.

Review: This is a good folding e-bike but it fits a specific niche use, which is basically short distance (under 15 miles for throttle-only) on smooth surfaces like well-maintained bike trails and sidewalks.  Because of the small wheel size (16") you will feel bumps a lot more than a full size e-bike with 20" or larger wheels.  Maybe if you are a teen or small adult this won't bother you as much but I am 6'2" and 260 and I could really feel the bigger bumps!  When it's folded it can easily fit in the cargo area of a crossover or SUV but I think it would be a very tight fit in the trunk of a small sedan.  The downsides are that the battery is removable but the manufacturer website doesn't even stock the battery, which is weird.  With modern e-bikes, it should be easy to design a two-battery system where it automatically switches to the 2nd battery when the 1st battery is drained.  Also, this bike doesn't come with a speedometer or odometer which is unacceptable for the price tag.  Finally, the stock seat is not comfortable and I had to buy a larger cushion aftermarket seat too better accommodate my larger butt size.

Review:
This bike is excellent; two thumbs up !
All the parts were present in excellent condition.(shipping was fast and prudent as well)
Assembly was simple.

For a safe ride make sure to:

- Tighten the stem of the handle really well or you will fall on face. Take it to a local bike shop and they will tighten it for you if you don't have the correct tools.
- Get adhesive stickers for the inside of the rims so you don't get flats often from the inside. They are cheap and all bike shops will have them; they last for about 10 years so no worries about replacing them.
- Adjust breaks properly.
- Tighten the wheel bolts well or they will slowly come off as you ride.
- Tighten all bolts really hard (especially the handle).

Other observations:
- The tires are in excellent condition for such a cheap (I got it for $170) bike.
- People were talking about how they replaced the breaks, peddles and the handle because they were not safe, this is an overreaction. All the parts are excellent in their factory condition. DO NOT waste money on new parts if the part that comes with the bike are not broken.

If you do all these things you will have an excellent bike in your hands. It feels like you are flying through the air when you ride it.

I hope this was helpful, thank you for reading.

Review:
What can I say about this bike that hasn't been said by other reviewers? This is a great bike for petite women with top brand-name components. It is very affordable even at regular price. However, I was lucky enough to have bought on sale directly through Target.

The bottom line is, I think Forge is the only brand that offer 15" frame, 24" wheels, and aluminum frame.

I don't understand why Target does not carry this particular brand in their store since so many people love it. It will certainly save me and a lot of people money on shipping charge and time consuming assembly.

The only reason I'm giving this a 4 star rating is because of the break. The bike assembly was simple enough, with the help of my husband ;-). However the break was a different story. I was struggling with it for over an hour before I decided to go on online to look to see if there is any instruction. The bottom line is if you don't have a man to help you out, be prepare to bring the bike to a bike shop to have it put together for a small fee and save you the headache. One other thing, for all the girly-girl out there, who wants to upgrade their bike with things like fenders, basket, bells and etc, be prepare to spend around an extra hundred for those things. Happy riding and I hope you will enjoy this bike as much as I do.

Review:
This is a review of the Schwinn Comfort Bicycle, Model Suburban DLX.

Here our my thoughts and observations:

-Nice strong frame with good welds and nice finish.
-This is a heavier bike.
-Strong 6061 aluminum frame with limited lifetime warranty by Schwinn!
-Good attachment points and cable routing system.
-Frame also has attachment points for a bottle cage and rear rack system. Those items are not included.
-Nicely cushioned seat with built-in suspension post and quick-release lever for easy adjustments.
-Handlebars come with fully adjustable quill stem for easy height and angle adjustments.
-Handlebars have nicely padded grips.
-Brakes and shifters are comfortably positioned to the inside of the grips.
-Rear shifter is an indexed 7-speed twist-type shifter.
-Front chainring shifter is a 3-speed friction-twist shifter.
-Front and rear brakes are V-rim brakes.
-This is a hardtail frame with a non-adjustable front suspension fork.
-Tires are 27.5 x 2.4 inches with a nice center bead and good traction lugs to the outside, making this bike versatile for street or gravel/dirt roads and trails, such as rails-to-trails conversions.
-Both wheels are sturdy 36-spoke aluminum with a nicely finished braking surface.
-Front and rear wheels also have quick-release hubs for easy flat repair.
-This is a 7-speed rear cassette with a triple chainring up front, making the bike versatile for tackling hills or riding distances.
-All shifting and braking components are Shimano, which is great!
-The bike is made in China.

There are no assembly instructions included that are specific to this bike model. The average person should consider taking this bike to a shop for assembly and adjustments.

Overall, a well made bike for a good price! It should last quite a long time! Recommended! 5 stars!

Review:
This is a great bike at a reasonable price. My son is 4 years old, 42 inches (106cm) tall, with a 16 inch (41cm) inseam and we bought the 14 inch bike for him--which turned out to be the perfect size. Thank goodness we didn't go larger, as we debated doing. It's hard to find 14" bikes in stores to try on for size, so I hope that helps. He loves his new bike! He went from a Strider balance bike to this one, so we didn't use the training wheels, but here are my thoughts on the rest of it:

1. Thick tires add stability. I'm sure the kids have to pedal more/harder, because of the added friction, but it's a great feature for kids learning to ride a bike without training wheels. We've gotten several comments from strangers about how amazed they were he was riding a two-wheeler and how much easier this bike looked due to the thick tires. (Some of his ability we owe to the strider balance bike, of course; it took him about 10 minutes to figure out how pedals worked, but other than that, he took off on this bike pretty readily. However, I think the thick tires definitely helped with the transition and continue to help him as a new rider.) Since his dad mountain bikes, my son loves going on the kiddie-level dirt trails "like my dad" and the tires on this bike are especially great for that kind of terrain.

2. Sleek design. It really is an attractive bike and yes, kids care that their bike likes "cool." (We got the white bike.)

3. Nice wide seat with a rim on the back. Comfortable, easy for kids to sit on and feel balanced and secure. This is something else I've heard comments about. ("What I wonderful wide seat!")

4. Two sets of brakes--there is a hand brake on the handlebars, and the pedal brakes as well. I mostly see my son (4yo) use the pedals, but he says he uses both. If nothing else, I think he likes that it looks like his dad's bike. It's nice to have both options.

5. I really like the the chain is enclosed with a plastic guard so he doesn't get caught in it or manage to pull it off. His pants don't get dirty and curious fingers don't get pinched. A neighbor kid always seems to have his (exposed) chain fall off and, so far, this hasn't been an issue at all with this bike.

6. The seat goes so low. Another bike I looked at did not adjust as low, despite being the same size bike. That was a MUST for him at this stage, since it would have been too tall for him when he was just learning to ride and needed to be able to reach the ground. Of course, the seat raises, too.

7. The bell broke in about a week's time. I don't know if it's because the attaching ring is just plastic, or if my son was too rough with it. That's disappointing, but doesn't affect the integrity of the bike itself, so...oh well. I worried that the water bottle holder, which is also plastic, would break with all the use; but so far it's held up great. Speaking of, the water bottle is a nice extra. Its not a make-or-break, but my son LOVES that he has his own real water bottle. He feels like he has a "real mountain bike" like his dad.

Other than the bell breaking, I would be hard-pressed to find any complaint about this bike. We can hardly get my son off his bike. He'd sleep in his helmet if we'd let him, just so he'd be ready to ride at any given moment.

If you're looking for a set of bike lights for safety, I really recommend these: [[ASIN:B00KYIECY8 The Friendly Swede Silicone 7 LED Bike Light Set, White and Red]]. They're great lights, inexpensive, very bright, easy to attach or remove, have several light pattern settings, and they fit nicely.

Review:
This is a very solid little bike that I ordered for my 5-year-old daughter. After about three weeks of pedaling around, I can say that she *LOVES* this bike. Dad's a big cyclist and she's been riding a trail-a-bike with me for the last two years, so this is technically the first bike of her own, not counting her Strider bike (which she inherited from big sister and hasn't wanted to use much).

She has ridden this bike to school daily for a week on top of wanting to ride it around the driveway or to the park on weekends or during the summer before school started.

Here's my take on this bike:

ASSEMBLY - I assembled this bike in <15 minutes using the tools RoyalBaby provided. I didn't really need the instructions, but I do most of my own bike maintenance. I know my way around a bike.

WEIGHT - The bike is a little lighter than a similar Magna bike a neighbor gave us. All of these low-end/entry steel frame kid's bikes are pretty heavy. The kids outgrow them so quickly, I have a hard time paying for aluminum frames and lighter components on a bike that might get used for a couple of years. That said, this bike is heavy enough that my daughter was pretty worn out at the end of a 2-mile ride.

COST - At this price, it's easy to find second-hand and even new (on sale) aluminum-frame bikes with 16" wheels. I don't think I would ever spend $180 for a steel-frame kid's bike unless I knew I had several kids/cousins in the pipeline to use it and wanted something that could survive a lot of abuse.

SIZING - I intentionally ordered this bike on the large side so that my 5-year-old could get two seasons of use out of it. If we're lucky, we might squeeze out three. The disadvantage is that the seat is just slightly too high for her to ditch the training wheels right away. And the training wheels aren't as adjustable as they should be (see the photos), so they have more contact with the ground than I would prefer. Otherwise, I like that in the 16" wheel size, this bike comes with both training wheels and a kickstand so that my daughter can grow into it (instead of quickly out of it).

SAFETY - The training wheels that come with this bike are basic (not heavy-duty) and adjust only at the wheel, not at the hub. This means that there are really only two settings in the 16" wheel version of this bike: all four wheels flat on the ground, or the two training wheels just slightly (one notch) up off the ground. The wheels arrived out-of-the-box in the former position, which is **dangerous**! Be careful to adjust to the latter setting when building this bike, otherwise: 1) traction / braking power is reduced 2) the rider can't lean into a turn and is more likely to topple over if going too fast.

Also related to safety, I like that this bike has both a handlebar brake for the front wheel and a coaster brake for the rear wheel. This seems like a reasonable step towards preparing the rider for a bigger bike with two handlebar brakes. The coaster brake works well and stops the bike quickly. I also like that the chain guard protects the entire chain (top and bottom).

Side-note: the training wheels that come with this bike have a rubber lining, making them much quieter than any other training wheels I've seen or used on kid's bikes.

My bottom line: if you really want a new bike for your kid or if you know this bike will get passed around and used by several kids, it's a reasonable value for the size and the quality. I'm taking off one star for the out-of-the-box setting on the training wheels, which is a safety hazard.

It got this model on sale through Amazon. I had researched over a dozen different makes and models. My first concern was the weight they would handle, because I'm well over 200lbs. It worked right the very first time. I'm 62, so my core was really out of shape and since I'd had medical problems in the past, I rode around on the grass in the back yard...What a work out! Much better an any stationary bike, and I've owned two! I've ridden it, now that I can go a bit further than a mile, downtown on the side streets, up and down hills, and steadily making progress in strength, balance, endurance (more lung expansion), and enjoyment.

Definitely recommend this bike, surprisingly. I'd always heard foreign bikes were better made, stronger, etc. This one does weigh a bit, but on the road...not even noticeable. If you are in it for exercise, the weight of the bike is minor...and it appears to have a very strong steel frame. The front forks have a nice shock absorber like motorcycles do, and that's nice keeping the bump jarring to a minimum.

Review:
I picked up the World DBX on a closeout and I'm very glad I did. I had been looking at some comparable bikes with similar features and they were twice or more what I paid. The genius of the bike is the rear caliper being mounted inside the rear triangle instead of outside which is standard. This allows standard rack and fenders to be mounted without having to go the disk special type racks.

The Avid BB7's disc brakes work great. I've got discs on my mountain bike and I don't think I'll ever go back to canti's again. The braking modulating is better than typical brakes and the rim getting hot is not an issue. Although, the rotors do get hot and it doesn't take much to get them hot. I got a nice singer on the leg after testing the brakes on a small downhill and then straddled the front wheel adjusting the handlebars afterward.

The inline brake handles are a great feature. It's easier and more natural to go from the hoods to the tops than the hoods to the drops for those with a mountain bike history. The caveat is that they eat up valuable bar space. After adding the bracket for my handlebar bag, I'm out of room to mount any lights or a computer. So I'll be picking up a bar extender/swing bar soon for the lights. Not sure about the computer, but I don't know if I want one. My commute seems much more relaxed not knowing how fast I'm going.

The components are fair to good. 105 rear derailer, tiagra front and tiagra sti shifters. The 105 rear handles the 9 cog rear with ease. I think the tiagra front suffices, but I might switch it out for an upper model. Same for the shifters.

I've read elsewhere that the wheels are very low end and heavier than a good wheel. I don't know really care as long as they last and are issue free. This isn't a race bike and I'm not counting oz.'s.

The fenders are a nice touch and have made a difference on my commute. Even though I'm technically in the desert, there's enough surface water to splatter things up nicely from sprinklers and water trucks spraying to keep dust down

The tires seem sufficient. I'm glad the bike can handle wide cycecross tires and I'll be taking some of those on my mountain tour next month. I'll also be upgrading to a more beefier tire for commuting. Continental Gators probably.

The pedals are clipless on one side and platform on the other. A great feature. However, I'm in love with my Powergrips and swapped those in. I'll probably go more clipless once I break my new shoes in on the mountain bike.

The gearing is sufficient. 27 speeds. 50/39/30 up front. 12-26 in the rear. If that isn't sufficient, a touring group up front might be better. I might go that route when I do more tour next month.

The bike has more heft than a lot of bikes and comes in at 23 lbs or so. But that's still lighter than most mountain bikes. And for a commuter, it's no big deal. The carbon fork helps with weight. I just hope it's durable.

Overall, I couldn't happier with this bike as a commuter.

These have been discontinued so they won't be around for long. The new line are flat bar with internal hubs and less gearing options. That just doesn't work for long commute with hills, heat, and wind.

Review:
I normally do not review my Amazon Purchased. But since an electric bike is an expensive purchase, I would like to share my experience. The cityscape bike appears to be well made and was easy to assemble. I did not have to do any tuning; it was a simple setup and my wife is very happy with the bike. We also receive a Iphone bike mount which was not shown on the purchase order in a separate shipment. Like any electric bike it is substantially heavier than my 21 speed mountain bike and is noticeable when putting on the bike rack. Since it is a step thru, you may need to get an Ebike Adapter to fit on your bike rack. I found the following review on Youtube to be useful using search words “Heybike Cityscape Commuter eBike Test and Review”
An important item you should look at with an ebike is the ability to replace the lithium battery after 3+ years, do they currently sell replacement batteries? Heybike does sell the replacement battery. Some of the other competitor bikes in the same range currently do not sell a replacement or second battery (good luck a couple of years from now).
The next important item is customer service. When I assembled the bike, the post allows the handlebars to be positioned according to height and body size. Unfortunately, the hex screw on one side was over tighten and would not loose as the compression fitting slipped. I sent an email to customer service. Within 12 hours they responded back that I could take it to a bike shop and they would refund the cost. I corresponded back and explained the problem and a new part would make the most sense. Within 12 hours they setup having the part shipped to me. They also noted since I mentioned my wife was short, they could send a different seat post which would drop the set down 2” lower if I wanted it. For the price, my wife is very happy with the purchase. I enjoyed the fast positive customer service which appears to be at Spokane in Washington state.

Review:
I REALLY like this bike. I've been able to ride it for two days now that the smoke has gone and I've put over 32 miles on it.
It is really well engineered - solid and adjustable just about every way you need it to be. Today I discovered that the seat post clamp has a thumb screw so I can easily adjust the seat on the fly. Not tools needed! It does mean you'd want to take the seat with you when the bike is out of sight.
It easily goes 23-26 mph without pedaling when you're in PAS 3. If you pedal at all the bike will supplement your pedaling to the top speed for the PAS level you have it set to. You can program it for up to 7 PAS levels and the top speed for each level will get more granular.
The speed limit on shared use paths around here is 15 mph and if I have the bike in PAS 1 it won't go over 14.
It goes fasted in each level when you use the throttle alone with no pedaling but it's quite easy to maintain a good speed with minimal pedaling due to the PAS boost.
It's heavy but you really don't notice while riding and only when you try to pick it up. That also makes it a very solid ride. I've had it on paved and gravel paths, up some long relatively steep hills and down the same. The fat tires make gravel inconsequential.
The power up the steepest hill from a dead stop gave me 7 mph which is just fine. The regeneration down long hills while coasting twice made the power bars increase by 1 so that works!
Speaking of power, I figure the battery is good for at least 32 miles and probably longer. That's with a consistent use of power assist, some stretches of throttle only, and fairly light pedaling. When I got home from my last ride there was still one bar showing with no indication of running out of power. When I check the meter on that battery itself there were two bars showing so I'm thinking Eahora may have built a reserve into the computer meter.
This bike is replacing an normal bike with a power front wheel replacement and this integrated eBike is a far superior experience.
At this point I only have good things to say about this bike. Also when I contacted Eahora about a possible accessory order problem I got a great response same day so their customer service seems very good too.

One thing to note - the computer you get on the bike is the one in the pictures online but the manual they have a link to on the XC200 page isn't the right one. Go to the Blog on the web site and you can download the owner's manual (doesn't come in the box) and the correct computer manual - 80C - from Google drive.

NOTE - I put this review on Eahora's web site as well...

10/13/2021 Update:

Unfortunately, I've lower my rating from 5 to 2 stars. After about 1.5 months of owning the bike, I began to encounter a couple of serious problems. The first problem is that the motor will randomly turn off and on. As I pedal, the motor will rapidly shut off and on, with the off phase lasting about a second or two. The problem persists until I stop and rest the bike for a few minutes. Sometimes the motor will just shut off and stay off for a minute or two. It first would only happen going up a hill, but later the problem progressed and would occur even on level terrain. When it first started happening, I would press the throttle, but that didn't make a difference. And a couple of times I received a "Throttle Error" message on the monitor. I contacted the seller and was promptly sent a new throttle, and so far that seemed to fix the throttle error message, but that didn't correct the problem with the motor shutting off. The help support was responsive to my emails and we traded several emails over the course of several weeks trying to determine the source of the problem, but to no avail.

The other problem I'm encountering with the bike is that the battery charge indicator stopped working. I can go for a 22-mile ride and the indicator will show a full battery charge; whereas, before the problem, it would show a half-full battery charge after riding that same distance.

The seller has made many attempts to resolve the issue and I appreciate the effort, but the problem is beyond their knowledge. They eventually referred me to a nearby bike shop where my bike currently is being evaluated. They've had the bike for over a week and are still trying to figure out the source of the problem.
___________________________
Original Review:

This was my first e-bike purchase, and I'm glad to say I had no buyer's remorse after riding it. If you like fun, this is it on two wheels. An e-bike puts the fun in bike riding because you can choose the amount of effort you want in pedaling the bike, or you can choose to not pedal at all if you like. With this e-bike, not only do you get that choice, but you get it with extra power and battery life.

I've only had the bike for 6 weeks so far, but I've taken it on different types of terrain and it's pulled through like a real trouper. I live in a hilly area, and with this e-bike, the hills are no issue. No more being distracted by pumping the pedals until my legs feel like they're going to burn out from under me. Just easy and fun riding while enjoying the scenery.

The fastest I've gone on this e-bike is 27 mph, which was downhill on the open road. In a car, that seems slow, but on a bike, it feels like you're on a jet. This e-bike can sail along at that speed smoothly.

This e-bike is lighter than many other e-bikes, but it feels sturdy and well-balanced.

One downside is the instruction manual, which is in pidgin English. I can make out the instructions, but it takes a little longer to figure it out than usual. However, the quality of the e-bike more than makes up for it.

One issue I had was with the headlight not working. When I press the button to turn on the light, the light indicator in the monitor turns on, but the light does not turn on. I checked the wire connector, but it seems the light is defective. I contacted the seller who had me take a couple of troubleshooting steps, but to no avail. So, the seller has shipped off a new headlight to me with no problem (good customer service). I'm not subtracting a star for the defective headlight since I realize issues like that are unavoidable, but it's important that the seller cares about customer satisfaction, product quality, and is prompt with implementing a resolution, which this seller has proven to be.

Review:
Some months ago, I bought the 26" 3-speed "Around the Block" cruiser and decided recently to buy this 24" single-speed "Around the Block" cruiser for our 10-year-old daughter. The bike arrived promptly in a week and assembly took about an hour. Since there are no hand brakes to deal with, you basically only need to assemble the front tire, the handlebar, the seat/saddle, the pedals, and the rear rack. Having a set of wrenches (especially in sizes 9/16 and metric 15) would greatly help with tightening properly all the bolts. It is also a good idea to buy a small WD-40 spray and coat well the coils under the seat - those tend to creek after a while unless oiled properly.

The only issue I had with the bike was the valve of one of the tires - it was difficult (for some reason) to make it work with my bike pump, but I was able after several attempts to inflate properly the tire to about 45-50 PSI.

Other observations: the teal color is nice and the bike itself behaves very well on the road - no loose bearings, clonking pedals, or squeaking saddle coils. The rear coaster break is very responsive and easy to use. I also added a handlebar mirror (this model fits great: https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B06XWRMZZF) as a safety precaution. The only downside is the seat/saddle - it is hard, and I mean HARD. Certainly not comfortable.

My impressions of the "Around the Block" cruisers are generally positive - they are not without issues, but are a step above what one could find in stores like Target, Walmart, or Meijer.

Review:
I bought this bike because I wanted something to ride around my community with my kids or commute the short distance to work. I already have a Specialized road bike but wanted something more casual and comfy and capable of light off road for dirt and gravel trails.

First the good stuff. This bike looks really nice, good lines, nice matte black paint job, moderately fat tires. The riding position is upright with the curved handlebars and the saddle is wide and has springs. It's pretty low fatigue to tool around on and I can do an hour or more on it with minimal discomfort. The 7 speed grip shift is pretty simple although it's a little high effort to twist and the gears seem to range from "not quite granny gear but ok for modest hills" to "faster than you'd expect for a bike like this". No problem riding streets and trails or over bumps, small curbs, tree roots etc. It's not a mountain bike but is perfectly fine for the boardwalks and unpaved trails where I live. Front and rear pull brakes like a real bike, fenders are nice if you ride in the rain or through puddles (a real concern when I was commuting).

Now the not so good. This bike is super heavy. It feels sturdy but man, it's like double the weight of my road bike. You won't want to carry it anywhere and you'll feel it if you want to ride faster than 5-7 mph. Build quality is just ok. My brakes squeal, the seat springs are noisy, the seat doesn't have a quick release so it's a PITA to adjust. Assembly was relatively straightforward if you have the tools but adjusting the brakes and derailleurs properly to get good stops and shifts through all the gears is tricky. You may have to put some real force into tightening the handlebars and seat post so they won't move. The tires are smaller diameter and fatter than a hybrid bike so you'll have to work harder to go far and fast. The fenders can get out of whack if you bump them so the tire will rub. I also get a weird rhythmic clacking on certain gears and the bike is noisy when coasting.

Overall if you want a good looking cruiser that's easy to ride and will handle most terrain without breaking the bank, this Huron is pretty good. If your budget is bigger, you can probably get something higher quality that's a little lighter, smoother, and with better parts. Also you may want to look into a hybrid or mountain bike instead if you're going to do minimal/mostly off road. I would definitely not buy this bike if you live on the 5th floor of an apartment and have to carry it up and down the steps because of the weight. For a simple relaxed ride that can go almost anywhere though, this bike should be plenty.

Review:
I am an experienced mountain biker, and have ridden for over 18 years. I had a intermediate level Giant several years back, but then suffered a shoulder injury, had a kid and got married, so I took a few years off from mountain biking and sold my Giant. I was looking to get back into mountain biking a few years ago, and after some research I decided to give this entry level bike a shot.

It came half assembled with everything you need to complete assembly yourself, however, I highly recommend you book some time at a local bike shop and have them professionally assemble and set the bike up for you. You will have a much better experience with a professionally set up bike. While you're there, you definitely want to have them put some new tires with more tread on. The tires it comes with are not suitable for any but the most basic flat trail riding. Depending on your bike shop, you can expect to spend 70-100 bucks getting all this work done, but trust me, it's worth it.

Once properly set up, this bike is awesome. I've broken down the different aspects of the bike below.

Frame: This bike has a standard aluminum frame, strong, but fairly heavy. Still, light enough to throw around on most trails with ease.

Brakes: Comes with basic Shimano Disk Brakes that have plenty of stopping power and are easy to tune.

Suspension: Basic suspension. the rear shock is highly adjustable to your preferences, the front shock less so, but still with a good feel and they get the job done, even on more advanced trails. Overall, I am very happy with the quality of the suspension, especially for the price of this bike.

Drivetrain: Shimano shifters, entry level but good quality. I've had some minor issues with the gears on this bike, but that's mainly the result of impact damage from rocks, tree trunks, roots, etc. Not an indictment of the quality of the hardware, as you'll have the same problems even on higher end bikes. If you break something, just take the bike in to your local shop and they will fix it like new. Things break when you smash them, that's true with any bike I've owned. Overall, the bike drives very well. I can tackle very steep hills with ease, and downhills are no problem either. the bike shifts very smoothly, even when climbing hills, so that's always a nice touch to a riding experience.

Wheels and Tires: The wheels are fine, they have stood up to some pretty serious abuse over the last couple years. The tires it comes with are garbage, so you will want to replace them asap.

Overall Riding Experience: All I can say is I am happy with this bike. It was the perfect bike to get back into riding after several years off. Even two years back in and almost back to my old level, I still don't feel like I need to upgrade to a better bike just yet. I just like this bike, and it suits me very well. It rides smoothly, and the hardware it comes with is high quality for the price. I have zero complaints with this purchase and I think this bike is a great place to start if you want to get into mountain biking. there are moments when I catch my foot on a root that I wish I had 27.5 or 29 inch wheels, but honestly, it's really a blessing in disguise, as I've learned to be more careful and precise in my lines to avoid foot and toe injuries, and it's made me a faster rider overall. plus, the 26 inch wheels really shine on downhills, which are my favorite lines to ride.
