Review:
UPDATE, November 2021. Earlier this year (in June) I was pedaling my way up a hill using motor assist when the bike just stopped. The lights were still on, everything looked like it should work, but it didn't.

That's when I entered a Kafkaesque nightmare of pain and suffering. First, I contacted the seller, who sent endless requests for me to send pictures and videos of what was going on. Even when I did, more requests came. This lasted for well over a month. At this point, the seller advised me to take it to a bike shop and get it fixed and they would reimburse me. No way was I going to do that.

So, I contacted Amazon, thinking the matter would be resolved rather quickly. No dice. Amazon refused to do anything, even though the seller had clearly advertised a six month warranty and the malfunction fell within that. They kept promising to do an A-Z claim, but then kept saying I needed to wait 48 hours. When I would come back after 48 hours, they would claim that I needed to wait 48 MORE hours. This also went on for months. Nobody at Amazon every solved my problem, despite all I've heard and experienced regarding Amazon's spectacular customer service.

One Amazon rep suggested I contact the manufacturer. So, I did. And guess what? The manufacturer's contact info was exactly the same as what the seller sent me. They were the same person. LOL. Can't make this up. And virtually every seller of Vivi bikes listed showed the same information. Unreal.

So, I went back to bugging the seller. At one point, he offered to take the bike back for a full refund, if I paid to have it shipped. To China. Literally, that would have been half the cost of the bicycle. So, I refused that, obviously.

Finally, in OCTOBER, after having missed half the summer and a large portion of fall riding, the seller agreed to refund $200 and send me a new motor controller in an attempt to fix it. I got the $200 refunded to my credit card, but the motor controller hasn't arrived yet (on a slow boat from China, no doubt, if it's coming at all).

So, I've been calling around to see if someone locally can fix my Chinese e-bike. Turns out, nobody will even look at it. So, if I ever get the motor controller (not holding my breath) I will be on my own getting it wired up and hope that this fixes the problem. If I install it correctly, that is. Maybe I will, maybe not. Who knows.

So, if you're thinking about getting one of these, buy a cheap standard non electric bike for $150 or so, flush $200 down the toilet, and take $200 out of your account for a few months and put it back in, and it will be a less frustrating approximation of my situation as it stands right now.

I'll be back if the seller sends the motor controller. In other words, I probably won't be back.

ORIGINAL REVIEW FOLLOWS.

First, some background.

Last year, a buddy of mine talked me into going on a bike trip. I pulled my 25 year old mountain bike out of storage. It was in not-so-good shape. I put a little money, time and elbow grease into it and got it ready for the trip. Except for one big hill, I enjoyed that trip more than I ever thought I would.

Ever since, I began thinking that a little help would make the ride that much more enjoyable. So, I started researching ebikes. My first thought was to buy a kit and install it on my old bike (kinda like those people who put flashy rims on a 1995 beater car), but I decided to skip the hassle and complication, and for a few (hundred) dollars more, get this from Amazon.

About me: I am pushing 50. I am not, nor have I ever been, a "mountain biker". I am not "extreme" nor "gnarly". I can't recall ever being "stoked". I don't refer to people as "dudes". I avoid energy drinks, don't wear spandex, and am not "extreme" in any sense of the word. I just wanted something that would help me to enjoy riding a bike without requiring me to wear myself out.

Once the bike arrived, my helper (pictured above) and I set out to begin assembly. There is nothing difficult about assembly, but the directions were somewhat confusing. For example, it said to set the fork of the bike so that the caliper was facing forward. This being the first disc brake bike I owned, I didn't understand what a forward or backward caliper looked like. After a little detective work, I figured it out. A better way of putting it would be, "Set the fork so that the caliper is on the same side of the bike that it is on the rear brake." That would make far better sense.

Despite this, the manual was NOT written in broken English. Everything was understandable from a linguistic standpoint. Some of the pictures and illustrations were of other bikes, but they were still useful, if somewhat small and lacking detail.

Everything else was straightforward. I started looking at the bike and evaluating it before I took my first ride (battery was still charging).

Things I liked and disliked to this point:

Finish is excellent. It's not high gloss, which is fine (that's the style these days). Everything looks powder-coated, nice and even. Welds are neat and solid. The seat wasn't bad, but I replaced it with a fat-boy seat anyway. Pedals are plastic, seem sturdy enough, but I'd like alloy.

Hardware: Most of it looks good, but the retaining pin for the front wheel screws on with a plastic nut, which I do NOT like. I may attempt to find a metric wing nut and washer in the same size and replace it. I just don't trust plastic hardware, and it seems a lot to ask from a piece of plastic to hold the front wheel on. Nevertheless, I haven't had any issues with it in the short time I've ridden the bike.

Appearance: I had considered the Ancheer in the same price range, but it looks like an old man's bike (said the almost 50 year old man). This looks way better than that. It has a blackout treatment on the wheels, appropriately knobby tires, modern looking color scheme and graphics, and just an all around look of quality. Not that the Ancheer isn't, but I much prefer the appearance of this. The down tube is over-sized and squared, which adds strength, but it just looks good too.

Tool kit: You get a multi-use bike wrench/Phillips screwdriver, several Allen wrenches, and a double ended two-sized wrench that looks like it was cast in someone's backyard, not forged, complete with flashing around the outside edge. They're functional, if not the highest quality. I used them a little to assemble and adjust the bike and they worked fine. I put them in a pouch which I attached to the bike, and will keep them there in case they are needed.

Battery: Can charge on or off the bike. I just use the key (don't forget to find this in the box, VERY important) to remove the battery, take it in the house, and charge it. The key does NOT enable or disable the battery. It simply locks the battery to the bike to prevent theft. There is a switch on the opposite side that turns the battery on and off, as well as a charging jack with a rubber cover to keep dirt out. The whole setup is very slick and works well. I charged the battery with the switch in both on and off position, and it didn't seem to make any difference. Also, there's a meter on the side of the battery that indicates charge when you push a button.

First ride: To start, the brakes needed some adjustment, which I did at the brake levers by turning the small adjusters where the cables came out. The brakes have a curious tendency: when going down a long hill, if you apply the brakes evenly, without increasing or decreasing pressure on the levers, it will feel like the brakes are a bit inadequate. But, after a short time, they will "heat up" and begin to grip better. There will be a noticeable "slow down" a few seconds after applying the brakes. This can be disconcerting if you're not prepared for it.

Also, the more I rode, the more the rear caliper made noise, even when I wasn't braking. It might need adjusted a bit more, or the pads may need scuffed, or both. Regardless, it's a minor issue.

What wasn't a minor issue was how far out of adjustment the derailleurs were. The front was so far out that I shifted down once and the chain came off completely. I wasn't far from home, so I just limped back in throttle mode (electric to the rescue!). I adjusted the limiter screws until they lined up correctly and haven't had a problem since. The rear was better, in that the chain never jumped off, but it occasionally "slips" a gear while pedaling. It helps to move the shift lever up or down a notch to make sure the derailleur lines up correctly over the sprocket. I still need to adjust this a little more for it to be perfect.

As far as the ride is concerned, I replaced the seat with a suspension seat made for fat boys like me, which really smooths out the bumps. The tires are rated for 45-60 PSI, and I only inflated to 45. This probably hurts my battery range, but it makes for a comfortable ride and slightly better grip on loose surfaces. I may compromise and set it at 50 or so. I figured the front shock would be a gimmick, but it really does absorb a lot of vibration and bumpiness and makes the ride much less fatiguing.

Electric power is nice. In my area, there are lots of hills. The motor won't take me up hills on throttle only, but it makes it easy to pedal up them. 250 watts equates to about 1/3 of a horsepower, so keep your expectations realistic and you won't be disappointed. This is an assistive motor more than one that does the work for you. That said, the only time I pedaled was when it seemed like I would lose sufficient momentum that my speed would drop below 10 mph. My last ride had elevation changes of over 100 feet, and grades as steep as 7%. I was able to pedal up them with minimal-to-moderate effort. I would classify my "in-shape" level as better than "Little Debbie Corp. sends me a Christmas card every year" but not quite as good as, "Doesn't huff and puff after walking up a flight of stairs".

Nevertheless, I rode for well over an hour on this thing, up and down hills, big stupid grin on my face, still getting some exercise, and only came home because it was getting dark (I set out to run the battery down and failed). You can buy more powerful bikes, but you'll probably have to go up a couple hundred dollars, and for me, it's worth the savings to pedaling a little bit, since I could use the activity anyway.

That said, on flat, level ground, the thing will move even fat boys along at a brisk pace. I could easily hit 18 mph (GPS confirmed) on throttle only on level ground, depending on whether the wind was at my back or I was driving straight into it. Either way, the bike moved along briskly without feeling unsafe. If you live in an area where the land is almost completely flat, you could do lots of local commuting with this thing, not having to pedal a bit. You can go up small grades with throttle mode only, and if things steepen up, you can pedal once or twice to keep the momentum up. In all, I rode about 7 miles on my first "real" ride on this bike and while I did get some exercise, I wasn't sore, wrung out, or exhausted when I got home. Perfect.

Pedal assist mode is interesting if you've never ridden a bike like this before. You start pedaling, and think, "This thing isn't working at all." Then all of a sudden, the pedals get really light and you surge forward. There were three levels to pedal assist, and I always used the highest level. It can be a bit disconcerting, because sometimes the motor doesn't shut off immediately when you stop pedaling. Squeezing the brakes seems to stop the motor, though.

About the only thing I can see that I DON'T like concerning the electric assist is that it's hard going from pedal assist to throttle mode. Depending on which level of PA you're in, you might have to hit the button 3X before it switches (it's sequential). In addition to changing gears, it can be a bit like driving a dual stick 18 wheeler...lol.

On the last leg of the journey, I hit another flat and switched to throttle mode, and confirmed that the top speed was still the same, meaning the battery wasn't significantly down on power after going 7 miles. The battery meter bobbed between 2-3 bars out of 5, which suggested that the battery was somewhere around half charged. This would put the range (for me) at about 14 miles, which is more than adequate considering the hills I was riding up and the extensive use of throttle-only in pretty much any situation that I could do it. Again, I set out to drain the battery and after over an hour of going up and down hills using pedal assist and riding throttle-only, I failed to do it.

Stability is great. At one point, i was blasting down a paved road at full throttle when, lo and behold, the pavement ended and it turned to slightly-smaller-than-golf-ball-sized gravel. I was afraid I was going to wipe out, but the bike was very composed (largely due to the front shock, I imagine) and didn't show any signs of sliding or tipping. I was able to apply the brakes and get the speed down safely and quickly without issue.

All in all, a very nice bike for the price, and thus far, I haven't seen anything about it that gives me second thoughts about my purchase. Keep in mind, this review was written in Week One, so your mileage may vary.

This is a long-term review and I will come back in a few months with updates about range, ruggedness, wear, etc.

I do have a concern that has nothing to do with the bike itself. Vivi sent me a card for a "$40 discount" if I put a 5 star review on Amazon, took a screenshot, and sent it to them.

Needless to say, I passed on that. Am I the only person who got this? Unfortunately, if people really did give this bike 5 stars, that practice certainly diminishes the reliability of the ratings.

Also, I am not listed as a "verified purchaser" because I bought this under a different listing, and reviewing for that has been shut down due to "unusual activity". Probably because of the $40 discount given for a 5 star rating, lol.

Thus far, the bike has been very good and I would buy it again without reservation.

Review:
This bike arrived very well packaged in a one-way-up-only box. Inside, the bike was very well protected by various packaging materials/sleeves and several sacrificial plastic parts that shielded it from punching holes through the cardboard. It would be great to have a bike repair station to mount the frame during assembly but I managed just fine with balancing it on these plastic pieces. Assembly is really easy as all of the gearing and brakes were pretty much dead-on out of the box. I had to make some slight adjustments for the linear pull brakes, but that's to be expected since I adjusted the handlebar height/angle etc. I would say I had everything up and on the road within about 20 minutes of opening the box.

The bike is relatively lightweight, a little better than what you would expect at this price point. The welds are clean and all of the lines for brakes/gearing run smoothly and are tethered to the frame perfectly. I was impressed by how smooth and quiet the derailleur works and the very low pedal resistance due to how smooth the tires are. The brakes and front-fork suspension operate very smoothly making for an easy and comfortable ride around the neighborhood. I'd say this is an ideal family style bike for when you're cruising around just for fun/fitness and maybe some light trails but its place is really on surface streets. Great bike!

Review:
I bought this bike on Amazon 14 months ago; I am very pleased with it. Today the odometer (add-on) hit 1108 miles. I have not had ANY breakdowns or repairs. Even no needed adjustments, except on day 1 adjusted the brakes, softened the rear spring, fine adjusted the front sprockets. Took 1 hour to unbox and assemble, going slowly. I oil the chain, gears, pedals, and hubs once a month. Good stopping with the front disc brake.
At 1100 miles, it is finally time for 2 new tires.
The seat was way too firm for my skinny back end so I replaced with a softer seat. Added bright lights, soft ergo handgrips, XOSS GPS speedometer/ heart rate / cadence, small mirror, and small fenders. I ride mostly on paved country roads with some offroad. I am going to buy this exact same model again for our 2nd house on the other coast.
Good styling; I get compliments "that's a great looking bike" from other riders of bikes that cost 4X this one.
Maybe this won't win offroad races, but for a daily driver, it does all I need.
Lastly, with no real other excercise, in the year since I bought this bike and ride every 2 or 3 days, I lost 22 pounds. (male, 60s)

Review:
I got my bike 2nd hand from my boss who never rode it. Literally rode it three times and it gathered dust in his garage! So, after my car broke down he sold me this bike for $60, originally paying around $200 for it I believe.

I get a lot of compliments on the paint job for this bike, a real nice shiny blue and silver. It's a road bike frame and handlebars with a mountain bike gearing system which labels this a "hybrid." it's 3 in the front and 7 in the back - not a typical setup for a real road bike, which would have 2 gears in the front and 8-10 gears in the back. I NEVER use 1st in the front, and heck, I don't even believe my bike can switch into that gear.

I personally did not like the action of the cheap end Shimano Touring rear derailleur - I upgraded to a Shimano Alivio on the rear derailleur and it shifts MUCH better, so much better, and it was only a $32 part at my local bike shop.

I can hit 28mph with some serious pedalling and maintain the distance comfortably on the bike. I would suggest getting
quick-change rim setups on the bike, $50/ea at my bike shop. Totally makes the difference if you run a flat or blow a tire while riding. Ever walked home before from a distance ride? Pfft... sucks!

If you are looking for a nicely priced bike, I would say that this will meet your needs. Better to spend the $$$ on this than a $400-$600 GIANT if you are just testing the waters on cycling for fitness or for fun.

BY THE WAY, this bike is made by KENT BICYCLES out of NJ. Took me forever to figure that out because I needed some replacement parts. Just FYI!

Review: I was skeptical about ordering this bike considering some of the lousy reviews it's received on Amazon.  But, when I looked at most of the negative reviews, they were more about the shipping than the actual bike itself.  IF your Mongoose Switchback arrives in a totally intact cardboard box (which mine did), what you'll find inside is a well-outfitted bike for the price.  First you'll notice the attractive matte paint on the (somewhat heavy, but not unwieldy) aluminum frame with internal cables. Shimano shifters, cassette, cogs, crank, and DISC brakes run smooth.  Hybrid Kendals tires on Xposure aluminum rims are perfect for different surfaces.  Xposure front shock is a bit soft, but is ample as long as you're under 200lbs.  Grips, seat, and handlebars feel firm.  The only knocks I have on this bike after riding it for a few hours over one week is the cheesy feel of the plastic shifters and I question how well the crank arms and pedals would perform taking it downhill.  But, as an everyday bike where it's not going to get much off road abuse, it's a great bike and fun to ride.

Review:
Let me start this off by saying, it’s a great bike. Absolutely amazing if you’re starting a new hobby like myself. While you might ask “why only 3.5 stars then?” We’ll get to that shortly.

I purchased this bike little over a year ago and have put on about 400 miles on it according to my biking computer and I’ve enjoyed it a lot and my new found hobby. This bike is on the lower end scale of price but it is really nice and looks much more like a 1000 dollar bike compared to others in the same category. The components are Shimano Claris, though not the best they still get the job done and it’s relativley smooth. The gearing is a 3X8 which is pretty standard in a lot of bikes and even in the ones you possibly own. While this is neither good nor bad it’s nice to have so many gearing options. The bike is a little stretched out at least in my experience, I’m 6’1 and I purchased the 58cm and sometimes feel as though I’m too extended on the handlebars. If you plan on getting this bike to get outside the house and find a new hobby then it’s a going to be a great new toy to have.

Downsides... We all knew they were coming. And I’m going to try to not be over critical on this aspect. First off maybe about 100 miles into having the bike my front tire make a lot of clicking sounds. So after trying to determine what it was and failing I took it to my local bike shop. Well the first 2 visits were unsuccessful and nothing was done *but I was charged* finally we discovered it needed a wheel true! The front tire was actually not positioning well on the hangers of the bike! Second problem occurred my 2nd ride this season, when I turn my handlebars the forks are free and swing about. Yes the top cap is screwed in all the way and everything is in order but what is causing all the problems I’m not so sure until I take it again to the shop.

In conclusion, the bike is great aside from the few dilemmas. I’ve used this bike in triathalons and races and it has always performed. I would definitely recommend this bike but please proceed with care because you might need to deposit more money if some of the parts are not functioning properly.

Review:

Review: I am a 71 year old male & I have been riding bicycles for many years. I have a Townie 21 speed which I absolutely love & I have put many miles on it. I have a one mile loop dirt road in my neighborhood & I have always wanted a 1 speed or 3 speed bike to ride this loop because I wanted something that would give me a good workout. The 1 mile loop is mostly uphill with about a 15 degree grade. I purchased the Urban Man 3 Speed Beach Cruiser with the intent of eventually being able to complete 10 miles on this 1 mile loop. I thought that this would provide me with a good cardio-vascular workout & provide more of a challenge than my 21 speed Townie, which I still use on longer rides. My Beach Cruiser arrived on schedule & very well packaged. The bike  was very easy to put together(its almost all assembled) & I road it the same afternoon. There is a very slight front wheel wobble but I got the wheel trued at my local bike shop for a minimal fee. I have ridden my new Urban Man on the 1 mile loop & now & up to 5 miles & it gives my 71 year old body a great workout! I have also ridden my new Urban Man many miles on mostly level streets & I am very impressed with how smooth & easy the ride is! This is a very well made bike & is priced very reasonably! I would recommend this bike to any serious or leisure bike enthusiast!

Review:
I love this bike! I was so excited to get this for my son's 4th birthday. My son is very short for a 4 year old and we hadn't been able to find any other big kid bikes that he could operate on his own. This bike is very easy to mount and dismount. My son jumped on the bike and pedaled away like it was nothing. My son uses the bike without training wheels and feels very confident and safe. He can stop and start the bike without any assistance. The bike is lightweight and very manageable for my son to pick it up of the ground and walk it when necessary.

The description says it fits a 3 yr old; that may be possible with training wheels, but I think that it may be a little tall for some 3 yr olds without the training wheels. My son is very short, so not a super good guage for height, but he would not have been able to reach the ground safely prior to turning 4.

The brake levers may be king of big for some small hands. My son is able to reach the hand brakes, but with some difficulty. We are going to see if they can be adjusted or may look into some smaller brake levers.

Overall this bike is amazing. I spent a lot of time looking for a suitable bike and this was the only one I found that met our criteria for an 18 inch bike with a low standover height/ seat height.

I forgot to mention, this bike is fast! We chose to upgrade from a 12 inch bike so that my son would be able to keep up with us and take on bigger hills. This boy just flew over hills with ease.

This was money well spent! I don't think I could find a better bike!

Review:
Took my dad about 2 hours to put this bike together.

Bike does not come with any tools so keep that in mind if purchasing.

Some damage upon arrival that clearly happened when bike parts were being made. Front wheel fender has a nice size dent in it that was painted over and glazed so it happened before shipping. Multiple scratches on frame of bike itself where paint is off. It doesn't bother me but again, it is something to keep in mind.

Bike does not save on space at all when folded down. The width is rather large on the bike - measuring just under 30 inches. So a word of warning to put this bike together outside or inside a house or shed with large framed doors. Whole measure twice cut once deal.

All in all, the bike is great quality for the price. Wish it could back pedal to brake the bike or reverse but sadly it does not. Hand brake is a bit touchy and the tires will definitely need air placed in them - not flat but definitely not in rideable condition upon arrival.

I, personally, found this bike hard to push up a driveway with barely any incline so i can only imagine it on an actual hill. I would safely call this bike a boardwalk bike that is meant for flat, even surfaces only. It won't do well on curbs or deep indented grass or rocks.

Still, when the bike is on smooth surfaces such as the road or sidewalks it rides very easily and can pick up decent speed.

I am 5 ft 2 inches and weigh 273 lbs. Seat is amazing. Springs in seat are nice. But the frame of the bike itself sits up high for me to easily get on without hiking my leg up and over to jump onto the bike, so be wary of that. Wish that the seat could lower down more but it is understandable so your legs do not get caught up in the spokes.

Bike did ship in relatively decent time, though. It came when Amazon said it would be arriving even though Amazon messaged me saying the seller had yet to confirm the order so they would not be able to verify if the bike had actually shipped.

Review:
We ordered this balance bike for our 18 month old daughter who outgrew her smaller balance bike. This bike is a good fit for her. The bike stands at 17" from ground to handlebars and 11" from ground to seat. The wheels are made of plastic. The back wheels are 5 3/4" in diameter and 1 1/2" wide. The front wheel is 8"inches in diameter and 2" wide. The rear wheels are spaced about 3" apart to try and provide some stability while your child rides the balance bike.

What we like:

- The height is perfect for our short 18 month old daughter. The handlebars are also at the the perfect height.

- The wheels roll quite smoothly and do not need air.

- The handlebars are limited as to how far they turn to prevent the bike from tipping over.

- Assembly was easy.

What we don't like:

- We were disappointed with several things about this bike. The first disappointment is that we did not receive the color that we ordered. We ordered a pink balance bike, but received a green bike instead.

- The tires or made of solid plastic. When your child tries to mount on a hard surface, the bike likes to kick out from under them because the tires do not grip. This also happens when they dismount the bike and occasionally when they turn. At the very least, these tires need a rubber band around the wheels so they grip the hard surface when mounting or dismounting the bike.

- The bike is made of cheap plastic. The seat and fenders bend easily. The front fender started showing stress marks (see picture) from the children pushing on the front fender.

- The wheels have a vent hole which allows water to collect inside if it sits in a puddle. The water does drain, but if it sits too long without drying out, it could begin to cause mold problems inside the wheel.

This could be a very good balance bike if the manufacturer wouldn't cut corners on the quality of materials used to make the bike. This bike tips way too easily because of the cheap, hard plastic tires that have no grip. With the issue of the hard plastic tires and the cheap plastic seat and fender, it was all we could do to find enough reasons to give this bike a 3-star rating.

Review:
First, what I was looking for and like about this Schwinn Hybrid Bike. It has Disc Brakes, front Suspension Forks, and Trigger-style Shifters (not the Twist-style shifters I dislike and seemed like were on every bike in this price range).

It showed up earlier than normal Amazon packages and was nicely packed and secure inside the box.
It was fairly easy putting together. I took my time and referred to both the Assembly section of the Owners Manual that came with it and... what was even more useful, the you-tube video on Schwinn's website labeled How to Assemble a Schwinn Comfort Hybrid Bike. I would highly recommend using the video 1st and then referring to the Manual second if something is a little different. (The manual says to remove the top bolt and cap on the fork assembly to attach the handle-bars, which is unnecessary, and even more important, the manual shows the wrong direction on putting on one of the pedals, when installing, you need to thread both pedals toward the front wheel like the video shows)

I was pleasantly surprised that the rear wheel and brakes, chain, gears, cables were already assembled and ready to go. The front wheel fit right on and the disc slid right into the brake caliper perfectly. (The last made in china bike I put together was not as ready to go as this Schwinn and was a lot more difficult putting together) This Schwinn was basically putting on the front wheel, handle bars and seat... done.

I bought the 17 inch frame bike since I am only 5'5" and was thinking it was going to be small like a kids bike was surprised again at how big the bike actually is. I was also afraid that it might be like some smaller bikes and the pedals and crank would be too high so as to having your knees too bent while riding and making it difficult and uncomfortable to ride, but this is not the case. I get just a very slight knee bend at bottom stroke which is just right.

So far, the only thing I have found wrong with this bike is where the holes for the water bottle holder are located. On this 17" frame they are set too high and close to the upper frame so once a holder is mounted, there is not enough room for a regular size water bottle.

All in all, I really like this bike and would highly recommend it.

Just word of advice if you are attempting to use this bike for long commutes and you are seeing the advertised 40miles. It fits my needs and you wont be disappointed if you do opt for it. This bike is well built, its got a solid frame and the front suspension performs as it should making bumps on the road a breeze to ride over. My bike will read an error  while riding: code e-10 on the lcd screen that comes and goes while riding, the user manual provided makes no mention of error codes. I would find it helpful to know what this code is, and if I received a defective part on my bike. Geleisen team, hoping one of you reaches out to me about it. Besides the error code on my bike I am happy with my choice in purchasing this model.

Review: My bike arrived 4 days after I ordered it.  It is well packed.  Installation accessories came in a small box (pedals, L shape wrench, hex wrench 8 in 1 repair tool, red reflector light).  The assembly is quick and easy, my best advise is to go on youtube and find several videos they have to guide you thru the installation for your particular model.  A few days after I ordered my bike, I received an email with a link to a video explaining the process of assembling it. Follow it and you won't have any issues. Turn the front fork around and ensure the front brake cables are not twisted around the fork of the bike.  Disengage the front brake to install the wheel and that the pattern on your front wheel is facing the same direction as the rear wheel. When I was installing it I noticed 3 nuts, but they are 4, one was hiding inside a round plastic cover.  Ensure you put the pedals correctly (each one has a letter on it (L left & R right).  After you are done, you do not need to tighten the chain on the bike (I didn't).  The bike is bit heavy but that's to be expected with a full-size bike.  Watch a video before folding the bike, its tricky at first, but its easy once you get the hang of it.  The seat that comes with the bike is very basic and lacks spring suspension, but if you want a wider seat, buy it!  Overall, I am happy with my purchase.  I will review this item after I put a few miles on the bike.  So far, I will recommend it, the best part is that it folds and you do not have to mess around with bike racks on your car!

Review:
I bought two of these:

The 16 inch pink bike for my daughter who will be turning 5 next week. I did not take her measurements, but she is a size 5/6 in tops and pants right now for size comparison. The bike is great with the training wheels, but it is too big for her to handle without them yet. If I had known what I know now, I would have bought her one size down to start off with. For now, she can ride this with the training wheels to get used to it and we will just keep her much smaller bike until she has made a full transition.

The 18 inch blue bike for my 7 1/2 year old Son - Again, no measurements, but he is a size 7/8 for size comparison. This bike just fits with room to grow. Last fall, it would have been a bit too big for him.

Sizing aside, here are my thoughts:

This is a great bike! It is nice and sturdy and I love the look. (especially the fact that the pink is still girly looking but has BLACK tires, not the dang white tires that most little girls bikes have) We live on a farm, so no sidewalks. Just dirt, gravel and grass to ride on. These bikes ride just fine on the rough terrain.

My kids love that the bikes came with a water bottle.

The 16" bike has a firmer white seat with what sort of looks like a hand grip in the back. The 18" bike has a slightly cushier black seat - like a normal bike seat. Neither of the kids have mentioned that they especially love or dislike their seats. Both bikes have brake, which is nice - although my daughter is still getting used to using it - it's a little big for her tiny hands. I would recommend getting the hang of that before going down any hills

And last - the assembly was pretty easy - They came mostly assembled already. It took about 15 minutes per bike (Putzing speed) to get everything ready to go. One bike came with the tires ready to go and one bike needed to have the tires inflated before riding.

Overall, these have been great bikes so far and I am very pleased with the purchase!

Review:
After looking around for a few days, I saw this bike on clearance at a bigbox and snapped it up. Our son is 4 years old, but in the lower 30% for height (just like mom) so he's probably closer in size to the typical 3 1/2 year old. It came out of the box well-packed and protected. Color and stickers are more appealing in person than in photos; frankly, I considered it ugly based on pics but in person, I really like the look.

Assembly took 10 minutes with tools handy, primarily 12, 13, 14 and 15mm wrenches. Probably could have done it in 5min, but I also checked the tightness on the pre-assembled parts and lubed the chain. Due to my sons height, I also had to tweak the seat and handlebars to accommodate his shorter arms and legs. Having owned a bicycle before, I did not need the instructions: you are basically just putting on the front wheel, installing the handlebars, training wheels and seat. But if you really are unaccustomed to mechanical stuff, the directions looked only mildly incomprehensible.

Addressing other issues mentioned in reviews: Mine came out of the box in perfect condition. Everything was protected with cardboard. The frame IS less robust than many other comparable 16" boys bikes right now, but does not seem more fragile than the bike I remember owning 30 years ago, and I beat the heck out of mine. Most of the other bikes I looked at out there seemed to have incredibly overbuilt frames, and thus heavier than is comfortable for a 3-5yo to learn on.

Dislikes: No kickstand, though not yet needed with training wheels. No reflectors, though the bike itself is very visible (yellow with silver stickers). Pedals seemed a little small? With rounded outside ends. I'd prefer a square pedal, my sons feet slipped off them a few times when he started out.

In short: it aint a cadillac, but its pretty decently built and a good non-licensed-theme basic starter bike for 3-5 year olds.

Review:
Took battery out to charge had to modify to get battery back in position to allow to be plugged back in.
I’m a bit bummed about operation of motor drive. This is my second e bike. Peddle assist motor control takes over and propelles bike rather than allowing rider to peddle over motor speed. Also gears on this bike are very low, 7th gear is like 4th on my other 7 gear bike.

After 10 days I have had to change the rear break shoe, it could not be adjusted, the new one came with better mounting screws and washers. I also put a bigger ring gear on the crank, that made me put on a longer chain but gear interferes with the lever to fold the bike. Now bike is almost perfect.

I have to edit this after a couple of weeks of riding and making a few modifications. After my first review I was contacted by this company to help me make this a GREAT bike. Battery was easily fixed with a shim, they are sending me refund for replacement brake shoe I could not adjust to stop scraping on disc. I purchased this bike for the frame design, it folds and has back rack built and welded in the frame for a grocery basket. I have never bought a bike without making it my own, This is my 4th E-Bike modification. This is my first folding E-Bike. I will admit I changed motor control to feel more like my big bike, that I have invested a great deal of money in.
So my final take, buy this great bike, the company will respond quickly to resolve any issue and are wonderful to work with.

Review:
This bike has given me an opportunity to ride again, and not fear not having enough strength in my legs to venture out to far from home. I love this bike. I have not traveled that far on the bike yet, as I am still getting used to the rapid acceleration. I was a little shakey when I first got on the bike.

The hardest thing for me was getting it out of the box. Putting the bike together went quickly. Yes, the bike is heavy, but is certainly manageable. The fact that the bike folds is a plus. I am a 71 year old female, and one of my friends now refers to me as a “biker 🐥”!

I have been using an electric scooter to get around, due to a hip surgery complication/problem, but I can see myself using the bike more. My confidence has increased since purchasing the e-bike. I’m looking forward to going on trips, and taking the e-bike along.

I had been researching what type of bike to purchase over the past year, and I came very close to purchasing a bike that cost twice as much as the Ancheer Folding E-Bike. This bike is perfect for me. I don’t see any problems with the care & maintenance of this bike. I have gotten lots of compliments from the public about this bike, and lots of questions.

The bike arrived earlier than expected, and nothing was broken, or missing when I opened the box.
